ŌĆ£We all know coal plants have this continuous impact that is going to elevate the risk of asthma, heart attacks and death,ŌĆØ said Elena Krieger, who oversees scientific research at PSE Healthy Energy, a California-based policy institute. ŌĆ£But it is very, very difficult to pinpoint a single death. And that makes it very hard to point the finger at any given facility.ŌĆØ
The harm is considerable.
According to ReutersŌĆÖ COBRA analysis, the roughly 200 coal plants still operating in the U.S. each year cause up to 8,400 premature deaths and $130 billion in excess healthcare costs. BerkshireŌĆÖs coal plants are estimated to cause up to 260 deaths and $3.9 billion in health expenditures, the model shows. ReutersŌĆÖ analysis, Krieger said, offers ŌĆ£a reasonable portrait of the magnitude of the health impacts from these coal plants.ŌĆØ
The news agencyŌĆÖs COBRA calculations were also reviewed by Nicholas Mailloux, an expert in the health effects of energy systems at the University of Wisconsin. Tim Canty, a University of Maryland professor who is an expert on pollution at coal plants, reviewed ReutersŌĆÖ calculations of NOx emissions.
NOx, among the worst air pollutants, is a precursor for hazardous ground-level ozone and fine particulate matter. Wildfires and vehicle tailpipes are also large sources of NOx, which can travel over 1,000 miles to cause faraway smog and soot. The particulate pollution NOx helps form can penetrate the bloodstream and brain, contributing to respiratory issues, cancers and other illnesses. Children are particularly vulnerable.
Joel Schwartz, a Harvard University epidemiologist who studies pollution and human health, said there is no known safe threshold for exposure to soot particles formed from NOx. ŌĆ£ItŌĆÖs affecting systems that do bad things all over the human body,ŌĆØ he told Reuters.
Coal plants are also big emitters of climate-warming carbon dioxide, or CO2, a gas not removed by SCR scrubbers. A research report from the University of Massachusetts-Amherst last year found that Berkshire ranked fourth among the top CO2 emitting companies ŌĆō from any industry ŌĆō in the U.S. The report relied on data from 2022.
Unlike many big industrial firms, Berkshire doesnŌĆÖt issue annual reports breaking out its company-wide CO2 emissions. The company says it is aiming for net zero greenhouse gas emissions by 2050.
Coal-fired electricity is largely in its twilight in the U.S.
The countryŌĆÖs remaining coal plants generate about 16% of its electricity. ThatŌĆÖs almost twice the level of the European Union, which has more aggressively pursued renewable energy. But itŌĆÖs a fraction of the 52% reliance on coal in 1990, when the U.S. had nearly 500 coal plants, according to data from the U.S. Energy Information Administration and from Eurelectric, a Brussels-based industry group.
Wind and natural gas, now the leading fuel for U.S. power generation, account for much of BerkshireŌĆÖs electrical output. But the company still relies on coal for over a fifth of its generation, according to company data.
Berkshire recently slowed some initiatives to wean itself further.
In Utah, its PacifiCorp unit, after announcing plans to invest heavily in renewable energy, last year changed course and said it would prolong the life of its two coal plants there. In an April statement, PacifiCorp said slower-than-expected changes to regulation made it attractive to do so.
ŌĆ£The twilight of coal seems to be turning into an odd sunrise,ŌĆØ said Ulla Reeves, director of the clean air program at the National Parks Conservation Association, one of several environmental groups that in federal court have sought stricter regulations against the Utah plants.
Berkshire began adding electric utilities to its portfolio in 2000, when it purchased Iowa-based MidAmerican Energy. Among that companyŌĆÖs five coal-fired plants is Walter Scott Jr Energy Center, a 50-year-old facility in Council Bluffs, Iowa, 10 miles down the Missouri River from BerkshireŌĆÖs Omaha headquarters. MidAmericanŌĆÖs four other Iowa coal plants all add to BerkshireŌĆÖs NOx tally, but Walter Scott, EPA data show, is the largest industrial source of NOx pollution in the Omaha area, emitting 4,000 tons in 2023 alone.
The facility is named in honor of one of BuffettŌĆÖs childhood friends, who was also a big investor in MidAmerican. It operates with two boilers, one of which doesnŌĆÖt have an SCR scrubber. Pollution from the plant settles over Omaha on days when winds blow from the south and southeast, a typical weather pattern, according to Dan Goldberg, a George Washington University professor who uses satellite data to examine real-time changes in air quality.
In 2023, metro Omaha residents had ŌĆ£goodŌĆØ air to breathe during only half the days of the year, according to standards used by the EPAŌĆÖs Air Quality Index. It ranked 299 in air quality out of 416 small and large U.S. metro areas, according to a Reuters analysis of EPA data. Metro Omaha had 25 days in 2023 when the air quality was deemed unhealthy for sensitive groups, ranking in the bottom 10% among U.S. metro areas by this metric. That was largely caused by lung-damaging ozone topping EPA limits on 23 days, the most so far for Omaha this century.
ŌĆ£If I had an asthmatic child, I would definitely not want to live in Omaha,ŌĆØ said Wilma Subra, a Louisiana-based scientist and environmental consultant who formerly served on the EPAŌĆÖs National Environmental Justice Advisory Council.
Omaha Mayor Jean Stothert didnŌĆÖt respond to messages seeking comment on the cityŌĆÖs air quality.
In its email to Reuters, Berkshire said MidAmerican is investing billions in renewable energy. Its Iowa coal plants operate under the ŌĆ£detailed oversight of local regulatory agenciesŌĆØ and as part of MidAmericanŌĆÖs ŌĆ£promise to customers to deliver safe, reliable and affordable energy.ŌĆØ
One day last year, a massive coal heap sat outside the Walter Scott plant. When wind kicks up over the Missouri River, black dust flies from the stockpile into nearby neighborhoods. ŌĆ£ItŌĆÖs like a black cloud rolling through,ŌĆØ said Treg Hecker, who lives two miles away and says he sometimes has to hose the powder off the sides of his house. Medical research shows that communities near coal stockpiles can have increased risk of lung, kidney and other ailments because of such dust.
ŌĆ£Our facilities employ methods to eliminate coal dust from leaving the facility,ŌĆØ Berkshire said in its email, ŌĆ£and our current leadership team is not aware of any formal complaints that have been filed with regulators.ŌĆØ
About 11 miles northwest of Walter Scott lies North Omaha, a predominantly Black area with the highest prevalence of asthma in Nebraska. The asthma rate in North Omaha is nearly 13% of the population, according to 2021 data from the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, outpacing the state average of 8.8% and the national average of 7.7%.
For years, members of OmahaŌĆÖs Black community have complained about pollution from another nearby coal plant, North Omaha Station. The plant, which operates without an SCR scrubber, is just two miles from the neighborhood. Operated by Omaha Public Power District, a state-run utility, it has been targeted at public hearings by neighborhood activists pressing for stricter pollution curbs there.
OPPD declined to comment.
Over the past 20 years, EPA data show, Walter Scott has produced 403,000 tons of NOx and soot-producing sulfur dioxide pollution, 22% more than North Omaha Station. Preston Love Jr, a longtime advocate for North Omaha residents, said the risks from BerkshireŌĆÖs plant have largely escaped community notice.
ŌĆ£People are not aware of what is happening to them,ŌĆØ Love told Reuters. ŌĆ£They live and die without ever knowing.ŌĆØ
In September 2023, Memphis Zabawa, a 12-year-old boy from West Omaha, another of the cityŌĆÖs neighborhoods, competed in a local cross-country race. Despite an asthma diagnosis, Memphis was running at a time of high pollution in Omaha. The cityŌĆÖs Air Quality Index, a metric public officials use to warn the public about unhealthy air, that week topped 154 for tiny soot pollution, a measure of fine particle emissions, the highest score of the year. Soot pollution on race day registered a score of 75, a ŌĆ£moderateŌĆØ level that can still pose risks for some asthmatics.
During the race, Memphis carried his asthma inhaler and used it at least once, an Omaha Police Department report shows. But while running down a hill, he collapsed. His heart stopped beating. A nurse raced to MemphisŌĆÖ side and started CPR, police said. Paramedics rushed him to a local hospital, where he recovered.
Medical research has shown that coal plant pollution can exacerbate asthma and that tiny soot particles can trigger heart attacks. ThereŌĆÖs no way to know, though, whether that weekŌĆÖs air quality, let alone emissions from local power plants, caused MemphisŌĆÖ cardiac arrest. Linking a precise cause to any individualŌĆÖs illness is often difficult, even impossible. MemphisŌĆÖ parents, Erin and Justin Zabawa, told Reuters they canŌĆÖt assign blame for their sonŌĆÖs collapse.
About 1,000 miles to the west, Utah is known for Rocky Mountain slopes, red rock arches and national parks with panoramic vistas. Less advertised are the stateŌĆÖs air quality problems, which experts say in part stem from heavy reliance on coal power ŌĆō source of nearly half of UtahŌĆÖs electricity, federal energy data show, around three times the national average.
Ringed by mountains, Salt Lake City, the stateŌĆÖs fast-growing capital, often ranks among the worst cities on the American Lung AssociationŌĆÖs list for ozone pollution and dangerous particulate matter. The valleyŌĆÖs topography traps smog from industrial emissions, vehicles and other sources, sometimes creating a bowl of hazy pollution.
Hunter and Huntington, the two coal plants Berkshire owns more than 100 miles to the south of Salt Lake City, generate enough electricity to power about 800,000 homes. Built in the 1970s, during a coal mining boom in central Utah, the plants became part of BerkshireŌĆÖs portfolio in 2006, when it acquired PacifiCorp, owner of utility companies in several western states. Neither of the plants is equipped with SCR scrubbers.
In 2023, EPA data show, Hunter and Huntington had NOx pollution rates around 60% higher than the national average for coal plants, and more than twice the average rate of plants with SCR scrubbers. The two plants are responsible for some of Salt LakeŌĆÖs smog, causing up to $8.4 million a year in healthcare costs there, according to EPA data.
Much of their emissions also fuel regional haze pollution elsewhere, notably at Arches and Canyonlands national parks. The natural range of visibility at Canyonlands, which attracts hundreds of thousands of visitors to its gorges and buttes each year, on a clear day can span a majestic 200 miles. But in 2023, pollution some days dimmed the range by nearly 40%, according to federal visibility data for national parks.
The pollution is caused by a variety of industrial sources and vehicles. Within five years, however, the Hunter and Huntington coal plants are projected to be the top two emitters of the NOx particle pollution at Canyonlands, according to the EPA. The plantsŌĆÖ NOx output also helps form health-harming ozone pollution. Canyonlands and nearby wilderness areas have exceeded the federal health limit for ozone on 19 different days since 2018, including three in 2024, according to EPA air monitor data.
Hunter and Huntington also embody BerkshireŌĆÖs success opposing stricter NOx curbs, including a well-documented legal battle that enabled it to avoid the installation of SCR scrubbers there. Led by Abel, the Buffett protege and Berkshire energy boss, pushback against regulators has been driven by his focus on the bottom line, according to people familiar with the companyŌĆÖs tactics.
Abel is ŌĆ£a consummate numbers guy,ŌĆØ said Steven Guyer, an attorney and former environmental director at MidAmerican, BerkshireŌĆÖs first utility. ŌĆ£Everything is about the earnings.ŌĆØ Guyer left the company in 2008 and is now an outspoken critic of BerkshireŌĆÖs environmental policies at the Iowa Environmental Council, an organization that advocates for clean energy.
In its email to Reuters, Berkshire said it has made some of PacifiCorpŌĆÖs plants cleaner even without SCR scrubbers. Last year, for instance, PacifiCorp converted two of the four coal boilers at its Jim Bridger plant, in Wyoming, to run on natural gas.
In 2015, during the administration of former President Barack Obama, the EPA was evaluating tougher ozone pollution standards. That year, according to agency memos, Abel met twice with Gina McCarthy, EPA administrator at the time. The memos didnŌĆÖt disclose details about the meetings.
The EPA declined to comment on the meetings.
McCarthy didnŌĆÖt respond to requests for comment.
The next year, the EPA ordered PacifiCorp to install SCR scrubbers at Hunter and Huntington. Within five years, the agency said, the scrubbers would cut NOx pollution from the two plants by around 80%. ŌĆ£EPA acts to reduce regional haze in Utah and improve visibility in National Parks,ŌĆØ read a press release about the decision at the time.
Months later, PacifiCorp filed suit to block the plan in a Denver federal court. During two years of litigation, Berkshire resisted the EPAŌĆÖs orders, arguing that the steep price tag for two SCR scrubbers at each of the plants would outweigh the benefits. The estimated total cost of $711 million, it said, would force PacifiCorp to hike rates for customers.
The EPA hired Andover Technology Partners, a Boston-area pollution control consultant, to audit BerkshireŌĆÖs estimate. Andover said SCR projects at the two plants could be done for $517 million, or about 30% less.
PacifiCorp, the consultancy found, overestimated costs by including items like parking lot signs, crushed rock for roadways and employee eye-wash stations. Those expenses, Andover said, have nothing to do with SCR controls, adding that PacifiCorp also inflated estimates for property taxes and the fuel needed to generate steam. ŌĆ£They make the projects look as expensive as possible,ŌĆØ said James Staudt, Andover TechnologyŌĆÖs owner.
In 2016 comments published by the EPA, PacifiCorp described AndoverŌĆÖs review as ŌĆ£grossly flawed.ŌĆØ The EPA, in the same comments, defended the report.
In 2020, under the administration of former President Donald Trump, the EPA reversed the Obama-era order to install SCR scrubbers at Hunter and Huntington. Because PacifiCorp in 2015 had shut another Utah plant, also coal-fired, it had helped the government meet long-term goals, the agency said.
As of 2023, Berkshire had avoided installing SCR scrubbers on all but four of 24 coal-fired boilers at a dozen power plants, EPA data show. In Utah and Wyoming alone, according to a letter PacifiCorp sent the EPA in 2022, Berkshire has saved up to $2 billion by not having to install more advanced pollution controls.
In recent years, air quality experts and clean power advocates had some new reasons to be hopeful about Utah. In a May 2023 regulatory filing, PacifiCorp said it planned to shutter Hunter and Huntington by 2032. In their place, the company wrote, it was considering construction of two small nuclear plants on the sites.
The company was planning other clean energy investments over the next decade, too. In regulatory filings, PacifiCorp said the investments would create 13 gigawatts of renewable wind and solar power infrastructure in various Western states, enough electricity to power roughly 10 million average U.S. homes.
But optimism was short-lived.
In June 2023, PacifiCorp lost a high-profile lawsuit filed by fire survivors in Oregon, where a jury found its power lines responsible for sparking a wildfire. The judgment raised the specter that Berkshire utilities could face billions more in similar liabilities. Forced to set aside cash, regulatory filings show, PacifiCorp pared back many of its planned investments in renewable power.
In April of last year, PacifiCorp said in a regulatory filing that it would keep its Utah coal plants running for years longer, still with no SCR scrubbers. HuntingtonŌĆÖs operations are now expected to last an additional four years, until 2036, and HunterŌĆÖs a full decade longer than previously foreseen, until 2042, the filing said.
Pollution from the plants doesnŌĆÖt just stay in Utah. COBRA modeling shows it also increases health harms hundreds of miles away, in big cities like Denver, Los Angeles and Phoenix.
Separately, the EPA recently suffered a setback to its ŌĆ£Good Neighbor Plan,ŌĆØ part of a longstanding initiative to limit the impact that coal plant emissions have on surrounding states. Under the plan, UtahŌĆÖs coal plants would be subject to new rules that crack down on interstate ozone pollution. In June, however, the U.S. Supreme Court blocked the plan , pending further litigation from opponents including Berkshire. Among utility rivals, the industrial giant has been the most active in its opposition, submitting 125 different written objections to the initiative, according to an EPA summary document.
The decision leaves ŌĆ£upwind States free to keep contributing significantly to their downwind neighborsŌĆÖ ozone problems,ŌĆØ wrote Justice Amy Coney Barrett in a dissenting opinion. ItŌĆÖs unclear what changes may occur to the federal regulatory landscape in the next Trump administration, but the incoming president campaigned on promises to curb environmental rules and encourage energy companies to further tap fossil fuels.
State officials in Utah have been friendly to the industry. As with other supporters of continued coal generation, Utah legislators have cited the fuelŌĆÖs reliability compared with wind and solar power, whose output can vary depending on weather and daylight. ŌĆ£Many people sleep better knowing these coal plants can still supply the grid,ŌĆØ said James Scott Holladay, an energy economist at the University of Tennessee.
In March, the Utah State Legislature passed a law that could preserve coal as a preferential power source there. Utah lawmakers also offered PacifiCorp protection against wildfire liabilities, allocating taxpayer money to a fund that could cover costs from blazes sparked by power lines.
At last yearŌĆÖs shareholder meeting, Buffett and Abel lauded the fund and praised Utah for providing electric utilities with a favorable regulatory environment. Utah, Abel said, is ŌĆ£the best example we have. ItŌĆÖs the gold standard across the country.ŌĆØ
By Tim McLaughlin and Joshua Schneyer
To determine BerkshireŌĆÖs NOx emissions from its fleet of 12 U.S. coal plants, and to compare their NOx emissions rate against those of other large corporate coal power plant operators, Reuters analyzed public EPA emissions data for 2023, the most recent full calendar year available.
The EPA breaks out the data for the 200 or so coal-powered plants that still supply electricity to the U.S. power grid.
The data show coal boilers at plants Berkshire controls released 35,891 tons of NOx in 2023, more than any other single coal plant fleet operator in the country. Reuters also compared Berkshire plantsŌĆÖ average NOx emissions rate to that of other large U.S. corporate coal plant fleets ŌĆō those owned by the 20 utility operators with coal plants in the Standard and PoorŌĆÖs Utilities Index.
The NOx rate takes into account a coal fleetŌĆÖs NOx emissions by weight relative to the heat energy it produces from burning coal, in British thermal units, or BTUs, a standard methodology used by the EPA.
BerkshireŌĆÖs coal fleet had a higher average NOx emissions rate ŌĆō 0.163 pounds per million BTUs ŌĆō than any of its big corporate utility peers. It did not have advanced pollution controls ŌĆō selective catalytic reduction, or SCR, scrubbers ŌĆō on most of its coal-fired boilers.
To estimate the healthcare costs and negative health outcomes attributable to BerkshireŌĆÖs coal plant emissions in the Omaha metropolitan area and nationwide, Reuters used the EPAŌĆÖs Co-Benefits Risk Assessment Health Impacts Screening and Mapping Tool, or COBRA.
COBRA provides the range of estimated health costs associated with coal plant pollution in a given area. Reuters used the EPAŌĆÖs baseline emissions data estimates for 2023. COBRA results for metro Omaha include the upper range of estimated impacts within the "Omaha, NEŌĆōIA, Metropolitan Statistical Area (MSA)," an eight county, bi-state area including the City of Omaha and surrounding areas.
To explore the air quality metrics in metro Omaha and elsewhere, Reuters analyzed the EPAŌĆÖs Air Quality Index data, which relies on air monitoring equipment nationwide. Reuters analyzed air quality data from 416 U.S. urban areas. The analysis excluded areas where air monitoring readings were incomplete.

item.title: Hungary's election winner Magyar says he does not back Ukraine's fast-track EU entry
item.description:
BUDAPEST, April 13 (Reuters) - Hungary's opposition ŌĆŗleader Peter ŌĆīMagyar, whose party won ŌĆŗelections on ŌĆŗSunday with a ŌüĀlandslide, ŌĆŗsaid on Monday ŌĆŗthat his government would not ŌĆŗsupport ŌĆŗUkraine's fast-track entry to ŌĆīthe ŌüĀEuropean Union.
Magyar told a briefing ŌĆŗthat ŌĆŗthe ŌüĀissue of Ukraine's ŌĆŗmembership would ŌĆŗhave ŌüĀto go to ŌüĀa ŌĆŗreferendum.

item.title: Israel presses assault on Lebanon border town ahead of US-hosted talks
item.description:
BEIRUT/JERUSALEM, April 13 (Reuters) - Israeli troops launched an attack to seize a key south Lebanon town ŌĆŗfrom Hezbollah fighters holed up inside on Monday, pressing the war on the Iran-backed group on the eve of historic talks between Israeli and ŌĆīLebanese government envoys.
With the Lebanese and Israeli ambassadors to the United States set to meet on Tuesday in Washington, Lebanon's foreign minister said Beirut would use face-to-face negotiations to press for a ceasefire in the war that has complicated wider diplomacy to halt the conflict in the Middle East .
But the outlook for the meeting -- a rare, face-to-face encounter between countries formally in a state ŌĆŗof war -- has been overshadowed, with Israel saying it won't discuss a ceasefire while Hezbollah has objected to negotiations with Israel, reflecting sharply worsening ŌĆŗpolitical tensions in Lebanon.
On the ground in south Lebanon, the Israeli military completed its encirclement of the town of Bint Jbeil ŌüĀjust over the border and had begun a ground assault there, an Israeli military spokesperson and Lebanese security sources said.
The Lebanese sources said Hezbollah fighters holed up ŌĆŗinside were ready to fight to the death, citing the strategic and symbolic significance of Bint Jbeil, a Hezbollah stronghold, provincial capital, and gateway to surrounding villages.
An Israeli military official said full operational control of Bint Jbeil would be achieved within days, and that only a small number of militants remained in the area.
On Sunday, Hezbollah said it had attacked Israeli forces in and around Bint Jbeil with rockets, artillery fire and suicide drones.
A foreign security official based in Lebanon said seizing Bint Jbeil would give ŌĆŗIsrael better control over the entirety of Lebanon's southeastern border strip, leaving the western area of the border zone, which is largely forest and harder to clear.
Hezbollah ŌĆŗopened fire on Israel in support of Tehran on March 2, igniting an Israeli offensive that Lebanese authorities say has killed more than 2,000 people and forced more than 1 ŌĆīmillion people ŌüĀfrom their homes.
Israel says it aims to occupy south Lebanon up to the Litani River, which meets the Mediterranean about 30 km (20 miles) from Israel's border.
Israel and the U.S. have said the campaign against Hezbollah was not part of a fragile Iran-U.S. ceasefire, though Pakistan's prime minister, a key intermediary, had said the truce would include Lebanon.
While fighting in Lebanon has not stopped, Israel has launched no airstrikes on Beirut since Wednesday, when it pounded the capital ŌĆŗduring an onslaught that killed hundreds of ŌĆŗpeople across the country.
The U.S. ambassador ŌüĀto Lebanon, Michel Issa, will host Tuesday's Washington meeting between Israeli ambassador Yechiel Leiter and his Lebanese counterpart Nada Hamadeh Moawad.
Lebanese culture minister Ghassan Salameh, speaking in an interview with Lebanese broadcaster Al Jadeed on Sunday, said seeking a ceasefire was ŌĆŗthe only substantive issue that Moawad had been authorised to discuss.
Israel's embassy in Washington last week said the talks would ŌĆŗconstitute the start of "formal ŌüĀpeace negotiations" and that Israel had refused to discuss a ceasefire with Hezbollah.
Lebanese Foreign Minister Youssef Raggi, a member of the staunchly anti-Hezbollah Lebanese Forces party, said Lebanon was trying to reach a ceasefire through direct negotiations.
In a phone call with German Foreign Minister Johann Wadephul, Raggi said "establishing this track has effectively established the separation between the Lebanese ŌüĀfile and ŌĆŗthe Iranian track", Raggi posted on X.
A senior Lebanese political source said the talks were taking ŌĆŗplace without any national consensus because both Hezbollah and its Shi'ite Muslim ally, Parliament Speaker Nabih Berri, opposed negotiations before a ceasefire. Another source familiar with their position said Lebanon should not sit at ŌĆŗthe table with Israel while "our people are being killed".

item.title: NATO allies refuse to join Trump's Strait of Hormuz blockade
item.description:
LONDON/PARIS, April 13 (Reuters) - The United States' NATO allies said on Monday they would not get involved in President Donald Trump's plan to blockade ŌĆŗthe Strait of Hormuz , further ratcheting up tensions within the increasingly fragile alliance.
Trump said the U.S. ŌĆīmilitary would work with other countries to block all maritime traffic in the waterway, after weekend talks failed to reach an agreement to end the six-week conflict with Iran . The U.S. military later specified that the blockade , due to start at 1400 GMT on Monday, would only apply ŌĆŗto ships going to or from Iranian ports.
"The Blockade will begin shortly. Other Countries will be involved with ŌĆŗthis Blockade," Trump said in a post on Truth Social on Sunday.
But NATO allies including Britain ŌüĀand France said they would not be drawn into the conflict by taking part in the blockade, saying instead ŌĆŗthat it was vital to open the waterway through which one-fifth of the world's oil usually passes, which Iran has ŌĆŗeffectively closed since the conflict began on February 28.
Their refusal to participate is yet another point of friction with Trump, who has threatened to withdraw from the military alliance and is weighing pulling some U.S. troops from Europe after several countries resisted supporting the U.S. campaign against Iran ŌĆŗby denying U.S. military planes use of their airspace.
"We're not supporting the blockade," British Prime Minister Keir Starmer ŌĆŗtold the BBC.
"My decision has been very clearly that whatever the pressure, and there's been some considerable pressure, we're not getting dragged into ŌĆīthe ŌüĀwar," he said.
NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte told European governments that Trump wants concrete commitments in the near future to help secure the Strait of Hormuz, diplomats told Reuters last week.
NATO could play a role in the strait if its 32 members could agree on the formation of a mission, Rutte said on April 9.
Several European countries have said they're willing to ŌĆŗhelp in the strait but ŌĆŗonly once there is a ŌüĀdurable end to hostilities and an agreement with Iran that their ships will not be attacked.
France will organise a conference with Britain and other countries to create a multinational mission ŌĆŗto restore navigation in the strait, French President Emmanuel Macron said on X on ŌĆŗMonday.
"This strictly defensive ŌüĀmission, distinct from the belligerents, will be deployed as soon as the situation allows," Macron said.
Britain is working on ways to reduce insurance premiums for ships passing through the strait once the fighting has stopped, according to a senior European official.
The Strait of ŌüĀHormuz ŌĆŗshould be reopened by diplomacy, Turkish Foreign Minister Hakan Fidan said on ŌĆŗMonday, adding that creating an international force to oversee it would be complicated as he called for NATO to reset its ties with Trump at ŌĆŗa summit in Ankara in July.

item.title: Diplomatic push underway on Hormuz fertiliser proposal, UN says, as shortages bite
item.description:
GENEVA, April 13 (Reuters) - A diplomatic push is under way on a U.N.-led proposal to ensure safe passage for fertiliser ŌĆŗshipments through the Strait of Hormuz, the U.N. said on Monday, with ŌĆīa source familiar with discussions emphasising growing urgency due to shortages.
The six weeks of fighting sparked by U.S.-Israeli joint strikes on Iran, which spread to Lebanon and the Gulf, have ŌĆŗkilled thousands of people across the Middle East, upending global supply chains following ŌĆŗa near-total halt to shipping via the Strait of Hormuz.
The U.S. ŌüĀmilitary said it will begin a blockade of all maritime traffic entering and ŌĆŗexiting Iranian ports and coastal areas on Monday, after failed weekend talks to ŌĆŗend the war jeopardized a fragile two-week ceasefire.
There is an urgent need to find a solution, a source familiar with the U.N.-led initiative told Reuters, noting that some farmers ŌĆŗin Latin America are already skipping second corn plantings and that African ŌĆŗgrowers face a critical window, with severe yield losses looming without fertilisers.
"Extensive meetings" have been ŌĆīheld ŌüĀin New York over the past two weeks on the design and operationalisation of the mechanism with the relevant member states, Juliette Touma, UNOPS director of communications, told Reuters.
The meetings, led by Jorge Moreira da Silva, executive ŌĆŗdirector of the United ŌĆŗNations Office for ŌüĀProject Services, are ongoing, Touma added.
The proposed mechanism , opens new tab seeks to facilitate safe transit of commercial vessels carrying fertilizers and related ŌĆŗmaterials through the strait and mitigate potential humanitarian fallout on fertilizer-dependent ŌĆŗnations in ŌüĀAsia and Africa, she said.
A more-than-90% drop in tanker traffic is threatening agricultural production and global food security, the UN Food and Agriculture Organization says.
Businesses seek ŌüĀa ŌĆŗmechanism that would restore freedom of navigation without ŌĆŗthe need for naval escorts or tolls, the source familiar with discussions said, adding that the initiative could ŌĆŗeventually expand to other goods.

item.title: Pakistan PM Sharif says 'full effort' still on to resolve US-Iran conflict
item.description: ISLAMABAD, April 13 (Reuters) - Full efforts ŌĆŗare still ŌĆībeing made to ŌĆŗresolve the ŌĆŗconflict between the ŌüĀU.S. ŌĆŗand Iran, ŌĆŗPakistani Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif ŌĆŗsaid ŌĆŗon Monday, a day ŌĆīafter ŌüĀtalks between the two ŌĆŗsides, ŌĆŗheld ŌüĀin Islamabad, ended ŌĆŗwithout ŌĆŗagreement.

item.title: Airlines cancel flights amid Middle East conflict
item.description:
April 13 - Global air travel remains severely disrupted, with many people unable to fly as planned to destinations after the Iran war forced the closure of major Middle Eastern hubs, including Dubai, Doha and Abu Dhabi.
Below is the latest on flights, in alphabetical order:
AEGEAN AIRLINES (AGNr.AT) , opens new tab
Greece's largest carrier cancelled flights to Riyadh and Amman until June 27 and to Tel Aviv and Beirut until June 26. It cancelled ŌĆŗflights to Erbil and Baghdad until July 2 and to Dubai until June 29.
Latvia's airBaltic says flights to Tel Aviv have been cancelled until May 31. Flights to Dubai ŌĆīare cancelled until October 24.
AIR CANADA (AC.TO) , opens new tab
The Canadian carrier has cancelled flights to Tel Aviv and Dubai until September 7.
The Spanish airline has cancelled flights to Tel Aviv until May 3.
AIR FRANCE-KLM (AIRF.PA) , opens new tab
Air France has suspended its Tel Aviv, Beirut, Dubai and Riyadh flights until May 3.
KLM has suspended flights to Riyadh, Dammam and Dubai until May 17.
CATHAY PACIFIC (0293.HK) , opens new tab
The Hong Kong airline has cancelled flights to Dubai and Riyadh until June 30. To cater for a surge in demand to Europe, it will operate ŌĆŗextra passenger flights to London, Paris and Zurich in April.
DELTA (DAL.N) , opens new tab
The U.S. carrier has cancelled its New York-Tel Aviv flights and delayed the restart of its Atlanta-Tel Aviv route until September 5. It ŌĆŗsaid the launch of its Boston-Tel Aviv route, planned for late October, has been delayed until further notice.
EL AL ISRAEL AIRLINES (ELAL.TA) , opens new tab
The Israeli carrier said customers who ŌüĀplanned to depart Israel through April 18 have had their flights cancelled, including relevant return flights.
It will increase the number of destinations to about 30 from April 13 and will gradually expand that number through ŌĆŗthe rest of the month.
The UAE airline has said it is operating a reduced flight schedule following a partial reopening of regional airspace.
The UAE carrier has said it is operating a commercial flight schedule between ŌĆŗAbu Dhabi and around 80 destinations.
FINNAIR (FIA1S.HE) , opens new tab
The Finnish carrier cancelled its Doha flights until July 2, while continuing to avoid the airspace of Iraq, Iran, Syria and Israel. The airline only restarts its Dubai flights in October.
FLYNAS (4264.SE) , opens new tab
The Saudi budget airline has suspended flights to Dubai, Abu Dhabi, Sharjah, Doha, Bahrain, Kuwait, Iraq and Syria until April 15.
IAG (ICAG.L) , opens new tab
IAG-owned British Airways is reducing flights to the Middle East when services resume, permanently dropping Jeddah as a destination, while adding capacity to India and Africa.
It ŌĆŗplans to reduce services to Dubai, Doha and Tel Aviv to one daily flight from JulyŌĆ» 1, and to cut Riyadh services from two daily flights to one from midŌĆæMay. Changes apply through the summer ŌĆŗseason that ends on October 24, with one Dubai service restarting on October 16.
IAG's Spanish low-cost airline Iberia Express has cancelled flights to Tel Aviv through May 31.
JAPAN AIRLINES (9201.T) , opens new tab
Japan Airlines has suspended scheduled Tokyo-Doha flights until May 10 and ŌĆīDoha-Tokyo flights ŌüĀuntil May 11. The airline also announced extra flights between Tokyo and London on April 25.
The Polish airline suspended its flights to Tel Aviv until May 31. It also cancelled flights to Riyadh until June 30 and to Beirut from March 31 to May 30. The airline plans to operate its winter route to Dubai in October.
LUFTHANSA GROUP (LHAG.DE) , opens new tab
Lufthansa, Swiss, Austrian Airlines, Brussels Airlines, ITA Airways and Edelweiss suspended flights to Dubai and Tel Aviv until May 31, and to Abu Dhabi, Amman, Beirut, Dammam, Riyadh, Erbil, Muscat and Tehran until October 24. Lufthansa Cargo is the same, except for the Tel Aviv suspension, which will last through April ŌĆŗ30.
Low-cost carrier Eurowings plans to suspend flights to Tel ŌĆŗAviv, Beirut and Erbil through April 30 and ŌüĀto Dubai, Abu Dhabi and Amman through October 24.
The Malaysian carrier suspended flights to Doha until June 14.
NORWEGIAN AIR (NAS.OL) , opens new tab
The low-cost airline has pushed back planned launches of its Tel Aviv and Beirut services to June 15.
PEGASUS (PGSUS.IS) , opens new tab
Turkey's Pegasus Airlines cancelled its Iran, Iraq, Amman, Beirut, Kuwait, Bahrain, Doha, Dammam, Riyadh, Dubai, Abu Dhabi ŌĆŗand Sharjah flights until May 1.
The Moroccan carrier says flights to Doha are cancelled until June 30 and those to Dubai until May ŌĆŗ31.
QANTAS (QAN.AX) , opens new tab
Australia's flag carrier is adding ŌüĀflights to Rome and Paris to meet an upswing in demand for European routes. Flights to Paris will increase to five return flights per week from three and the Perth-Singapore service will increase from daily to 10 flights per week. An updated schedule will come into effect progressively for flights from mid-April and run until late July.
The carrier said it is gradually increasing flights from Doha to more than 120 destinations by mid-May.
SINGAPORE AIRLINES (SIAL.SI) , opens new tab
The carrier ŌüĀextended its ŌĆŗSingapore-Dubai flights suspension until May 31, while adding services on the Singapore-London Gatwick and Singapore-Melbourne routes from late March until October ŌĆŗ24 to meet higher demand.
TURKISH AIRLINES (THYAO.IS) , opens new tab
SunExpress, Turkish Airlines' joint venture with Lufthansa, has cancelled flights to Dubai until April 30.
WIZZ AIR (WIZZ.L) , opens new tab
The low-cost airline suspended flights to Israel until April 25, and to Dubai, Abu Dhabi and Amman from mainland European destinations until mid-September. All ŌĆŗflights to Medina have been suspended indefinitely.

item.title: Nigerian airstrike at village market is latest in which civilians have been killed
item.description:
LAGOS, April 13 - A Nigerian airstrike at a village market in which more than 200 people are feared dead is the latest incident in ŌĆŗwhich the military has hit civilian targets while pursuing Islamist ŌĆīmilitants or armed groups.
Here are some of the recent incidents where airstrikes killed civilians.
April 2026 - At least 200 people were feared dead after Nigerian military jets struck a village ŌĆŗmarket while pursuing Islamist militants in the northeast of the ŌĆŗcountry on Saturday night, a councillor for the area and ŌüĀresidents said on Sunday.
January 2025 - Nigeria's Air Force said it was investigating ŌĆŗreports of civilian casualties during an airstrike that targeted armed gangs in the ŌĆŗnorthwest . Residents told Reuters that at least 15 civilians, including local security guards, were killed.
December 2024 - Nigeria's military acknowledged that an airstrike targeting a Lakurawa militant group hideout ŌĆŗin the northwestern Sokoto state killed 10 civilians because of secondary explosions.
September ŌĆŗ2024 - At least 24 people were killed in an airstrike on a village in ŌĆīKaduna state ŌüĀduring an Air Force operation targeting armed gangs and their hideout.
April 2024 - An airstrike on a village in Nigeria's northwestern Zamfara state killed at least 33 people during Eid after a military operation targeting armed kidnapping ŌĆŗgangs and their ŌĆŗhideouts.
December 2023 - At ŌüĀleast 85 people, including women and children, were killed following a military drone attack in northern Kaduna state. ŌĆŗTwo military officers faced court-martial for the incident after ŌĆŗPresident Bola ŌüĀTinubu ordered an investigation.
January 2023 - Dozens of ethnic Fulani herders were killed in an aerial bombing in the central Nigerian state of Nasarawa as they ŌüĀwere ŌĆŗunloading cattle retrieved from authorities in a ŌĆŗneighbouring state. The incident was the subject of a Reuters special report and the Nigerian Air ŌĆŗForce later acknowledged responsibility for it.

item.title: EU's von der Leyen compares Orban defeat to Hungary's anti-Soviet uprising
item.description:
April 13 - EU Commission President Ursula von der Leyen hailed Hungary's election as "a victory for fundamental freedoms", and compared the ousting of ŌĆŗnationalist Viktor Orban to the country's 1956 anti-Soviet uprising ŌĆīand its 1989 break with communism.
Hungarians were waking up to a political earthquake after a landslide victory for the centre-right opposition reverberated everywhere from ŌĆŗWashington to Kyiv, sending local markets surging and turning ŌĆŗBudapest into a party zone.
"I really want to ŌüĀsay to the Hungarian people, you've done it again!" von ŌĆŗder Leyen told reporters in Brussels on Monday.
"Again against all odds, ŌĆŗlike you did in 1956 when you courageously stood up, like you did in 1989 when you were the first to cut the barbed ŌĆŗwire that was dividing our continent."
Orban was Russian President ŌĆŗVladimir Putin's main ally in the European Union, and regularly played an adversarial ŌĆīrole ŌüĀin the bloc, frustrating its efforts to aid war-torn Ukraine.
His exit after 16 years as prime minister could move Hungary more towards the mainstream in the EU, analysts have said.
Hungary's ŌĆŗ1956 uprising against ŌĆŗthe Soviet Union ŌüĀwas brutally crushed by the Red Army.
On the 50th anniversary of the revolt, Orban's Fidesz party ŌĆŗsought to align itself with the rebels' anti-communist ŌĆŗideals, ŌüĀto the outrage of the left, liberals and some on the right.
Orban, a fiery anti-communist youth leader during the Cold War, was ŌüĀa ŌĆŗpatriotic hero to supporters, but critics ŌĆŗat home and abroad accused him of taking Hungary on an authoritarian path.

item.title: 'We have been in exile': Norway government back to old quarters 15 years after Breivik bomb
item.description:
OSLO, April 13 (Reuters) - Fifteen years after far-right fanatic Anders Behring Breivik detonated a bomb damaging much of Norway's government quarters, the prime minister and his government on Monday celebrated the ŌĆŗreturn of several ministries to the redeveloped area.
On July 22, 2011, Breivik ŌĆŗdetonated a car bomb outside the PM's office, killing eight, before gunning ŌüĀdown 69 , mostly teenagers, at a Labour Party youth camp on Utoeya ŌĆŗisland.
The explosion in Oslo caused massive damage to the office tower then housing the ŌĆŗPM's office as well as adjacent government buildings, forcing ministries to move into temporary sites scattered across the city.
Monday marks the official reopening of the quarters following extensive renovations of existing ŌĆŗbuildings, as well as the construction of new ones.
"We have been in exile," ŌĆŗPrime Minister Jonas Gahr Stoere told reporters on Monday as he showcased his new office ŌĆīfor ŌüĀthe first time, adding that the return was a historic day.
"It is an important part of history for us: it was the Labour Party that was bombed out of these offices, and it is the Labour Party that is coming back," ŌĆŗhe said.
Today, Stoere ŌĆŗleads a minority ŌüĀLabour government. At the time of the bombing, he was foreign minister of a Labour-led government of then-prime minister Jens ŌĆŗStoltenberg.
In classic Scandinavian style, Stoere's office is clad in Norwegian ŌĆŗwood and ŌüĀis decorated with artwork representing various Norwegian landscapes.
Stoere brought his own personal touch to the office ŌĆö a 1995 picture of then South African President Nelson Mandela, looking through ŌüĀthe ŌĆŗbars of his former prison cell on Robben ŌĆŗIsland together with ex-Norwegian Prime Gro Harlem Brundtland, Stoere's former boss.
"This picture is a reminder that we ŌĆŗare part of something bigger," Stoere said.

item.title: Pope Leo criticizes 'neocolonial' powers hours after Trump attack
item.description:
ALGIERS, April 13 (Reuters) - Pope Leo criticized violations of ŌĆŗinternational law by 'neocolonial' world ŌĆīpowers in a forceful speech on Monday ŌĆŗduring an Africa ŌĆŗtour, hours after U.S. ŌüĀPresident Donald Trump's direct ŌĆŗattack on the ŌĆŗleader of the 1.4-billion-member Church.
The first U.S. pope urged ŌĆŗleaders in Algeria ŌĆŗon the first day of ŌĆīa ŌüĀfour-nation tour to build a society based on principles of justice ŌĆŗand ŌĆŗsolidarity.
"Today, ŌüĀthis is more urgent than ever ŌĆŗin the face ŌĆŗof ŌüĀcontinuous violations of international law and neocolonial ŌüĀtendencies," ŌĆŗLeo said.

item.title: France's Macron to hold talks with Venezuelan opposition leader Machado
item.description:
PARIS, April 13 (Reuters) - French President Emmanuel Macron will ŌĆŗhold talks on Monday ŌĆīwith exiled Venezuelan opposition leader Maria Corina Machado , the ŌĆŗFrench presidency said.
It did ŌĆŗnot say what would ŌüĀbe discussed and ŌĆŗgave no further details of ŌĆŗthe planned discussion.
Machado has been in exile since leaving ŌĆŗthe country to ŌĆŗcollect the Nobel Peace Prize last ŌĆīDecember.
She ŌüĀis under investigation in Venezuela and the interim president, Delcy Rodriguez, ŌĆŗhas ŌĆŗsaid ŌüĀMachado should have to "answer to Venezuela" ŌĆŗfor her support ŌĆŗof ŌüĀU.S. military action against Caracas.
The United States captured ŌüĀVenezuela's ŌĆŗlongtime leader Nicolas ŌĆŗMaduro in January.

item.title: Wall Street's earnings fantasies may soon get harsh reality check
item.description:
ORLANDO, Florida, April 13 (Reuters) - Something doesn't quite add up. U.S. oil prices have risen over 70% in the last year, inflation remains above 3%, geopolitical risk is off the charts. But Wall Street's earnings outlook could not be rosier.
As the first quarter reporting season kicks into gear this week, the Nasdaq (.IXIC) , opens new tab is higher ŌĆŗthan it was before the Iran war started on February 28, and the broader S&P 500 (.SPX) , opens new tab isn't far behind. It's almost as if the last six weeks didn't happen.
But ŌĆīeven if the shaky ceasefire in the Middle East turns into a permanent cessation in hostilities - a big "if" - the economic damage and price impact will still last for months, possibly years.
The magnitude of these shifts, their duration, and ultimate impact on the U.S. economy are all up for debate. But their existence isn't.
Bank of America economists sum it up neatly: "Even if the ceasefire persists, we are unlikely to move back to the pre-war scenario."
Yet that's exactly where Wall Street seems ŌĆŗto think we're heading.
In some ways, the stock market's nonchalance shouldn't come as a surprise, as Wall Street has proven surprisingly bulletproof amid the ructions of U.S. President Donald Trump's second term.
The ŌĆŗrecovery from his "Liberation Day" tariff turmoil a year ago was stunning. It underscored how resilient Wall Street has become to shocks and how confident investors are in ŌüĀthe money-spinning powers of artificial intelligence.
In that light, the LSEG consensus forecast for 13.9% year-on-year growth in first quarter earnings seems reasonable. That would be in line with the previous four quarters and may help ŌĆŗjustify the Nasdaq and S&P 500's return to pre-war levels.
The outlook for the rest of this year, however, is more puzzling.
Consensus forecasts for earnings growth in the remaining three quarters are 20%, 22%, and ŌĆŗ19.9%, respectively. That would represent the strongest growth in U.S. corporate earnings since 2018, excluding the pandemic-distorted year of 2021, according to Tajinder Dhillon, head of earnings research at LSEG.
But if corporate America is to rack up profits at such a breakneck speed, it will have to overcome a growing number of obstacles.
The whiff of stagflation is in the air.
The Consumer Price Index report on Friday showed that gasoline prices in March spiked 21% on the month, ŌĆŗwhile other motor fuels, including diesel, soared more than 30%. Both were record rises.
Meanwhile, consumers have never been more glum. The University of Michigan's survey also released on Friday showed that consumer sentiment had ŌĆŗslumped to its lowest on record.
It's easy to see why. Growth this year will likely be weaker than it would have been absent the war.
Bank of America economists lowered their 2026 U.S. gross domestic product (GDP) growth forecast by 50 ŌĆībasis points ŌüĀto 2.3% ŌĆō a significant revision. They also raised their core personal consumption expenditures (PCE) inflation outlook by 70 bps to 3.1%.
And that may be optimistic: with oil 70% more expensive than it was a year ago, headline inflation may creep up toward 4%, moving even farther from the Federal Reserve's 2% target.
This has altered the expected path of U.S. interest rates in the coming months. At the start of the year, futures markets implied 60 bps of easing by December. Those expectations have now evaporated, and the Fed's next move appears as likely to be a hike as a cut ŌĆō something unthinkable only six weeks ago.
Of course, none ŌĆŗof this is set in stone. The economy is ŌĆŗmuch less oil-intensive than it used to ŌüĀbe, consumer sentiment increasingly bears little relation to actual spending patterns, and companies could prove resilient if these shocks are short-lived.
Still, it would be remarkable if none of this dented profits.
For starters, much of this earnings boom is expected to be powered by the tech industry. The all-powerful sector's earnings are expected to ŌĆŗrise 46% in the first quarter, meaning tech would account for nearly 80% of the expected $74 billion total profit growth.
But "Big Tech", with its $800 billion ŌĆŗcapital expenditure plans on energy-intensive ŌüĀdata centers and other artificial intelligence infrastructure this year ŌĆō much of it debt-financed ŌĆō is now particularly sensitive to both soaring energy prices and the cost of money.
Equity investors are optimistic by nature, but the positivity permeating Wall Street is striking. The question now is whether corporate guidance in the coming weeks deflates those high hopes.
(The opinions expressed here are those of Jamie McGeever , a columnist for Reuters)
Save the date: On April 23 at 9 a.m. EDT /0100 GMT, ROI ŌüĀcolumnists Mike Dolan ŌĆŗand Jamie McGeever will join LSEG for a webinar discussion, "ROI Markets Unpacked: Rethinking safe havens in uncertain times". Click here to ŌĆŗsign up. , opens new tab
Enjoying this column? Check out Reuters Open Interest (ROI), , opens new tab your essential new source for global financial commentary. Follow ROI on LinkedIn, , opens new tab and X. , opens new tab
And listen to the Morning Bid daily podcast on Apple , opens new tab , Spotify , opens new tab , or the Reuters app , opens new tab . Subscribe to hear Reuters journalists discuss the biggest news in markets and ŌĆŗfinance seven days a week.

item.title: Intergovernmental deal for $25 billion Nigeria-Morocco gas pipeline due this year, official
item.description:
RABAT, April 13 (Reuters) - An intergovernmental agreement (IGA) on a planned $25 billion Nigeria-Morocco gas pipeline will be signed this year, the head of Morocco's hydrocarbons and mining ŌĆŗagency (ONHYM) said.
Agreed a decade ago, the project - known as the African ŌĆīAtlantic Gas Pipeline - would run 6,900 km on a hybrid offshore-onshore route with a maximum capacity of 30 billion cubic metres (bcm), including 15 bcm to supply Morocco and support exports to ŌĆŗEurope, ONHYM's Amina Benkhadra told Reuters by email.
The pipeline, which has the ŌĆŗbacking of the Economic Community of West African States (ECOWAS), has completed ŌüĀits feasibility study and front-end engineering design (FEED) stages.
Following the intergovernmental agreement, a high ŌĆŗauthority for the pipeline will be established in Nigeria, bringing together ministerial representatives from ŌĆŗeach of the 13 participating countries to provide political and regulatory coordination, Benkhadra said.
A project company will also be created in Morocco as a joint venture between ONHYM and the Nigerian ŌĆŗNational Petroleum Company (NNPC) to lead the execution, financing and construction phase, she said.
The ŌĆŗpipeline would spur economic integration across West Africa by expanding electricity generation and facilitating industrial and ŌĆīmining ŌüĀdevelopment, while helping Morocco position itself as an energy bridge between Africa and Europe, she said.
Initial segments of the project would connect Morocco to gas fields in Mauritania and Senegal, and link Ghana to Cote d'Ivoire further south, before a final ŌĆŗsegment connects Ghana to ŌĆŗNigeria's gas fields, ŌüĀshe said.
First gas from the initial phases is expected in 2031, Benkhadra said.
"The project does not rely on a single ŌĆŗglobal final investment decision," she said, adding that each segment ŌĆŗis designed ŌüĀto be developed as "standalone system" to allow for early value build up, she said.
No final funding commitments have been secured yet, she said, adding that the financing structure ŌüĀwill be ŌĆŗled by the project company, which will mobilise ŌĆŗa mix of equity and debt.
"The project is attracting strong interest due to its scale, its phased ŌĆŗstructure, and its strategic positioning," Benkhadra said.

item.title: BP to buy interest in three offshore Namibian blocks as shift to oil and gas sharpens
item.description:

The deal marks BP's entry as an operator in the southern African country, a global oil and gas exploration hot spot ŌĆŗwhich hopes ŌĆŗto produce ŌüĀits first oil by 2030.

item.title: India gets first Iranian oil in 7 years, ship tracking data shows

A VLCC carries 2 million barrels of oil.

item.title: UBS raises Brent oil price view as Strait┬Āof Hormuz disruptions persist
item.description:
April 13 - The path of least resistance for crude oil prices continues to remain upward as long as flows through the Strait ŌĆŗof Hormuz stay restricted, UBS said in a note on Monday ŌĆīas it raised its Brent crude prices forecasts.
The bank increased its Brent price forecast to $100 a barrel by the end of June 2026, $95 by the end of September, and $90 ŌĆŗby the end of December. It also expects Brent to trade at $85 ŌĆŗa barrel by the end of March 2027.
"There is elevated uncertainty ŌüĀin respect to these forecasts depending on how the conflict evolves, what ŌĆŗenergy infrastructure is disrupted, and when flows through the strait resume," it noted.
A ŌĆŗprolonged disruption would raise the risk of a short-term price overshoot, with Brent potentially trading above $150 a barrel, while heightening the likelihood of demand destruction, UBS said.
It added that scarcity-driven ŌĆŗhoarding could further amplify price swings.

item.title: Saudi state-backed utility invests in UK energy tech firm Kraken, forms joint venture, Sky News reports

item.title: Italy rallies round pope as Trump attack tests ties with Meloni
item.description:
ROME, April 13 (Reuters) - Italian political and church figures rallied behind Pope Leo on Monday after Donald Trump attacked ŌĆŗthe pontiff, leaving Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni to balance Italy's close ties to the Vatican and her own alliance with the ŌĆīU.S. president.
Trump set off the furore by calling Leo "terrible", drawing a rare, direct response from the pope, who said he had "no fear" of the U.S. administration and would continue speaking out against the U.S.-led war on Iran and in defence of migrants.
Meloni, who has cultivated close relations with Trump in recent years, issued a statement backing Leo as he flew ŌĆŗoff on an ambitious four-nation visit to Africa, but made no direct mention of the U.S. president's broadside.
"May the Holy Father's ministry help foster ŌĆŗthe resolution of conflicts and the return of peace, both within nations and among them," she said, making clear ŌüĀher support for the pope without openly criticising the U.S. president.
The omission was leapt on by political opponents, who ŌĆŗsense that Meloni's closeness to Trump is becoming an electoral handicap in a country where 66% of people have a negative view of the U.S. leader, ŌĆŗtied to his aggressive foreign policy.
"As a Catholic, I am outraged by a prime minister who invokes Christian values but cannot find the strength and courage to condemn Trump's unacceptable blasphemy against the pope and the Catholic world," said Angelo Bonelli, a prominent figure in the left-wing AVS party, who referenced a later post by Trump showing an ŌĆŗAI-generated image of himself as Jesus.
However, Deputy Prime Minister Matteo Salvini, who has also in the past associated himself with Trump, was more explicit ŌĆŗin his criticism of the U.S. leader, highlighting how Europe's far-right is trying to draw back from the MAGA orbit.
"Pope Leo is a spiritual leader for billions of Catholics, ŌĆībut ŌüĀbeyond that, if there is one person striving for peace, it is Pope Leo, and so attacking him does not seem either wise or helpful," he said in a statement.
The pope is the bishop of Rome and spiritual leader to millions of Italian Catholics, making politicians of all stripes extremely wary about taking him on.
"It has been centuries since such a blatant act of aggression against the Roman Pontiff was seen," said former centre-left Prime Minister Matteo ŌĆŗRenzi, adding that it was vital ŌĆŗfor Catholics and non-believers alike to ŌüĀdefend Leo.
"He is, after all, a 'builder of bridges,' unlike Trump, a destroyer of relationships and of civilisation. The only advantage is this: Trumps come and go, popes remain," he said.
The comment echoed an Italian saying "chi mangia papa crepa" ŌĆŗwhich roughly means, "whoever tries to devour the pope dies" -- a proverb born of centuries of tension between successive ŌĆŗpapacies and temporal rulers.
"Trump ŌüĀhas made the mistake of the century, because 'chi mangia papa crepa' has been borne out repeatedly," said church historian Alberto Melloni, pointing to Italy's royal family, the House of Savoy, which clashed repeatedly with the Vatican only to be swept away while the papacy lived on.
Antonio Spadaro, a Roman Catholic priest and undersecretary ŌüĀof the ŌĆŗVatican's Dicastery for Culture and Education, said Trump's attack revealed his own weakness.
"If Leo were ŌĆŗirrelevant, he would not merit any comment. Instead, he is invoked, named, opposed -- a sign that his words matter," Spadaro wrote on X. "This is where the Church's moral force emerges. Not ŌĆŗas a counter-power, but as a space in which power is judged by a standard it does not control."

item.title: Red Cross calls consecutive strikes in Lebanon 'gravely concerning'
item.description:
GENEVA, April 13 (Reuters) - The International Committee of the Red Cross said it was deeply concerned by ŌĆŗattacks on medical workers in Lebanon after a deadly strike on ŌĆīa Red Cross centre in the country on Monday and the death of a volunteer a day earlier.

The ICRC said the Lebanese Red Cross centre in the district ŌüĀof Tyre, a city on Lebanon's coast, was hit by the strike. ŌĆŗIt did not comment on who was responsible or give details of the ŌĆŗvictim.
The Israeli army did not immediately respond to a request for comment on Monday's incident.
On Sunday, the Lebanese Red Cross said one of its volunteers, Hassan Badawi, had died from his ŌĆŗinjuries after a strike by an Israeli drone in the district of ŌĆŗBint Jbeil in southern Lebanon.
Israel's military said it had struck a "Hezbollah terrorist" in the area and ŌĆīthat ŌüĀthe incident was under review after it received reports of injury to a Red Cross team.
Agnes Dhur, head of the ICRC delegation in Lebanon, said in a statement on Monday: "The loss of those who dedicate their lives to saving ŌĆŗothers is gravely ŌĆŗconcerning, given the ŌüĀimpact on the civilians who depend on their help."
"Humanitarian and medical personnel must be protected. They must be allowed to ŌĆŗreach and help the wounded, and return unharmed," she added.
The ŌĆŗlatest war ŌüĀin Lebanon began on March 2, when Lebanese armed group Hezbollah fired at Israeli positions in support of its patron Iran.
Israel has since escalated its air and ŌüĀground ŌĆŗcampaign in the country where its operations have killed ŌĆŗmore than 2,000 people, displaced more than one million, and triggered a warning that hospitals could run out ŌĆŗof life-saving supplies.

item.title: Trump says Iranian 'fast-attack' ships that come close to US blockade will be eliminated
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 13 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump warned on Monday that any Iranian "fast-attack" ŌĆŗships that go near a U.S. maritime blockade on ŌĆīIran would be eliminated.
Trump made the threat shortly after the U.S. blockade on vessels entering and departing Iran had been due to come ŌĆŗinto effect at 1400 GMT on Monday.
Describing Iran's navy ŌĆŗas "completely obliterated" during the six-week-long war between the ŌüĀU.S. and Iran, Trump posted on Truth Social: "What we have ŌĆŗnot hit are their small number of, what they call, 'fast attack ŌĆŗships,' because we did not consider them much of a threat."
"Warning: If any of these ships come anywhere close to our BLOCKADE, they will ŌĆŗbe immediately ELIMINATED, using the same system of kill ŌĆŗthat we use against the drug dealers on boats at Sea. It is ŌĆīquick ŌüĀand brutal," Trump wrote.
Trump was referring to the dozens of U.S. strikes carried out against suspected drug boats in the Caribbean and Pacific since September in a campaign that has ŌĆŗkilled at least ŌĆŗ110 people.
Iran has ŌüĀeffectively closed the Strait of Hormuz, cutting off a vital waterway that normally carries about ŌĆŗa fifth of global oil and liquefied natural ŌĆŗgas ŌüĀsupplies, in retaliation for U.S.-Israeli strikes.
Iran's conventional navy has largely been destroyed but Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps still have plenty of ŌüĀoptions ŌĆŗincluding fast-attack craft, mini submarines, mines ŌĆŗand even jet skis packed with explosives, said Tom Sharpe, a retired Royal ŌĆŗNavy commander, last month.

item.title: China calls US-Iran ceasefire 'very fragile', urges unified opposition to escalation
