ROI delivers thought-provoking, ŌĆŗdata-driven analysis of everything from swap rates to soybeans. Markets are moving faster than ever. ROI can help you keep up. Follow ROI on LinkedIn , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .
The views expressed here are those of the author, a columnist for Reuters.

item.title: Equinor sells $169 million stake in solar firm Scatec
item.description:
OSLO, April 14 (Reuters) - Norway's ŌĆīEquinor (EQNR.OL) , opens new tab said on Tuesday it has sold 12.9 million ŌĆŗshares in solar ŌĆŗenergy group Scatec (SCATC.OL) , opens new tab for ŌüĀaround 1.6 billion Norwegian ŌĆŗcrowns ($169.31 million), completing a transaction ŌĆŗfirst flagged on Monday.
The shares were sold at 125 ŌĆŗcrowns each, a 7% ŌĆŗdiscount to Monday's closing price ŌĆīof ŌüĀ134.4 crowns.
Equinor maintains a stake in Scatec of about 8.05% following the ŌĆŗtransaction.
Equinor ŌĆŗdid ŌüĀnot provide a reason for its ŌĆŗplanned divestment.
Scatec's share ŌĆŗprice ŌüĀrose 26.6% this year before Tuesday's transaction, according ŌüĀto ŌĆŗLSEG data.
($1 = ŌĆŗ9.4503 Norwegian crowns)

item.title: Philippines seeks US extension on waiver to buy Russian oil
item.description:
MANILA, April 14 (Reuters) - The Philippines' Secretary of Energy Sharon Garin on ŌĆŗTuesday said the country has asked the United States ŌĆīto extend a waiver to purchase Russian oil and petroleum products.
Garin said the government is optimistic about extending the waiver which expired on April 11, but ŌĆŗis preparing alternative supply arrangements in case the request is ŌĆŗdeclined.
"We are awaiting their response, but we are very ŌüĀpositive on getting this other window," Garin said at a news ŌĆŗbriefing.
The Philippines is diversifying its energy sources and supply options are not ŌĆŗlimited to Russia, with the government also eyeing producers in South America, including Colombia and Argentina, as well as Canada and the U.S., Garin said.
"We wanted ŌĆŗto open the Russian window because we want more options. We ŌĆŗneed diversification," she said.
Ambassador to the United States Jose Manuel Romualdez last month ŌĆīsaid ŌüĀManila was working with the U.S. to secure waivers and exemptions that will allow the Philippines to obtain oil from U.S.-sanctioned countries.
President Ferdinand Marcos Jr on Monday suspended excise tax on kerosene and liquefied petroleum gas ŌĆŗafter Congress granted ŌĆŗhim emergency powers ŌüĀto adjust fuel tax rates. His team of economic managers have opposed the suspension of excise tax ŌĆŗon diesel and gasoline.
Secretary of Finance Frederick Go on ŌĆŗTuesday ŌüĀsaid the Development Budget Coordination Committee composed of those economic managers "has determined that suspending excise taxes on diesel and gasoline would not likely provide ŌüĀmeaningful ŌĆŗrelief, as any reduction in retail pump ŌĆŗprices would be marginal and largely offset by prevailing market dynamics."

item.title: HSBC chair says Middle East peace deal needed to restore global energy flows
item.description:
HONG KONG, April 14 (Reuters) - HSBC Holdings (HSBA.L) , opens new tab Chair Brendan Nelson said on Tuesday that a Middle East peace ŌĆŗdeal was essential to ensure a substantial resumption of global energy ŌĆīflows, with oil-driven inflation looming as a major risk to the world economy.
Nelson, speaking at the HSBC Global Investment Summit in Hong Kong, added that as long as ŌĆŗuncertainty persists, energy prices would remain elevated.
Oil prices have surged since ŌĆŗthe Iran war began, and prices remain close to $100 a ŌüĀbarrel as investors position themselves for prolonged tensions over the ŌĆŗcrucial Strait of Hormuz, through which a fifth of global oil ŌĆŗand gas typically passes.
Nelson warned that current global growth, trade and inflation projections should be "approached with considerable caution" given that the impacts of the ŌĆŗIran conflict are yet to be fully understood.
"The longer the ŌĆŗdisruption continues, the more the indirect effects from higher energy costs will lift ŌĆīinflation ŌüĀand depress growth," Nelson said.
With a swift reopening of the strait looking unlikely, Nelson said he expected interest rates to be held steady in the U.S., Europe and Britain this year ŌĆŗas a rise ŌĆŗin short- and ŌüĀlong-term market rates had tightened financial conditions.
The U.S. Navy began a blockade of the strait on Monday, ŌĆŗfollowing the breakdown of weekend talks to end ŌĆŗthe ŌüĀsix-week-long war.
ANZ analysts estimate about 10 million barrels per day of crude supply have been effectively removed from the market, adding that a ŌüĀprolonged ŌĆŗU.S. blockade could curb an additional 3 ŌĆŗmillion to 4 million barrels per day.

item.title: Philippines, US military drills underscore Washington's defence commitment, US official says
item.description:
MANILA, April 14 (Reuters) - The latest round of military ŌĆīdrills involving the Philippines, the United States and several partner nations will underscore Washington's "ironclad" commitment to its treaty ally and to the region, even as global attention remains fixed on the Middle East, a U.S. military official said ŌĆŗon Tuesday.
Running from April 20 to May 8 across multiple locations in the Philippine ŌĆŗarchipelago, the annual "Balikatan" or "shoulder-to-shoulder" drills will see more than 17,000 troops participate in ŌüĀone of the largest and most complex training programmes yet, expanding beyond bilateral exercises into a ŌĆŗbroader multinational effort.
"Balikatan represents an opportunity to showcase our ironclad alliance with the Philippines and demonstrate our ŌĆŗcommitment to a free and open Indo-Pacific," Colonel Robert Bunn, U.S. spokesperson for the exercises, said at a press conference in Manila.
Japan's participation in the programme is set to expand this year, with members of its Self-Defense Force taking ŌĆŗpart in live-fire drills for the first time, using its Type 88 surface-to-ship missile to help ŌĆŗsink a decommissioned vessel during a maritime strike exercise.
Tokyo has been strengthening its defence engagement with Manila after ŌĆīthey signed ŌüĀa reciprocal access agreement in 2024 , allowing them to deploy their militaries in each other's territory.
The exercises are also set to highlight the Philippines' widening network of security partnerships with Canada , France , New Zealand and Australia, with the countries contributing naval vessels, aircraft and troops.
Bunn said the scale and scope of this year's ŌĆŗBalikatan demonstrate the country's sustained ŌĆŗcommitment to the ŌüĀIndo-Pacific, despite competing global demands.
Thousands of U.S. personnel are deploying to the Philippines for the drills, reinforcing alliance readiness, regional stability, and the shared goal ŌĆŗof maintaining a free and open region, U.S. and Philippine military officials ŌĆŗsaid.
The expanded drills ŌüĀcome amid rising tensions between the Philippines and China in the South China Sea, where Manila has accused Beijing of increasingly aggressive actions, an accusation China rejects.
Activities will span air, land, sea, and cyber domains, ŌüĀincluding maritime ŌĆŗoperations, integrated air and missile defence, counter-landing live-fire exercises, ŌĆŗand humanitarian missions.
Colonel Dennis Hernandez, Philippine spokesperson for the exercises, said the country has the right to bolster its defence capabilities, ŌĆŗadding that the drills were not directed at any country.

item.title: Indonesia's foreign ministry urges caution over U.S. military overflight proposal
item.description:
JAKARTA, April 14 (Reuters) - Indonesia's foreign ministry ŌĆŗhas warned the defence ministry in a letter that an American proposal to give its military "blanket" permission to fly over Indonesian territory ŌĆīrisked entangling Jakarta in potential South China Sea conflicts, sources said.
The letter, marked as urgent and confidential, was delivered in early April ahead of a meeting of Defence Minister Sjafrie Sjamsoeddin and his U.S. counterpart Pete Hegseth in Washington on Monday, according to two Indonesian sources with knowledge of the matter.
The letter has not been previously reported and it urged the ŌĆŗdefence ministry to delay any final agreement with Washington. It is not clear if the overflight proposal was discussed by Hegseth and Sjafrie.
The two ŌĆŗIndonesian sources said the proposal had been slated to be signed at Monday's meeting.
A Pentagon statement that followed the meeting said ŌüĀthe two countries established a major defence cooperation partnership and listed a number of ways in which the two countries would build defence ties, but did ŌĆŗnot refer to overflights.
A U.S. official, speaking on condition of anonymity, said that just because the overflights were not mentioned in the statement did not mean they ŌĆŗwere not discussed in private.
The Pentagon did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
Indonesia's defence ministry did not respond to a Reuters question on whether the proposal was discussed between the two, but said the U.S. proposal was being carefully reviewed by Jakarta, which had made a number of adjustments keeping in mind the country's sovereignty and national interests.
Indonesia, an ŌĆŗarchipelago of more than 17,000 islands, is strategically located on the southern entry point of the South China Sea where it controls the Natuna Islands, and ŌĆŗbalances close ties with both China and the United States.
China claims sovereignty over almost all the South China Sea - a waterway carrying more than $3 trillion in annual commerce - despite overlapping ŌĆīclaims by ŌüĀthe Philippines, Brunei, Malaysia and Vietnam.
The letter from the foreign ministry said the U.S. proposal should be treated with caution because it would allow Washington to maximise surveillance and reconnaissance using Indonesian waters and territory, and could affect relations with other strategic partners in the region, including China.
It said an agreement with the U.S. would give "the impression that Indonesia is involved in an alliance with the implication of increased national security risks because it places Indonesia as a potential target in ŌĆŗa regional conflict situation."
It noted that a ŌĆŗnumber of U.S. military aircraft ŌüĀhad carried out surveillance operations in South China Sea on 18 occasions between January 2024 to April 2025, which constituted a violation of IndonesiaŌĆÖs territorial waters and airspace.
IndonesiaŌĆÖs protests regarding this operation have never received a proper response from the U.S., ŌĆŗit added.
Spokespersons for Indonesia's foreign and defence ministries, as well as the Pentagon did not immediately respond to requests ŌĆŗfor comment on ŌüĀthe reservations raised by the Indonesian foreign ministry.
Indonesia has a non-aligned foreign policy and President Prabowo Subianto has vowed to befriend all countries.
Indonesia is a key member of President Donald Trump's Board of Peace initiative, and has committed the largest contingent of troops for the body's proposed stabilisation force in Gaza.
He has also remained close to Beijing. His ŌüĀfirst foreign ŌĆŗvisit as president in 2024 was to Beijing and he attended a military parade hosted by President ŌĆŗXi Jinping late last year, being photographed with U.S. adversaries Russian President Vladimir Putin and North Korean leader Kim Jong Un.
The U.S. and Australia joined the Philippines for their second joint maritime exercises in the ŌĆŗSouth China Sea this year, amid tensions with China in the disputed waterway.

item.title: China Evergrande's billionaire boss falls from circles of power to fraud plea
item.description:
HONG KONG, April 14 (Reuters) - Mingling with power brokers at centenary celebrations of the Chinese Communist Party on July 1, 2021, a beaming Hui Ka Yan betrayed no sign of the pressure mounting on his property company, China Evergrande Group, the world's most indebted.
Hui looked relaxed in navy-blue suit and open-necked ŌĆŗshirt as he stood on a podium overlooking the festivities in Tiananmen Square, an invitation many considered an official show of support ŌĆīfor the billionaire businessman.
Flanked by the business elite, the founder of Evergrande, started in 1996 in the southern city of Guangzhou, spoke of his debt deleveraging goals, just a month after having outlined them in a rare meeting with more than 1,000 suppliers.
Now, nearly five years later, with Evergrande in liquidation over total liabilities of more than $300 billion, roughly the equivalent ŌĆŗof Finland's gross domestic product, Hui, 67, has pleaded guilty to charges such as fundraising fraud and bribery.
His plea, after three years spent in ŌĆŗdetention, draws a line under the collapse of an empire that thrust China's property sector into the glare of global ŌüĀinvestors.
The liquidators of Evergrande, appointed by a Hong Kong court, declined to comment. Reuters was unable to seek comment from Hui. The municipal government of the ŌĆŗsouthern hub of Shenzhen did not respond to a Reuters request for comment.
Hui, a former steel technician, raised by his grandmother in a rural village in the ŌĆŗcentral province of Henan, built his fortune on the back of low-priced homes.
Under him, the property developer expanded aggressively by raising loans to support its land-buying sprees and selling homes at lower margins for quick turnover.
Evergrande grew to achieve 700 billion yuan ($103 billion) in annual sales by 2020.
In 2017, Hui was Asia's richest man, with a net worth of $45.3 billion, according ŌĆŗto Forbes. By 2023, his net worth was estimated at $3 billion.
Hui was known for keeping a low public profile and being a workaholic, who at times ŌĆŗdemanded that others followed his work style, three employees told Reuters.
He also set ambitious targets; when questioned by investors and reporters in the past decade about his highly leveraged ŌĆīprojects, Hui ŌüĀsaid Evergrande's high turnover and asset value were sufficient to cover its debts.
Hui did not shy away from new ventures, especially in support of China's larger goals. He dabbled in electric cars and soccer, both passions of Chinese President Xi Jinping.
Outside mainland China, Hui mixed with Hong Kong tycoons, becoming a core member of the "poker club", a tight-knit circle that often did investment deals together, said three people familiar with it.
"He was very composed when he was first brought to ŌĆŗthe club; he knowingly lost a lot ŌĆŗof money in the games and ŌüĀgained the fondness of Cheng," said one of the sources, referring to Cheng Yu Tung, the late founder of the New World Development group.
Cheng injected $150 million into Evergrande a year before its Hong Kong IPO in 2009, according to Evergrande's ŌĆŗlisting prospectus, helping it through a crunch during the financial crisis in the wake of its aggressive expansion.
Hui's debt-laden ŌĆŗbusinesses worried regulators who ŌüĀhad warned Evergrande to get its house in order for fear of contagion.
Speaking at the 2018 China Charity Awards as a winner for an eighth consecutive year, Hui said Evergrande had paid tax totalling 185 billion yuan in the past 22 years and donated more than 10 billion yuan.
"Without the country's policy to reform higher education, ŌüĀI could ŌĆŗnot have left the village. Without the country giving me a scholarship of 14 yuan every ŌĆŗmonth, I could not have completed university," Hui said.
"Without the country's good policy to reform and open up, Evergrande would not have become what it has today. Therefore, everything that Evergrande and I ŌĆŗhave, they are all given by the Party, by the country, and by society."
($1=6.8171 Chinese yuan)

item.title: School shooting in Turkey leaves 16 wounded, governor says
item.description:
ANKARA, April 14 (Reuters) - A teenager opened fire at ŌĆŗa school and ŌĆīwounded at least 16 people, including students and ŌĆŗteachers, before ŌĆŗkilling himself in Turkey's southeastern ŌüĀprovince of Sanliurfa, ŌĆŗgovernor Hasan Sildak ŌĆŗtold reporters on Tuesday.
The former student, 19, used a ŌĆŗrifle during the ŌĆŗattack, after which those wounded ŌĆīwere ŌüĀrushed to the hospital in the Siverek district, Sildak said. ŌĆŗTwelve ŌĆŗof ŌüĀthe wounded were still in ŌĆŗthe hospital.
The assailant ŌĆŗkilled ŌüĀhimself when police tried to capture him ŌüĀat ŌĆŗthe scene, ŌĆŗSildak added.

item.title: Russian Foreign Minister Lavrov arrives in China for talks on Iran and Ukraine
item.description:
MOSCOW, April 14 (Reuters) - Russian Foreign Minister Sergei Lavrov arrived in China on Tuesday for talks ŌĆŗon the wars in Ukraine and Iran ŌĆīas well as bilateral ties.
Lavrov was shown on Russian state television arriving in Beijing where is ŌĆŗexpected to hold talks with Chinese ŌĆŗForeign Minister Wang Yi.
"A thorough exchange of ŌüĀviews is expected on a number of 'hot ŌĆŗtopics' and regional issues, including the Ukrainian ŌĆŗcrisis and the situation in the Middle East," Russia's Foreign Ministry said in a statement.
Lavrov and Wang will ŌĆŗdiscuss bilateral cooperation and cooperation in ŌĆŗmultilateral forums such as the United Nations, BRICS, the ŌĆīShanghai ŌüĀCooperation Organization, the G20, and the Asia-Pacific Economic Cooperation (APEC), the ministry said.
China and Russia declared a "no limits" partnership in February 2022 when ŌĆŗRussian President ŌĆŗVladimir Putin ŌüĀvisited Beijing, days before he sent tens of thousands of troops ŌĆŗinto Ukraine. Putin has sometimes described ŌĆŗChina ŌüĀas an "ally".
In its annual threat assessment, U.S. intelligence agencies cast China and Russia as the ŌüĀmain ŌĆŗcompetitors for Washington on everything ŌĆŗfrom the Arctic and artificial intelligence to space and ŌĆŗnuclear weapons.

item.title: Senior Russian commander killed in March air crash, official says
item.description:
MOSCOW, April 6 (Reuters) - A senior Russian air force commander was killed when a military transport aircraft crashed in ŌĆŗRussian-controlled Crimea last week killing 30 people, according to ŌĆŗa senior official cited by Russian media on Monday.
Alexander ŌüĀOtroshchenko, commander of the Mixed Air Force Corps of ŌĆŗthe Northern Fleet, died in the crash, Andrei Chibis, governor of ŌĆŗthe northern Murmansk region where the fleet is based, was quoted as saying.
The An-26 military transport plane crashed into a cliff in Crimea ŌĆŗon March 31. Russia's Defence Ministry said shortly afterwards that ŌĆŗthe preliminary cause was technical malfunction.
The An-26 has been in service since ŌĆīthe ŌüĀlate 1960s and has also been used by airlines to carry freight, but the model has been involved in a number of deadly crashes over the last decade.
A Ukrainian An-26 ŌĆŗcrashed during a ŌĆŗflight in Ukraine's ŌüĀsoutheastern Zaporizhzhia region in 2022, killing one person. Another aircraft crashed on a training flight ŌĆŗin northeastern Ukraine in 2020, killing all but ŌĆŗone ŌüĀof the 27 people on board.
Eight people, including five Russians, were killed when an An-26 crashed in South Sudan in 2020. ŌüĀFour ŌĆŗof 10 people on board were ŌĆŗkilled when an An-26 crashed on landing in Ivory Coast in West Africa ŌĆŗin 2017.

item.title: Iran war upends IEA's global oil market outlook
item.description:
LONDON, April 14 (Reuters) - The International Energy Agency ŌĆŗsharply cut its forecasts for global ŌĆīoil supply and demand growth, saying both are now expected to fall from 2025 levels as war ŌĆŗin the Middle East disrupts oil ŌĆŗflows and weighs on the global economy.
The ŌüĀIEA now sees global oil demand ŌĆŗfalling by 80,000 barrels per day in 2026, ŌĆŗcompared with a projected year-on-year rise of 640,000 bpd in its previous monthly report.
"Demand destruction will spread ŌĆŗas scarcity and higher prices persist," ŌĆŗthe IEA said, adding that the deepest cuts in ŌĆīoil ŌüĀconsumption have come from the Middle East and Asia-Pacific so far.
The Paris-based watchdog forecasts global oil supply to fall by 1.5 ŌĆŗmillion bpd this ŌĆŗyear, ŌüĀcrashing from a 1.1 million bpd projected rise last month.
Attacks on ŌĆŗenergy infrastructure in the Middle East ŌĆŗand ŌüĀIran's effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz have led to the largest oil ŌüĀsupply disruption ŌĆŗin history, the IEA ŌĆŗsaid, with 10.1 million bpd lost in March.

item.title: Britain will have enough energy this summer despite Iran turmoil, grid operators say
item.description:
LONDON, April 14 (Reuters) - Britain ŌĆŗwill have enough gas and electricity supply this summer, the countryŌĆÖs network operators said on Tuesday, despite ŌĆīdeclining domestic gas production and fears over energy supplies from the Middle East.
Conflict in the Middle East has disrupted energy shipments through the Strait of Hormuz and halted Qatari LNG production, which accounts for around a fifth of global liquefied natural gas supply.
ŌĆ£While the situation ŌĆŗin the Middle East has understandably raised questions about BritainŌĆÖs gas supplies, our forecasts indicate the market ŌĆŗhas the capacity to deliver sufficient supply to meet demand this summer,ŌĆØ Glenn Bryn-Jacobsen, director ŌüĀof energy systems and resilience at National Gas, said in a statement with its Gas Summer Outlook for 2026.
Domestic gas supply from the UK Continental Shelf is expected to be 6% lower than last summer, at 13.1 billion ŌĆŗcubic metres, reflecting years of declining North Sea output.
Imports of gas from Norway are expected to rise by 24% to 12.2 bcm, while imports of LNG from other countries are expected to soar by 65% to 2.7 bcm, National Gas said.
Wholesale gas prices are up ŌĆŗ50% since the start of the conflict, forcing Britain to pay more to attract LNG cargoes from alternative sources.
ŌĆ£The ŌĆŗduration of these higher prices will depend on how long it takes Qatari production to recover and shipping to resume through the ŌĆīStrait of ŌüĀHormuz,ŌĆØ the outlook said.
Domestic energy consumers will be protected from the higher wholesale prices by regulator OfgemŌĆÖs price cap , with the current level set until the end of July.
Total gas demand is expected to be similar to last summer, around 29.8 bcm.
Gas-fired power plants typically provide around 30% of the countryŌĆÖs electricity but demand is volatile over the summer months, ŌĆŗNational Gas said, depending on ŌĆŗthe availability of wind ŌüĀand solar power.
Strong wind and solar power output and seasonally lower demand mean Britain is likely to see a surplus of electricity supply this summer, the National Energy ŌĆŗSystem Operator said in its outlook.
NESO, which is responsible for the country's electricity networks, ŌĆŗsaid it had ŌüĀincreased flexibility, such as measures to encourage customers to shift demand to times when supply is high, with some energy suppliers paying customers, usually via money off their bills, to use extra energy at certain times.
Peak summer electricity demand is forecast ŌüĀat 29.7 ŌĆŗgigawatts, unchanged from 2025.
The outlooks cover the period from April 1 ŌĆŗto September 30, when gas and electricity demand is typically lower.
Winter outlooks covering October to March, to be published later in the year, could reflect ŌĆŗgreater impact from the Middle East crisis, the operators said.

item.title: Russia's oil and fuel export revenues rebound in March, IEA says
item.description:
MOSCOW, April 14 (Reuters) - Russia's revenues from crude oil and refined products rose in March, rebounding from February when ŌĆŗthey fell to their lowest level since the ŌĆīstart of the Ukraine conflict in 2022, as prices surged due to the Iran war , the International Energy Agency said on ŌĆŗTuesday.
Russia's commodity revenues are a vital part of ŌĆŗthe state budget and are needed to support ŌüĀrising military spending.
The Paris-based IEA said Russia's crude ŌĆŗoil exports rose by 270,000 barrels per day last ŌĆŗmonth from February to 4.6 million bpd, mostly driven by higher seaborne shipments as the Druzhba pipeline remained offline.
Flows via the Druzhba ŌĆŗpipeline to Hungary and Slovakia across Ukrainian territory ŌĆŗhave remained shut following the attacks on the pipeline infrastructure at ŌĆīthe ŌüĀend of January.
The IEA also said Russia's oil and oil product export revenues nearly doubled, to $19 billion in March from $9.75 billion in February as global oil ŌĆŗprices surged.
It ŌĆŗsaid that ŌüĀRussia's crude production increased last month to 8.96 million bpd from 8.67 million bpd ŌĆŗin February.
The agency also said that Russia ŌĆŗmay ŌüĀstruggle to increase oil production above the levels seen in early first quarter in the near term given ŌüĀdamage ŌĆŗto port and energy infrastructure.
Russian ŌĆŗBaltic and Black Sea ports and refineries have been repeatedly damaged by ŌĆŗUkrainian drone strikes.

item.title: Nornickel seeks new market for palladium in EVs with catalyst for lithiumŌĆæsulphur batteries
item.description:
MOSCOW, April 14 (Reuters) - Russia's Nornickel said on Tuesday it aims to complete the development of a palladium-based catalyst for lithium-sulphur (Li-S) batteries ŌĆŗwithin three years, potentially creating a major new source of ŌĆīdemand for palladium in electric vehicles.
LiŌĆæS batteries in theory offer higher energy density as well as significantly lower cost and weight than lithium-ion batteries ŌĆŗcurrently used in most EVs. But they have so ŌĆŗfar failed to reach mass-market production due to their ŌüĀextremely limited service life.
The project by Nornickel - the world's ŌĆŗlargest producer of palladium, accounting for some 40% of global output - ŌĆŗis aimed at sharply extending the lifespan of Li-S batteries to more than 1,000 charging cycles.
"I think this is a three-year horizon for us ŌĆŗto further refine the technology so that it can compete ŌĆŗwith existing solutions. Overall, lithium-sulphur technologies look quite promising in terms of energy ŌĆīdensity (driving ŌüĀrange),ŌĆØ Nornickel Vice President Vitaly Busko said.
If the technology proves successful, it would "open up huge new markets for palladium", Nornickel said, estimating potential demand of at least 1.5 million ounces a ŌĆŗyear.
Nornickel this week ŌĆŗopened a ŌüĀpalladium laboratory in Moscow to develop new applications for palladium beyond autocatalysts for non-electric vehicles, which ŌĆŗcurrently account for more than 80% of global ŌĆŗdemand.
The metals ŌüĀgiant is investing $100 million in a programme aimed at generating around 1.7 million troy ounces of new annual palladium demand by ŌüĀ2030 ŌĆŗto 2035, to offset potential losses from ŌĆŗrising EV adoption, and has already identified a nearŌĆæterm commercial application in fibreglass production in ŌĆŗChina.

item.title: ANZ raises oil price forecasts on Middle East supply losses
item.description:
April 14 (Reuters) - ANZ now expects Brent crude to end the year at $88 a barrel and remain above $90 a barrel for ŌĆŗthe rest of 2026, up from its earlier forecast that had ŌĆīassumed prices closer to $80, due to the loss of supply from the Gulf, it said on Tuesday.
The revision reflects export disruptions, logistics constraints and precautionary shut-ins by core Gulf ŌĆŗproducers that have sharply reduced supply even where production capacity has ŌĆŗnot been physically damaged in the U.S.-Israeli war with Iran, ŌüĀANZ analysts said in a research note.
The war, which broke out on ŌĆŗFebruary 28, has resulted in the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz, ŌĆŗthrough which about one-fifth of global oil flows.
ANZ estimates that about 10 million barrels per day of crude supply have been effectively removed from the market relative to the ŌĆŗbank's January baseline.
"The oil market no longer needs a worst-case escalation to ŌĆŗjustify higher pricing levels," the analysts said, adding that tight supply-demand balances alone could sustain ŌĆīBrent ŌüĀprices near or above current thresholds, even in the absence of further geopolitical risks.
While some supply could return to the market if security conditions improve, ANZ cautioned that any recovery is likely to be slow and uneven.
The bank ŌĆŗsaid between 1 million ŌĆŗbpd and ŌüĀ2 million bpd of output could face permanent or semi-permanent disruption due to reservoir damage, deferred maintenance, and financial ŌĆŗchallenges.
OECD commercial oil inventories were already near historically low levels ŌĆŗbefore the ŌüĀdisruptions, leaving limited room for stockpile releases to stabilise prices, ANZ said. Without a sharp drop in global demand, the bank said the market may need ŌüĀprice-driven ŌĆŗdemand destruction to rebalance, keeping volatility high into ŌĆŗ2027.
Benchmark Brent crude futures settled on Monday at $99.36 per barrel, while U.S. West Texas Intermediate (WTI) futures ŌĆŗfinished at $99.08 per barrel.

item.title: Former NATO chief targets PM Starmer, warning UK's security in 'peril'
item.description:
LONDON, April 14 (Reuters) - Britain's national security is "in peril" because ŌĆŗof political complacency and under-investment in defence, former NATO chief George Robertson will say ŌĆīon Tuesday, in a rare public rebuke of Prime Minister Keir Starmer's handling of military spending.
Robertson, who helped draft a Strategic Defence Review commissioned by Starmer when he came to power in 2024, is expected to say in a lecture that Britain ŌĆŗhas become increasingly exposed to external threats.
Robertson, who served in the 1990s as defence secretary ŌĆŗfrom Starmer's Labour Party, told the Financial Times there was a gap between the ŌüĀprime minister's rhetoric and action on defence, and Starmer was "not willing to make the necessary investment".
In his ŌĆŗlecture, to be delivered later on Tuesday in Salisbury, southern England, he is expected to call out finance ŌĆŗminister Rachel Reeves for devoting "only 40 words" to defence in a budget speech last autumn and not mentioning it at all in an update last month, the FT said.
"Britain's national security and safety is in peril," he is due to ŌĆŗsay, according to an excerpt reported by the FT and the BBC. "We are under-prepared. We are under-insured. ŌĆŗWe are under attack. We are not safe."
Robertson's office did not immediately respond to a request for comment. There ŌĆīwas no ŌüĀimmediate response from Starmer's office.
Starmer has blamed under-investment in the military on 14 years of rule by the rival Conservative Party, and has promised the largest sustained rise in defence spending since the Cold War, to reach 3% of national output in the next parliament.
The government has said it would publish a 10-year ŌĆŗdefence investment plan soon, ŌĆŗaimed at meeting the ambitions ŌüĀset out in the 2024 review co-written by Robertson, which called for a shift towards drones, digital warfare and data-driven combat systems reflecting lessons drawn from the war ŌĆŗin Ukraine.
Starmer said last week that the war in Iran must be a ŌĆŗturning point for ŌüĀBritain, pledging to strengthen the economy and military to cope with a more "volatile and dangerous" world.
But Robertson will accuse Britain's political leadership of a "corrosive complacency" towards defence and describe decisions made by "non-military experts in the Treasury" as "vandalism".
"We ŌüĀcannot ŌĆŗdefend Britain with an ever-expanding welfare budget," he is expected to ŌĆŗsay.
He will say the security outlook has deteriorated sharply following Russia's invasion of Ukraine and heightened tensions in the Middle East, calling ŌĆŗit one of the most dangerous periods in decades.

item.title: Kremlin says some internet restrictions are necessary for security reasons
item.description:
MOSCOW, April 14 (Reuters) - The Kremlin said on Tuesday that some ŌĆŗinternet restrictions which it ŌĆīconceded had caused disruption for many Russians were necessary for ŌĆŗsecurity reasons and did ŌĆŗnot represent a return to ŌüĀthe past.
Russia has tightened ŌĆŗits control over the internet ŌĆŗsince the start of its war in Ukraine in February 2022, ŌĆŗblocking messenger apps and ŌĆŗvirtual private networks (VPN) used to bypass online ŌĆīcensorship, ŌüĀand shutting down the mobile internet.
Kremlin spokesman Dmitry Peskov suggested that some of ŌĆŗthe measures ŌĆŗwere ŌüĀtemporary in nature and would be lifted ŌĆŗonce it was safe ŌĆŗto ŌüĀdo so, while others were related to tech companies ŌüĀnot ŌĆŗcomplying with ŌĆŗRussian law.

item.title: UK's Reeves 'frustrated and angry' over US Iran war strategy, Mirror reports
item.description:
LONDON, April 14 (Reuters) - British finance minister Rachel Reeves said on ŌĆŗTuesday she was "very frustrated ŌĆīand angry" over what she said was the United ŌĆŗStates' failure to have ŌĆŗa clear exit plan or ŌüĀobjectives for the war ŌĆŗin Iran, according to ŌĆŗthe Mirror newspaper.
"This is a war that we did not start. ŌĆŗIt was a war ŌĆŗthat we did not want. I ŌĆīfeel ŌüĀvery frustrated and angry that the U.S. went into this war without a ŌĆŗclear ŌĆŗexit ŌüĀplan, without a clear idea of what ŌĆŗthey were trying to ŌĆŗachieve," ŌüĀReeves told the newspaper.
"And as a result the ŌüĀStrait ŌĆŗof Hormuz is ŌĆŗnow blocked," she added.

item.title: Macron, Starmer to chair conference on defensive Hormuz mission on Friday, Elysee says
item.description:
PARIS, April 14 (Reuters) - French President Emmanuel Macron ŌĆŗand British Prime Minister ŌĆīKeir Starmer will chair a video ŌĆŗconference in Paris ŌĆŗon Friday for countries ŌüĀthat are willing ŌĆŗto contribute to ŌĆŗthe defensive multilateral mission to restore freedom of navigation ŌĆŗin the ŌĆŗStrait of Hormuz when security ŌĆīconditions ŌüĀpermit, the French presidency said on Tuesday.
Senior diplomats will ŌĆŗhold a ŌĆŗvideo ŌüĀcall on Wednesday ahead of ŌĆŗthe leaders ŌĆŗmeeting, ŌüĀtwo European diplomats said.

item.title: US truckers' diesel spend hits record high on Middle East conflict
item.description:
NEW YORK/LOS ANGELES, April 14 (Reuters) - The more than 3 million U.S. truckers who move goods for everything from grocery stores to factories to construction sites are grappling with the highest diesel prices in years, compounding pressure on the industry as Iran war-related ŌĆŗoil price spikes also threaten to weaken freight demand.
Diesel is the second-largest operating expense for truckers and the national average retail price has jumped $1.89, or 50%, since the start of the ŌĆīIran war effectively halted shipping through the Strait of Hormuz, a vital artery for global energy flows. Crude oil prices, which underpin diesel and gasoline, also surged, driving up transportation costs and prices for many consumer goods.
Experts warned there was no relief in sight as diesel prices in logistics hubs including California and Texas hit all-time highs and a ceasefire announced last week by the U.S. and Iran looked fragile.
Trucking is a barometer of how the U.S. economy is doing. In 2024, the industry moved 11.3 billion tons of ŌĆŗfreight - nearly three-quarters of the national total, including manufactured and retail goods - and generated $906 billion in revenue, according to American Trucking Associations.
As of Monday, U.S. fleets on average spent $5.52 per gallon on diesel, surpassing ŌĆŗthe prior all-time high of $5.50 set in June 2022 after Russia invaded Ukraine, according to data from fleet management technology provider Samsara (IOT.N) , opens new tab . The firm's fuel spend data, which ŌüĀaccounts for discounts and surcharges, is from more than 5,500 fleets of all sizes across the entire U.S. and represents nearly 1 billion gallons of fuel purchases.
"Not one firm had $5.60 a gallon diesel on their proverbial budget ŌĆŗbingo card for 2026," said Jason Miller, a supply chain professor at Michigan State University.
Delivery firm FedEx (FDX.N) , opens new tab , which also operates one of the nation's largest trucking firms, said the fallout from the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran could weigh on ŌĆŗfourth-quarter performance if soaring fuel costs prompt customers to pull back.
A March poll from DAT Freight & Analytics shows 18% of over 540 surveyed trucking firms had halted operations due to the spike in fuel prices. About 44% of the firms, which were of various sizes from across the U.S., were being more selective about load weights and about 45% were driving fewer miles.
U.S. trucking is dominated by small businesses. As of June 2025, there were almost 580,000 active U.S. motor carriers registered, with ŌĆŗ91.5% of those operating 10 or fewer trucks, ATA said, citing Department of Transportation data.
The surge in diesel prices has wiped out profits for most small carriers and owner-operators from December, January and February, DAT's principal analyst Dean ŌĆŗCroke said in a market update, adding most other operators are still slightly above breakeven.
Heather Hickson Griffith, a former Marine who has more than a decade of experience behind the wheel of a big rig, is paying up to $8 per gallon ŌĆīin California - a ŌüĀprice that is eating through her savings faster than during the 2022 fuel-price spike.
As a result, the Oklahoma-based heavy equipment hauler has stopped eating at restaurants to help save money. Her husband Daniel Griffith is running cargoes on the East Coast, where fuel is cheaper. GKZ Trucking, their small company of 21 owner-operators, does not have the heft of large trucking firms to recoup higher fuel costs - and is more vulnerable to cash flow and profit squeezes when prices soar.
Independent drivers like Hickson Griffith often pay out-of-pocket for fuel and can have trouble convincing customers to reimburse them when prices rise. Large companies, on the other hand, often negotiate volume discounts from fuel sellers and ŌĆŗuse surcharges to claw back higher fuel costs from ŌĆŗclients.
Without relief, Hickson Griffith said, "By the end of ŌüĀthe year I am going to be hurting to the point of no return."
Soaring diesel prices could force thousands of small operators out of business, worsening already tight trucking capacity, said Avery Vise, vice president of trucking at FTR Transportation Intelligence.
Freight rates are expected to rise even more sharply than in 2022, Vise said.
Transportation accounts for a ŌĆŗsmall portion of the overall cost of goods - but can top 20% for staples like milk, researchers at Texas A&M Transportation Institute found.
So far, inflation is lagging levels ŌĆŗseen in the 2022 Russia-Ukraine ŌüĀwar energy shock, when pandemic-related supply chain disruptions and massive federal spending contributed to soaring prices.
Nevertheless, high fuel prices have become a political headache for President Donald Trump and his Republican Party as midterm elections near.
States including California, Hawaii, Nevada, North Carolina and Texas have reported diesel prices hitting record highs since the Middle East conflict escalated.
The jump in diesel prices has far outpaced rises in gasoline, said Kelly Soderlund, SamsaraŌĆÖs head of insights, adding the two prices ŌüĀrose in tandem ŌĆŗduring the early days of the Ukraine war. That means today's fuel prices are hitting the trucking industry harder than everyday consumers, ŌĆŗshe said.
Motorists should prepare for another round of price surges after ceasefire talks between the U.S. and Iran yielded no agreement over the weekend, sending oil prices sharply higher, said Patrick De Haan, head of petroleum analysis at GasBuddy.
"The move toward a full blockade of the Strait of Hormuz ŌĆŗis compounding global supply concerns and risks further disrupting flows," he said.

item.title: Australian state secures own fuel supply
item.description:
PERTH, April 14 (Reuters) - The premier of Western Australia, Australia's mining powerhouse, ŌĆīsaid on Tuesday the state had secured a strategic reserve of 4 million litres of diesel to be held at the Port of Wyndham in the Kimberley region in its far north.
While the north of Western Australia is known ŌĆŗfor its heavily diesel-reliant iron ore mining sector dominated by the likes of BHP (BHP.AX) , opens new tab , Rio ŌĆŗTinto (RIO.AX) , opens new tab and Fortescue (FMG.AX) , opens new tab , the fuel will be directed to remote, off-grid Aboriginal communities ŌüĀand agriculture, Premier Roger Cook said in a statement.
The reserve could rise to 12 million litres, or ŌĆŗaround 75,000 barrels of oil, all to be owned by the state.
"Having access to this fuel ŌĆŗnow, with the ability to store more in the future, is a crucial step towards shoring up our state's fuel security for the future,ŌĆØ Cook said.
Remote communities and some agricultural producers are often more reliant on spot market ŌĆŗdiesel purchases than long term contracts, the state government said in the same statement.
Western Australia Energy and ŌĆŗDecarbonisation Minister Amber-Jade Sanderson flagged a domestic fuel supply separate to the federal government's moves to ensure national fuel security ŌĆīon ŌüĀSunday.
The state government has made a deal with local independent supplier Cambridge Gulf, which has placed additional orders for diesel from overseas suppliers and will store it free of charge.
ŌĆ£This approach complements the federal government's Minimum Stockholding Obligation (MSO), which requires fuel companies to hold reserves,ŌĆØ the state government said in its ŌĆŗstatement.
Canberra relaxed the MSO ŌĆŗon companies last month, ŌüĀallowing more fuel to get to market as supplies of petrol and diesel tightened amid panic buying set off by the closure of the ŌĆŗStrait of Hormuz, which saw roughly 20% of global oil and natural gas ŌĆŗexports before the ŌüĀIran war.
The state government earlier introduced emergency powers to ensure fuel suppliers provided ŌĆ£detailed supply chain informationŌĆØ.
One of the planks of the national plan Canberra announced was coordination between states in addition to a slew of ŌüĀother measures ŌĆŗsuch as cutting excise, allowing higher sulphur levels and securing supply ŌĆŗfrom Asian trade partners.
Wyndham port has previously been used as an export point for incremental amounts of conventional onshore oil ŌĆŗproduced by Australian junior Buru Energy (BRU.AX) , opens new tab from its now-closed Ungani field.

item.title: UK product testing firm Intertek weighs break up, shares jump
item.description:
April 14 (Reuters) - British product testing and certifications ŌĆīgroup Intertek (ITRK.L) , opens new tab is considering splitting into separate energy/infrastructure and testing/assurance businesses, it said on Tuesday, sending its shares as much as 14% higher.
The company said the move could help boost ŌĆŗgrowth and returns for shareholders and that, if it decided to ŌĆŗproceed, the plan could be implemented by mid-2027.
RBC analysts said ŌüĀany demerger could lead to higher costs and reduced investments, but added that ŌĆŗconsolidation in the sector suggested neither of the two businesses would remain independent ŌĆŗfor long.
Shares in London-listed Intertek were up 11% at 0755 GMT, compared with a 0.3% rise in the FTSE 100 (.FTSE) , opens new tab index.
Several British firms have looked to reshape their businesses ŌĆŗas tougher market conditions push them to rethink the path to maximum returns.
Outsourcing ŌĆŗfirm Capita (CPI.L) , opens new tab sold its private sector contact centre business in March, while engineer Smiths Group (SMIN.L) , opens new tab last year ŌĆīsold ŌüĀits baggage-screening unit. Chemicals company Johnson Matthey (JMAT.L) , opens new tab is in the process of selling its catalyst technologies business.
The potential separation of Intertek Energy & Infrastructure and Intertek Testing & Assurance could be via either a sale or a spin-off, the ŌĆŗcompany said.
"ŌĆŹWe believe that ŌĆŗtwo specialist scale ŌüĀglobal ATIC (assurance, testing, inspection, and certification) businesses could be best positioned to accelerate growth and deliver greater value for ŌĆŗshareholders," CEO Andr├® Lacroix said.
Intertek Testing & Assurance reported 5.6% like-for-like ŌĆŗrevenue growth ŌüĀin 2025 and contributed just over half of the group's revenue, according to the company's website. Intertek Energy & Infrastructure's revenue rose 2% on the same basis.
The ŌüĀcompany reaffirmed ŌĆŗits guidance for mid-single-digit like-for-like revenue growth ŌĆŗthis year after a 5.4% increase in the first quarter, which JP Morgan analysts described as "reassuring".
($1 = 0.7400 ŌĆŗpounds)

item.title: Shell, in contrast to BP, allows AGM vote on Follow This climate resolution
item.description:
LONDON, April 14 (Reuters) - Shell (SHEL.L) , opens new tab will allow its shareholders to ŌĆīvote on a resolution tabled by climate activist investor group Follow This at its annual general meeting, ŌĆŗrecommending a vote against it, according to ŌĆŗits notice of meeting.
This stands in contrast ŌüĀto BP (BP.L) , opens new tab , whose board decided not to ŌĆŗinclude the Follow This resolution on its agenda, ŌĆŗtriggering some of its shareholders and influential proxy adviser groups to recommend votes against the board's wishes.
The Follow This ŌĆŗresolution calls on Shell to disclose how ŌĆŗits strategy would perform under scenarios of declining demand ŌĆīfor ŌüĀoil and gas. BP has said the resolution was invalid and would be ineffective if it were to pass at its AGM.
Shell said Follow ŌĆŗThis's questions ŌĆŗare comprehensively ŌüĀcovered by Shell's existing disclosures which enable shareholders to model the ŌĆŗfinancial resilience of the company in any ŌĆŗprice ŌüĀscenario of their choosing.
Shell also said that were the Follow This resolution to pass, it would ŌüĀbe ŌĆŗagainst good governance because it ŌĆŗwould hold Shell to certain scenarios that are subject to ŌĆŗchange.

item.title: Delta Air Lines scales back sustainable fuel and net-zero targets, Bloomberg News Reports
item.description:
April 14 (Reuters) - Delta Air Lines (DAL.N) , opens new tab quietly removed two key environmental targets from its sustainability webpage late last week, Bloomberg ŌĆŗNews reported on Tuesday.
The Atlanta-based carrier deleted its ŌĆīpledge to use sustainable aviation fuel for 10% of its jet fuel by 2030, the report said, adding that the ŌĆŗairline has also rephrased its quest to achieve ŌĆŗnet-zero emissions by 2050 as an "aspiration," rather than ŌüĀa "goal."
SAF, made largely from waste or used cooking oil, ŌĆŗcan cut emissions significantly compared with traditional jet fuel. However, ŌĆŗit remains two to five times more expensive than conventional fuel.
The Bloomberg report said, quoting a Delta spokesperson, that the airline ŌĆŗstill views SAF as one of the most important ŌĆŗways to decarbonise flight, but its slow development threatens the industry's ŌĆīclimate ŌüĀambitions.
Delta did not immediately respond to a Reuters request for comment.
In February, Willie Walsh, director-general of the International Air Transport Association, said that shortages of efficient new ŌĆŗaircraft and alternative ŌĆŗfuels were ŌüĀpushing up profits for suppliers and putting at risk the industry's flagship emissions goal.
IATA's ŌĆŗroughly 350 airlines adopted the target in ŌĆŗ2021 to ŌüĀcurb emissions that represent 2% to 3% of the global total. The effort relies heavily on the use of ŌüĀSAF ŌĆŗand timely access to new ŌĆŗplanes and engines, delayed by supply chain problems.

item.title: Airlines urge EU to step in as Iran war chokes jet fuel supply
item.description:
BRUSSELS, April 14 (Reuters) - European airlines have urged the European Union to step in with emergency measures to tackle repercussions from the Iran war, including widespread ŌĆŗairspace closures and mounting concerns over jet fuel shortages, a document ŌĆīseen by Reuters showed.
Industry group Airlines for Europe (A4E) has requested that the EU introduce a raft of crisis response measures, including EU-level monitoring of jet fuel supplies, a temporary suspension of ŌĆŗthe EU's carbon market for aviation, and scrapping certain aviation taxes, it ŌĆŗsaid in the document.
The aviation sector has been hit by airspace closures ŌüĀsince the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran began on February 28, with the European ŌĆŗUnion Aviation Safety Agency banning European airlines from operating in the airspace of several Gulf ŌĆŗcountries, including the UAE and Qatar until April 24.
The sector is also staring at a jet fuel crunch after the closure of the Strait of Hormuz. Last week, industry group Airports Council ŌĆŗInternational Europe (ACI) warned that Europe could face a systemic jet fuel shortage in three ŌĆŗweeks.
The A4E document urged Brussels to consider joint EU purchasing of kerosene, which is a form ŌĆīof ŌüĀjet fuel.
The EU introduced joint natural gas buying to attempt to shore up supplies, after Russia slashed gas deliveries to Europe in 2022. However, the model has not been applied to oil or kerosene so far.
A4E, whose members include Lufthansa (LHAG.DE) , opens new tab , Air ŌĆŗFrance-KLM (AIRF.PA) , opens new tab and easyJet (EZJ.L) , opens new tab , ŌĆŗalso urged the ŌüĀEU to amend its legal requirement for countries to maintain 90 days of emergency oil reserves as this currently does not ŌĆŗinclude a specific requirement on jet fuel.
The document also asked ŌĆŗfor clarification ŌüĀon existing legislation, including confirmation that airspace closures due to conflict and resulting operational effects will be considered as justified non-use of slots.
The European Commission has said it ŌüĀwill propose a ŌĆŗpackage of measures on April 22 to attempt ŌĆŗto offset the fallout of the Iran war in energy markets, but has not confirmed if this ŌĆŗwill include specific measures on jet fuel.

item.title: Swedish green steel startup Stegra secures $1.7 billion from Wallenberg-led group
item.description:
STOCKHOLM, April 14 (Reuters) - Swedish ŌĆīgreen steel startup Stegra said on Tuesday it had raised 1.4 billion euros ($1.7 billion) from an investor consortium led by the Wallenberg family, providing a lifeline to complete construction of its greenfield plant.
The financing provides a reprieve for one of ŌĆŗthe few remaining large green steel projects in Europe, where high investment costs and ŌĆŗthe challenges of scaling green hydrogen have forced several planned projects to be ŌüĀdelayed, downsized or cancelled.
Stegra, formerly known as H2 Green Steel, has sought for months to raise more cash ŌĆŗto finish its flagship hydrogen-based steel plant in northern Sweden.
"This financing reflects the strong conviction in ŌĆŗStegra's business model among new and existing investors, as well as lenders," CEO Henrik Henriksson said in a statement.
Henriksson later told a press conference that he would not announce a specific start for production but that there ŌĆŗwould be a gradual phased ramp-up of operations.
Start of production has been pushed back several times, ŌĆŗwith the latest indications being 2027 prior to this funding news.
Europe has been at the forefrontof efforts ŌĆīto ŌüĀdecarbonise steelmaking to meet its climate targets, but developers have slowed or halted investments in green hydrogen, raising doubts over how quickly industries can move away from fossil fuels.
The Wallenberg-led consortium also includes Singapore state investor Temasek and IMAS Foundation, an investment arm within the IKEA sphere.
Wallenberg Investments said ŌĆŗit had committed to ŌĆŗinvesting 250 million euros. ŌüĀIts chair, Marcus Wallenberg, said the project had "clear importance to Sweden" and the group saw "a commercially viable way forward".
The Wallenberg family is Sweden's most ŌĆŗinfluential business dynasty, controlling firms worth hundreds of billions of dollars, including ŌĆŗSEB (SEBa.ST) , opens new tab , Ericsson (ERICb.ST) , opens new tab , ŌüĀAtlas Copco (ATCOa.ST) , opens new tab , Electrolux (ELUXb.ST) , opens new tab and Investor AB (INVEb.ST) , opens new tab .
The new financing has been agreed in principle, and is subject to credit approvals from lenders and finalisation of documentation, Stegra said.
The investors intend to nominate Wallenberg-veteran Leif Johansson ŌüĀas new ŌĆŗboard chair. Johansson has previously held the chairman role ŌĆŗat companies such as Ericsson and AstraZeneca, and been CEO of Electrolux and Volvo Group.
($1 = 0.8500 euros)

item.title: Lebanese and Israeli envoys to meet as Israel presses war on Hezbollah
item.description:
JERUSALEM/BEIRUT, April 14 (Reuters) - Israeli and Lebanese envoys will meet in Washington on Tuesday as Israel presses its war on Hezbollah , a diplomatic milestone ŌĆŗovershadowed by conflicting agendas with Israel ruling out a ceasefire and demanding Beirut disarm the Iran-backed group.
The meeting comes at a ŌĆīcritical juncture in the crisis in the Middle East , a week into a fragile ceasefire between the United States, Israel and Iran. The parallel war between Israel and Hezbollah in Lebanon has been a complicating factor in Pakistan's mediation to end the wider conflict.
U.S. Secretary of State Marco Rubio is set to attend the meeting between Israeli ambassador to the U.S., ŌĆŗYechiel Leiter, and his Lebanese counterpart, Nada Hamadeh Moawad, at 11 a.m. (1500 GMT), a State Department official said.
It marks a rare encounter between ŌĆŗrepresentatives of governments that have remained technically in a state of war since Israel was established in 1948.
The government led by President Joseph Aoun and Prime Minister Nawaf Salam has called for negotiations with Israel despite objections from Hezbollah, reflecting worsening tensions between ŌĆŗthe Shi'ite Muslim group and its opponents.
Hezbollah opened fire in support of Tehran on March 2, sparking an Israeli offensive that has killed more than 2,000 ŌĆŗpeople and forced 1.2 million from their homes, according to Lebanese authorities.
In Lebanon, the dead include 252 women and 166 children, the health ministry says. Sources familiar with the matter said on March 27 that more than 400 Hezbollah fighters have been killed. Hezbollah attacks have killed 13 Israeli soldiers and two Israeli civilians since March 2, Israel says.
Lebanese ŌĆŗofficials have said Moawad only has authority to discuss a ceasefire in Tuesday's meeting.
But Israeli government spokesperson Shosh Bedrosian said Israel would not discuss a ŌĆŗceasefire.
"What we're looking for ... is to see that Lebanon is committed to disarming Hezbollah ... demilitarizing southern Lebanon as well, and also to have a peace agreement," she said on ŌĆīMonday.
The Lebanese ŌüĀstate has been seeking to disarm Hezbollah peacefully since a war between the militia and Israel in 2024. Any move by Lebanon to disarm it by force risks igniting conflict in a country shattered by civil war from 1975 to 1990. Moves against Hezbollah by a Western-backed government in 2008 prompted a short civil war.
The current government banned Hezbollah's military wing after it opened fire on Israel last month.
Israel and the U.S. have ŌĆŗsaid the campaign against Hezbollah was not ŌĆŗpart of the Iran-U.S. ceasefire, ŌüĀthough Pakistan's prime minister had said the truce would include Lebanon, as Iran had demanded.
While Israel has pressed attacks in Lebanon, it has launched no airstrikes in Beirut since last Wednesday, when it pounded the capital in a 10-minute ŌĆŗbarrage that killed hundreds of people across Lebanon.
The following day, U.S. President Donald Trump, in an interview with ŌĆŗNBC News, said Israeli ŌüĀPrime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu had told him he would "low-key it" in Lebanon.
A U.S. State Department official said that Israel was at war with Hezbollah, not Lebanon, and so there was no reason they should not talk, describing the talks as direct, high-level and the first of their kind since 1993.
The conversation would "scope the ongoing ŌüĀdialogue about how ŌĆŗto ensure the long-term security of Israel's northern border and to support the Government of ŌĆŗLebanon's determination to reclaim full sovereignty over its territory and political life".
Hezbollah leader Naim Qassem on Monday called on the government to cancel the meeting, saying Hezbollah would continue to confront Israeli ŌĆŗattacks on Lebanon.

item.title: Orban's defeat topples a pillar of Europe's far right, prompts scrutiny of MAGA links
item.description:
BERLIN, April 14 (Reuters) - Europe's far right has lost one of its biggest champions with the defeat of Hungary's nationalist leader Viktor Orban in Sunday's election.
Orban's government provided a blueprint for right-wing populist and illiberal politicians, earning plaudits from the likes ŌĆŗof U.S. President Donald Trump and Russian President Vladimir Putin.
In his 16 years in office, he promoted ethno-nationalism, clamped down on civil society and the media, and fought ŌĆīagainst immigration, LGBTQ rights and liberalism. He was also the European leader with by far the closest ties to Trump's MAGA movement ŌĆō illustrated by Vice President JD Vance's visit to Budapest to endorse him last week.
His loss - blamed on Hungarians' dissatisfaction with the economy, corruption and curbs on democratic freedoms - deprives the growing European far right not only of a model for the nationalist governments they aspire to establish across the region, but a deeply resourced ally who ploughed hundreds ŌĆŗof millions of dollars into championing those ideologies.
"Orban has been pretty much the figurehead of the European far right for the last few years and even beyond the European far ŌĆŗright," said Gabriela Greilinger, a doctoral researcher based in the United States, focusing on the European far right and democratic erosion.
"He has been the model ŌüĀbecause he was able to cling to power for so long and really entrench himself and his ideology in the state. And that's something that most other far-right parties have not been able ŌĆŗto do so far."
Orban's closeness to the MAGA movement is now seen as a double-edged sword by some far-right politicians, with Trump's threats to seize Greenland and the war with Iran contributing to his deep ŌĆŗunpopularity , opens new tab in Europe.
Orban's "ostentatious friendship" with the current U.S. administration "hung like millstones around Orban's neck," wrote Alternative for Germany lawmaker Matthias Moosdorf on X on Monday.
Orban's legacy of regularly wielding Hungary's veto power at the European Union, often to block financing for Ukraine or Russian sanctions, drew praise from other political leaders keen to see the bloc undermined.
"He was a thorn in the side to the EU and that was a good thing," said Ben Habib, the ŌĆŗleader of the Advance UK party, an anti-immigration party launched in Britain last year.
In the wake of Orban's defeat by the pro-EU Peter Magyar, Alice Weidel, co-leader of Alternative for Germany, wrote on ŌĆŗX: "His achievements for his homeland and his contributions to Europe continue to inspire us to stand up for a continent of sovereign nations."
Much of Orban's influence came not only from his success at home, but his ability to ŌĆīspread his ideas ŌüĀand policies.
With government funding and corporate stakes, Orban gave the equivalent of over a billion dollars to the likes of the Mathias Corvinus Collegium (MCC), a private research institute, and the Danube Institute, which have acted as ideological arms of his Fidesz party.
"Budapest became a pilgrimage - people were over there all the time from MAGA world, so it was a successful strategy," said Daniel Fried, a fellow at the Atlantic Council think tank in Washington, D.C.
Political conferences hosted groups from other parts of Europe and the U.S., including some of the most influential organisations in the current Trump administration such as the Heritage Foundation, ŌĆŗAmerica First Policy Institute and Alliance Defending Freedom.
The ŌĆŗinfluence of the Orban-aligned institutes was also palpable ŌüĀin Washington, D.C. as Trump prepared to begin his second term as U.S. president, said Jacob Ross, a research fellow at the German Council on Foreign Relations.
"I was really surprised to see how many Hungarian delegations were there, how active the Hungarian embassy in D.C. was interacting with Heritage," he ŌĆŗsaid.
Magyar said on Monday the government would no longer use taxpayer money to finance organisations like MCC or political party events.
But the institutes have ŌĆŗalready been effective, and are ŌüĀunlikely to disappear even with their funding changed, said Greilinger.
"Most of these organisations are also there to ensure that these ideas kind of live on even beyond the rule of the leader, or in this case, beyond the rule of Viktor Orban," she said.
Orban's European allies also said they believed momentum was still behind them, and that after so long in power governments often face growing dissatisfaction.
"We are ŌüĀgoing to miss ŌĆŗthe support of the Hungarian government, but let's see how we move from here," said Ta╠énger Corre╠éa, a member of ŌĆŗthe European Parliament in the Patriots for Europe group, which includes Fidesz.
Corre╠éa said polls were promising for France's National Rally ahead of next year's presidential elections, and last year his own Chega party became the second-largest parliamentary force in Portugal.
"It's not ŌĆŗpleasant that one of our members lost an election," said Corre╠éa.
"But it's life, we move on."

item.title: Cuba's top destinations deserted, without power or fuel under US sanctions
item.description:
CIENAGA DE ZAPATA, Cuba, April 14 (Reuters) - The sun is setting in P├Īlpite, a small town on the edge of Cuba's vast Zapata Swamp, when suddenly the road swarms with activity. But not with the red land crabs that once attracted hundreds of thousands of tourists annually to one of the island's top eco-tourism destinations.
The crowds now are local residents pouring out of their homes in search of a cell ŌĆŗphone signal when the lights flicker back on amid a U.S. energy blockade that has condemned a large swath of the island to live without electricity.
"What tourist will want to ŌĆīvisit us under these conditions?" laments Manuela Arencibia B├Īez, owner of a rental house - now mostly empty - just down the road in the beachfront Playa Larga, 2 miles away and the region's top overnight destination.
She told Reuters she'd lost count of all the reservations she'd had to cancel, including those from tourists already in the country unable to find a taxi driver with sufficient gas to make the two hour trip south and east from Havana.
Official data underscore Arencibia's concerns. International tourist ŌĆŗarrivals plunged 56% in February from one year prior. Both of Playa Larga's hotels are closed. Most of its tourist attractions are shuttered as well.
Cuba has for decades been plagued by shortages, ŌĆŗhampered by a state-run economy and a Cold War-era U.S. trade embargo that complicates everything from financial transactions to the import of fuel. But this time ŌüĀis different, more than a dozen residents and tourism industry workers of the Zapata region told Reuters.
"We are much worse off even than during the coronavirus pandemic," said Arencibia, rattling off a list of lost ŌĆŗreservations from countries like Switzerland, Canada, France and Germany.
Power cuts now run for 22 hours a day, with just a couple hours of light in which residents scramble to call loved ones in Miami, or Havana, ŌĆŗor to prepare food before it rots in refrigerators.
Water is running short across many communities. Medical services - always complicated in far-flung corners of the country - are now a remote possibility, hampered by power cuts and lack of communications. Fuel for independent travelers who once flocked to the area in rental cars is largely unavailable.
Trump's fuel blockade coincided with what has historically been peak tourism season for Cuba, all but ensuring a disastrous year, a death knell for an industry already knee-capped by ŌĆŗshortages but that in 2024 still accounted for as much as 10% of foreign currency earnings.
Many hotels and services, from Havana east to top beach destinations Varadero, Cayo Santa Mar├Ła and Cayo Coco, were forced to close ŌĆŗwhen fuel shortages prompted many airlines to cut flights.
"In these months, I am always booked," said Fidel Silvestre Fuentes, a 67-year-old rental home owner who has long provided accommodations to birdwatchers who come from across the world to see ŌĆīthe bee ŌüĀhummingbird, the world's smallest, among other endemic species. "Now, we're empty."
The Zapata Swamp region, much of it contained within a pristine national park - nonetheless has more private rental homes open to tourists than even Cuba's top sun and beach destination, Varadero. Now though, it feels like a ghost town.
The potholes have grown deeper along the region's single access road. The coastal route between the white sands of Playa Larga, at the foot of the bay, and Playa Gir├│n, further out, feels abandoned, inhabited mostly by stray crabs, wayward residents on horseback and men and women on bikes.
Even the region's top attractions are closed. The Cueva de los Peces, ŌĆŗa transparent seemingly bottomless cave filled with colorful tropical ŌĆŗfish, has been shuttered for two months, a ŌüĀguard told a reporter.
The impacts are especially dire in regions like Zapata, which offers residents few other options for work beyond tourism.
"If you don't a rental home, you work in a restaurant; or if you don't work in the sector, maybe you are related to someone who does," said Jorge Alberto Brito, a ŌĆŗhat and souvenir seller who now lives off a scarce few pesos a day. "Without a doubt we have hit rock bottom."
Fidel Fuentes Ray├│, who rents a ŌĆŗhome in Playa Larga, is ŌüĀamong the lucky few with money enough to purchase solar panels and a lithium battery for storage, giving his home a leg up on the competition.
It hasn't helped, he says.
"Tourists do not come to Cuba for comfortable accommodation, they come to but to tour the National Park and enjoy bird watching, diving services, boat rides, fly fishing... experiences that are just not available now," he said, citing fuel shortages and transportation challenges.
Still, a scattered few tourists still ŌüĀroll the ŌĆŗdice on travel to the region.
Blair Andrews, an American tourist, has returned repeatedly to Cuba, enough so to be on a ŌĆŗfirst name basis with the guides at a Bay of Pigs scuba diving operation.
The 51-year-old told Reuters electricity and cell service were the least of her concerns.
"I come back because the Cuban people are good hosts and have a beautiful culture," she said as ŌĆŗshe strapped a tank to her back before diving into the clear aqua-green water of the Bay of Pigs. "I'm very sad about what's happening to them."

item.title: Russian strike on Ukraine's Dnipro kills four, officials say
item.description:
April 14 (Reuters) - A Russian strike ŌĆŗon Ukrainian ŌĆīcity of Dnipro ŌĆŗkilled ŌĆŗfour people and injured ŌüĀ25, ŌĆŗUkrainian officials ŌĆŗsaid on Tuesday.

item.title: Hormuz mission talks to focus on sanctions, seafarer releases, shipping readiness ŌĆō source
item.description:

item.title: Nigeria police arrest 33-member gang over Kwara church abductions
item.description:
ABUJA, April 14 (Reuters) - Nigerian police have arrested a 33-member gang over a November
attack ŌĆŗin which 38 people were abducted from ŌĆīa church in central Kwara state, police said on Tuesday, as part of a nationwide crackdown on ŌĆŗviolence.
Federal police spokesperson Anthony Okon Placid ŌĆŗsaid the gang carried out the November ŌüĀ18 attack on Christ Apostolic Church ŌĆŗin Eruku town and was also responsible for ŌĆŗother kidnappings, cattle rustling, and armed robbery across the region.
The 33 suspects were arrested in coordinated raids ŌĆŗacross Kwara and neighbouring Kogi state following ŌĆŗhuman and technical intelligence, part of a wider operation ŌĆīthat ŌüĀhas detained 50 suspects since January, Placid said in a statement.
Nigeria has long grappled with insecurity, from mass kidnappings for ransom ŌĆŗto banditry and ŌĆŗmilitant ŌüĀattacks. The Eruku assault intensified pressure on the government, already under ŌĆŗscrutiny from U.S. President Donald Trump, ŌĆŗwho ŌüĀhad threatened military action over what he calls persecution of Christians.

item.title: Italy suspends defence cooperation deal with Israel
item.description:
ROME, April 14 (Reuters) - Italy's government has decided to ŌĆŗsuspend the automatic renewal of a defence ŌĆīcooperation deal with Israel, Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni said on Tuesday, citing the conflicts in the Middle East .
Meloni's ŌĆŗright-wing government has been one of ŌĆŗIsrael's closest allies in Europe, but in ŌüĀrecent weeks it has criticised Israeli attacks on ŌĆŗLebanon. Those affected have included Italian troops serving ŌĆŗthere under a U.N. mandate.
"In light of the current situation, the government has decided to suspend the automatic ŌĆŗrenewal of the defence agreement with Israel," ŌĆŗMeloni was quoted as saying in Verona, nothern Italy, ŌĆīby ŌüĀItalian news agencies.
A defence ministry source said one of the consequences is that Italy will no longer cooperate with Israel on military training.
Meloni ŌĆŗtook the ŌĆŗdecision on ŌüĀMonday with her foreign and defence ministers, Antonio Tajani and Guido ŌĆŗCrosetto, as well as Deputy Prime ŌĆŗMinister ŌüĀMatteo Salvini, the source said, speaking on condition of anonymity.
A spokesperson for Israel's foreign ministry ŌüĀdid ŌĆŗnot immediately respond to a ŌĆŗrequest for comment from Reuters.

item.title: No discussions between Qatar and Iran over payment of funds to stop Iranian attacks, Qatari official says

item.title: Kremlin says it is glad Hungary's Magyar seems ready for 'pragmatic dialogue' with Russia
item.description:
MOSCOW, April 14 (Reuters) - The Kremlin said on Tuesday that it was pleased that Hungarian Prime Minister-elect Peter ŌĆŗMagyar seemed ready for pragmatic dialogue with Russia and ŌĆīwould base its own position on the specific steps taken by his new government
Russian hardliners have mourned the defeat of Viktor Orban, ŌĆŗa close partner of Moscow's, who lost a weekend ŌĆŗelection to upstart centre-right rival Magyar. But the ŌüĀKremlin quickly said it was ready for talks with ŌĆŗthe country's new leadership.
"For now, we can note with satisfaction, ŌĆŗas far as we understand, his (Magyar's) willingness to engage in pragmatic dialogue," Kremlin spokesman Dmitry Peskov told reporters.
"In this instance, there is ŌĆŗmutual willingness on our part, and we will then ŌĆŗproceed to take our cue from the specific steps taken by the ŌĆīnew ŌüĀHungarian government."
Russia sells oil and gas to Hungary and is building a new nuclear power plant south of Budapest. Orban was useful to Moscow because he often spoke out ŌĆŗagainst EU ŌĆŗsanctions on Russia ŌüĀover its war in Ukraine, opposed Kyiv's bid to join the EU, and had ŌĆŗblocked a 90-billion-euro ($105-billion) EU loan to Ukraine.
Magyar ŌĆŗhas combined ŌüĀpro-EU and pro-NATO rhetoric with a public recognition that he will need to hold talks with Russian President Vladimir Putin ŌüĀand ŌĆŗcontinue to buy Russian oil and ŌĆŗgas for now despite talk of diversifying and reviewing contracts.

item.title: Morning Bid: War-weary markets filter out noise
item.description:
April 14 - What matters in U.S. and global markets today
By Mike Dolan , opens new tab , Editor-at-Large, Finance and Markets
Markets are clinging to hopes that there will be an eventual breakthrough in U.S.-Iran negotiations, with oil prices retreating and stocks rebounding even as the U.S. blockade ŌĆŗof Iranian ports takes effect .
The half-hearted stock selloff early on Monday, which later unwound , indicated that the impact of each twist and turn in the ŌĆīconflict appears to be dissipating, with investors sticking to the view that weŌĆÖll soon see significant de-escalation and a freeing up of oil supplies.
I'll get into that and more below.
But first, check out my latest column on why Hungary's political shift could have big implications for EU assets.
And listen to the latest episode of the Morning Bid daily podcast , where I discuss the S&P 500's return - ŌĆŗjust about - to its pre-war level.
Finally, don't forget to mark April 23 in your calendar, when I'll be joining my ROI colleague Jamie McGeever for ŌĆŗa timely webinar discussion on rethinking safe-haven assets in uncertain times. Sign up here , opens new tab .
Despite the imposition of ŌüĀthe U.S. blockade and hard talk between Washington and Tehran, reports over the past 24 hours have suggested there is still some dialogue between them and that talks could resume ŌĆŗthis week .
ThatŌĆÖs been enough to guide Brent and WTI crude back below $100 per barrel. OilŌĆÖs retreat helped spark a rally on Wall Street on Monday, with the S&P 500 ŌĆŗending up about 1%, leaving it higher than it was at the outbreak of the war more than six weeks ago.
That momentum has so far carried on into Tuesday, with Asian stock indexes closing in the green, European shares rising , and Wall Street futures ticking up . The dollar index fell to its lowest point in a month-and-a-half on the upturn in risk sentiment.
Turning to corporate news, earnings season ŌĆŗis well and truly underway as traders await more updates from the big U.S. banks today. Goldman Sachs, which reported on Monday, stumbled in fixed income and ŌĆŗcurrency trading during the quarter, pushing its shares down despite headline results beating estimates.
JPMorgan, Citi, and Wells Fargo are up next in whatŌĆÖs expected to be another impressive corporate scorecard, despite the ŌĆīoil shock ŌüĀat the end of the quarter.
The IMF, meantime, will have the unenviable task today of releasing its forecasts for the world economy for this year. Both it and the World Bank have already signaled they will downgrade their global growth projections and raise their inflation predictions because of the war.
On the U.S. macro front, existing home sales fell to a nine-month low in March, pushed down by a lackluster labor market and stumbling household purchasing power and wealth. The outlook for the year appears less than stellar ŌĆŗas mortgage rates rise amid the ŌĆŗIran war.
Elsewhere, ChinaŌĆÖs exports fell in March ŌüĀas the Iran war appeared to hamper AI-driven tech demand. Outbound shipments grew by just 2.5%, a five-month low, and far below the 21.8% surge seen in February. Economists polled by Reuters had forecast growth of 8.3%.
Chart of the day
Goldman Sachs beat expectations ŌĆŗfor quarterly profit on Monday, driven by strength in dealmaking and equities trading, but the U.S. investment bank's shares fell ŌĆŗ2% on weakness in ŌüĀfixed income markets, interest rate trading, mortgages and credit. The bank's revenue hit $17.2 billion, however, its highest three-month tally since a record of $17.7 billion in Q1 2021.
Today's events to watch
* U.S. March PPI (8:30 a.m. EDT)
* U.S. 12-month bill auction (11:30 a.m. EDT)
* FedŌĆÖs Michael Barr, Boston FedŌĆÖs Susan Collins, Richmond FedŌĆÖs Thomas Barkin, and Philadelphia FedŌĆÖs Anna Paulson all speak
* ŌüĀU.S. corporate ŌĆŗearnings: JPMorgan, Citigroup, Wells Fargo
* IMF publishes its latest World Economic Outlook (9 a.m. EDT)
Want to receive ŌĆŗthe Morning Bid in your inbox every weekday morning? Sign up for the newsletter here . You can find ROI on the Reuters website , opens new tab , and you can follow us on LinkedIn , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .
Opinions expressed are those of the author. They ŌĆŗdo not reflect the views of Reuters News, which, under the Trust Principles , opens new tab , is committed to integrity, independence, and freedom from bias.

item.title: No shortage of jet fuel in EU, but concerns remain, Commission says

BRUSSELS, April 14 (Reuters) - There is no shortage of jet fuel in ŌĆŗthe European Union at the ŌĆīmoment, but supply problems could occur and the situation remains a ŌĆŗtop concern, the European Commission ŌĆŗsaid on Tuesday.
European airlines have urged ŌüĀthe European Union to step ŌĆŗin with emergency measures to ŌĆŗtackle repercussions from the Iran war, including widespread airspace closures and mounting concerns ŌĆŗover jet fuel shortages, a ŌĆŗdocument seen by Reuters showed.
"There is no ŌĆīevidence ŌüĀfor fuel shortage in the European Union at present, but supply issues could occur in ŌĆŗthe near ŌĆŗfuture," ŌüĀa Commission spokesperson told reporters in Brussels.
"Crude oil ŌĆŗsupplies to the European ŌĆŗrefineries ŌüĀremain stable, with no need for additional stock releases at the ŌüĀpresent. ŌĆŗHowever, that remains ŌĆŗour primary concern," the spokesperson added.

item.title: Russia imposes temporary export controls on helium
item.description:
MOSCOW, April 14 (Reuters) - Russia has imposed temporary export controls on helium to maintain a stable ŌĆŗsupply to the domestic market, where the gas ŌĆīis mainly used in the production of fibre optics, the government said in a statement on Tuesday.
The war in the Middle East has tightened ŌĆŗthe supply of the gas, which is also used ŌĆŗin several key stages of chipmaking, including cooling, leak ŌüĀdetection and precision manufacturing processes.
The decree added helium to a ŌĆŗlist of commodities whose export outside the Eurasian Economic Union (EEU) ŌĆŗrequires special permission by top government officials. The new export regime will be in force until the end of 2027.
Russia is the third-largest global ŌĆŗproducer of helium, a byproduct of natural gas processing, accounting ŌĆŗfor around 8% of the global production, according to Gazprombank's analysts. At ŌĆīhome, ŌüĀhelium is important for the production of fibre optics, increasingly used by the army to control drones.
However, Russia trails far behind the United States and Qatar, with the Middle Eastern country ŌĆŗproducing over one-third ŌĆŗof the ŌüĀglobal supply in 2025.
Earlier this month, Russian Prime Minister Mikhail Mishustin said that global supply disruptions ŌĆŗcaused by the war in the Middle East ŌĆŗhave opened ŌüĀup new trade opportunities for Russia, but price stability in the domestic market remains a priority.
He mentioned helium among commodities whose ŌüĀexports ŌĆŗRussia can increase. Gazprom's (GAZP.MM) , opens new tab Amur Gas ŌĆŗProcessing Plant in the Far East is the biggest producer of helium in ŌĆŗRussia.

item.title: Yancoal Australia to buy 80% stake in Kestrel coal mine for up to $2.4 billion
item.description:
April 14 (Reuters) - Yancoal Australia (YAL.AX) , opens new tab , said ŌĆīon Tuesday it has agreed to acquire an 80% stake in the Kestrel coking coal mine in Queensland for up to $2.4 billion, a move that strengthens its position as one ŌĆŗof the country's largest coal miners.
Yancoal, which has a market capitalisation of about $6.77 ŌĆŗbillion, will purchase the majority stake in the Bowen Basin-based mine ŌüĀfrom private equity firm EMR Capital and Alamtri Resources Indonesia (ADRO.JK) , opens new tab .
The remaining 20% is ŌĆŗheld by Japanese trading house Mitsui & Co (8031.T) , opens new tab , which did not immediately respond to a ŌĆŗReuters request for comment.
The transaction comprises an upfront cash payment of $1.85 billion on completion and up to $550 million in additional annual payments over five years, subject to certain conditions.
Yancoal said ŌĆŗit intends to fund the acquisition via a combination of available cash and ŌĆŗa $1.2 billion five-year syndicated acquisition loan facility, among other options.
The company, controlled by China's Yankuang Energy (600188.SS) , opens new tab , ŌĆīsaid ŌüĀthe acquisition would strengthen its position in the Bowen Basin, with Kestrel located near YancoalŌĆÖs existing Middlemount joint venture and Yarrabee operation.
The acquisition was a strategic fit for the company and adds a high-quality mine to its portfolio, CEO Sharif ŌĆŗBurra said.
"Kestrel delivers ŌĆŗincreased scale and ŌüĀdiversification to YancoalŌĆÖs portfolio and is expected to contribute premium metallurgical coal into our product mix."
Kestrel, the largest producing underground ŌĆŗcoal mine in Australia, recorded saleable production of 5.9 ŌĆŗmillion tons ŌüĀin 2025, Yancoal said.
