The deal is expected to close by the end of the third quarter of 2026.
EMR and Alamtri had bought the 80% stake in the coal ŌüĀmine ŌĆŗfrom mining giant Rio Tinto (RIO.AX) , opens new tab for $2.25 billion in ŌĆŗ2018.
Yancoal has applied to resume trading on the Hong Kong Stock Exchange on Wednesday, after being halted ahead of ŌĆŗthe acquisition announcement.

item.title: Sinopec buys Russian oil to replace Mideast supply after US waiver, sources say
item.description:
SINGAPORE, April 14 (Reuters) - Chinese state oil giant Sinopec (600028.SS) , opens new tab has bought Russian oil loading in March and April to replace Middle Eastern crude after ŌĆŗthe U.S. temporarily waived sanctions to ease tight supplies globally, several trade ŌĆīsources said.
One source pegged Sinopec's purchase volume at 8 to 10 cargoes of ESPO blend crude exported from the eastern port of Kozmino, while another estimated it at about 10 cargoes of ŌĆŗESPO.
Each ESPO cargo is 740,000 barrels.
Sinopec bought the cargoes at premiums of $8 ŌĆŗto $10 per barrel to ICE Brent, a third person said. Russian ŌüĀcrude traded at a discount of about $10 per barrel before the Iran conflict.
The sources ŌĆŗspoke on condition of anonymity.
The U.S. Treasury Department allowed purchases of Russian oil and products ŌĆŗat sea from mid-March with a 30-day waiver that expired on April 11, as part of efforts to control global energy prices during the U.S.-Israeli war with Iran.
The waiver led the trading ŌĆŗarms of Sinopec and PetroChina to make inquiries with suppliers for possible purchases. They had ŌĆŗsuspended seaborne purchases of Russian crude since October due to Western sanctions, Reuters reported previously.
It was ŌĆīunclear ŌüĀif PetroChina has bought seaborne cargoes since.
Sinopec did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
Sinopec, the world's largest refiner, usually sources roughly half of its crude from the Middle East, leaving it particularly exposed to the near-closure of ŌĆŗthe Strait of Hormuz ŌĆŗamid the U.S.-Israeli ŌüĀwar on Iran.
Last month Sinopec told a results briefing it was cutting runs by 5% in March because of the disruption, while ŌĆŗassessing the possibility of purchasing Russian oil under waiver.
China's seaborne Russian ŌĆŗcrude imports ŌüĀin March stood at 1.82 million bpd, off February's record of 1.92 million bpd, Kpler data showed. April's imports stand at 1.92 million bpd so far.
The U.S. waiver boosted demand ŌüĀfrom ŌĆŗIndian refiners who snapped up millions of barrels of ŌĆŗRussian oil at sea. Market participants expect Washington to extend the waiver , although it has made no announcement.

item.title: BP flags 'exceptional' trading results amid oil price volatility
item.description:
LONDON, April 14 (Reuters) - BP (BP.L) , opens new tab said on Tuesday it expects its huge oil trading desk ŌĆīto post "exceptional" results for the first quarter, signalling a windfall from the spike in oil prices triggered by the U.S.-Israeli war against Iran.
This broadly echoed the first-quarter outlook from European rival Shell (SHEL.L) , opens new tab , which also ŌĆŗflagged strong results in oil trading , an area where European majors are more active than ŌĆŗU.S. competitors, triggering analysts to increase their earnings forecasts.
Citi analysts raised their ŌüĀearnings forecast for BP by 20%, expecting first-quarter adjusted net income of $2.6 billion.
BP also said ŌĆŗin a quarterly trading statement that its net debt would rise to between $25 billion and $27 ŌĆŗbillion from just over $22 billion in the previous quarter because of movements in working capital, an accounting measure of liquidity based on current assets minus liabilities.
Global benchmark Brent crude soared ŌĆŗto multi-year highs near $120 a barrel after U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran began in late February, ŌĆŗfollowed by Tehran's closure of the vital Strait of Hormuz shipping route and attacks on Gulf neighbours.
Brent ŌĆīaveraged ŌüĀaround $78 a barrel during the January-to-March quarter, compared with $63 in the fourth quarter and $75 a barrel during the same time last year, according to Reuters calculations.
BP's overall oil and gas production is expected to be broadly flat on the quarter, it said.
Refining margins rose to $16.9 a ŌĆŗbarrel in the first ŌĆŗquarter from $15.2 in the ŌüĀprevious three months, it said, adding this would help boost results in its refined products business by $100 million to $200 million.
Energy companies typically ŌĆŗdo not reveal full results of their trading divisions.
BP is due to report first-quarter results on April 28.
Meg O'Neill became the company's fifth CEO since 2020 this month, pledging to continue with a one-year-old revamp to redirect billions of spending ŌüĀfrom ŌĆŗlow-carbon projects into oil and gas to increase profitability.
She ŌĆŗwill face shareholders at the company's annual general meeting on April 23 after influential proxy advisers and some shareholders ŌĆŗhave supported votes against the board's wishes.

item.title: Tunisian judge orders detention of former head of anti-corruption body
item.description:
TUNIS, April 14 (Reuters) - A Tunisian judge ordered the detention of former anti-corruption chief Chawki Tabib on Tuesday, in the latest incident in what critics describe ŌĆŗas an escalating crackdown on high-profile figures in the country.
A judicial ŌĆīofficial said the case against Tabib, who is also a former head of the Tunisian Bar Association, concerned financial and administrative mismanagement during his tenure heading the anti-corruption ŌĆŗbody, which was dissolved by President Kais Saied in 2021.
Boubaker Ben ŌĆŗThabet, the current head of the Tunisian Bar Association, told ŌüĀReuters that Tabib's detention violated legal procedures and amounted to harassment.
Saied dissolved ŌĆŗthe elected parliament in 2021 and began ruling by decree, a move the ŌĆŗopposition called a coup.
In 2022, he also dissolved the Supreme Judicial Council and sacked dozens of judges. He said the steps were legal and aimed at fighting corruption.
Tabib said ŌĆŗon Sunday, before his detention, that the new case was part of ŌĆŗa campaign targeting him. He had previously been placed under a travel ban.
His detention ŌĆīcame a ŌüĀday after courts sentenced other public figures in separate cases, including El Hiwar TV owner Sami Fehri to five years in prison on financial charges, and media commentator Sonia Dahmani to 18 months over media comments.
Last month, ŌĆŗa Tunisian court ŌĆŗhanded down a ŌüĀtwo-year prison sentence to news website editor Ghassen Ben Khelifa , a move the journalists' union described as part of a "systemic" ŌĆŗattack on free speech.
The leaders of the main opposition parties ŌĆŗin ŌüĀTunisia have been jailed within the last three years, along with dozens of politicians, journalists, activists and businessmen on charges of conspiring against state security, money ŌüĀlaundering and ŌĆŗcorruption.
The opposition says Saied is targeting the ŌĆŗcountryŌĆÖs elite to silence dissent and strengthen one-man rule. Saied says he is purging corruption and "traitors", ŌĆŗinsisting no one is above accountability.

item.title: Pope Leo, after Trump's attack, honours saint in Algeria who decried wars
item.description:
ANNABA, Algeria, April 14 (Reuters) - Pope Leo paid homage to his spiritual roots on Tuesday, travelling to the ancient birthplace in Algeria of St. ŌĆŗAugustine of Hippo, who inspired him to become a priest.
The first ŌĆīAmerican pope has drawn the ire of U.S. President Donald Trump after sharply criticising the Iran war . In honouring Augustine, Leo was highlighting a personal connection to a major figure of the ŌĆŗearly Church who first proposed criteria to evaluate whether wars could be ŌĆŗconsidered just.
Augustine, who died in the year 430, said wars ŌüĀshould only be waged in order to defend against aggression or protect ŌĆŗthe innocent, with the intention of restoring a state of peace and never ŌĆŗout of a desire for cruelty.
Those criteria, with updates, remain Catholic doctrine. They have been invoked by some critics of the Iran war to argue that the conflict, started by ŌĆŗsurprise U.S.-Israeli airstrikes against Iran on February 28, is unjust.
Washington Cardinal Robert ŌĆŗMcElroy is among those critics. He said on Sunday that the war was "morally illegitimate", citing ŌĆīAugustine's ŌüĀprinciples.
Leo, who is on an ambitious, 10-day tour of four African countries, told Reuters on Monday that he planned to keep criticizing the war, despite Trump's comments.
He did not offer remarks on Tuesday as he visited the remnants of the city ŌĆŗof Hippo, now ŌĆŗAnnaba, on Algeria's ŌüĀnortheast coast. Under light rain, he laid a wreath of yellow and white roses and helped several Algerian Muslim ŌĆŗScouts in tan uniforms plant a sapling.
At a later meeting ŌĆŗon Tuesday ŌüĀat a nearby nursing home run by Catholic sisters, the pope, a member of the Augustinian religious order, spoke out again.
"God's heart is torn apart by ŌüĀwars, violence, ŌĆŗinjustice and lies," Leo told the residents of ŌĆŗthe Little Sisters of the Poor home. "Our Father's heart is not with the wicked, the arrogant ŌĆŗor the proud."

item.title: Aid groups use land routes to deliver humanitarian relief to Iran
item.description:
GENEVA, April 14 (Reuters) - International humanitarian groups said ŌĆŗon Tuesday they had sent emergency relief through overland routes to Iran, some of ŌĆītheir first deliveries of aid since U.S.-Israeli strikes began in late February.
Aid workers say needs are high in the wake of six weeks of strikes, but stocks of emergency humanitarian supplies became stuck in Dubai warehouses as shipping ŌĆŗand air routes were blocked by the expanding conflict.
Iranian authorities say more than 3,000 people ŌĆŗhave been killed in Iran during the war and the U.N. refugee agency ŌüĀsays up to 3.2 million have been displaced.
The International Federation of Red Cross and Red ŌĆŗCrescent Societies said it had delivered some 200 trauma kits as well as tents and blankets on ŌĆŗtrucks sent along a new route from Turkey.
The trucks crossed the Iranian border on Sunday and are set to arrive in the capital Tehran on Tuesday, said IFRC spokesperson Tommaso Della Longa.
"For us it's very important as ŌĆŗit represents a new route for getting aid into Iran and we're very optimistic to scale ŌĆŗup," he told a Geneva press briefing.
"Before it was very easy to take a flight or a boat and ŌĆībring ŌüĀaid directly to Iran in a couple of hours."
Separately, the International Committee of the Red Cross said it had made its first shipment to Iran since the conflict began, with five trucks arriving from Jordan on Monday. Another nine are expected later this week.
It also handed supplies to the ŌĆŗIranian Red Crescent, the only ŌĆŗhumanitarian group working ŌüĀacross Iran, with 100,000 responders, four of whom have been killed in the war.
"Needs are high, medical needs in particular, but also the psychological toll ŌĆŗon people is immense," Della Longa said in a statement.
"Our partners at ŌĆŗthe Iranian ŌüĀRed Crescent have been providing psychological care in addition to the work they did throughout, literally digging people out of buildings."
The ICRC trucks contained supplies like buckets, blankets and mattresses to meet the needs ŌüĀof some ŌĆŗ25,000 people, including those displaced by the conflict.
Following the collapse ŌĆŗof weekend negotiations to end the war, sources told Reuters that negotiating teams from the U.S. and Iran could return to ŌĆŗIslamabad this week to resume talks.

item.title: Malaysia brings home high-value art, including a Picasso, seized in 1MDB probe
item.description:
KUALA LUMPUR, April 14 (Reuters) - Malaysia's anti-graft agency said on Tuesday it had successfully repatriated four high-value pieces of artwork, including a 1961 Pablo Picasso print, recovered in connection with ŌĆŗa multibillion-dollar scandal at state fund 1MDB .
Over the past decade, Malaysia has sought ŌĆīto recover more than $4.5 billion siphoned from 1Malaysia Development Berhad between 2009 and 2014 in a complex, globe-spanning scheme.
U.S. investigators have said the funds - allegedly misappropriated by high-level officials of 1MDB and their ŌĆŗassociates - were used to buy everything from luxury hotels to jewelry and ŌĆŗartwork.
The Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission said in a statement the recovered artworks were ŌüĀvalued at around $198,000 and include Picasso's "LŌĆÖEcuy├©re et les clowns" (1961), Joan Mir├│'s "Composition" (1953), Maurice Utrillo's "Maison de ŌĆŗRendez-vous de chasse de Henri IV, Rue St. Vincent, Montmartre" (1934) and Balthus' "├ētude pour femme ŌĆŗcouch├®e" (1948).
"All artworks have been placed in a specially designated location under strict security control and are currently undergoing an environmental stabilisation process to ensure optimal preservation prior to uncrating, authentication, and valuation by the ŌĆŗNational Visual Arts Development Board," the agency said.
The MACC said in December it would ŌĆŗrepatriate a dozen pieces of art held in the United States, following discussions with officials from ŌĆīthe Federal ŌüĀBureau of Investigation and U.S. Justice Department.
The Picasso and Mir├│ pieces were among the 12 artworks that were recovered from a former 1MDB lawyer and then stored at the Christie's and Sotheby's auction houses, the MACC said.
Authorities have said the artwork may be ŌĆŗpublicly displayed at Malaysia's ŌĆŗnational art gallery ŌüĀbefore potentially being auctioned for sale.
"Each recovered asset, including these artworks, demonstrates that there is no safe haven for illicit proceeds to ŌĆŗbe concealed, even when involving complex cross-border financial networks," the MACC ŌĆŗsaid on ŌüĀTuesday.
To date, Malaysia has recovered about 31.2 billion ringgit ($7.90 billion) in assets, representing nearly 70% of all identified properties linked to 1MDB, it said.
1MDB, co-founded by former prime minister Najib ŌüĀRazak, ŌĆŗhas been the subject of corruption and money laundering ŌĆŗinvestigations in at least six countries. Najib, who has consistently denied wrongdoing, was jailed in 2022 after being convicted ŌĆŗof graft linked to the scandal.
($1 = 3.9490 ringgit)

item.title: Greenland appoints former PM Egede as foreign minister
item.description:
COPENHAGEN, April 14 (Reuters) - Greenland's prime minister said on Tuesday he had appointed ŌĆŗhis predecessor Mute Egede as foreign ŌĆīminister, handing him the job of steering relations with the United States amid pressure from U.S. President Donald ŌĆŗTrump for control over the island.
Prime Minister ŌĆŗJens-Frederik Nielsen said Egede's ministerial portfolio will ŌüĀalso include those of mineral resources and ŌĆŗbusiness policies.
Trump has repeatedly said that the United ŌĆŗStates needs Greenland, a part of the Kingdom of Denmark, for security reasons, causing a rift with European NATO ŌĆŗallies who reject his claim.
Seeking to salvage ŌĆŗrelations, Greenland, Denmark and the U.S. in late January and have said negotiations are still ongoing, with further meetings scheduled to take place.
As prime minister until mid-2025, Egede, the current finance minister, repeatedly ŌĆŗemphasised that ŌĆŗGreenland is ŌüĀnot for sale and that its people will determine their own ŌĆŗfuture, rejecting Trump's approach.
Greenland's delegation in the ŌĆŗinitial ŌüĀWashington talks was led by Vivian Motzfeldt, who stepped down from the job of foreign ŌüĀminister ŌĆŗlast month when her party resigned ŌĆŗfrom the coalition government over issues unrelated to the U.S. ŌĆŗconflict.

item.title: Former Chinese defence, auto executive Liu Weidong handed 13-year prison sentence over graft
item.description:
BEIJING, April 14 (Reuters) - Liu Weidong, a former deputy general manager of ŌĆŗChina South Industries Group Corporation ŌĆīand an auto industry veteran, was sentenced to 13 years in ŌĆŗprison on Tuesday for ŌĆŗtaking bribes, state-run Xinhua news agency ŌüĀreported.
($1 = 6.8158 Chinese yuan renminbi)

item.title: Fujimori heads toward Peru runoff as vote count enters third day
item.description:
LIMA, April 14 (Reuters) - Conservative Keiko Fujimori will advance to a presidential ŌĆŗrunoff in Peru as the vote count entered a ŌĆīthird day on Tuesday, with a former Lima mayor and centerŌĆæleft candidate locked in a tight battle for ŌĆŗsecond place, according to the official count.
Fujimori, a former ŌĆŗcongresswoman and the daughter of former President ŌüĀAlberto Fujimori, will advance to a presidential run-off, expected on ŌĆŗJune 7, as she held 16.9% of the votes ŌĆŗwith 74% of votes counted, the official election tally showed.
Right-wing former Lima mayor, Rafael Lopez Aliaga, and center-left Jorge Nieto ŌĆŗare vying for second place with about 13% ŌĆŗand 12% of the votes respectively, as the count by Peru's ŌĆīelectoral ŌüĀbody, ONPE, moved into a third day.
Outsider Ricardo Belmont stood in fourth place securing just below 10% of the votes.
With none of the leading candidates ŌĆŗanywhere near ŌĆŗthe 50% ŌüĀrequired to win outright, a runoff will be required, prolonging political uncertainty in ŌĆŗthe world's thirdŌĆælargest copper producer.
The tally ŌĆŗfor Sunday's ŌüĀelection has moved into a third day after officials extended voting hours into Monday for more than 50,000 ŌüĀpeople ŌĆŗwho were unable to cast ŌĆŗballots as widespread delays and logistical failures hampered voting day.

item.title: Pope Leo's visit revives priest's hopes for peace in anglophone Cameroon
item.description:
DAKAR, April 14 (Reuters) - Armed with pistols and an AK-47 assault rifle, the rebels ambushed Rev. Killian Ndonui Nshamikara in January as he was driving through a village in western ŌĆŗCameroon, kidnapping him and demanding a ransom of more than $25,000.
For the 40-year-old Catholic priest, the scene was stressful but familiar: It marked his third time being ŌĆīabducted during the conflict in CameroonŌĆÖs two English-speaking regions, which has killed thousands since erupting nearly a decade ago.
He has managed to negotiate his release each time.
Pope Leo ŌĆÖs trip to anglophone Cameroon - part of a whirlwind 10-day Africa tour - is expected to shine a rare spotlight on the violence there, which is typically overshadowed by unrest elsewhere in the region, from jihadist attacks in the Sahel to rebel advances in mineral-rich eastern Congo.
The ŌĆŗpontiff arrives in the Central African nation on Wednesday and is scheduled to spend much of Thursday in Bamenda, Cameroon's biggest English-speaking city, holding Mass and a ŌĆ£meeting for ŌĆŗpeaceŌĆØ in a cathedral.
The Catholic Church is the leading Christian denomination in the English-speaking regions. A separatist alliance ŌüĀsaid it would observe a three-day ceasefire to allow civilians and visitors to move freely during the pope's visit.
Ndonui is among those hoping that LeoŌĆÖs arrival will revive flagging peace efforts, ŌĆŗeven if the odds are stacked against a speedy resolution to a conflict rooted in the country's complex colonial history.
ŌĆ£Cameroon is a nation in need of healing,ŌĆØ he said.
Cameroon, a former German ŌĆŗcolony, was partitioned by Britain and France after World War One. The French part won independence in 1960 and was joined a year later by the smaller English-speaking British area to the west.
At the time, many in the British territory wanted to form their own state, but they were not given the option under a U.N.-officiated referendum. Secessionist sentiments have simmered ever since.
The current conflict erupted in 2016 following protests by English-speaking ŌĆŗlawyers and teachers who complained of marginalisation. Calls to form a new state called Ambazonia gained momentum, and an armed group called the Ambazonia Defence Forces began attacking government soldiers. Dozens ŌĆŗof other groups formed to attack troops and civilians.
Nearly a decade later, the fighting has morphed into a brutal standoff. More than 6,500 people have been killed and more than half ŌĆīa million displaced, ŌüĀaccording to the International Crisis Group.
The Catholic Church has not been spared. Ndonui said hundreds of priests had been kidnapped and at least five killed, including a Kenyan missionary.
In August 2018, gunmen killed Akiata Gerald, a 22-year-old seminarian who lived with Ndonui, storming the parish and shooting him at close range.
ŌĆ£His life, full of promise and dedication to God, was cut short in a senseless act of violence," Ndonui said.
He blames the rebels for the killing, though he said he did not know which specific group was responsible.
Efforts to broker a ŌĆŗpeace deal have so far amounted to little.
The ŌĆŗmain separatist leaders were not represented at ŌüĀa national dialogue convened by the government in 2019. Mediation attempts involving Switzerland and Canada have also stalled.
The consequences can be seen on the streets of Bamenda, a once-bustling regional capital that has been reshaped by a decade of clashes, with checkpoints and general strikes paralysing daily ŌĆŗlife.
Leo will have a tough time inspiring a new, meaningful push for peace on his own, said Enowbachem Agbortanyi, a Yaounde-based political ŌĆŗanalyst.
ŌĆ£The pope can propose, but ŌüĀhe cannot enforce,ŌĆØ he said.
ŌĆ£That responsibility lies primarily with the government, which holds legitimate authority.ŌĆØ
The government has described the fighting in anglophone Cameroon as a "socio-political crisis" that is largely under control. A government spokesperson did not respond to a request for comment on the possible impact of the pope's visit.
Lucas Asu, spokesperson for the Ambazonia Governing Council, one of the main separatist groups, told Reuters he ŌüĀsuspected President Paul ŌĆŗBiya's government would "try to spin the pope's visit as an indication of the end of the conflict and ŌĆŗa return to normalcy".
But while he stressed the visit would have no bearing on how the fighting unfolds, he added that it would be a moment for Catholics in anglophone Cameroon to celebrate.
"The Ambazonia Governing Council calls on the ŌĆŗAmbazonian Christians, and especially the Catholics, to turn up in the millions and joyfully welcome their pope," he said.

item.title: US producer prices increase less than expected in March
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 14 (Reuters) - U.S. producer prices increased less than expected in March as the cost of services was unchanged, but surging energy prices ŌĆŗbecause of the war with Iran were fanning inflation pressures.
The ŌĆīProducer Price Index for final demand rose 0.5% last month after a downwardly revised 0.5% gain in February, the Labor Department's Bureau of Labor Statistics said on ŌĆŗTuesday.
A jump in energy prices was partially offset by ŌĆŗsteady prices for services. March's PPI data likely only ŌüĀshowed the initial impact of the Middle East conflict. Economists polled ŌĆŗby Reuters had forecast the PPI accelerating 1.1% after a previously reported ŌĆŗ0.7% gain in February.
In the 12 months through March, the PPI advanced 4.0% after increasing 3.4% in February.
Further increases are likely as oil prices shot ŌĆŗup on Monday to more than $100 a barrel after the ŌĆŗU.S. military said it would blockade ships leaving Iran's ports.
Oil prices have jumped ŌĆīmore ŌüĀthan 35% since the U.S.-Israeli war with Iran started at the end of February.
The BLS reported last week that the Consumer Price Index logged its biggest monthly increase in nearly four years in March ŌĆŗamid a record ŌĆŗjump in the ŌüĀcost of gasoline and diesel.
The Federal Reserve tracks the Personal Consumption Expenditures price indexes for its ŌĆŗ2% inflation target.
Prior to the PPI report, economists estimated ŌĆŗthat ŌüĀPCE inflation, excluding the volatile food and energy components, increased 0.2% in March after rising 0.4% for two consecutive months. That would translate to ŌüĀa ŌĆŗyear-on-year increase of 3.1%, up from 3.0% ŌĆŗin February. Economists expect the oil price shock will have a moderate impact on ŌĆŗthe so-called core inflation.

item.title: Jet fuel shock from Iran war worsens crisis for global airlines
item.description:
BENGALURU/BRUSSELS/LONDON, April 14 (Reuters) - Air travel's worst crisis in years lurched deeper on Tuesday as Qantas Airways warned ŌĆīof spiralling costs, Lufthansa said it may have to ground planes and Virgin Atlantic flagged a looming supply crunch, with the Iran conflict squeezing fuel supplies.
The war has upended routes between Asia and Europe that relied on Gulf hubs, while a doubling of jet fuel prices and tightening of supplies are hitting airlines hard. Since the U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran began on February 28, carriers have hiked air fares, introduced ŌĆŗfuel surcharges and cut routes.
Underscoring efforts to preserve cash, Qantas has delayed a planned share buyback, citing higher and volatile fuel prices, one of the first ŌĆŗmajor carriers to stall shareholder returns.
Meanwhile, Lufthansa (LHAG.DE) , opens new tab CEO Carsten Spohr warned that jet fuel supplies will remain constrained, driving up costs.
"Kerosene will remain ŌüĀin short supply and therefore more expensive for the rest of the year," Spohr told German newspaper Frankfurter Allgemeine Zeitung.
Lufthansa has not yet grounded planes due to shortages ŌĆŗbut this "may be unavoidable" as kerosene availability is already critical at some airports, particularly in Asia, he said.
In South Korea, low-cost carrier T'way Air (091810.KS) , opens new tab plans to furlough some cabin crew without ŌĆŗpay in May and June, a local report said, among the first carriers to reduce staffing.
A two-week ceasefire has provided little relief with the Strait of Hormuz still shut, removing roughly a fifth of global oil and liquefied natural gas supplies from the market and refineries will take time to repair damage inflicted on them.
"Despite the pause in the conflict we remain concerned about jet kerosene supply and price ŌĆŗincrease," UBS analyst Jarrod Castle said in a note on Tuesday, adding that December jet kerosene futures prices are still up more than 50%ŌĆēyear-on-year.
Fuel, typically airlines' second-largest cost after ŌĆŗlabour, accounts for about 27% of operating expenses. Prices have more than doubled since the conflict began, far outpacing a roughly 50% rise in crude prices before the ceasefire.
The turmoil may spur ŌĆīconsolidation, with ŌüĀstronger airlines gaining share from weaker rivals, analysts and executives said.
Reuters reported on Monday that United Airlines (UAL.O) , opens new tab CEO Scott Kirby pitched the potential for merging with American Airlines (AAL.O) , opens new tab days before the U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran.
Flight capacity, in particular from the Middle East but also into Europe, has shrunk and is not projected to recover to pre-conflict levels anytime soon, analysts said.
Virgin Atlantic CEO Corneel Koster said in an interview with the Financial Times that the airline has about six weeks of secure jet fuel supplies ŌĆŗbefore the outlook gets more uncertain.
And European airlines ŌĆŗon Tuesday urged Brussels to step in ŌüĀwith emergency measures to cushion the impact, including EU-level kerosene purchasing, a temporary suspension of the bloc's carbon market for aviation and scrapping certain aviation taxes.
Industry group Airports Council International Europe (ACI) warned last week that Europe could face a systemic jet fuel shortage in three ŌĆŗweeks.
Several carriers, including SAS, are not hedged, leaving them fully exposed to soaring fuel costs. Delta Air Lines (DAL.N) , opens new tab last week said ŌĆŗits jet fuel bill ŌüĀthis quarter would be some $2 billion more than last year.
While Qantas has hedged much of its crude exposure, it remains significantly exposed to the spike in jet fuel spreads.
To offset rising costs, the Australian flag carrier is raising fares and shifting capacity toward stronger routes such as Europe, where demand remains firm, while trimming domestic capacity by about 5 percentage points in the June ŌüĀquarter.
Lufthansa's Spohr said ŌĆŗrecord revenues on Asian routes were also helping offset the impact of rising kerosene costs.
But the airline ŌĆŗhas prepared contingency plans, including cutting its capacity by 2.5% or 5% and grounding 20 to 40 older, less fuel-efficient aircraft earmarked for early retirement.

item.title: Exclusive: -TrumpŌĆÖs son Eric to join father's state visit to China
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 14 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump's son Eric Trump ŌĆŗand his wife, Lara, will ŌĆīaccompany the president on his May trip to China, a ŌĆŗspokeswoman for Eric Trump ŌĆŗtold Reuters on Tuesday.
"Eric and ŌüĀLara Trump are proud ŌĆŗto accompany the president on ŌĆŗhis state visit," said the spokeswoman. "Eric is deeply proud of his ŌĆŗfather and the accomplishments ŌĆŗof this term, and is attending in ŌĆīa ŌüĀpersonal capacity as a supportive son.
"He does not have business ventures in China ŌĆŗnor plans ŌĆŗon ŌüĀdoing business in China. He will not ŌĆŗbe participating in private ŌĆŗmeetings, ŌüĀbut will instead stand alongside the president to mark ŌüĀthis ŌĆŗhistoric occasion."

item.title: Pope Leo issues warning on democracy after Trump criticism
item.description:
ANNABA, Algeria, April 14 (Reuters) - Pope Leo warned of the risk of democracies sliding into "majoritarian tyranny" on Tuesday, in a ŌĆŗletter issued by the Vatican two days after U.S. ŌĆīPresident Donald Trump attacked the pontiff on social media.
The first U.S. pope, writing to participants of a Vatican meeting about the use of power in democratic ŌĆŗsocieties, said democracies remained healthy only when they were ŌĆŗrooted in moral values.
"Lacking this foundation, (democracy) risks becoming either ŌüĀa majoritarian tyranny or a mask for the dominance of ŌĆŗeconomic and technological elites," said Leo in the letter.
The text, released ŌĆŗas the pope was undertaking an ambitious, 10-day tour of four African countries, did not directly address the U.S. or name any specific democracies.
Trump sharply ŌĆŗcriticized Leo as " terrible " on Sunday night, after the pope had emerged ŌĆŗin recent weeks as a growing critic of the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran .
Leo told Reuters ŌĆīon ŌüĀMonday that he planned to keep criticizing the war, despite Trump's comments.
In Tuesday's letter, the pope said the Catholic Church taught that power could not be seen as an end in ŌĆŗitself "but as a ŌĆŗmeans ordered toward ŌüĀthe common good".
"This implies that the legitimacy of authority depends not on the accumulation of economic ŌĆŗor technological strength, but on the wisdom and ŌĆŗvirtue ŌüĀwith which it is exercised," said Leo.
The pope also urged leaders in democratic societies to avoid any temptation to hoard power.
"Temperance ... proves ŌüĀessential ŌĆŗfor the legitimate use of authority, for ŌĆŗtrue temperance restrains inordinate self-exaltation and acts as a guardrail against the abuse ŌĆŗof power," he said.

item.title: Despite Israeli firepower, Netanyahu struggles for political gains in Iran war
item.description:
DUBAI, April 14 (Reuters) - The Iran war was meant to deliver a defining victory over Tehran that would secure Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu's place in history. More than six weeks into the conflict, he has been unable to translate military might into political ŌĆŗgain.
Despite Israel's overwhelming firepower, its enemies across every front have been weakened but not neutralised. Even after heavy Israeli-U.S. airstrikes and the loss of senior leaders, Iran remains intact and defiant.
Tehran's nuclear stockpiles endure, its ŌĆīmissile capability is now proven and it holds sway over the Strait of Hormuz , the artery for a fifth of global oil flows.
Palestinian Islamist militant group Hamas has not been disarmed or dismantled in Gaza , and Iran-backed Hezbollah continues to fire rockets at northern Israel from Lebanon .
"Netanyahu is not winning," said Danny Citrinowicz, a senior researcher on Iran at Israel's Institute for National Security Studies. "This war is a strategic failure. There is a gap between what he promised at the start of the campaign and where we ended up."
Netanyahu, 76, is paying a political price ŌĆŗfor a military campaign launched with U.S. President Donald Trump that has failed to deliver a decisive outcome, political analysts in the region say.
Netanyahu's approval ratings have slipped and, with legislative elections due by late October, the political ŌĆŗrisks he faces are rising.
Netanyahu's office did not respond to a Reuters request for comment for this article. The prime minister has criticised those who he said were diminishing ŌüĀIsrael's achievements in Iran, saying Israel has emerged stronger and Iran weaker.
"There are massive achievements here. This is a historic change. We crushed the nuclear program. We crushed the missiles. We crushed the regime," he said in a statement on Saturday.
At ŌĆŗthe start of the war, Netanyahu told Iranians they would be "called upon to take to the streets" and topple their clerical rulers. Security officials have since grown increasingly skeptical such an outcome will materialise any time soon, a senior Israeli military ŌĆŗofficial said.
Two Israeli officials told Reuters the initial expectation had been for a swift operation to "finish the job" in three weeks. The war has instead expanded into a wider confrontation with regional and global implications.
Aviv Bushinsky, a former Netanyahu adviser, said the war on Iran had initially restored NetanyahuŌĆÖs standing, which had been damaged by the Hamas attack on Israel on October 7, 2023, that led to the Gaza war.
In the early stages of the Iran war , his hardline posture towards Hamas and Hezbollah resonated with parts of the Israeli public, but polls show ŌĆŗhis ratings have slipped since then.
An April 11 survey by Hebrew University's Agam Labs found only 10% of Israelis viewed the war as successful, while support for Netanyahu was at 34%, down from 40% at the start of the war. More ŌĆŗthan half rated his leadership as poor or very poor.
Political analysts in the region say that although the military campaign built almost entirely on airpower has been tactically impressive and produced operational gains, it has not compounded into a coherent and durable strategic endgame.
"There is ŌĆīthis idea that ŌüĀF-15s and F-35s (fighter jets) can shape or remake the Middle East - that if you kill enough Iranian leaders, the regime will fall," said Citrinowicz. "ItŌĆÖs a flawed assumption, and the cost of it keeps getting higher every time."
Bushinsky also questioned the value of IsraelŌĆÖs reliance on targeted assassinations although Iranian Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei was among those killed in such strikes.
"There is always someone who replaces them," Bushinsky said. "It wakes the bear, it doesnŌĆÖt kill it."
Israeli officials and a Western source said Netanyahu had been informed of a ceasefire plan that was agreed last week only when it was in its final stages. The Western source said Netanyahu had been angered by being left out of the process.
The prime minister has sought since then to counter any ŌĆŗperception that he was sidelined in the Pakistan-brokered talks, ŌĆŗand on Tuesday issued a statement saying U.S. Vice ŌüĀPresident JD Vance had called him from his plane to brief him on the talks.
Netanyahu has also launched a campaign to convince voters the war has been worth the cost.
The war effort has run up about $11.5 billion in budgetary costs, with a large share spent on defence, Israel's finance ministry said on Sunday.
NetanyahuŌĆÖs dilemma, diplomats in the region say, ŌĆŗis likely to deepen in the absence of decisive military victories, with security problems remaining for Israel in Gaza and the Israel-occupied West Bank , and the conflict with Lebanon continuing.
Netanyahu could ŌĆŗseek to block any immediate diplomatic ŌüĀbreakthrough in the war with Iran, calculating that a U.S.ŌĆōIran agreement would exacerbate his political troubles, regional diplomats say.
Netanyahu's office did not immediately respond when asked for comment on this assertion.
Israel has said it would accept a deal that curbs IranŌĆÖs missile and nuclear programmes and removes enriched uranium.
The war has crossed a critical threshold for Washington, experts on Iran say, with Iran realising it can survive a conflict with the U.S. and threaten its enemy by attacking Gulf infrastructure and controlling the Strait of Hormuz.
"You can't ŌüĀput the genie ŌĆŗback in the bottle," Citrinowicz said of Iran's control of the strait. "The Iranians feel strengthened now, they feel emboldened and they want much more ŌĆŗthan they were offered in previous talks."
The biggest losers, said Aaron David Miller, a former U.S. Middle East negotiator, are the Gulf Arab states. The region, he said, faces the prospect of dealing with a more bitter, harder-line Iranian leadership.
Abdulaziz Sager, chairman of the SaudiŌĆæbased Gulf Research Center, said Gulf ŌĆŗstates would accept the risks of heightened confrontation with Iran if necessary to keep the strait open, rather than allow Tehran to threaten shipping or Gulf ports.

item.title: Israeli fire kills six in Gaza, including two children, medics say
item.description:
CAIRO, April 14 (Reuters) - Israeli fire killed at least six Palestinians, including two children, in separate incidents across the ŌĆŗGaza Strip on Tuesday, local health officials said, in ŌĆīthe latest violence to undermine a U.S.-brokered ceasefire agreement.
Four people, including a young child, were killed in a strike that targeted a police vehicle in Gaza ŌĆŗCity, the Hamas-run Interior Ministry said. A police officer was ŌĆŗamong the dead, while nine bystanders were wounded, some ŌüĀcritically, it said.
In the north of the enclave, near Jabalia, Israeli ŌĆŗfire killed three-year-old Yahya AlŌĆæMalahi, health authorities and his family said.
Israel's military ŌĆŗdid not immediately provide comment on either incident.
In northern Gaza, Israel's military said it killed a man who had approached the armistice line with Hamas, describing ŌĆŗhim as an armed militant.
Health authorities confirmed a man had been ŌĆŗkilled in the area, without providing details.
The ceasefire that began last October halted ŌĆītwo ŌüĀyears of full-blown war but left Israeli troops in control of a depopulated zone that makes up well over half of Gaza, with Hamas in power in the remaining, narrow coastal strip.
Israel has escalated its ŌĆŗattacks on Hamas-led ŌĆŗpolice and security ŌüĀforces since October, killing dozens, the group's officials in Gaza have told Reuters, accusing Israel of trying ŌĆŗto cause chaos and anarchy.
Israel says it aims to ŌĆŗthwart ŌüĀattacks by Hamas and other militant factions.
More than 750 Palestinians have been killed since the ceasefire deal took effect, while militants have killed four Israeli ŌüĀsoldiers. ŌĆŗIsrael and Hamas have traded blame for ŌĆŗceasefire violations.
Palestinians also say Israeli forces have been expanding the zone they occupy. Israel ŌĆŗdenies this.

item.title: Lula seen ahead of Flavio Bolsonaro in Brazil election second-round, CNT/MDA poll shows
item.description:
SAO PAULO, April 14 (Reuters) - Brazil's President Luiz Inacio ŌĆŗLula da Silva ŌĆīis seen ahead of Senator Flavio Bolsonaro ŌĆŗin a potential ŌĆŗrunoff in the October ŌüĀgeneral elections, a ŌĆŗCNT/MDA poll showed ŌĆŗon Tuesday.
Lula would receive 44.9% of the votes in ŌĆŗa runoff, ŌĆŗcompared with 40.2% for Flavio ŌĆīBolsonaro, ŌüĀson of former President Jair Bolsonaro, according to the poll.
CNT/MDA ŌĆŗinterviewed ŌĆŗ2,002 ŌüĀpeople from April 8-12 in ŌĆŗ140 Brazilian municipalities. ŌĆŗThe ŌüĀsurvey's margin of error is of ŌüĀ2.2 ŌĆŗpercentage points ŌĆŗin either direction.

item.title: 'Swift work to be done'┬Āafter call with Hungary's Magyar, EU's von der Leyen says
item.description:
BUDAPEST, April 14 (Reuters) - European Commission ŌĆŗPresident Ursula von der Leyen said on Tuesday that she spoke with Hungary's election winner Peter ŌĆīMagyar and there is swift work to be done to introduce reforms needed for unfreezing EU funding.
"We discussed immediate priorities," von der Leyen said in a post on social media platform X.
"There is swift work to be done to restore, realign and reform. Restore ŌĆŗthe rule of law. Realign with our shared European values. And reform, to unlock the opportunities ŌĆŗoffered by European investments," she added.
Hungary has a total of about 17 billion euros of ŌüĀEU funds frozen over rule of law concerns under the outgoing government of Prime Minister Viktor Orban.
Hungary is ŌĆŗalso still waiting for the European Commission to sign off on a 16 billion euro defence investment plan under the ŌĆŗbloc's SAFE loans scheme for defence projects.
Magyar, whose Tisza party won a two-thirds supermajority in Hungary's parliament, has pledged to prioritise unlocking the funds and is now under pressure to quickly implement reforms to unlock the money after his government takes office, likely in ŌĆŗmid-May.
"The ball is in Magyar's court to prove he's serious about his campaign promises of wanting to mend ties ŌĆŗwith Brussels," said one EU diplomat. "Now it's time to show, not tell," they added.
Part of Hungary's EU funding is frozen due ŌĆīto lack ŌüĀof compliance with EU standards in areas such as academic freedom and right to asylum. Around 10 billion euros from the bloc's post-pandemic recovery fund is frozen until Hungary fulfills requirements to strengthen judicial independence and accountability, with a deadline of August 31 to implement reforms.
Magyar said in a post on Facebook after the call that von ŌĆŗder Leyen told him the ŌĆŗEuropean Commission would work ŌüĀclosely with the new Hungarian government to make sure results are delivered within the ŌĆ£extremely tight deadlineŌĆØ available to release the funds.
Officials and analysts say introducing the required reforms will ŌĆŗnot be an easy feat.
"The most important constraint is the deadline on 31st August ŌĆŗfor completing all ŌüĀRecovery and Resilience Facility milestones ŌĆō a window that may prove too narrow to unlock the roughly 10 billion euros of frozen EU funds available," said Liam Peach, an economist at Capital Economics.
Credit ratings agency Moody's said on Monday that "Tisza's parliamentary ŌüĀsupermajority will ŌĆŗfacilitate the implementation of these plans". But it added that "officials appointed ŌĆŗby Fidesz remain in position across key institutions" and "political differences across the country's institutions can complicate or delay legislative change, limiting the new governmentŌĆÖs ŌĆŗability to deliver deep reforms."

item.title: US Treasury's Bessent says China has been 'unreliable partner' by hoarding oil during war
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 14 (Reuters) - U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said on Tuesday ŌĆŗthat China had been an ŌĆīunreliable global partner during the Middle East war by hoarding oil ŌĆŗsupplies and limiting exports of ŌĆŗcertain goods, much as they ŌüĀhoarded medical supplies during the ŌĆŗpandemic.
Bessent told reporters he had ŌĆŗspoken with Chinese officials about the issue. He dodged a question about whether ŌĆŗthe dispute would derail ŌĆŗU.S. President Donald Trump's plan to visit ŌĆīBeijing ŌüĀat the end of the month, but said Trump and Chinese President Xi Jinping had ŌĆŗa very ŌĆŗgood ŌüĀworking relationship.
"I think the message for the visit ŌĆŗis stability. We've had great ŌĆŗstability ŌüĀin the relationship since last summer; that emanates from the ŌüĀtop ŌĆŗdown," he said. "I ŌĆŗthink that communication is the key."

item.title: Trump, India's Modi speak by phone, Indian media says
item.description: April 14 (Reuters) - U.S. President ŌĆŗDonald ŌĆīTrump and Indian ŌĆŗPrime ŌĆŗMinister Narendra ŌüĀModi ŌĆŗspoke by ŌĆŗtelephone on Tuesday, ŌĆŗIndian ŌĆŗmedia reported, adding ŌĆīthat ŌüĀthe call between ŌĆŗthe ŌĆŗtwo ŌüĀleaders lasted ŌĆŗnearly ŌĆŗ40 ŌüĀminutes.

item.title: Erdogan tells Hungary's Magyar he believes bilateral ties will progress, Turkish presidency says
item.description:
ANKARA, April 14 (Reuters) - Turkish President Tayyip ŌĆŗErdogan told ŌĆīHungarian election winner Peter Magyar ŌĆŗin a ŌĆŗphone call on Tuesday ŌüĀthat he ŌĆŗbelieved Turkey-Hungary ŌĆŗties would progress further as he ŌĆŗcongratulated him ŌĆŗon his victory, the ŌĆīTurkish ŌüĀpresidency said.
In a statement, the presidency said ŌĆŗErdogan ŌĆŗalso ŌüĀtold Magyar he wanted ŌĆŗto ŌĆŗdeepen ŌüĀthe strategic partnership between the ŌüĀNATO ŌĆŗallies.

item.title: In India, $1 housekeepers spark a consumer, worker frenzy despite safety risks
item.description:
MUMBAI, April 14 (Reuters) - At Indian startup Pronto's training hub, women hone their chopping and mopping skills while learning how to send SOS signals if they feel unsafe inside customers' homes. They are set to join India's newest consumer craze: house help for $1 ŌĆŗan hour.
Indu Jaiswar, 35, hopes doing household chores in her first job can help fund her son's dream of becoming a doctor. "This is what we've been doing in our own homes for years. Might as ŌĆīwell get paid for it," said the mother of two.
In a country with an entrenched culture of outsourcing household work, Indian startups Pronto and Snabbit and listed rival Urban Company (URBN.NS) , opens new tab are training thousands of domestic helpers. Urban Company estimates India's rapidly growing cleaning services market is worth an estimated $9 billion and spread across 53 million households.
Like Uber drivers, the helpers receive bookings on their apps, directing them to apartments in assigned neighbourhoods within minutes and press a countdown timer in their apps before starting work. The potential annual earnings from working eight hours a day can be as high as $5,000 - ŌĆŗa figure that far surpasses India's per capita income of around $3,000.
The companies are betting big, burning millions of dollars to lure busy professionals in cities like New Delhi and Mumbai with under 99 rupee ($1) offerings that have no ŌĆŗglobal parallel. Similar services can cost around $30 an hour in the United States, and around $7 in China.
However, the craze among consumers and workers is tempered by concerns about women's safety in a ŌüĀcountry with high rates of sexual harassment. Unlike e-commerce couriers who spend just brief moments at doorsteps, housekeepers may spend hours inside private homes, exposing them to greater risks.
Soumya Chauhan, a principal at Dutch e-commerce investor Prosus, which has a stake in ŌĆŗUrban Company, said she views worker safety as the fundamental operational challenge to solve.
"The platforms that successfully crack the safety protocols will earn the deepest consumer loyalty and the most sustainable market returns," she said.
Cognisant of the challenges for a business that ŌĆŗmainly employs women, Snabbit and Pronto said they have an in-app SOS button that alerts area supervisors in case of distress, while Pronto also offers self-defence training.
"In the offline world, the rate of abuse for a lot of these domestic workers is super high," said Pronto's 23-year-old CEO Anjali Sardana, adding that her company is trying to comfort its workers by assuring legal and medical support when needed.
Urban Company, which also offers services like plumbing, declined to comment for this story. It has previously said it offers a women-only safety helpline and an SOS app feature.
Shabnam Hashmi, a ŌĆŗwomen's rights activist, said the companies run extensive background checks on workers before onboarding them but should also check customer credentials. Currently users can simply log in on apps to book home help.
"How is it ever possible for these jobs to be safe ŌĆŗfor women - even with an SOS button? Unless they carry cameras, which is of course impossible, there is no way to know what happens behind that door," she said.
Pronto worker Jaiswar has found her own workaround: she always calls a customer before visiting a home and ŌĆīgoes "only if there's ŌüĀa woman present".
The companies meanwhile are getting record orders.
Urban Company recorded its highest daily home services bookings of 50,000 in February. Snabbit's have grown to 35,000 orders a day.
Bain Capital-backed Pronto logged a record 22,000 daily bookings in March, up from 2,500 daily orders in October, and raised $25 million in new funding.
Pronto CEO Sardana said she started the business last year after spotting an opportunity to serve three sides: strong demand from customers for reliable maids, workers' need for more stable and safer jobs, and a gap in the market for a scalable service.
"It's possible to build a win-win-win business," she told Reuters.
Fuelling the trend is also India's lack of a do-it-yourself culture, and Indians' love for getting things done cheap.
In Bengaluru, 30-year-old Dhruv, who uses only a first name, ŌĆŗsaid he spent 100 rupees ($1) per hour for Urban Company's service ŌĆŗto help unpack his utensils and hang curtains after ŌüĀmoving house.
That helped him "save quite a bit of time and effort," but the price does matter: "I wouldn't pay 400 or 500 rupees for it."
Snabbit founder Aayush Agarwal said his service was becoming popular among young couples and singles who want to schedule housekeepers and not hire monthly domestic helpers who are infamous for skipping work.
Pronto is offering some visits for 25 rupees in Facebook ŌĆŗads with taglines like "Maid on Leave? Don't grieve", while an Urban Company three-visit pack costs 66 rupees an hour.
Snabbit ads said a customer booked a helper "just to peel 20 potatoes", while ŌĆŗanother had lined up a worker ŌüĀto "separate LEGO blocks by colour."
Like many startups in their growth phase, the companies are paying their workers out-of-pocket to make the jobs attractive, but also doling out hefty discounts to reel in customers.
In October to December, Urban Company disclosures show it received 1.61 million home-help orders with each incurring a loss of 381 rupees ($4). The company says its "discounts are moderating" but its order values need to almost double to break even.
"Over a period of time, it is safe to say that it will become an earn-as-you-go model," said Rahul ŌüĀTaneja, partner at Lightspeed, ŌĆŗwhich has backed Snabbit.
At the Pronto centre, where workers get a uniform and are trained to wear polished shoes, posters revealed potential payouts: home helpers ŌĆŗcan earn $1.60 per hour for 12 hours of work daily in a month, 48% more than what a new customer pays.
At more than $500 a month, that's a big allure for Nisha Chandaliya, 22, who needs to support her ailing mother and has quit a call-centre job that stretched long hours and paid ŌĆŗonly $180 a month.
"It's exhausting to clean six-seven homes, but I need the stability. I can't afford to go back," she said.
($1 = 93.3010 Indian rupees)

item.title: Two arms brokers on trial in UK for missiles and fighter jet deals with Libya and Sudan
item.description:
LONDON, April 14 (Reuters) - Two arms brokers arranged illegal deals to supply ex-Soviet surface-to-air missile systems to South Sudan and fighter jets to Libya ŌĆŗduring its civil war, British prosecutors told a London court ŌĆīon Tuesday.
British national David Greenhalgh, 68, and Greek national Christos Farmakis, 48, are charged with 11 and 12 offences respectively of involvement in the unlicensed supply of arms ŌĆŗbetween 2009 and 2016.
They have pleaded not guilty and their trial ŌĆŗbegan this week at Southwark Crown Court. Farmakis has decided ŌüĀnot to attend his trial, which is proceeding in his absence, the ŌĆŗjury was told.
Prosecutor Edmund Burge said Greenhalgh and Farmakis had "very close connections ŌĆŗto senior figures" in South Sudan, with Farmakis appointed as South Sudan's "honorary consul" to Greece and Cyprus.
The two defendants brokered a deal for a "complete air defence missile system", which ŌĆŗwas acquired from Ukraine for nearly $55 million, between 2009 and 2011, the ŌĆŗprosecutor said.
South Sudan was still formally part of Sudan until it achieved independence in ŌĆī2011, ŌüĀBurge added, meaning the semi-autonomous region was subject to a British arms embargo.
Greenhalgh and Farmakis discussed obtaining a Ugandan end-user certificate as "cover" to obscure the intended destination of the missile system, Burge said.
Burge said Farmakis was arrested ŌĆŗin 2016 after using ŌĆŗhis email address ŌüĀfor his job at the government-funded Greater London Enterprise to privately arrange arms deals.
Farmakis' email account forwarded an ŌĆŗemail to his boss, which led to the authorities ŌĆŗdiscovering documents ŌüĀrelating to plans to sell fighter jets and other arms to Libya in the wake of the 2011 Arab Spring, Burge added.
The prosecutor said emails and ŌüĀother ŌĆŗdocuments suggested Greenhalgh and Farmakis had arranged to ŌĆŗsupply weapons to Iran, Iraq and Syria in breach of arms embargoes.
The trial, which is ŌĆŗdue to conclude in June, continues.

item.title: Drone attacks in Sudan kill two, injure 56, MSF says
item.description:
April 14 (Reuters) - Medecins Sans Frontieres said on Tuesday it has recorded two deaths and treated 56 wounded ŌĆŗpeople following five drone attacks carried out ŌĆīby the Sudanese Armed Forces (SAF) in the Darfur region.
MSF works in nine states across Sudan, responding to emergencies ŌĆŗand providing medical care amid a war ŌĆŗbetween the Sudanese army and the paramilitary Rapid ŌüĀSupport Forces.
"As Sudan enters its fourth year ŌĆŗof war, these attacks by the Sudanese Armed ŌĆŗForces demonstrate complete disregard for civilian life," the Geneva-based medical charity said in a statement. "We call on the warring parties ŌĆŗin Sudan to protect civilians."
The U.N. human ŌĆŗrights office has said there has been a sharp rise ŌĆīin ŌüĀthe use of drones in Sudan this year with more than 500 civilians killed from such strikes between January and mid-March.
"Children in Sudan continue to ŌĆŗbear the ŌĆŗheaviest toll, ŌüĀwith drones responsible for nearly 80% of all reported child killings and ŌĆŗinjuries," Eva Hinds, UNICEF spokesperson in Sudan, ŌĆŗtold ŌüĀreporters in Geneva. At least 245 children were reportedly killed or injured in the first three months ŌüĀof ŌĆŗ2026, a sharp increase compared ŌĆŗto the same period last year, Hinds said.

item.title: Europe could see jet fuel shortage by June, IEA says
item.description:
LONDON, April 14 (Reuters) - Europe could start seeing physical shortages of jet fuel by June due to the Iran war if the region is ŌĆŗonly able to replace half of the fuel supplies ŌĆīit normally gets from the Middle East, the International Energy Agency said in its monthly report.
Global jet fuel and kerosene demand averaged 7.8 million ŌĆŗbarrels per day in 2025, with Gulf exports the largest ŌĆŗsource to the global market, averaging nearly 400,000 bpd, ŌüĀthe report added.
Here are some key details:

item.title: Economic pressure on Russia key for peace, Italy's Meloni says
item.description:
ROME, April 14 (Reuters) - Economic pressure on Russia remains "the most effective weapon" to pressure it to end its conflict with Ukraine, Italian Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni said on Tuesday, when ŌĆŗasked whether Europe should ease its ban on Moscow's gas imports.
Meloni spoke ŌĆŗon the sidelines of a wine fair after the head of energy ŌüĀgroup Eni (ENI.MI) , opens new tab said that the EU restriction should be reconsidered as the bloc grapples ŌĆŗwith the fallout of the Iran conflict on energy supplies.
Energy worries have mounted in ŌĆŗItaly and across Europe after the U.S.-Israeli war against Iran led to the closure of the Strait of Hormuz , a waterway that normally carries about a fifth of global LNG supplies, to most ŌĆŗshipping.
"The economic pressure that we have exerted on Russia over these years is ŌĆŗthe most effective weapon to help build peace, and therefore we must be very careful about ŌĆīhow we ŌüĀmove forward," Meloni told reporters.
Moscow faces a ban on imports of liquefied natural gas on short-term contracts from April 25 and on long-term contracts from January 1, 2027. Eni CEO Claudio Descalzi said it was unclear how to replace 20 billion ŌĆŗcubic metres of Russian ŌĆŗLNG.
Speaking in the ŌüĀnorthern Italian city of Verona, Meloni, who is due to meet President Volodymyr Zelenskiy in Rome on Wednesday, said she hoped ŌĆŗprogress towards peace could be made before the ban takes ŌĆŗeffect.
Her ally, ŌüĀthe far-right League party, which has often expressed pro-Russia views, is also urging her to reopen supplies from Moscow.
Party leader Matteo Salvini said on Tuesday he had discussed with ŌüĀEconomy Minister ŌĆŗGiancarlo Giorgetti an unspecified plan to freeze energy ŌĆŗprices at levels seen before the Iran war broke out on February 28, but did not provide details on ŌĆŗthe potentially costly move.

item.title: Spain's kerosene, fuels supply guaranteed at the moment, energy minister says
item.description:
MADRID, April 14 (Reuters) - Spain's fuels supply is not at risk at the moment ŌĆŗand the country is in ŌĆīa "privileged situation" compared with European peers, Energy Minister Sara Aagesen said on Tuesday.
The U.S.-Israeli ŌĆŗwar on Iran and subsequent ŌĆŗblockade of the Strait of Hormuz ŌüĀhave sparked a supply crisis, ŌĆŗwith European airlines calling on the European Union ŌĆŗto deploy emergency measures to address growing concerns over jet fuel shortages.
Asked whether the ŌĆŗSpanish government can guarantee the supply ŌĆŗof kerosene, diesel and gasoline, Aagesen said that "at ŌĆīthis ŌüĀmoment, I can say yes".
"Our situation is not only robust, it's privileged," she said, thanks to a fleet ŌĆŗof eight ŌĆŗrefineries ŌüĀproducing 80% of the kerosene consumed in Spain.
"This is ŌĆŗa very advantageous situation compared ŌĆŗto ŌüĀmany European partners, where the opposite is true: they are dependent on ŌüĀimports," ŌĆŗshe said, adding that ŌĆŗSpanish refiners have not suffered from supply disruptions.

item.title: IMF slashes growth forecast for Middle East as Gulf exporters reel from impact of war
item.description:
DUBAI, April 14 (Reuters) - The Middle East and ŌĆŗNorth Africa region is expected to have a sharply slower growth this year as oil-exporting ŌĆīcountries grapple with the fallout from the Iran war , the International Monetary Fund said on Tuesday.
The region's real GDP growth forecast was slashed to 1.1% in the IMF's latest World Economic Outlook, 2.8 percentage points lower than its January projection.
Growth is expected to ŌĆŗrebound to 4.8% in 2027. The IMF said, however, that its estimates for 2027 assumed energy ŌĆŗproduction and transportation in the region are normalised over the next few months. It ŌüĀnoted that this assumption may need to be revised if the conflict drags on.
Tehran's attacks on Gulf ŌĆŗneighbours, in response to U.S.-Israeli strikes that began in late February, have damaged major energy facilities and disrupted ŌĆŗshipping through the Strait of Hormuz , which would normally handle about 20% of global oil and liquefied natural gas flows.
The war has also created inflationary pressures and clouded the global economic outlook.
U.S.-Iran talks to resolve the conflict broke down on the weekend and ŌĆŗthe U.S. military has begun a blockade of Iran ports, though efforts to maintain dialogue continue.
The IMF said it revised GDP projections for countries in the region much lower due to diminished production and ŌĆīexports. ŌüĀThe degree of revision depended on "damage suffered in energy and transportation infrastructure as well as the dependence on the Strait of Hormuz and availability of alternative export routes," it added.
Saudi Arabia, the world's top oil exporter and the Arab world's biggest economy, is now expected to see growth of 3.1% in 2026, 1.4 percentage ŌĆŗpoints lower than a January ŌĆŗestimate. The kingdom, however, ŌüĀis expected to be less severely affected by the war than its Gulf neighbours.
Iran's economy is forecast to shrink 6.1% in its fiscal year that began on ŌĆŗMarch 21, before rebounding to 3.2% growth the year after. Before the war, ŌĆŗit was expected ŌüĀto expand 1.1% this fiscal year.
Bahrain, Iraq, Kuwait and Qatar are also now expected to see their economies contract this year, the IMF report said, without giving specific estimates. A separate and more comprehensive Middle East regional economic ŌüĀoutlook ŌĆŗis due to be published on April 16.
GDP growth revisions were ŌĆŗmilder for regional oil and gas importers. Growth in Egypt, for example, is projected to slow to 4.2% in 2026 from an earlier estimate ŌĆŗof 4.7%, and is forecast to recover to 4.8% in 2027.

item.title: Oil tankers transiting Strait of Hormuz since start of Iran war
item.description:
April 14 (Reuters) - The U.S. blockade on vessels entering or leaving Iranian ports adds to uncertainty over shipping through the Strait of Hormuz, which has been all-but-shut since the start of the Iran war.
Ordinarily, roughly one fifth of global oil and gas exports transit the Strait of Hormuz.
Here's a look at ŌĆŗsome of the non-Iranian oil tankers that have passed the strait since the start of the U.S-Israeli war with Iran on February 28, ŌĆīlisted by cargo destination.
Liberia-flagged Very Large Crude Carrier (VLCC) Serifos entered and exited the Hormuz Passage trial anchorage that bypasses Iran's Larak Island on April 10. The tanker, carrying crude loaded from Saudi Arabia and the United Arab Emirates in early March, is expected to arrive at Malaysia's Malacca port on April 21.
Ocean Thunder , loaded with Iraqi crude and chartered by a unit of Malaysian state energy ŌĆŗfirm Petronas, transited the waterway on April 5 and is expected to discharge its cargo of 1 million barrels of Basrah Heavy crude in Malaysia's ŌĆŗPengerang on April 18.
Both tankers are among seven Malaysia-linked vessels cleared by Iran to transit the strait, two people familiar with ŌüĀthe matter told Reuters.
China-flagged VLCCs Cospearl Lake and He Rong Hai exited the Strait of Hormuz on April 11. Cospearl Lake, laden with Iraqi oil, is expected to ŌĆŗarrive at China's Zhoushan port on May 1. He Rong Hai is heading to Myanmar to discharge its Saudi crude cargo.
Both VLCCs are chartered by Unipec, the trading arm ŌĆŗof Chinese energy giant Sinopec (600028.SS) , opens new tab .
VLCC Dhalkut, which passed the strait on April 2, is heading to Myanmar to discharge Saudi crude on April 22, Kpler data showed. Crude discharged at Myanmar typically goes to PetroChina's (601857.SS) , opens new tab Yunnan refinery.
On March 31, a Chinese foreign ministry spokesperson said that three Chinese ships recently sailed through the Strait of Hormuz after coordination with relevant parties.
At least two VLCCs and ŌĆŗtwo suezmax tankers exited the Gulf in March and April to offload crude at India.
VLCC Habrut, which crossed the strait on April 2, is heading to Paradip to ŌĆŗdischarge Abu Dhabi crude for Indian Oil Corp (IOC.NS) , opens new tab on April 15.
Meanwhile, VLCC Marathi discharged Saudi crude at the Sikka port for Reliance Industries (RELI.NS) , opens new tab on March 28, Kpler data showed.
The Liberia-flagged Smyrni ŌĆīsuezmax tanker ŌüĀexited the strait on March 12 and discharged 1 million barrels of Saudi crude at Mumbai for state refiner Hindustan Petroleum Corp (HPCL.NS) , opens new tab on March 16, the data showed. Another suezmax Shenlong left the strait on March 6 and discharged the same amount of Saudi crude at Mumbai on March 11.
Gabon-flagged tanker Msg, laden with residue fuel, is heading to India's Pipavav port after passing the strait on April 9, Kpler data showed. Liberia-flagged Navara, which sailed through the strait on March 31, discharged fuel oil at ŌĆŗSikka port on April 8, the data ŌĆŗshowed.
Two India-bound liquefied petroleum gas (LPG) tankers ŌüĀcarrying about 94,000 metric tons of the cooking gas have safely transited the Strait of Hormuz and are heading towards India, the government said in late March.
BW Tyr discharged at Mumbai and Pipavav between April 5 and April 7, while BW Elm discharged ŌĆŗat three Indian ports between April 6 and April 15, Kpler data showed.
Prior to this, four other Indian-flagged LPG tankers ŌĆŗhave moved out of ŌüĀthe strait ŌĆö Shivalik, Nanda Devi , Pine Gas, and Jag Vasant .
Two Pakistan-flagged tankers entered the Gulf on April 12.
The Aframax tanker Shalamar is heading to the United Arab Emirates on Monday to load Das crude, the data showed, while the Panamax-sized Khairpur is heading to Kuwait to load refined products, according to the data.
Aframax tanker P. Aliki passed through the strait on March 28 ŌüĀand discharged ŌĆŗSaudi crude at Karachi on March 31.
A Thai oil tanker owned by Bangchak Corporation (BCP.BK) , opens new tab has safely passed through ŌĆŗthe Strait of Hormuz following diplomatic coordination between Thailand and Iran, and was not required to pay to escape the blockade, a Thai official and the oil major that owns the vessel said on ŌĆŗMarch 25.
The Suezmax tanker Pola discharged 1 million barrels of Khafji crude at Thailand and Singapore, Kpler data showed.

item.title: Canadian insurers fortify homes, urge Carney to put climate first as wildfire season kicks off
item.description:
TORONTO, April 14 (Reuters) - Worried about wildfires sending claims soaring this year, Canada's property and casualty insurers are pushing owners to flood- and fire-proof ŌĆŗhomes and urging the government to take climate issues more seriously despite economic turmoil.
Insurers Intact Financial (IFC.TO) , opens new tab , TD Insurance (TD.TO) , opens new tab , Wawanesa and Definity Financial (DFY.TO) , opens new tab face financial pressure as claims surge and ŌĆīin turn push up home-insurance premiums, which rose about 6% last year in Canada.
They worry Prime Minister Mark CarneyŌĆÖs prioritization of energy and the economy over risks from climate change could contribute to more wildfire- and flood-related damage over time.
Canada, which has nearly 10% of the world's forest, has seen record wildfire and flood damage in recent years. But calamity-prone regions are still insurable in Canada, unlike in some other countries where companies will not insure houses for wildfires.
This year is expected to be ŌĆŗamong the hottest years on record, according to Environment and Climate Change Canada, increasing the likelihood of wildfires. Research has linked humanŌĆædriven climate change, fueled by rising carbon emissions, to hotter and ŌĆŗmore frequent heatwaves, which dry out ecosystems and create conditions that fuel wildfires and intensify floods.
Carney in 2015 as the Bank of England governor delivered ŌüĀa historic speech about climate change and its impact on financial stability. But he is now counting on the oil sector to help the Canadian economy weather a hit from U.S. President Donald Trump's tariffs and has ŌĆŗpledged to support global efforts to stabilize energy prices due to the Iran war, putting some initiatives to combat climate change on the back burner.
"It feels to me like this (climate) has been somewhat deprioritized. ŌĆŗAnd that's why we're going to, as an industry, keep it at the top of the table," Rowan Saunders, the CEO of the country's fourth-largest property and casualty insurer Definity, said in an interview.
To be sure, Canada's insurers continue to enjoy strong profits despite the pressure from climate-related disasters.
The industry's major players reported higher profits last year and spent less on paying out claims in 2025 than in 2024, when those hit a record. Net income rose ŌĆŗ56.7% for the Canadian property and casualty insurance industry last year, according to data from the industry regulator, which requires they hold strong capital levels.
Still, insurers have invested millions to encourage policyholders to ŌĆŗrebuild floodŌĆæ and wildfireŌĆæresilient homes, folding those upgrades into claim settlements. They have partnered with municipal governments and invested in wildfire risk modeling.
They are also rewarding consumers for weatherproofing their homes, with discounts for completing risk assessments ŌĆīor installing ŌüĀfire-resistant roofing and siding.
The insurance industry's proposals to Ottawa, presented to parliament last year, include updating building codes to make new homes resilient to climate change, investing in natural infrastructure and preventing new construction in areas already prone to floods and wildfires.
Canada in 2024 recorded one of its worst fires as flames engulfed nearly a third of the structures in the tourist town of Jasper and forced thousands to evacuate. At the same time, a hailstorm in Calgary and floods in Toronto elevated insurance claims to a record of C$9.4 billion that year.
In the past three years, a total of about 32 million hectares ŌĆŗof land has been scorched by wildfires.
Insurers ŌĆŗworry in particular that CarneyŌĆÖs plan to ŌüĀaddress a chronic housing shortage does not adequately consider severe weather risks.
Ottawa has proposed , opens new tab building 3.9 million homes, of which about 500,000 could be in high-risk flood areas, and over 200,000 in high-risk wildfire areas, said Liam McGuinty, vice-president of federal affairs at the Insurance Bureau of Canada.
"We've got to stop this ŌĆŗproblem from getting worse," McGuinty said.
Reuters spoke to insurance companies, reinsurers, climate groups and insurance lobby groups that said climate has taken a backseat ŌĆŗas the Carney government ŌüĀprioritizes trade and the global energy crisis.
Canada's environment ministry said climate remains a critical issue for the government and fighting climate change continues. Since 2015, the government has invested more than C$6.6 billion to help communities be better prepared and manage wildland fire, among other projects, it said.
In his first budget, Carney eliminated a cap on oil and gas emissions in favor of other climate measures like strengthened industrial carbon pricing and ŌüĀthe deployment of ŌĆŗcarbon capture and storage technology. But now, the carbon price piece of his plan is in jeopardy, Reuters has reported.
"We're at ŌĆŗa point now in Canada where we can have what used to be a year's worth of severe weather losses happening in a single day. And we don't have the level of public investment commensurate to that reality right now," said David ŌĆŗLeibl, vice president of sustainability and corporate affairs at Winnipeg-based insurer Wawanesa. "We need to close that gap."

item.title: Trump turns on Meloni, says he is 'shocked' by Italian leader
item.description:
ROME, April 14 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump told an Italian newspaper on Tuesday he was "shocked" by Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni and had expected her to be more courageous, delivering a ŌĆŗblunt public rebuke to one of his closest European allies.
Meloni had been ŌĆīa vociferous supporter of Trump, but she has criticised his decision to go to war with Iran , and on Monday, denounced his weekend criticism of Pope Leo as "unacceptable".
Trump responded in an interview with Corriere della Sera, saying ŌĆŗMeloni was "very different from what I thought" and denouncing her for refusing to ŌĆŗhelp re-open the Strait of Hormuz, which has been blocked by Iran.
"I'm ŌüĀshocked by her. I thought she had courage. I was wrong," he is quoted as ŌĆŗsaying in the Italian-language article posted online.
The White House declined to comment on the reported ŌĆŗquotes. Meloni's office also declined to comment.
The criticism marks a dramatic change in tone toward Meloni, who was the only European leader to attend the president's inauguration in 2025.
Only last month he told Corriere della ŌĆŗSera that Meloni was "a great leader", but on Tuesday he accused her of failing ŌĆŗto back U.S. efforts over energy security and Iran, and said Italy wanted America "to do the job for ŌĆīher."
Asked ŌüĀabout her condemnation of his comments on Pope Leo, he said: "She is the one who is unacceptable, because she does not care whether Iran has a nuclear weapon and would blow Italy up in two minutes if it had the chance."
Meloni had hoped that her ŌĆŗclose relationship with the ŌĆŗU.S. president would strengthen ŌüĀher standing at home and abroad, but instead it risks becoming a political liability.
Some 66% of Italians now hold a negative view ŌĆŗof the U.S. leader and pollsters say Meloni's ties to the ŌĆŗWhite House may ŌüĀhave contributed to her defeat last month in a referendum on judicial reform.
The war in Iran has pushed up energy prices in Italy, which is heavily dependent on oil and gas imports.
"They (Italy) ŌüĀpay the ŌĆŗhighest energy costs in the world and are not ŌĆŗeven ready to fight for the Strait of Hormuz... They depend on Donald Trump to keep it open," ŌĆŗTrump said.

item.title: Trump says Iran talks could resume over next two days, NY Post reports
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 14 (Reuters) - U.S. President ŌĆŗDonald Trump said on ŌĆīTuesday that Iran talks could resume in Pakistan ŌĆŗover the next ŌĆŗtwo days, according to an ŌüĀinterview with the ŌĆŗNew York Post.
"You should ŌĆŗstay there, really, because something could be happening over the ŌĆŗnext two days, ŌĆŗand we're more inclined to ŌĆīgo ŌüĀthere," Trump was quoted as saying.
Trump said Pakistan's Army chief, Field ŌĆŗMarshal Asim ŌĆŗMunir, ŌüĀwas doing a "great job" on ŌĆŗthe talks.
"He's fantastic, and ŌĆŗtherefore ŌüĀit's more likely that we go back ŌüĀthere," ŌĆŗTrump said.

item.title: Brazil's Lula calls for former spy head return after US detention
item.description:
BRASILIA, April 14 (Reuters) - Brazilian President Luiz Inacio Lula da ŌĆŗSilva on Tuesday called ŌĆīon former intelligence chief Alexandre Ramagem, who was detained ŌĆŗby ICE in the ŌĆŗUnited States the previous ŌüĀday, to return to ŌĆŗBrazil to serve a ŌĆŗcoup-plotting prison sentence.
"I believe Ramagem will come back to Brazil, he ŌĆŗhas to come ŌĆŗback to serve his sentence," Lula ŌĆīsaid ŌüĀin an interview with a local outlet.
The U.S. agency reported on its ŌĆŗwebsite on ŌĆŗMonday ŌüĀthat Ramagem was in its custody, ŌĆŗwithout providing details, after ŌĆŗhe ŌüĀfled Brazil in September following his conviction for ŌüĀplotting ŌĆŗa coup ŌĆŗwith ex-President Jair Bolsonaro.

item.title: Brazil to pursue rare earths policy this year, not state-owned firm
item.description:
BRASILIA, April 14 (Reuters) - Brazil's mining regulator chief said on Tuesday he expected a national policy for critical minerals to be unveiled within the next two to ŌĆŗthree months, adding that a proposal to create a state-owned company for rare ŌĆīearths was not a government initiative.
Mauro Sousa, director-general of Brazil's National Mining Agency (ANM), told a press conference that 13 bills currently before Congress addressed rare earths and critical minerals.
In recent days, new bills proposing the creation ŌĆŗof a state-backed entity dubbed "Terrabras" have been introduced by lawmakers, prompting mining industry association ŌĆŗIbram to push back against the idea.
The group said on Monday ŌüĀthat Brazil's weakness in rare earths mining did not stem from a lack of state ŌĆŗinvolvement, but rather from limited industrial-scale separation and refining capacity, and other factors such as insufficient ŌĆŗfinancing and legal uncertainty that deters investment.
"We have not yet examined these (Terrabras) proposals in detail to assess their content," said Sousa.
He said the government was in talks with congressman Arnaldo Jardim on a bill to create ŌĆŗa national policy for critical and strategic minerals - which did not include a provision for ŌĆŗa state-run company - and had requested that uranium be included.
The bill sponsored by Jardim was expected to be ŌĆīapproved later ŌüĀthis year, as discussions were already at an advanced stage, added Carlos Leonardo Durans, director of the Department of Development of Intermediate Inputs at the Ministry of Development, Industry, Trade and Services.
"We want to facilitate capital inflows with value-added requirements, but so far there has been no ŌĆŗdiscussion about creating a ŌĆŗstate-owned critical minerals company," ŌüĀhe said at the same press conference.
President Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva has repeatedly said Brazil should not remain merely an exporter of ŌĆŗcommodities in the rare earths sector, stressing the need to develop domestic ŌĆŗprocessing capacity ŌüĀin Latin America's largest economy.
Despite little production, Brazil has vast reserves of these minerals, which are essential for high-tech equipment manufacturing.
At the same event, Julio Nery, mining affairs director at Ibram, said more ŌüĀthan 10 "highly ŌĆŗpromising" exploration projects were under way in Brazil, particularly ŌĆŗtargeting ionic clays, but noted they still depended on geological studies.
"To bring a mine online from scratch, from the first ŌĆŗdrilling, it usually takes around 10 years," Nery said.

item.title: Libya starts trial run of gas pipeline as NOC pushes export growth
item.description:
CAIRO, April 14 (Reuters) - LibyaŌĆÖs National Oil Corporation (NOC) ŌĆīhas begun trial operation of a long-delayed gas pipeline aimed at easing production bottlenecks and supporting future exports, the company said late on Monday.
The ŌĆŗNorth African country, the continent's second-largest oil producer and a ŌĆŗmember of OPEC, relies heavily on hydrocarbons, but its ŌüĀability to attract foreign investment and expand output has been ŌĆŗhampered by years of political and economic turmoil since 2011.
In recent ŌĆŗmonths, NOC has sought to reverse that trend, announcing new oil and gas discoveries and awarding exploration blocks in its first licensing round since 2007, signalling a ŌĆŗrenewed push to bring in international partners as the nation looks ŌĆŗto boost production to 1 billion standard cubic feet per day and raise exports ŌĆīto ŌüĀEurope by the early 2030s.
The new 42-inch pipeline links the Intisar A/103 field to the Brega gas distribution network over about 130 km (80 miles). NOC said it would enable the recovery of ŌĆŗabout 150 million ŌĆŗcubic feet of ŌüĀgas per day that had previously been flared, boosting supplies to domestic consumers and freeing up ŌĆŗvolumes for export.
Libya holds an estimated 80 trillion ŌĆŗcubic feet ŌüĀof gas reserves, but exports via the Greenstream pipeline to Italy remain limited.
NOC said the project, which had been stalled for about ŌüĀ16 ŌĆŗyears, would also eliminate ŌĆ£back pressureŌĆØ that had ŌĆŗdisrupted operations and forced some fields to shut down, improving overall efficiency across the ŌĆŗgas network.

item.title: Zambia forgoes $200 million in revenue with fuel tax suspension

item.title: Canada to temporarily suspend federal fuel tax on diesel and gasoline
item.description:
OTTAWA, April 14 (Reuters) - Canada will suspend the ŌĆŗfederal fuel excise tax ŌĆīon gasoline and diesel from April 20 to ŌĆŗSeptember 7 to help ŌĆŗconsumers and businesses deal ŌüĀwith rising prices, ŌĆŗPrime Minister Mark Carney told ŌĆŗreporters on Tuesday.
"(This) is a responsible measure that will ŌĆŗreduce operating costs ŌĆŗfor truckers and businesses in the ŌĆīfood, ŌüĀagriculture, housing, construction, and delivery sectors," he said. The measure is ŌĆŗexpected ŌĆŗto ŌüĀreduce the cost of regular gasoline ŌĆŗhy 10 cents ŌĆŗa ŌüĀliter and diesel by four cents a liter, ŌüĀhe ŌĆŗadded.
(Reporting by ŌĆŗDavid Ljunggren, editing by Maria Cheng)
((Reuters ŌĆŗOttawa bureau; david.ljunggren@tr.com , opens new tab ))
Keywords: CANADA POLITICS/TAX

item.title: Shell in talks with UAE's ADNOC for South Africa fuel outlet sale, Bloomberg News reports
item.description:
