April 14 (Reuters) - Shell (SHEL.L) , opens new tab is in advanced talks with Abu ŌĆŗDhabi's state oil company ADNOC to sell ŌĆīits retail fuel stations in South Africa in a deal likely to be valued at about $1 billion, Bloomberg News ŌĆŗreported on Tuesday, citing people familiar with ŌĆŗthe matter.
ADNOC emerged as the preferred bidder after ŌüĀShell's negotiations with commodity trader Gunvor Group fell ŌĆŗthrough, and an agreement could potentially be reached ŌĆŗas early as this quarter, the report said.
The talks come amid volatility in the energy market following the Middle East ŌĆŗconflict, prompting the British oil major to trim ŌĆŗits first-quarter gas production outlook .
A deal would see the sale of ŌĆīShell's ŌüĀ600 retail fuel outlets, giving ADNOC about 10% of the market in South Africa, Bloomberg said.
Late in 2024, Shell, which has been in South Africa ŌĆŗfor more ŌĆŗthan a ŌüĀcentury, disclosed plans to exit its downstream businesses in the region.
ADNOC, which before ŌĆŗthe Iran war produced some 4% of ŌĆŗglobal ŌüĀoil output, had previously disclosed plans to invest $150 billion between 2026 and 2030 to drive growth and ŌüĀmeet ŌĆŗglobal energy demand.
Shell and ADNOC ŌĆŗDistribution did not immediately respond to Reuters requests for comment.

item.title: US solar and wind contract prices rose sharply in first quarter
item.description: April 14 (Reuters) - U.S. solar and wind contract prices rose sharply in the first quarter ŌĆŗas project developers faced tariffs , labor shortages and ŌĆīpermitting challenges amid soaring demand for power from data centers, according to a report published ŌĆŗon Tuesday.

item.title: French group Veolia aims $1.2 bln in revenue from data centres, chips by 2030
item.description:
LONDON, April 14 (Reuters) - French utility Veolia (VIE.PA) , opens new tab aims to almost double its revenue ŌĆŗfrom providing energy and water ŌĆīmanagement solutions to data centres and chipmakers by 2030, it said on Tuesday, ŌĆŗbetting on strong demand from ŌĆŗboth sectors.
The company expects 1 billion ŌüĀeuros ($1.18 billion) in annual revenues ŌĆŗfrom data centres and the semiconductor ŌĆŗindustry, compared to the 560 million euros it made in 2025, as it seeks ŌĆŗto expand its presence in ŌĆŗthese fast-growing markets.
The tech sector's expansion of ŌĆīdata ŌüĀcentres, driven by surging demand for AI following the widespread adoption of ChatGPT, has strained power supplies ŌĆŗand raised ŌĆŗconcerns ŌüĀover global grid capacity.
Veolia said it planned to use ŌĆŗits proprietary technologies to help ŌĆŗmeet ŌüĀrising demand for integrated solutions in water management, local energy and ŌüĀhazardous ŌĆŗwaste treatment linked to ŌĆŗthe expansion of these industries.
($1 = 0.8477 euros)

item.title: Nigeria wildlife trafficking persists as protection law awaits presidential signing
item.description:
LAGOS, April 14 (Reuters) - NigeriaŌĆÖs customs said on Tuesday its officials had intercepted two hyenas, a porcupine and 24 protected birds, the ŌĆŗsixth major wildlife seizure since parliament passed a longŌĆæawaited protection law ŌĆīin October that has not yet come into force.
The bill, intended to overhaul NigeriaŌĆÖs wildlife protection framework and sharply increase penalties, cleared both chambers of the ŌĆŗNational Assembly late last year but has yet to receive ŌĆŗpresidential assent.
ŌĆ£There was an omission in the schedules where some ŌüĀspecies classified as endangered globally are not considered endangered in ŌĆŗNigeria,ŌĆØ said Terseer Ugbor, a lawmaker involved in drafting the bill. ŌĆ£We had ŌĆŗto go back and correct that.ŌĆØ
Ugbor said the revisions have now been completed and the bill was ready for signing.
Conservation groups said they were pushing for the ŌĆŗbill to be signed quickly.
ŌĆ£Signing the law will significantly empower investigators, ŌĆŗprosecutors and the judiciary to curb wildlife trafficking,ŌĆØ said Linus Unah, West Africa ŌĆīdirector ŌüĀat conservation group Wild Africa.
The bill would align domestic law more closely with international conventions, expand protected species lists and introduce tougher sanctions, including longer prison terms and higher fines.
As it awaits the presidential signature, ŌĆŗthe wildlife trafficking the ŌĆŗlaw is meant ŌüĀto curb has continued - as the latest seizures highlight.
Since October, the Nigeria Customs Service has intercepted elephant ŌĆŗivory in Abuja, live pangolins in northern and southwestern ŌĆŗstates, ŌüĀa lion cub and monkeys near the Benin border, and most recently the animals hidden in a vehicle in the northeastern city of Maiduguri, ŌüĀaccording ŌĆŗto customs statements.
Abdullahi Maiwada, a customs spokesperson, ŌĆŗsaid the agency could not comment on delays in the lawŌĆÖs enactment but welcomed stronger ŌĆŗlegal frameworks to support enforcement.

item.title: Carney vows to tackle cost of living in Canada with new majority government, suspends fuel tax
item.description:
OTTAWA, April 14 (Reuters) - Prime Minister ŌĆŗMark Carney said he would prioritize lowering the cost of living in Canada, tackling a ŌĆīhousing shortage, and building major infrastructure projects to help make Canada's economy more independent with his Liberal Party's new parliamentary majority.
After his party swept three special elections on Monday, capping off an extraordinary few months in Canada when several opposition ŌĆŗmembers joined him, Carney said he accepts the country's support "with humility, determination and a clear understanding ŌĆŗof what this moment demands."
The first Liberal majority government in Canada since 2019 ŌüĀshould allow Carney more freedom to pass the legislation he says is needed for the country to ŌĆŗthrive in "a more divided and dangerous world," including the uncertainty caused by U.S. President Donald Trump and the ŌĆŗongoing war in the Middle East .
Carney has sought to make Canada less dependent on the United States.
"We have to change fundamentally our economy to be stronger, more independent, more prosperous," he said at a Tuesday press briefing to announce a temporary ŌĆŗsuspension of a fuel tax . He said the measure would reduce Canadians' bills at the gas station by ŌĆŗ10 cents per litre on regular gasoline and 4 cents on diesel.
"Canada's new government has been relentlessly focused on making ŌĆīlife ŌüĀmore affordable for Canadians," he said.
Carney's Liberals won three special elections in Ontario and Quebec, giving him a 174 seat majority in the 343-seat House of Commons.
Carney said he had no plans to shuffle his cabinet before Parliament's summer recess or to call an early election, following criticism from political opponents that five recent defections of ŌĆŗlegislators to Carney's Liberals ŌĆŗundermine the credibility of ŌüĀhis majority government.
Conservative leader Pierre Poilievre took to X to express his dissatisfaction at both the process and the result: "The Carney Liberals did not win a ŌĆŗmajority government through a general election or today's by-elections. Instead, it was won ŌĆŗthrough backroom ŌüĀdeals with politicians who betrayed the people who voted for them."
Carney said in response that Canada was a parliamentary democracy and Canadians elect their local representatives.
Elizabeth McCallion, an assistant professor of political science at the University of Toronto, ŌüĀsaid ŌĆŗCarney's decision to cut the fuel tax comes days after ŌĆŗthe Conservatives argued for a cut and parties were becoming surprisingly aligned on some policies.
"This will make it more difficult for the ŌĆŗopposition parties to actually be adversarial and challenge the government," she said.

item.title: Fire breaks out at gunpowder plant in Russia's Kazan, Ifax reports
item.description:
MOSCOW, April 14 (Reuters) - A fire has ŌĆībroken out at a gunpowder plant in ŌĆŗthe Russian ŌĆŗcity of Kazan, injuring ŌüĀtwo people, Interfax ŌĆŗnews agency reported ŌĆŗon Tuesday, citing local authorities.
The fire was not ŌĆŗcaused by an ŌĆŗexternal action, and production ŌĆīat ŌüĀthe plant has not been halted, local authorities were ŌĆŗquoted by ŌĆŗRussian ŌüĀnews agencies as saying.
The ŌĆŗinjured are receiving ŌĆŗmedical ŌüĀcare and their lives are not ŌüĀin ŌĆŗdanger, the ŌĆŗauthorities said.

item.title: Fertiliser shortages due to Iran war are a key worry for developing world, UN agency says
item.description:
BRUSSELS, April 14 (Reuters) - Fertiliser shortages due to the Iran war are a pressing concern for developing countries and gains from rising oil and gas prices for developing world ŌĆŗproducers are likely to be short-lived, the head of the United Nations trade ŌĆīagency said on Tuesday.
"The more immediate issue is fertiliser, because that then affects food security and food security is always the basis for stability," said Pamela Coke-Hamilton, executive director of the International Trade Centre (ITC), adding ŌĆŗthat oil and gas could be secured from other places so the situation was "not ŌĆŗas dire" even if price hikes were a problem.
Coke-Hamilton, whose agency focuses ŌüĀon promoting trade for developing countries, noted that a third of global urea would normally ŌĆŗpass through the Strait of Hormuz, which Iran and the United States are blockading.
"There are ŌĆŗsignificant issues with respect to availability of fertilisers and also there's a timeline for agriculture in terms of ensuring you have enough for the next harvest, which is being missed now," she told Reuters in ŌĆŗan interview.
The U.N. said on Monday that a diplomatic push was under way on a ŌĆŗU.N.-led proposal to ensure safe passage for fertiliser shipments through the Strait of Hormuz.
The ITC said dependence on nitrogen ŌĆīfertilisers ŌüĀfrom Gulf producers is highest in several Asian and African developing countries, such as Kenya, Uganda, South Africa, Thailand and Sri Lanka.
Shortages typically lead to reduced fertiliser use and lower yields, rather than changes in harvesting time, with this effect more pronounced in regions such as ŌĆŗSub-Saharan Africa and South ŌĆŗAsia, where production is ŌüĀmore dependent on rains, planting windows are narrower and farmers more sensitive to input costs, the ITC said.
Alternative suppliers, particularly in North Africa, ŌĆŗcould help fill the gap, the ITC said, with Egypt holding ŌĆŗa potential $1.6 ŌüĀbillion of untapped exports and Algeria a further $1.3 billion.
The ITC said countries such as Nigeria, Kazakhstan, Brazil, Angola and Libya may benefit from increased oil revenues, but these gains would be limited ŌüĀas all ŌĆŗbut Kazakhstan remained net importers of refined products.
Higher natural ŌĆŗgas prices may benefit countries such as Algeria, Malaysia, Turkmenistan and Azerbaijan, but expansion of supply is likely to ŌĆŗbe limited in the short term, the ITC said.

item.title: Macron to host call with EU leaders on social media ban for minors
item.description:
PARIS, April 14 (Reuters) - French President Emmanuel Macron will host a ŌĆŗvideo call with other ŌĆīEU leaders and European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen ŌĆŗto push for a ŌĆŗcoordinated approach on banning social ŌüĀmedia for minors, Macron's office ŌĆŗsaid Tuesday.
Spanish Prime Minister Pedro ŌĆŗSanchez and representatives of Italy, the Netherlands and Ireland will attend ŌĆŗthe conference call, among ŌĆŗothers, on Thursday, Macron's office said, adding ŌĆīthat ŌüĀthe final list of attendees will be announced later.
"The main goal is to ŌĆŗact in ŌĆŗa ŌüĀcoordinated manner and push the European Commission, ŌĆŗin the positive sense ŌĆŗof ŌüĀthe term, to move ahead at the same pace ŌüĀas ŌĆŗmember states," a ŌĆŗpresidential aide told reporters.

item.title: Six ships turned around as part of Strait of Hormuz blockade, US military says
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 14 (Reuters) - No ships have made it past a U.S. naval blockade of Iran's ports and coastal areas, and six ŌĆŗmerchant ships have followed orders to turn back, the U.S. ŌĆīmilitary said on Tuesday, providing the first details on a day-old effort ordered by President Donald Trump after peace talks between the U.S. and Iran broke down.
The U.S. military ŌĆŗhas said that the blockade, which started on Monday, would only ŌĆŗapply to ships going to or from Iran, including all Iranian ŌüĀports on the Gulf and Gulf of Oman.
"During the first 24 ŌĆŗhours, no ships made it past the U.S. blockade and six merchant vessels ŌĆŗcomplied with direction from U.S. forces to turn around to re-enter an Iranian port on the Gulf of Oman," the U.S. military's Central Command said in the statement.
More than ŌĆŗ10,000 U.S. military personnel, more than a dozen warships and dozens of ŌĆŗaircraft were enforcing the blockade, it said.
"The blockade is being enforced impartially against vessels ŌĆīof ŌüĀall nations entering or departing Iranian ports and coastal areas," the statement added.
In a note sent to seafarers about the blockade on Monday, the U.S. military said: "Any vessel entering or departing the blockaded area without authorization is subject to ŌĆŗinterception, diversion, and ŌĆŗcapture."
The Monday note ŌüĀsaid the blockade would include all of Iran's coastline, but humanitarian shipments including food, medical supplies and other essential ŌĆŗgoods would be permitted, subject to inspection.
Trump announced the ŌĆŗblockade following ŌüĀthe breakdown of weekend talks to end the six-week-long war between the U.S. and Iran, sending oil prices back above $100 a barrel.
The blockade adds to ŌüĀuncertainty ŌĆŗaround how ships will transit the crucial ŌĆŗwaterway, used to move one-fifth of the world's oil and gas supplies.

item.title: In a first, Libya's rival forces take part in joint US-led military exercises
item.description:
BENGHAZI, Libya, April 14 (Reuters) - Forces from Libya's east- and west-based administrations participated in U.S. special forces exercises in the central city ŌĆŗof Sirte on Tuesday in the first such joint military ŌĆīevent including the former civil war rivals.
Libya has been divided since 2014 when war broke out in the wake of the uprising that overthrew ŌĆŗlongtime leader Muammar Gaddafi three years earlier.
The fighting culminated ŌĆŗin 2019-2020 when military commander Khalifa Haftar's Libyan National ŌüĀArmy, or LNA, based in the eastern city of Benghazi, ŌĆŗtried to take the capital Tripoli.
Tensions have cooled considerably since then, ŌĆŗand the two sides last week agreed to the first unified budget in more than a decade to determine how to spend the billions of dollars ŌĆŗof oil revenues the country earns each year.
The "Flintlock" special operations ŌĆŗexercises, run by the United States Africa Command, known as AFRICOM, opened in ŌĆīSirte ŌüĀon Tuesday including forces from both the LNA and the U.N.-recognised Government of National Unity, which is based in Tripoli.
Haftar's son, Saddam, who serves as the LNA's deputy commander, said in a ŌĆŗspeech that the ŌĆŗexercise "reaffirms LibyaŌĆÖs position ŌüĀas a reliable partner in supporting regional and international peace and security."
AFRICOM said in a statement ŌĆŗthat forces from more than 30 countries would ŌĆŗtake ŌüĀpart in the exercises, which will also be held in Ivory Coast later in the month.
Italy played a significant role in planning ŌüĀand carrying ŌĆŗout Flintlock in Libya, which marked ŌĆŗa "historic milestone" and supported "the continued development of a unified Libyan military," the statement said.

item.title: Irish junior minister resigns in protest of government response to fuel protests
item.description:
DUBLIN, April 14 (Reuters) - An Irish junior minister resigned on Tuesday in protest over the government's response to a wave of public demonstration against surging ŌĆŗfuel prices last week and said he would ŌĆījoin the opposition in voting no confidence in the coalition.
The government is still expected to survive the confidence motion the opposition put down ŌĆŗafter protesters blockaded oil infrastructure and left about a ŌĆŗthird of Ireland's petrol stations without fuel. The disruption ŌüĀended on Monday.
However, the resignation of Michael Healy-Rae during the debate ŌĆŗcame as an unexpected blow to the government. Healy-Rae is ŌĆŗone of a number of independent lawmakers whose support the centre-right-led coalition relies on for its majority.
"The leader of the country should have ŌĆŗlistened and because of the fact that I believe this ŌĆŗgovernment has let the people of Ireland down, I will be voting ŌĆīno ŌüĀconfidence in the leader of the country and I will be tendering my resignation as a Minister of State from now," Healy-Rae told parliament.
He added that his constituents in rural county ŌĆŗKerry did not ŌĆŗwant to ŌüĀsee him "or any Healy-Rae" to back the government. That suggested his brother and fellow independent ŌĆŗlawmaker, Danny, would also withdraw his support, further ŌĆŗcutting ŌüĀthe government's majority.
In a bid to ease the discontent, the government announced 500 million euros ($586 million) worth of spending increases and tax ŌüĀcuts ŌĆŗto soften the impact on consumers ŌĆŗand businesses on Sunday. That was on top of a 250 million euro package introduced ŌĆŗthree weeks ago.

item.title: Exclusive: US to allow waiver on Iran oil to expire, administration sources say
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 14 (Reuters) - The Trump administration will allow a 30-day waiver ŌĆŗof sanctions on Iranian oil at sea ŌĆīto expire later this week, two administration officials told Reuters on Tuesday, as the U.S. imposes a blockade on ŌĆŗshipments from Iranian ports.
The waiver, which the ŌĆŗTreasury Department issued on March 20, allowed ŌüĀsome 140 million barrels of oil to reach ŌĆŗglobal markets and helped relieve pressure on energy ŌĆŗsupply during the war on Iran, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said last month. The waiver is set to ŌĆŗexpire on April 19.
The move comes after lawmakers ŌĆŗfrom both political parties criticized the administration for temporarily relaxing ŌĆīsanctions ŌüĀon Tehran and Moscow, while the U.S. and Israel are at war with Iran and as Moscow continues its war with Ukraine.
One of ŌĆŗthe U.S. officials ŌĆŗsaid Washington ŌüĀhas a range of authorities it can apply to institutions that ŌĆŗbuy Iranian oil, including secondary sanctions. "In addition, ŌĆŗwith ŌüĀthe snapback of U.N. sanctions on Iran, and its history of trying to hide behind seemingly ŌüĀlegitimate ŌĆŗactivity to conduct its illicit ŌĆŗconduct, any activity with Tehran could trigger additional sanctions," the source ŌĆŗsaid.

item.title: OMV denies breaking Austria's rules on lowering petrol prices
item.description:
VIENNA, April 14 (Reuters) - Austria said it was ordering an official review of whether partially state-owned oil and gas company OMV (OMVV.VI) , opens new tab is complying with newly introduced rules ŌĆŗon lowering petrol prices, as the company denied on Tuesday ŌĆīit was breaking them.
The U.S.-Israeli war against Iran has led to the largest oil supply disruption in history and sent oil prices surging, with European governments scrambling to ŌĆŗprotect consumers.
Austria's government has introduced a so-called "petrol price brake" under which ŌĆŗthe increased value-added tax take from higher petrol prices is passed ŌüĀon to consumers in the form of a reduction in tax ŌĆŗon petrol and diesel, initially set at 5 euro cents (6 U.S. cents) per ŌĆŗlitre.
National broadcaster ORF reported on Tuesday OMV that had informed wholesale customers that it would only be reducing the price of diesel by 2.8 cents a litre.
"We and E-Control ŌĆŗwere informed at short notice of OMV's planned price adjustment," Austria's economy ŌĆŗministry said when asked about the report, referring to the national energy regulator tasked ŌĆīwith ŌüĀenforcing the rules.
"The burden of proof now lies with OMV. We will in any case task E-Control with carrying out a special verification," it added. E-Control said in response to a question from Reuters that it would soon ŌĆŗrequest the relevant ŌĆŗinformation from OMV.
Asked ŌüĀfor comment, OMV did not deny the reported price change but said it was obeying the rules, arguing that ŌĆŗthe calculation was different when taking into account fuel ŌĆŗthat was ŌüĀimported rather than refined in Austria.
OMV was fully passing on the 5 cent reduction on products from Schwechat, its main refinery in Austria, it said.
"This results ŌüĀin ŌĆŗa combined calculation based on OMV's production ŌĆŗin Schwechat and import volumes, which determines the OMV selling price for wholesale customers," the company ŌĆŗadded.
($1 = 0.8473 euros)

item.title: BP's new CEO to simplify company structure into upstream, downstream units
item.description:
LONDON, April 14 (Reuters) - BP < BP.L , opens new tab will reorganise into ŌĆītwo main business units - upstream and downstream - under new CEO Meg O'Neill, who took the helm on April 1 to become the oil major's fifth chief ŌĆŗsince 2020, a spokesperson said on Tuesday.
"In service of becoming ŌĆŗa simpler, stronger, more valuable BP, we intend to ŌüĀbuild an organisation with a clear upstream and downstream," the spokesperson ŌĆŗsaid, confirming an earlier Financial Times report about the change.
There is no set ŌĆŗtimeline for the new structure, BP said.
BP currently has three main business units. Its gas and low carbon unit includes gas-focused production. The oil production and operations ŌĆŗunit includes its oil-focused production, its U.S. onshore business and refinery operations, ŌĆŗwhile customers and products encompasses fuel sales, petrol retail stations and lubricants.
This week, Emma Delaney ŌĆīleft ŌüĀher role as BP's customers and products chief and is set to become OMV (OMVV.VI) , opens new tab chief executive in September.
U.S. hedge fund Elliott , which holds a stake of just over 5% in BP, has called for the company to ŌĆŗreorganise into one ŌĆŗupstream and ŌüĀone downstream unit, a source said a year ago.
Two weeks ago, BP named Carol Howle as O'Neill's deputy ŌĆŗchief executive, saying she would oversee the company's ongoing ŌĆŗportfolio ŌüĀreview and strategy development, and be in charge of supply, trading and shipping.
BP had two main units for upstream and downstream before then-CEO Bernard Looney overhauled the organisation ŌüĀin ŌĆŗ2020 as part of a major push into renewable ŌĆŗenergy, a strategy for which the company was punished by investors.

item.title: Vitol to invest $130 million to boost South African storage capacity in Durban
item.description:
CAPE TOWN, April 14 - Vivo Energy, a subsidiary of global trader Vitol, is investing around $130 million to bulk ŌĆīup its fuel storage capacity in South Africa's major port city Durban along the east coast, a senior company executive said on Tuesday. The investment, planned before the U.S.-Israeli war with Iran upended global energy markets and choked off ŌĆŗthe Strait of Hormuz transit route, could help South Africa better weather future supply ŌĆŗcrises, said George Roberts, chief executive officer of Vivo Energy's local unit Engen.
Southern ŌüĀand East African countries including South Africa, which is a net importer of crude and ŌĆŗrefined petroleum products, are vulnerable to Middle East supply disruptions, analysts said, a problem exacerbated by inadequate ŌĆŗinfrastructure and storage capacity.
Roberts told Reuters the company was adding approximately 125,000 cubic meters storage capacity in Durban to increase its overall storage capacity in the area to 500,000 cubic metres, expecting it to come on stream ŌĆŗfrom the third quarter of 2026 through to Q3 2027 and help act as a future ŌĆŗbuffer to unexpected supply shocks such as those emanating from the Middle East.
"This will allow you to increase ŌĆīthe stock ŌüĀlevels in-country and therefore if something like this happens again, it gives us more time to go and find product elsewhere to bring to South Africa given it takes on average 20, 25 days to ship product to South Africa depending on where it comes from," he said.
The ŌĆŗadditional capacity derives from converting ŌĆŗold refinery tanks ŌüĀin Durban, and upgrading another receiving facility at Island View, part of ongoing efforts by Vivo Energy to convert the fire-damaged Engen refinery into a ŌĆŗstorage terminal for a range of products, including diesel, petrol and jet ŌĆŗfuel.
Vivo Energy, ŌüĀwhich markets Shell - except in South Africa - and Engen brands, is a leading African fuel retailer with more than 4,000 service stations across the continent. The company is currently investing in LPG and refined ŌüĀpetroleum ŌĆŗstorage facilities in Ivory Coast, Senegal and Morocco, said Roberts.
"Besides ŌĆŗUganda we have invested in our depot assets in Tanzania and Mozambique in recent years and if the right opportunity comes ŌĆŗup in those countries, we could invest more," Roberts said.

item.title: US extends waiver for Lukoil gas stations outside of Russia
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 14 (Reuters) - The Trump administration has extended a waiver ŌĆŗallowing Lukoil retail service stations to ŌĆīoperate outside of Russia until late October, a narrow allowance in sanctions the U.S. imposed last ŌĆŗyear to curb revenues supporting Moscow's ŌĆŗwar in Ukraine.
The waiver for about ŌüĀ2,000 stations throughout Europe, Central Asia, the ŌĆŗMiddle East and the Americas has been ŌĆŗextended until October 29, the Treasury Department said on its website on Tuesday.
President Donald Trump imposed sanctions ŌĆŗon Lukoil (LKOH.MM) , opens new tab and Rosneft (ROSN.MM) , opens new tab , Russia's two ŌĆŗbiggest oil companies, in October, stirring up interest from ŌĆīpotential ŌüĀbuyers of Lukoil's estimated $22 billion of international assets.
Lukoil has about 200 branded gas stations in New Jersey, Pennsylvania and New York. ŌĆŗIt is one ŌĆŗof ŌüĀthe top motor fuel retailers in Moldova and Bulgaria and operates ŌĆŗabout 600 stations in Turkey ŌĆŗand ŌüĀmore than 300 in Romania.
The Treasury also issued a license allowing certain transactions with ŌüĀLukoil ŌĆŗrefining entities in Bulgaria, ŌĆŗincluding Lukoil Neftohim Burgas JSC.

item.title: Gunvor's net profits fell sharply in management buyout year
item.description:
LONDON, April 14 (Reuters) - Global commodity trading house Gunvor recorded an 85% drop in ŌĆīnet profits in 2025 to $104 million, according to financial results released on Tuesday.
The sharp drop came as Gunvor employees staged a management buyout to replace former CEO and co-founder Torbjorn Tornqvist late last year, in a bid to distance the firm from its history of close ties to Russia after the U.S. dubbed it the " KremlinŌĆÖs ŌĆŗpuppet ".
The net profit figure of $104 million includes writedowns and other impairments worth a total of $462 million incurred by the new management ŌĆŗteam, the firm said.
The buyout saw Gunvor valued at around $5 billion, with Tornqvist providing a loan of more than $4 ŌüĀbillion to employees to make the deal happen, Reuters reported in February.
Tornqvist received $1 billion in upfront payments in the buyout, Bloomberg News ŌĆŗreported on Tuesday, as well as $200 million in assets and the cancellation of approximately $500 million in loans that were "mainly" to him.
The full details of ŌĆŗthe buyout were not made public, but Gunvor said at the time that the buyout would be funded by a mix of partners' own equity and a vendor loan from Tornqvist.
Commodity trading houses including Gunvor grappled with politically driven heightened market volatility last year, adjusting to lower profit ŌĆŗmargins after the COVID-19 demand recovery and Russia-Ukraine war-associated market dislocations that drove record earnings in 2022-2023.
"Throughout most of 2025, the energy markets ŌĆŗremained structurally tight yet politically volatile, whereby trading margins were driven less by fundamental supply and demand imbalances and more by navigating fragmentation, sanctions, and regional ŌĆīdislocations ŌüĀin flows," Gunvor CEO Gary Pedersen said.
Pedersen said the company had already made the equivalent of its 2025 gross profit, $1.63 billion, in the first quarter, after what he called a pick-up in "constructive volatility" late last year.
Gunvor's rivals including Trafigura and Glencore also saw lower earnings from energy trading last year. Vitol , the world's largest independent oil trader, does not disclose a net profit figure in its results.
Pedersen highlighted ŌĆŗthe company's "particular emphasis on investments and ŌĆŗdevelopment within the U.S. market" ŌüĀin his statement accompanying the results.
So far this year, Gunvor has been awarded two contracts to borrow crude oil from the U.S. Strategic Petroleum Reserve as part of the administration's bid to calm surging prices as a ŌĆŗresult of the war in Iran, alongside other companies.
Relations with the U.S. were frayed after Gunvor was ŌĆŗdubbed the "Kremlin's puppet" by ŌüĀthe U.S. Treasury last year, prompting the Switzerland-based trader to withdraw its bid for Russian energy company Lukoil's foreign assets.
The firm was founded in 2000 by Swedish billionaire Tornqvist and Russian oligarch Gennadiy Timchenko, naming the firm after Tornqvist's mother. New CEO Pedersen previously told Reuters that he had no ŌüĀplans to ŌĆŗrebrand Gunvor after the buyout.
Gunvor's traded volumes rose to 253 million tons last year ŌĆŗfrom 232 million tons in 2024.
The privately owned firm does not disclose a full suite of financial results, releasing only a limited set of figures.
Group equity was $6.1 billion at ŌĆŗthe end of the 2025 financial year, from $6.5 billion a year earlier.

item.title: US power and natgas prices turn negative in Texas and California as mild weather cuts demand
item.description:
NEW YORK, April 14 (Reuters) - U.S. spot power and ŌĆŗnatural gas prices in Texas and California traded in negative territory for Tuesday as mild weather kept both heating and ŌĆīcooling use low, allowing ample amounts of hydro and other renewable sources of energy to meet more demand.
Negative prices are a sign that energy firms were producing too much power or gas, requiring some firms to cut back on output or pay others to take their power or gas.
In West Texas, average cash gas ŌĆŗprices at the Waha Hub remained in negative territory for a record 47 straight days as pipeline constraints continued to trap gas ŌĆŗassociated with oil production in the Permian region, the nation's biggest oil-producing shale basin.
Analysts have long said negative ŌüĀprices were a sure sign that the Permian region, which spans West Texas and eastern New Mexico, needs more gas pipes.
More pipes are ŌĆŗon the way later this year, but not soon enough to handle all the gas currently coming out of the ground.
Daily Waha prices first ŌĆŗaveraged below zero in 2019. They did so 17 times in 2019, six times in 2020, once in 2023, 49 times in 2024, 39 times in 2025, and a record 56 times so far this year, according to pricing data from financial firm LSEG.
Waha prices have averaged a negative $1.53 per million British thermal units (mmBtu) ŌĆŗso far in 2026, compared with a positive $1.15 in 2025 and a positive $2.88 over the past five years (2021-2025).
Power prices in some Electric Reliability ŌĆŗCouncil of Texas (ERCOT) regions, such as the West and Panhandle, were also negative for several hours.
Electric prices also turned negative in California.
Spot ŌĆīpower at ŌüĀthe South Path 15 (SP-15) Hub fell below zero for the first time since March 2025, dropping to a negative 72 cents per megawatt hour (MWh) for Tuesday, down from a positive $1.88 for Monday.
That compares with a record low of negative $16.85 per MWh in May 2024, and positive averages of $19.80 so far in 2026, $28.44 in 2025, and $53.02 over the past five years (2021-2025). SP-15 prices averaged in negative territory two times in 2025 and 18 ŌĆŗtimes in 2024.
With little demand for ŌĆŗpower, next-day gas prices at ŌüĀthe PG&E Citygate in Northern California fell to a record low for a second day in a row, dropping to $1.21 per mmBtu for Tuesday. That compares with averages of $1.95 so far in 2026, $3.42 in 2025, ŌĆŗand $5.47 over the past five years.
Energy traders noted power prices often turn negative in western parts of ŌĆŗthe U.S. in the ŌüĀspring when warmer weather melts snow in the mountains, boosting the supply of water available for hydropower.
The U.S. Northwest River Forecast Center (NWRFC), which tracks water flows in the Pacific Northwest, projected the amount of water at the Dalles Dam on the Columbia River between Washington and Oregon would average 103% ŌüĀof normal ŌĆŗduring the October-September water season in 2026.
That is well above levels of 80% of ŌĆŗnormal in fiscal 2025, 77% in 2024 and 76% in 2023.
The Dalles is the next-to-last dam on the lower Columbia River and a key point to measure the volume ŌĆŗof water available for power generation in the Northwest.

item.title: Suelopetrol has not been notified of Chevron's expansion at Venezuela joint venture, company says
item.description:
April 14 (Reuters) - Venezuelan oil producer Suelopetrol said on Tuesday ŌĆŗit has received no ŌĆīnotice of a shareholders meeting or resolution authorizing ŌĆŗU.S. Chevron (CVX.N) , opens new tab to increase ŌĆŗits stake at a ŌüĀkey Venezuela project to ŌĆŗ49%, Suelopetrol's international arm ŌĆŗsaid in a release.
Suelopetrol, which according to the terms of ŌĆŗa 2024 extension ŌĆŗof the joint venture owns 1% ŌĆīof ŌüĀthe project and could expand to up to 3% if the structure ŌĆŗchanges, ŌĆŗsaid ŌüĀit is entitled to a complete ŌĆŗaccount of the ŌĆŗshare ŌüĀtransfer and a disclosure of current shareholding in ŌüĀthe ŌĆŗproject in Venezuela's ŌĆŗOrinoco Belt.

item.title: Around 250 missing after Rohingya boat capsizes in Andaman Sea, UN agencies say
item.description:
April 14 (Reuters) - Around 250 people were missing after a boat carrying Rohingya refugees and Bangladeshi nationals capsized in the ŌĆŗAndaman Sea, the United Nations' refugee and migration ŌĆīagencies said in a joint statement on Tuesday.
The UN agencies said the trawler carrying around 250 men, women and children reportedly ŌĆŗsank due to heavy winds, rough seas and overcrowding. ŌĆŗIt had departed from Teknaf in southern Bangladesh ŌüĀand was bound for Malaysia.
"This tragedy highlights the devastating ŌĆŗhuman cost of protracted displacement and the continued absence ŌĆŗof durable solutions for the Rohingya," said the United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees, and the International Organization for Migration.
For years, many ŌĆŗof Myanmar's Rohingya Muslim minority have embarked on rickety wooden ŌĆŗboats to try to reach neighbouring countries, including Malaysia, Indonesia and Thailand, ŌĆīin ŌüĀa bid to flee persecution in Myanmar or overcrowded refugee camps in Bangladesh.
The agencies called on the international community to step up and sustain funding for lifesaving assistance for ŌĆŗRohingya refugees in ŌĆŗBangladesh as ŌüĀwell as support for Bangladeshi host communities.
In 2017, Myanmar's armed forces launched an offensive that ŌĆŗforced at least 730,000 Rohingya from their ŌĆŗhomes and ŌüĀinto neighbouring Bangladesh, where they recounted killings, mass rape and arson. A U.N. fact-finding mission concluded the 2017 military ŌüĀoffensive ŌĆŗhad included "genocidal acts".
Buddhist-majority Myanmar has denied ŌĆŗgenocide, and says the U.N. fact-finding mission was not objective or reliable.

item.title: Carney vows to tackle cost of living in Canada with new majority government, suspends fuel tax
item.description:
OTTAWA, April 14 (Reuters) - Prime Minister ŌĆŗMark Carney said he would prioritize lowering the cost of living in Canada, tackling a ŌĆīhousing shortage, and building major infrastructure projects to help make Canada's economy more independent with his Liberal Party's new parliamentary majority.
After his party swept three special elections on Monday, capping off an extraordinary few months in Canada when several opposition ŌĆŗmembers joined him, Carney said he accepts the country's support "with humility, determination and a clear understanding ŌĆŗof what this moment demands."
The first Liberal majority government in Canada since 2019 ŌüĀshould allow Carney more freedom to pass the legislation he says is needed for the country to ŌĆŗthrive in "a more divided and dangerous world," including the uncertainty caused by U.S. President Donald Trump and the ŌĆŗongoing war in the Middle East .
Carney has sought to make Canada less dependent on the United States.
"We have to change fundamentally our economy to be stronger, more independent, more prosperous," he said at a Tuesday press briefing to announce a temporary ŌĆŗsuspension of a fuel tax . He said the measure would reduce Canadians' bills at the gas station by ŌĆŗ10 cents per litre on regular gasoline and 4 cents on diesel.
"Canada's new government has been relentlessly focused on making ŌĆīlife ŌüĀmore affordable for Canadians," he said.
Carney's Liberals won three special elections in Ontario and Quebec, giving him a 174 seat majority in the 343-seat House of Commons.
Carney said he had no plans to shuffle his cabinet before Parliament's summer recess or to call an early election, following criticism from political opponents that five recent defections of ŌĆŗlegislators to Carney's Liberals ŌĆŗundermine the credibility of ŌüĀhis majority government.
Conservative leader Pierre Poilievre took to X to express his dissatisfaction at both the process and the result: "The Carney Liberals did not win a ŌĆŗmajority government through a general election or today's by-elections. Instead, it was won ŌĆŗthrough backroom ŌüĀdeals with politicians who betrayed the people who voted for them."
Carney said in response that Canada was a parliamentary democracy and Canadians elect their local representatives.
Elizabeth McCallion, an assistant professor of political science at the University of Toronto, ŌüĀsaid ŌĆŗCarney's decision to cut the fuel tax comes days after ŌĆŗthe Conservatives argued for a cut and parties were becoming surprisingly aligned on some policies.
"This will make it more difficult for the ŌĆŗopposition parties to actually be adversarial and challenge the government," she said.

item.title: South AfricaŌĆÖs Ramaphosa to tap Roelf Meyer as next US envoy, Bloomberg News reports
item.description:
April 14 (Reuters) - South African President ŌĆŗCyril Ramaphosa ŌĆīis set to ŌĆŗtap veteran ŌĆŗpolitical negotiator Roelf ŌüĀMeyer ŌĆŗas the ŌĆŗcountryŌĆÖs next envoy to the ŌĆŗUnited ŌĆŗStates, Bloomberg News reported ŌĆīon ŌüĀTuesday, citing people familiar ŌĆŗwith ŌĆŗthe ŌüĀmatter.
Reuters could not ŌĆŗimmediately ŌĆŗverify ŌüĀthe report.

item.title: UN's Guterres says highly probable Iran talks will restart
item.description:
April 14 (Reuters) - U.N. Secretary-General Antonio Guterres said on Tuesday the indication the ŌĆŗUnited Nations had was that it was ŌĆīhighly probable talks to end the Iran war will restart.
U.S. President Donald Trump said on Tuesday that Iran talks could resume ŌĆŗin Pakistan over the next two days, ŌĆŗaccording to an interview with the New York ŌüĀPost.
Asked what the United Nations knew about ŌĆŗsuch prospects, Guterres told reporters at the U.N., "The indication ŌĆŗwe have is that it is highly probable that these talks will restart."
Guterres said he met on Tuesday with ŌĆŗthe deputy prime minister of Pakistan, and praised ŌĆŗPakistan's peace efforts.
"I consider it essential that these negotiations go ŌĆīon," ŌüĀGuterres said.
"I think it would be unrealistic to expect... such a complex problem, long-lasting problem, could be resolved in the first session of a ŌĆŗnegotiation. So we ŌĆŗneed negotiations ŌüĀto go on, and we need a ceasefire to persist as negotiations ŌĆŗgo on."
Trump was quoted as saying that "something ŌĆŗcould ŌüĀbe happening over the next two days, and we're more inclined to go there," referring to ŌüĀPakistan.
He said ŌĆŗPakistan's Army chief, Field Marshal ŌĆŗAsim Munir, was doing a "great job" on the talks.

item.title: US oil export ban: a┬Ābad idea whose time is coming
item.description:
NEW YORK, April 14 (Reuters Breakingviews) - For every problem, thereŌĆÖs a ready-made easy solution that is also just plain wrong. A ban on U.S. oil exports fits into the category.
Prices for ŌĆŗcrude and the products made from it are rising because of disruption in the Strait of Hormuz. Asian and ŌĆīEuropean buyers, desperate for replacements, are therefore looking elsewhere. The United States happens to be a large producer.
President Donald Trump, he of the "drill, baby, drill" school of thought, is proud of the supply. He bragged , opens new tab on social media of many empty and inbound oil tankers. The country he leads already exports 4 million ŌĆŗbarrels per day, and is the biggest gasoline exporter, to boot. Selling more overseas would naturally raise prices at home.
Diesel ŌĆŗon the U.S. Gulf Coast already costs 50% more now than at the start of March. A ŌüĀtripling of large tankers on their way to those ports, according to cargo tracker Vortexa, will accelerate the trend.
High energy prices are ŌĆŗpolitical Kryptonite and the Trump administration likes to project a populist image. Interior Secretary Doug Burgum told oil industry executives last month ŌĆŗthat an export ban was not under consideration, Politico reported , opens new tab . If prices spike high enough, though, officials may face pressure to reconsider.
Any outbound blockage would introduce new market distortions. Drillers would receive less of a price signal to boost production. Moreover, while the U.S. fracking boom now extracts plenty of relatively pure oil, ŌĆŗmany domestic refineries were designed to process heavy, cheap crude containing impurities. To compensate, the country sells the former overseas and imports ŌĆŗthe latter.
An export ban would leave the United States with a glut of light, sweet crude. Refiners can pivot, but with opportunity costs. Expensive purification ŌĆīequipment would ŌüĀbe left unused and there isn't enough capacity to handle all the excess.
In addition, light U.S. oil tends to make heavy naphtha used for gasoline. Such derivative products are sold abroad. A crude ban would increase the imbalance, leaving the country with more gasoline and less diesel.
Finally, oil production, shipping and refining is one of the world's most complex systems. Changing flows suddenly would introduce unknown outcomes. Recessions ŌĆŗelsewhere hurt the U.S. economy, too. ŌĆŗCrude and related products are ŌüĀalready in short supply. Making U.S. refiners more inefficient wonŌĆÖt help, while a ban would invite retaliation. In this case, one simple answer would leave many difficult questions.
Follow Robert Cyran on Bluesky , opens new tab .

item.title: Higher oil prices, higher yields, no more rate cuts? No problem for US stocks
item.description:
NEW YORK, April 14 (Reuters) - The U.S. stock market is back to where it was when the Iran war began over six weeks ago, which investors say is a reflection of bets that the conflict will not be long-lasting. But what if that thinking is wrong?
The ŌĆŗbenchmark S&P 500's (.SPX) , opens new tab round trip comes despite a sharply different investing backdrop compared with February 27, just before the U.S.-Israeli military strikes began the Middle East conflict. Oil prices are some ŌĆī40% higher. Concerns about inflation have driven up benchmark Treasury yields. Those same concerns have led markets to largely rule out previously anticipated interest-rate cuts this year.
All of those factors could present obstacles to stock performance, should they persist.
"There's a lot of complacency that this can resolve itself fast. What's priced in is that we have an off-ramp," said Brad Conger, chief investment officer at Hirtle Callaghan, which oversees assets from endowments and foundations. "I think we're a lot worse off than February 27th, and we're at ŌĆŗthe same price."
Investors are seizing on what they see as a solid economic backdrop, in particular a strong outlook for corporate profits that has actually improved since the war began. They are also mindful ŌĆŗof the stock market's resilience during this three-year-old bull market and wary of missing out on rallies.
The S&P 500 has rebounded after dropping ŌüĀin the initial weeks after the crisis began. The index in late March closed down over 9% from its late-January all-time high, nearly reaching a 10% decline that would have indicated a correction. The S&P 500 ŌĆŗon Monday closed up 0.1% since the war began, and just over 1% away from its record high. Stocks were moving higher again on Tuesday, with the S&P 500 last up 1% on the day.
Optimism about a resolution accelerated ŌĆŗfollowing a two-week ceasefire agreement reached last week . But much about the situation remained uncertain and investors were still bracing for war-related developments to drive asset volatility.
The market was looking at "temporary risks which will be overcome in fairly short order, as opposed to being the start of a new ... regime of higher inflation, higher energy prices, higher interest rates," said Peter Tuz, president of Chase Investment Counsel Corp. "Because if that were the new regime, there is very little reason to believe the market would be as ŌĆŗstrong as it is right now."
Central to the performance of stocks is the movement in oil prices. Sustainably higher crude prices stand to pressure consumers who have to spend more on ŌĆŗgasoline, while also raising costs for businesses.
One sign that investors are expecting the war to wind down in the near term is markets showing that oil prices are expected to be more moderate by year-end, said Angelo Kourkafas, senior global investment ŌĆīstrategist at ŌüĀEdward Jones. The front-month contract for U.S. crude is hovering at around $95 a barrel, while the December contract is at $77, according to LSEG data.
"Markets are now seeing the energy disruption as something that is near-term," Kourkafas said. "There is this notion that, yes, there is a lot of near-term disruption, but it is temporary in nature. And then once we go past that, we're going to go back to the prevailing economic resilience that we had before."
The rise in oil prices has already influenced U.S. inflation, with the monthly Consumer Price Index rising in March by the most in nearly four years.
Inflationary fears have led investors to dial back expectations for Federal Reserve rate cuts, which ŌĆŗhad been a source of optimism about U.S. equities ŌĆŗheading in to the year. Fed funds futures ŌüĀwere last pricing in just 6 basis points of easing by December, according to LSEG data as of Tuesday, or less than one standard 25-basis-point cut. Prior to the war, roughly two such quarter-percentage-point cuts had been expected by December.
Oil-driven inflation is also a main culprit driving up Treasury yields. The benchmark 10-year Treasury yield ŌĆŗwas last around 4.3%, up from 3.96% on February 27.
Higher benchmark yields could present headwinds for equity performance, including by translating into higher borrowing costs for companies ŌĆŗand consumers.
One ŌüĀpositive factor for stocks since the war began: Estimates for U.S. corporate profits are even more robust. S&P 500 companies are expected to increase earnings by 19% in 2026, up from an expected 15% increase just before the war began, according to LSEG IBES.
The increased earnings outlook has arguably made stocks look more enticing. The price-to-earnings ratio for the S&P 500, based on profit estimates for the next 12 months, stood at 20.4 on Monday, down from over 23 ŌüĀin late October, ŌĆŗaccording to LSEG Datastream.
"Estimates keep going higher despite the surge in oil prices and the implications that has for inflation," said Chris ŌĆŗFasciano, chief market strategist at Commonwealth Financial Network. "More attractive valuations and higher earnings estimates make me feel OK about the backdrop."
The upbeat earnings outlook will be tested in the coming weeks as companies report their first-quarter results.
"People are really expecting astounding earnings growth from companies ŌĆŗin aggregate this year," Tuz said. "It's way too early to say whether it's going to prove to be an accurate number or not."

item.title: US Democratic lawmaker pushes for probe into oil trades after Reuters report
item.description:
April 14 (Reuters) - U.S. Democratic Congressman Ritchie Torres on Tuesday wrote to U.S. market regulators ŌĆŗpushing for an investigation into large oil trades placed just ŌĆīhours before the announcement of the U.S.-Iran ceasefire that Reuters first reported last week.
Torres, who wrote to regulators last week to push for a probe into lucrative recent trading ŌĆŗactivity on oil markets back in March, on Tuesday urged Securities ŌĆŗand Exchange Commission Chairman Paul Atkins and Commodity Futures Trading ŌüĀCommission Chairman Michael Selig to open a joint investigation into the ŌĆŗtrades.

item.title: Global carbon markets hit record $79 bln revenue in 2025, ICAP says
item.description: LONDON, April 14 (Reuters) - Global emissions trading systems (ETS) generated a record $79 billion in revenues in ŌĆŗ2025, the International Carbon Action Partnership ŌĆīsaid on Tuesday in its annual Emissions Trading Worldwide Status Report.

item.title: Exclusive: Congo to receive first group of deportees from US this week, sources say
item.description:
DAKAR, April 14 (Reuters) - Democratic Republic of Congo is set to receive more than 30 deportees from the United States ŌĆŗthis week, four sources told Reuters, the latest example of Washington using agreements with African governments to accelerate migrant removals.
The deportees are all from ŌĆīcountries other than Congo, and at least some are from Central and South America, according to one source and U.S. court documents. One source familiar with the matter said they would total 37, while another put the figure at 45.
They will be the first to land in the Central African country as part of an agreement with the Trump administration announced on April 5 , two days after ŌĆŗReuters reported the two countries were negotiating a deal for Congo to receive third-country deportees.
The move coincides with the Trump administration's efforts to implement a U.S.-brokered peace ŌĆŗdeal between Congo and Rwanda aimed at ending fighting with Rwanda-backed M23 rebels in eastern Congo that has killed thousands and ŌüĀdisplaced hundreds of thousands more. It also follows the signing of a strategic partnership granting the United States preferential access to Congo's critical minerals.
The timing of the arrival of ŌĆŗthe deportees and details on how they will be accommodated in Congo have not been previously reported.
The deportees are expected to reach Congo by Friday and be housed in ŌĆŗa hotel near Kinshasa's main airport, three of the sources said, speaking on condition of anonymity because of the sensitive nature of the move, which has attracted criticism from human rights groups and opposition politicians in Congo.
The U.S. has previously sent third-country deportees to African states including Ghana , Cameroon , Equatorial Guinea and , drawing criticism from legal experts and rights groups over the legal basis for the transfers and ŌĆŗthe treatment of deportees sent to countries where they are not nationals.
Some of the deportees were later returned to their home countries despite receiving court-ordered protection in the U.S. ŌĆŗmeant to prevent that from happening.
A Congolese government spokesperson did not immediately respond to requests for comment on Tuesday. Kinshasa has previously stressed it will not spend any money as part of ŌĆīits third-country ŌüĀdeportees deal with the United States.
A State Department spokesperson said Washington had "no comment on the details of our diplomatic communications with other governments."
The hotel near the airport in Kinshasa is due to house the deportees for only 10 to 15 days, one of the sources told Reuters. The source was unable to say what will happen to the deportees after that period.
The hotel will remain open to other guests and the deportees will be free to move around, a diplomat and a senior humanitarian source said.
The ŌĆŗdeportees will be accommodated in single rooms, ŌĆŗwith two meals a day provided. ŌüĀThe site is being secured by Congo's national police and a private security firm.
Neither Washington nor Kinshasa has said how many deportees would ultimately be sent to Congo.
The deportees arriving this week will receive assistance from the U.N.-related International ŌĆŗOrganization for Migration (IOM), two sources said.
IOM has also provided assistance to third-country deportees sent from the U.S. to Eswatini and Cameroon.
IOM ŌĆŗsaid in a statement ŌüĀit had no role in the deportations themselves, which it said were handled by the two governments. It said it could, at the request of Congolese authorities, provide "post-arrival humanitarian assistance".
An IOM source told Reuters earlier this month that the U.S.-Congo deportation deal could involve migrants from South America, including Venezuelans.
Reuters identified at least four migrants whose lawyers were told by U.S. ŌüĀImmigration and ŌĆŗCustoms Enforcement that they would be deported to Congo this week. They are from Colombia, Peru, Chile ŌĆŗand Guatemala.
An April 8 court order from a federal judge in California, reviewed by Reuters, said the U.S. government planned to remove a Peruvian migrant to Congo. The migrant had his request for asylum rejected but ŌĆŗhad been granted protection against deportation to Peru because he feared persecution.

item.title: South African president appoints Roelf Meyer as next ambassador to US
item.description:
JOHANNESBURG, April 14 (Reuters) - South African ŌĆīPresident Cyril Ramaphosa has chosen Roelf Meyer, a former chief government negotiator during talks to end white minority rule in the 1990s, to be his country's next ambassador to ŌĆŗthe United States.
"I can confirm that President Cyril Ramaphosa has appointed ŌĆŗMr Roelf Meyer as South Africa's Ambassador to the US," Ramaphosa's ŌüĀspokesperson Vincent Magwenya told Reuters on Tuesday.
Meyer, who later served in the ŌĆŗunity government of Nelson Mandela, did not immediately respond to a request for ŌĆŗcomment.
South Africa has not had an ambassador in Washington since its last one, Ebrahim Rasool, was expelled in March 2025 after angering the Trump administration.
Relations between Washington and Pretoria ŌĆŗhave been tense during President Donald Trump's second term.
Trump has made false claims ŌĆŗabout the persecution of South Africa's white Afrikaner minority, and created a refugee programme for ŌĆīthem -- ŌüĀwhich Pretoria regards as a preferential immigration scheme for whites.
Meyer, 78, is himself Afrikaner. The veteran politician began his career as a parliamentarian in 1979 under P.W. Botha , the defiant face of white rule at the height of the anti-apartheid ŌĆŗstruggle. He later served ŌĆŗas minister of ŌüĀdefence and then constitutional affairs under President F.W. de Klerk.
He gained prominence negotiating for the National Party during talks ŌĆŗto end apartheid, becoming its chief negotiator in 1993, ŌĆŗthe year ŌüĀbefore South Africa's first democratic elections.
Ramaphosa was the chief negotiator for the African National Congress, the liberation movement at the time, and both men were recognised for ŌüĀtheir ŌĆŗwork in breaking deadlocked negotiations.
Ramaphosa "describes him as a ŌĆŗtrue citizen committed to a non-racial South Africa", according to a biography of Meyer on South ŌĆŗAfrica's presidency website.

item.title: Israel, Lebanon united in liberating Lebanon from Hezbollah, Israeli ambassador to US says
item.description: WASHINGTON, April 14 (Reuters) - The Lebanese ŌĆŗgovernment made it ŌĆīclear during U.S.-brokered talks ŌĆŗwith Israel ŌĆŗthat they no ŌüĀlonger want ŌĆŗto be "occupied" ŌĆŗby Hezbollah and that there were ŌĆŗconversations about ŌĆŗlong-term vision for ŌĆīclearly ŌüĀdelineated border, Israel's ambassador to the United ŌĆŗStates ŌĆŗYechiel ŌüĀLeiter told reporters ŌĆŗon Tuesday.

item.title: Ukraine to produce drones in Norway, Oslo says
item.description:
OSLO, April 14 (Reuters) - Norway and Ukraine will strengthen their bilateral defence cooperation, ŌĆŗincluding by producing Ukrainian drones in the Nordic ŌĆīcountry, the Norwegian government said on Tuesday.
Under the agreement, Norway will support the production of drones in Ukraine, while ŌĆŗthe latter will share data, information and knowledge ŌĆŗwith Norway, Oslo said in a statement. Ukrainian ŌüĀdrones will also be produced on Norwegian territory, ŌĆŗit said.
"We can learn from the experiences that Ukraine ŌĆŗis making in this hard-won fight against the Russian aggression," Prime Minister Jonas Gahr Stoere told a joint press conference with ŌĆŗUkrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskiy.
"It is crucial that we ŌĆŗlearn from these experiences," he said.
Norway, along with the other ŌĆīNordic ŌüĀand Baltic countries, has been a staunch supporter of Ukraine.
Stoere's government has set up a long-term funding plan for Kyiv, with the backing of all parties ŌĆŗin parliament, ŌĆŗamounting to ŌüĀsome $28 billion between 2023 and 2030, making it one of Kyiv's most generous backers ŌĆŗper capita.
Earlier on Tuesday, Zelenskiy was in Berlin to ŌĆŗmeet ŌüĀGerman Chancellor Friedrich Merz. The pair agreed defence cooperation plans including a deal on drone production that Zelenskiy said ŌüĀcould ŌĆŗbecome one of the largest ŌĆŗof its kind in Europe.

item.title: War spurs EU plan for electricity tax cuts, faster shift from fossil fuels, draft shows
item.description:
BRUSSELS, April 14 (Reuters) - The European Union plans ŌĆŗto reduce electricity taxes and expand clean technologies faster to cut consumers' exposure to soaring ŌĆīoil and gas prices, as part of its plan to offset the Iran war's energy impact, a draft document showed.
Europe's heavy reliance on oil and gas imports has left it exposed to spiralling prices since the Strait of Hormuz, a vital global ŌĆŗoil and gas shipping route, was effectively closed and Tehran started attacking energy infrastructure in the Middle ŌĆŗEast.
The draft European Commission proposal, due to be published on 22 April, sets out ŌüĀshort-term measures aimed at curbing energy bills, as well as plans to wean Europe off fossil fuels ŌĆŗfaster, to protect the continent from future oil and gas supply disruptions.
"The benefits of this transition clearly outweigh its ŌĆŗcosts. Europe cannot afford to remain exposed to increasingly frequent energy shocks," the draft said. "Every delayed investment in the energy transition risks greater cost for society at a later stage."
The EU executive in May will propose legal changes to the bloc's taxation ŌĆŗrules that would ensure electricity is taxed at a rate below that of fossil fuels, and make ŌĆŗit easier for governments to cut energy-intensive industries' electricity taxes to zero, according to the draft.
It said the EU will also ŌĆīpresent ŌüĀcountries' energy ministers next month with a catalogue of energy-saving investments and low-carbon technologies to replace oil and gas, and a legal proposal requiring countries to incentivise investments in smart grid technologies, to help bring more clean energy sources into the power mix.
The draft was reported earlier on Tuesday by Bloomberg News.
European gas prices nearly ŌĆŗdoubled in the three weeks ŌĆŗafter the U.S.-Israeli war ŌüĀon Iran began on February 28, and despite retreating since, were still around 35% above pre-war levels on Tuesday.
Other elements of the draft EU plan, which could ŌĆŗchange before it is published, would see the EU step in, starting this ŌĆŗmonth, to coordinate ŌüĀcountries' gas storage filling, to avoid price hikes caused by companies rushing to buy at the same time.
Brussels also plans to propose an electrification target before summer, to push industries to switch from fossil fuels to electricity, the ŌüĀdraft said.
A ŌĆŗEuropean Commission spokesperson declined to comment on the draft document.
Changing EU ŌĆŗtax rules requires the unanimous approval of member nations, and recent attempts have failed. A 2021 Commission proposal to amend electricity taxes - ŌĆŗwhich also aimed to incentivise the shift from fossil fuels - is still stalled .

item.title: US solar cell maker Suniva investing $350 million in South Carolina expansion
item.description:
April 14 (Reuters) - U.S. solar manufacturer Suniva said on Tuesday it will invest $350 million in a South Carolina cell factory, with production starting in early 2027.
The ŌĆŗfacility will represent a substantial expansion of the United States' capacity ŌĆīto produce solar cells, the building blocks of solar panels that convert sunlight into electricity.
It is expected to open in the second quarter of 2027 and will create more ŌĆŗthan 550 jobs, Suniva said.
American-made solar panels and the components that go ŌĆŗinto them are in high demand due to new U.S. ŌüĀpolicies that restrict Chinese-owned factories and companies that use their products from ŌĆŗaccessing federal clean energy tax credits.
"When it comes to solar cells, there is ŌĆŗa dearth of those in the U.S.," Suniva President Matt Card said in an interview. "We've had a constant push from the market for more and more and more and more, ŌĆŗand that demand has been so high that it exceeded what we ŌĆŗcould do at our current facility."
Suniva, which is based in Atlanta, was the first company ŌĆīto resume ŌüĀU.S. commercial production of silicon-based solar cells in 2024 after the industry was paused for years due to competition from low-priced imports from Asia.
The Laurens County, South Carolina, facility will mark a major increase in Suniva's manufacturing capacity, which ŌĆŗwill go from ŌĆŗ1 GW currently ŌüĀto 5.5 GW once fully operational.
Without giving specifics, Card said a substantial portion of the factory's output through 2030 ŌĆŗhas been pre-sold.
The United States currently boasts just 3.2 GW ŌĆŗof total ŌüĀsolar cell capacity but 60 GW of module capacity, according to the Solar Energy Industries Association. That means that today, most solar cells that supply domestic ŌüĀmodule factories ŌĆŗare imported.
Other companies operating or planning domestic cell ŌĆŗcapacity include privately-held ES Foundry in South Carolina, Hanwha Corp's (000880.KS) , opens new tab Qcells in Georgia, T1 Energy (TE.N) , opens new tab in ŌĆŗTexas and Canadian Solar (CSIQ.O) , opens new tab in Indiana.

item.title: Williams CEO says PA-NY Constitution natgas pipe could be online as soon as 2027
item.description:
NEW YORK, April 14 (Reuters) - U.S. energy company Williams Cos (WMB.N) , opens new tab CEO Chad Zamarin said on Tuesday the long-delayed Constitution ŌĆŗnatural gas pipeline from Pennsylvania to New York could be ŌĆīoperational as soon as the end of 2027, if all goes as planned with the controversial project.
Zamarin made the comments in Brooklyn, New York, at the ŌĆŗgroundbreaking of another long-delayed gas pipe, the company's Northeast Supply Enhancement (NESE) ŌĆŗproject.
"We are working with the states to get final commercial ŌüĀagreements, so that's really the most important next step. If we ŌĆŗcan accomplish that within the next couple of months then Constitution ŌĆŗwill be online by the end of 2027," Zamarin said.
After years of fighting for permits, especially water permits, from state regulators, Williams canceled Constitution in 2020 and ŌĆŗNESE in 2024.
In May 2025, U.S. President Donald Trump's administration used ŌĆŗNew York's reconsideration of Williams' proposed gas pipes in the state as part of ŌĆīa ŌüĀdeal with New York Governor Kathy Hochul to lift a federal ban on the construction of Norwegian energy firm Equinor's (EQNR.OL) , opens new tab Empire Wind offshore wind farm off New York.
Hochul did not agree to approve either pipe project but ŌĆŗsaid the state ŌĆŗwould work ŌüĀwith the U.S. administration and private entities on projects that meet the legal requirements under New York law.
Constitution ŌĆŗis designed to move around 0.65 billion cubic ŌĆŗfeet per ŌüĀday of gas from Pennsylvania to New York.
NESE is designed to move around 0.4 bcfd of gas from Pennsylvania, across New Jersey and into ŌüĀNew ŌĆŗYork.
One billion cubic feet of gas is ŌĆŗenough to supply around five million U.S. homes for a day.

item.title: King Charles to head to US to woo Trump and restore 'closest of friendships'
item.description:
LONDON, April 14 (Reuters) - King Charles will have tea and a private meeting with President Donald Trump during his U.S. state visit later this month, as ŌĆŗthe British government hopes his "soft power" can heal a damaging rift between the allies caused by the Iran war.
The monarch and his ŌĆīwife Queen Camilla head across the Atlantic on April 27, a four-day trip ostensibly to mark the 250th anniversary of U.S. independence from Britain but one that now has far greater significance amid a growing spat between Trump and Prime Minister Keir Starmer.
"The visit ... recognises the challenges the United Kingdom, the United States, and our allies face across the world," a Buckingham Palace ŌĆŗspokesperson said on Tuesday. "This visit is a moment to reaffirm and renew our bilateral ties as we address those challenges together, in the ŌĆŗUK's national interest."
Unveiling details of the trip, the spokesperson said Trump and first lady Melania Trump would greet the ŌüĀking and queen in Washington with a private tea, followed by a ceremonial welcome at the White House, a state dinner, and a meeting between the ŌĆŗmonarch and president.
As previously announced, the king will also address Congress , becoming just the second British monarch to do so following his mother Queen Elizabeth in 1991. The ŌĆŗroyal couple will later visit New York where they will meet families of victims of the September 11, 2001, attacks.
The U.S. leg of their tour ends with a visit to Virginia, before Charles heads off to Bermuda, a British overseas territory where he is the head of state.
Amid the pomp of a royal state visit, the British government hopes the trip ŌĆŗwill demonstrate the value of what a foreign office spokesperson called "the closest of friendships".
That characterisation has come under strain since the U.S. and Israel launched an ŌĆŗattack on Iran in late February, with Trump frequently singling out Britain and Starmer personally for failing to provide active support for the offensive.
He has dismissed Starmer as "not Winston Churchill" and ŌĆīBritain's aircraft ŌüĀcarriers as "toys", although the president's affection for Charles and the British royals has remained unwavering.
Opinion polls show Trump is deeply unpopular in Britain, and some politicians have said the visit should be called off.
"(Trump) is a dangerous and corrupt gangster, and that is how we must treat him," Ed Davey, leader of the Liberal Democrat party, said in parliament on Monday.
"I really fear for what Trump might say or do while our king is forced to stand by his side. ŌĆŗWe cannot put His Majesty in that ŌĆŗposition."
In response, Starmer, who has sought ŌüĀto distance himself from the war but also avoid any direct rebuke of the president, reiterated the importance of the two countries' ties.
"The monarchy, through the bonds that it builds, is often able to reach through the decades on a ŌĆŗsituation like this," Starmer said.
One issue neither Trump nor the king will want to overshadow the visit is ŌĆŗdiscussion about Jeffrey Epstein.
Charles' ŌüĀbrother Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor is facing police scrutiny over his ties to the late disgraced U.S. financier while Melania Trump gave an extraordinary address last week to deny that she had had any relationship with Epstein.
Some U.S. lawmakers have said the king should meet with Epstein's victims , but notwithstanding Camilla's longtime support for causes which seek to end sexual and ŌüĀdomestic violence, a ŌĆŗpalace source said this would not be possible because it might impact on potential criminal ŌĆŗaction.
"We fully understand and appreciate the survivors' position," the source said.
"Even though the risk may be small that a meeting or any public comments could impact on those inquiries, or the proper course ŌĆŗof the law, that is a risk that we simply can't take, for the best interest for the survivors themselves."

item.title: Trading Day: Wall Street jumps on optimism over revived US-Iran talks, focus on earnings
item.description:
NEW YORK, April 14 - Stocks rallied and crude fell on Tuesday as the promise of ongoing talks between the United States and Iran boosted hopes of de-escalation in the Middle East, while corporate earnings took center stage.
And before you read on, don't forget to sign up , opens new tab for Jamie ŌĆŗMcGeever's upcoming LSEG webinar on April 23, where he'll talk about safe havens in uncertain times with his ROI colleague ŌĆīMike Dolan.
Here are a few articles I recommend to help you make sense of what happened in markets today.
Today's Key Market Moves
Today's Talking Points
* Bessent says core inflation cooling, rate cuts needed
U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent expressed confidence that core inflation would continue to come down despite the Iran war, and reiterated his ŌĆŗcall for the Federal Reserve to cut its key interest rate.
But two of the three major inflation indicators released for the month of March ŌĆŗso far show core inflation, which excludes food and energy items, ticking higher. The third, average hourly wage growth, registered a cooler reading than the previous month.
* But ŌĆīFed's Goolsbee ŌüĀsays rate cuts might have to wait
Chicago Federal Reserve President Austan Goolsbee said the central bank might need to put rate cuts on hold until 2027 if fallout from the war on Iran delays inflation's long meandering journey down to the Fed's average annual 2% growth rate.
At their last meeting monetary policymakers kept the key Fed funds target rate in the 3.50%-3.75% range, although a majority projected that at least one cut to that rate could ŌĆŗlikely be appropriate this year.
* Small ŌĆŗbusiness sentiment sours
U.S. small business ŌüĀsentiment slid to an 11-month low in March as surging oil prices overshadowed benefits from low taxes, according to the National Federation of Independent Business.
The index dipped below the NFIB's 52-year average, while its uncertainty component jumped four ŌĆŗpoints to 92, well above its historical average of 68. The share of survey participants expecting improved business ŌĆŗconditions sank to ŌüĀthe most pessimistic level since October 2024.
What could move markets tomorrow?
Want to receive ŌĆŗTrading Day in your inbox every weekday morning? Sign up for my newsletter here .
Opinions expressed are those of the author. They do not reflect the views of Reuters News, which, under the Trust ŌĆŗPrinciples , opens new tab , is committed to integrity, independence, and freedom from bias.

item.title: Venezuela's Rodriguez calls for US sanction removal, says licenses are not enough
item.description:
CARACAS, April 14 (Reuters) - Venezuela's interim President Delcy ŌĆŗRodriguez on Tuesday ŌĆīcalled again for U.S. sanctions on the ŌĆŗSouth American country ŌĆŗto be removed and ŌüĀsaid the ŌĆŗlicenses Washington has been ŌĆŗissuing to authorize transactions in the industry do ŌĆŗnot provide ŌĆŗlong-term legal security.
Rodriguez, who met ŌĆīU.S. ŌüĀAssistant Secretary of Energy Kyle Haustveit on Tuesday, said ŌĆŗshe ŌĆŗhas ŌüĀbeen listening to recommendations and ŌĆŗopinions from energy ŌĆŗexecutives ŌüĀon how to advance projects and ŌüĀregulation.

item.title: Algeria grants BP prospecting rights in eastern basin to boost exploration
item.description:
April 14 (Reuters) - Algeria has granted BP (BP.L) , opens new tab a new prospecting authorization in the country's eastern ŌĆŗbasin, the hydrocarbons regulator ALNAFT said ŌĆīon Tuesday, as the government seeks to boost exploration in underexplored regions.
A prospecting authorization ŌĆŗallows a company to perform preliminary ŌĆŗexploration activities, such as surveys and ŌüĀstudies, to assess resource potential. It ŌĆŗexcludes drilling or extraction, which require separate ŌĆŗpermits.
The authorization is part of ALNAFT's strategy to enhance Algeria's mining appeal and unlock new ŌĆŗexploration opportunities, the regulator said in ŌĆŗa statement.
Algeria's mining domain spans more than 1.7 million ŌĆīsquare ŌüĀkm (656 million square miles) and includes vast underexplored areas with significant potential, according to ALNAFT.
The initiative targets deploying advanced ŌĆŗexploration technologies ŌĆŗto enhance ŌüĀunderstanding of the eastern basin's subsurface and evaluate its energy ŌĆŗpotential, it said.
BP's return to ŌĆŗAlgeria "sends ŌüĀa strong signal of renewed confidence from international investors in the country's mining ŌüĀdomain ŌĆŗand in the partnership ŌĆŗframework promoted by ALNAFT," the statement said.

item.title: US Democrats will try, and try again, to rein in Trump's Iran war powers
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 14 (Reuters) - The U.S. Senate will vote ŌĆŗas soon as Wednesday on the latest Democratic-led effort to rein in President Donald Trump's war powers, and party ŌĆīleaders promised on Tuesday to keep bringing up such resolutions as long as the Iran war continues.
"Forty-five days into this war, Congress has been sidelined because our Republican colleagues refuse to take a strong stand against this war and duck it completely because they're afraid of Trump," Democratic Leader Chuck Schumer of New York ŌĆŗsaid in a Senate speech on Tuesday.
Trump said on Tuesday talks to end the Iran war could resume in Pakistan over ŌĆŗthe next two days, after the collapse of weekend negotiations prompted Washington to impose a blockade on Iranian ŌüĀports. Failure to reach an agreement in those talks raised doubts over the survival of a two-week ceasefire that still has a week ŌĆŗto run.
Congressional Democrats have tried and repeatedly failed in recent months to pass war powers resolutions to force Trump to stop military action and ŌĆŗobtain lawmakers' authorization before launching military operations, in both Venezuela and Iran.
Democrats are attempting to link their efforts to rein in Trump on Iran to affordability, as disruptions in shipments of oil and natural gas have caused a run-up in U.S. gasoline prices and agricultural products such as fertilizers - on top of the long ŌĆŗlist of other high consumer prices.
Few issues resonate with U.S. voters more deeply than price increases, and the latest inflationary upswing is unsettling Republican ŌĆŗinsiders worried about their party's prospects less than seven months before November elections that will determine control of Congress.
Schumer said 10 ŌĆīmore ŌüĀwar powers resolutions have been filed and Democrats intended to bring them up every week while the conflict in Iran, which began on February 28, continues.
Trump's fellow Republicans, who hold slim majorities in both the Senate and House of Representatives, have blocked the resolutions that have come up to date and there has been no indication that any are shifting their position.
Republican lawmakers say they support Trump's actions and do not ŌĆŗexpect the war to continue for ŌĆŗmuch longer. "The military effort here ŌüĀhas been extraordinarily successful," Senate Republican leader John Thune of South Dakota told a news conference.
"I think the administration has a clear objective, a clear plan, and if they can execute on it that question (of ŌĆŗwhether Congress should authorize a prolonged conflict) won't be a necessary one that we will be forced ŌĆŗto answer," Thune ŌüĀsaid.
Although the U.S. Constitution says that Congress, not the president, can declare war, that restriction does not apply for short-term operations or if the country faces an immediate threat.
The White House says Trump's actions are legal and within his rights as commander-in-chief to protect the U.S. by ordering limited ŌüĀmilitary operations.
Timing ŌĆŗof the vote had not been announced by Tuesday evening, but Senate aides said ŌĆŗthey expected the next resolution - sponsored by Senator Tammy Duckworth of Illinois, a combat veteran - to come to the floor as soon as Wednesday.
House of Representatives aides said they ŌĆŗexpected a vote on a similar Iran war powers resolution in that chamber as soon as Thursday.

item.title: Canada oil and gas profits to surge on Iran war, but firms hold off new investment
item.description:
CALGARY, April 14 (Reuters) - Canadian oil and ŌĆŗgas producers anticipate sharply higher profits in 2026 as prices surge due to the Iran war but will ŌĆīchannel those earnings back to shareholders rather than invest in major new capital projects, senior executives said on Tuesday.
At an annual conference in Toronto, the executives acknowledged their companies' financial fortunes have changed dramatically in the wake of the Middle East conflict that has disrupted global oil ŌĆŗand gas supplies and sent global crude benchmarks Brent and West Texas Intermediate soaring.
But in Canada, the world's fourth-largest ŌĆŗoil producer, the commodity price shock will not translate into a dramatic uptick in drilling ŌüĀor a quick sanctioning of a new oil sands project, CEOs said. They pointed to uncertainty about how long the ŌĆŗhigh prices will last as well as ongoing concern about perceived regulatory and policy barriers in the country.
"We are a commodity-based ŌĆŗbusiness. When we see global prices rise for energy, we participate in that," said Jon McKenzie, CEO of Cenovus Energy (CVE.TO) , opens new tab , one of Canada's largest oil sands producers, in an interview.
"But I don't think it's going to have any strategic or long-term impacts on anybody's operating plans at ŌĆŗthis point."
Many Canadian energy companies went into 2026 anticipating WTI prices would average around $60 a barrel, ŌĆŗbut since the war began, prices have surged into the $90 to $100 a barrel range.
For producers, this will mean a "massive" change in profitability compared ŌĆīto 2025, ŌüĀsaid Mike Verney, executive vice president at consultancy McDaniel & Associates.
"Our cash flow was going to be at about C$650 million ($472.21 million)," said Brian Schmidt, CEO of Tamarack Valley Energy (TVE.TO) , opens new tab . "And what we're forecasting now, it'll probably be somewhere around C$1 billion."
But Schmidt said unless a new crude export pipeline is built in Canada, the country's oil producers cannot significantly increase their output because existing ŌĆŗpipeline capacity is nearly maxed out .
Executives ŌĆŗalso expressed uncertainty about their ŌüĀability to grow unless the Canadian and Alberta governments reach an agreement with industry soon on industrial carbon pricing , a policy that Canada's oilpatch has said threatens its competitiveness globally.
The Iran ŌĆŗwar has changed Tourmaline Oil's (TOU.TO) , opens new tab cash flow expectations for this year and likely for next ŌĆŗyear as well, ŌüĀsaid Jamie Heard, the company's vice president, capital markets.
While those cash flows are not yet expected to reach 2022 levels ŌĆö when the Canadian industry as a whole reaped record profits due to the Ukraine war and resulting global commodity shock ŌĆö they could creep ŌüĀclose to ŌĆŗthat range, he said.
Tourmaline will return the bulk of its Iran-related profits ŌĆŗto shareholders, Heard said, possibly in the form of a special dividend.
"But we understand war premiums to be fickle and so we want to earn those ŌĆŗcash flows before we announce new allocations," he added.
($1 = 1.3765 Canadian dollars)

item.title: Female Iranian student arrives in Iran after release in France, state TV reports
item.description:
