April 15 (Reuters) - Iranian student Mahdieh ŌĆŗEsfandiari arrived in Iran ŌĆīafter being released in France, Iranian state TV reported ŌĆŗon Wednesday, after ŌĆŗtwo French nationals facing security charges ŌüĀwere allowed to ŌĆŗleave Iran following three-and-a-half years ŌĆŗin detention.
Esfandiari, who was convicted at the end of February ŌĆŗfor glorifying terrorism ŌĆŗin anti-Israel social media posts, was ŌĆīreleased ŌüĀafter serving almost a year in prison.
"I think it's clear for everyone ŌĆŗthat ŌĆŗthere ŌüĀis no freedom of speech, at least ŌĆŗnot in France ŌĆŗwhere ŌüĀI was. The court's ruling was very unjust," ŌüĀEsfandiari ŌĆŗtold state television.

item.title: Vice President Vance says US-Iran mistrust cannot be solved overnight
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 14 (Reuters) - U.S. Vice President JD Vance said on Tuesday there ŌĆŗwas a lot of mistrust between Washington and ŌĆīTehran that cannot be resolved overnight but he added that Iranian negotiators wanted to make a deal and that ŌĆŗhe felt "very good about where we are."
Talks to ŌĆŗend the Iran war could resume in Pakistan ŌüĀover the next two days, President Donald Trump said ŌĆŗon Tuesday, after the collapse of weekend negotiations ŌĆŗprompted Washington to impose a blockade on Iranian ports.
A fragile two-week ceasefire between the U.S. and Iran still has a week ŌĆŗto run. Vance was involved in the talks ŌĆŗlast weekend in Pakistan.
"There is a lot of, of course, ŌĆīmistrust ŌüĀbetween Iran and the United States of America. You are not going to solve that problem overnight," Vance said during a Turning Point USA event.
Iranian negotiators ŌĆŗwanted to make ŌĆŗa deal, ŌüĀhe said. "I feel very good about where we are," Vance added.
The Iran war ŌĆŗbegan when the U.S. and Israel ŌĆŗattacked Iran ŌüĀon February 28. Iran responded with its own strikes on Israel and Gulf states that host U.S. ŌüĀbases. ŌĆŗU.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran and Israeli ŌĆŗattacks in Lebanon have killed thousands and displaced millions .

item.title: World Bank could provide up to $100 billion in funds for countries hit by war, Banga says
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 14 (Reuters) - The World Bank could mobilize $80 billion to $100 billion in funding over ŌĆīthe next 15 months for countries hit hard by the war in the Middle East, eclipsing the $70 billion it provided during the COVID pandemic, the bank's president, Ajay Banga, said on Tuesday.
That would include $20 billion to $25 billion in coming months through a crisis response ŌĆŗwindow that allows countries to withdraw up to 10% of funds earlier than planned from previously approved ŌĆŗprograms, with another $30 billion to $40 billion that could come from repurposing existing programs in ŌüĀabout six months, he said.
Banga's comments, on the sidelines of the spring meetings of the International Monetary Fund and ŌĆŗWorld Bank, reflect growing recognition of the huge impact the war is already having on global growth and inflation, ŌĆŗwith developing countries likely to be hit the hardest.
The IMF on Tuesday cut its global growth outlook due to war-driven energy price spikes, offering a range of scenarios that all include lower growth and higher inflation. Absent the conflict, the IMF said it would have upgraded ŌĆŗits growth outlook by 0.1 percentage point to 3.4%.
If the war lasted longer and greater needs emerged, the ŌĆŗbank would have to turn to its balance sheet and headroom to find additional funding to reach the $80 billion to $100 billion, Banga ŌĆītold ŌüĀan event hosted by the Bretton Woods Committee. That would come on top of the bank's normal lending.
"I'm trying to create a toolkit that has a tiered response capacity, depending on how this continues, to at least be able to bring adequate firepower to do something about it," he said.
Banga, who met with the head of the International ŌĆŗEnergy Agency and IMF chief ŌĆŗKristalina Georgieva on Monday, ŌüĀstressed that it would take time for the energy market to settle down, even if the war ended and there was no more structural damage to energy infrastructure.
The global ŌĆŗeconomy can still recover rapidly from the shock of the Middle East war if ŌĆŗthe conflict ends ŌüĀin the next weeks, but the situation will be worse if it drags through the summer, Georgieva said in separate remarks to the same event.
Georgieva said the International Monetary Fund was in talks with countries hit hard by higher energy ŌüĀprices and ŌĆŗsupply chain disruptions to discuss their financial needs.
Both Banga and Georgieva ŌĆŗurged countries to focus on narrowly targeted and temporary measures to ease the pain of higher energy prices, and to avoid broader energy subsidies ŌĆŗthat could wind up further stoking inflation.

item.title: US military says strike in eastern Pacific kills four men
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 14 (Reuters) - The U.S. military said on Tuesday ŌĆŗit carried ŌĆŗout a strike ŌüĀon a vessel ŌĆŗin the ŌĆŗeastern Pacific, killing four men.
The strike comes ŌĆŗa day ŌĆŗafter the U.S. military ŌĆīsaid ŌüĀthat a strike, also in the eastern Pacific, ŌĆŗhad ŌĆŗkilled ŌüĀtwo men.
The Trump administration ŌĆŗhas been ŌĆŗtargeting ŌüĀvessels suspected of transporting narcotics ŌüĀwith ŌĆŗmilitary strikes.

item.title: Erika Kirk withdraws from event with JD Vance over threats, VP says
item.description:
April 14 (Reuters) - Erika Kirk , widow of slain activist Charlie Kirk , withdrew on Tuesday from ŌĆŗa Turning Point USA event featuring U.S. Vice ŌĆīPresident JD Vance due to threats on her life, the vice president said.
"I know that she did get some ŌĆŗthreats," Vance told an audience of University of ŌĆŗGeorgia students in Athens, Georgia. "I was a little ŌüĀworried that we were going to have to ŌĆŗcancel the event because Erika was not going to ŌĆŗcome, and she was very worried about it."
Vance said he had talked to the Secret Service and was not concerned about ŌĆŗhis own safety. He gave no details about ŌĆŗthe threats to Erika Kirk, who is the CEO of Turning ŌĆīPoint ŌüĀUSA.
The Secret Service and Turning Point did not immediately respond to requests for comment.
A gunman, who faces murder charges , killed Charlie Kirk in September during a campus ŌĆŗspeaking event ŌĆŗat Utah ŌüĀValley University in Orem, Utah. Authorities describe the shooting as politically motivated. Kirk co-founded ŌĆŗTurning Point USA to galvanize young voters ŌĆŗfor ŌüĀconservative causes.
The killing intensified debate over political violence , opens new tab in the United States, prompting condemnation from officials across the political ŌüĀspectrum ŌĆŗand leading to increased security ŌĆŗat subsequent campus events involving high-profile political figures.

item.title: NAACP sues Musk's xAI, alleging illegal operation of gas turbines
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 14 (Reuters) - The largest U.S. civil rights group on Tuesday sued xAI and a subsidiary, claiming they illegally operated more than two dozen gas turbines ŌĆŗin Mississippi to power its Colossus 2 data center, posing a ŌĆīhealth risk to local residents.
The NAACP, represented by Earthjustice and the Southern Environmental Law Center, sued xAI and subsidiary MZX Tech, charging they violated the federal Clean Air Act ŌĆŗby running 27 gas-fired turbines before getting necessary air permits for its ŌĆŗmassive data center that powers xAI's Grok chatbot.
Elon Musk's artificial ŌüĀintelligence startup xAI has invested more than $20 billion to build the data center ŌĆŗin Southaven with the full backing of Mississippi Governor Tate Reeves, but the ŌĆŗfacility, as well as Colossus 1 just over the border in Memphis, Tennessee, has met heavy opposition from local communities due to their effect on local air and environmental quality.
"By ŌĆŗlooking to evade clean air laws to operate dirty turbines that emit ŌĆŗpollution and known carcinogens, these companies are following a shameful, familiar pattern: asking Black and ŌĆīfrontline communities ŌüĀto bear the toxic brunt of ŌĆśinnovation,ŌĆÖŌĆØ said AbreŌĆÖ Conner, director of the Center for Environmental and Climate Justice at the NAACP.
The NAACP announced its intention to sue xAI and MZX in February because the Clean Air Act requires ŌĆŗ60 days of notice ŌĆŗahead of filing ŌüĀa lawsuit.
Mississippi regulators held one public hearing that month about permits for those turbines after just a few days ŌĆŗof public notice for the hearing, and subsequently approved the ŌĆŗpermits.
xAI was ŌüĀnot immediately available for comment.
Earthjustice said that xAIŌĆÖs Southaven power plant has the potential to emit more than 1,700 tons of smog-causing nitrogen oxides (NOx) each year, a ŌüĀmajor ŌĆŗsource of smog in the greater Memphis area. ŌĆŗThey are also estimated to emit 180 tons of fine particulate matter, 500 tons of carbon monoxide, ŌĆŗand 19 tons of cancer-causing formaldehyde.

item.title: US destroyer interdicts two oil tankers attempting to leave Iran, official says
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 14 (Reuters) - A U.S. destroyer interdicted ŌĆŗtwo oil tankers attempting to leave Iran on Tuesday, a day after U.S. President Donald Trump's blockade went into ŌĆīeffect, and instructed them to turn around, a U.S. official said, speaking on condition of anonymity.
The ships had left Chabahar port on the Gulf of Oman and were contacted by the warship via radio communication, the official said. It was unclear whether any further warnings were given.
The disclosure adds further detail to the start ŌĆŗof Trump's blockade, which aims to pressure Iran to end its effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz, a choke ŌĆŗpoint for about 20% of the world's oil.
Trump is hoping the blockade will force Iran to accept America's terms ŌüĀfor ending a war launched by the U.S. and Israel on February 28, including opening up the Strait of Hormuz. Trump says ŌĆŗthat was also a condition of a week-old ceasefire with Iran due to expire next week.
Experts are cautious. Noam Raydan at The Washington Institute ŌĆŗfor Near East Policy said tracking data did show one tanker making a U-turn after the start of the blockade but cautioned that a lot of ships working with Iranian oil go dark.
"We just don't know yet how effective it is. We are still in day two," Raydan said.
The U.S. official said the two ŌĆŗtankers were among the six merchant vessels the U.S. Central Command said in a statement earlier on Tuesday had followed orders to "turn around to ŌĆŗre-enter an Iranian port on the Gulf of Oman."
Central Command said no ships have made it past the blockade since it went into effect on ŌĆīMonday at ŌüĀ10 a.m. in Washington (1400 GMT).
The blockade is a massive undertaking involving more than 10,000 U.S. forces, over a dozen warships and dozens of aircraft, the U.S. military says.
The U.S. military says it will support freedom of navigation for vessels transiting the Strait of Hormuz, as long as they are not going to or from Iran.
Trump announced the blockade following the breakdown of weekend talks to end the ŌĆŗwar. Oil prices jumped back above $100 a ŌĆŗbarrel before easing on Tuesday ŌüĀon hopes of further talks.
If Trump's strategy succeeds, he would eliminate Iran's greatest point of leverage in negotiations with the U.S. and clear the strait again for global trade. But a blockade, experts say, is ŌĆŗan act of war that requires an open-ended commitment of a significant number of warships.
It could also ŌĆŗtrigger fresh retaliation ŌüĀfrom Tehran and put tremendous strain on an already fragile ceasefire.
Iran's threats to shipping have caused global oil prices to skyrocket about 50%. Roughly 5,000 people have died in the hostilities.
Thousands of U.S. military strikes have severely weakened Iran's military. But analysts say Tehran has emerged from the conflict ŌüĀas a ŌĆŗvexing problem for Washington, with a more hard-line leadership and a buried stockpile of ŌĆŗhighly enriched uranium.
Raydan said to expect likely Iranian retaliation if the blockade succeeds and lasts for an extended period, noting Iranian threats to strike Gulf states that host ŌĆŗU.S. forces and Iran's past attacks on ships.
"We're in the testing period," Raydan said.

item.title: US alleges well-known Mexican human rights activist works for drug cartel
item.description:
MEXICO CITY, April 14 (Reuters) - The U.S. on Tuesday imposed sanctions on a well-known human rights activist in Mexico who has for years levied charges of human ŌĆŗrights abuses against the country's Armed Forces, alleging he worked on behalf ŌĆīof a powerful drug cartel.
The U.S. Treasury Department accused Raymundo Ramos, president of the Committee for Human Rights of Nuevo Laredo, of "posing as a 'human rights' activist" for more than a decade in order ŌĆŗto fabricate false accusations against the Army and protect members of the ŌĆŗpowerful Cartel of the Northeast (CDN).
"On the CDN payroll, Ramos engages in these ŌüĀactivities with the goal of boosting the public opinion of CDN and discrediting ŌĆŗMexican authoritiesŌĆÖ law enforcement initiatives against the cartel," the Treasury Department said in a statement.
Washington's ŌĆŗmove blocks any assets Ramos might have in the United States and bars U.S. persons from working with him.
Ramos did not immediately respond to ReutersŌĆÖ requests for comment about the accusations. Based in ŌĆŗNuevo Laredo, an ultra-violent city in the state of Tamaulipas, bordering the U.S., ŌĆŗRamos has long been one of the most high-profile human rights activists operating in the area.
In 2023, ŌĆīRamos ŌüĀraised the alarm over alleged extrajudicial killings committed by the Armed Forces and publicized video of Army members shooting and killing five young men in a pickup truck, including one with a shot to the back of the neck. A year earlier, he ŌĆŗaccused members of ŌĆŗthe Navy of forcibly ŌüĀdisappearing civilians.
In both instances, Mexican authorities detained military officials and carried out investigations. It's unclear whether the investigations resulted in formal ŌĆŗcharges or convictions.
The Mexican attorney general's office did not respond to ŌĆŗa request ŌüĀfor comment about the accusations against Ramos or whether it is independently investigating him.
In 2020, the Mexican government targeted Ramos' phone with the Pegasus spyware program, according to MexicoŌĆÖs ŌüĀDigital Rights ŌĆŗDefense Network and the Toronto-based digital watchdog Citizen ŌĆŗLab. He was among hundreds of Mexican activists and journalists spied on by the government using the spyware, ŌĆŗthe groups said.

item.title: Canada, UK and other nations condemn killings of UN peacekeepers in Lebanon
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 14 (Reuters) - Canada, ŌĆŗthe UK and other ŌĆīcountries condemned the killings of UN peacekeepers in Lebanon in ŌĆŗthe conflict between Israel ŌĆŗand the Hezbollah and called ŌüĀfor an end to ŌĆŗhostilities.
ŌĆ£Australia, Brazil, Canada, Colombia, Indonesia, ŌĆŗJapan, Jordan, Sierra Leone, Switzerland and the United Kingdom remain deeply ŌĆŗconcerned by the worsening ŌĆŗhumanitarian situation and displacement crisis in Lebanon," ŌĆīthe ŌüĀcountries said in a joint statement issued on Tuesday.
The statement comes after the ŌĆŗdeaths of ŌĆŗthree ŌüĀpeacekeepers last month that the UN said ŌĆŗin preliminary findings were due ŌĆŗto ŌüĀan Israeli tank projectile and an improvised explosive device ŌüĀmost ŌĆŗlikely placed ŌĆŗby Hezbollah.

item.title: Oil prices fall for a second day on expectations US-Iran talks may resume
item.description:
TOKYO, April 15 (Reuters) - Oil prices fell for a second day ŌĆīon Wednesday on expectations peace talks between the U.S. and Iran may resume and eventually release supply from the key Middle East producing region trapped by the closure of the Strait of Hormuz.
Brent crude futures fell 52 cents, or 0.55%, ŌĆŗto $94.27 a barrel at 0054 GMT after falling 4.6% in the previous session. U.S. West Texas ŌĆŗIntermediate crude was down $1.04, or 1.1%, to $90.24 after dropping 7.9% the session before.
Talks ŌüĀto end the war between the U.S. and Israel and Iran could resume in Pakistan over the ŌĆŗnext two days, U.S. President Donald Trump said on Tuesday, after the collapse of negotiations over the weekend prompted ŌĆŗWashington to impose a blockade on Iranian ports. This has increased optimism talks could eventually settle the conflict and open up crude oil and fuel flows.
The war has shut the Strait of Hormuz, a key waterway for crude and refined ŌĆŗproduct flows out of the Gulf to global buyers, particularly in Asia and Europe.
Despite a two-week ceasefire, ŌĆŗtransit through the strait remains uncertain, with traffic at only a fraction of the 130 or so vessels that moved ŌĆīthrough ŌüĀthe waterway before the war, sources said on Tuesday.
A U.S. destroyer stopped two oil tankers from leaving Iran on Tuesday, a U.S. official said.
"While diplomatic headlines suggest the possibility of renewed U.S.-Iran talks and even a temporary easing of transit restrictions, the physical reality remains fragmented," the Schork Group said in a note.
"The result is ŌĆŗa market that continues to ŌĆŗprice optionality around flow ŌüĀdisruption rather than a return to equilibrium."
The market stands to lose some access to further supply after two U.S. administration officials told Reuters on Tuesday the U.S. ŌĆŗwill not renew a 30-day waiver of sanctions on Iranian oil at sea ŌĆŗthat expires ŌüĀthis week, and quietly let a similar waiver on sanctions on Russian oil expire over the weekend.
Later in the day, markets will be watching for official U.S. inventory data from the Energy Information Administration due at 10:30 ŌüĀa.m. ET (1430 ŌĆŗGMT).
U.S. crude oil stockpiles were expected to have risen slightly ŌĆŗlast week, while distillate and gasoline inventories likely fell, a Reuters poll showed.
Market sources familiar with American Petroleum Institute figures said on Tuesday U.S. ŌĆŗcrude oil inventories jumped for the third straight week.

item.title: Germany must brace for prolonged Iran war energy shock, says finance minister
item.description:
BERLIN, April 14 (Reuters) - Germany must assume that ŌĆŗthe energy price shock caused by the Iran war will persist, leaving the ŌĆīeconomic situation extremely fragile, Finance Minister Lars Klingbeil said on Tuesday before travelling to the International Monetary Fund spring meetings .
The IMF cut Germany's growth forecasts for this year and next on Tuesday, projecting ŌĆŗgrowth rates of 0.8% in 2026 and 1.2% in 2027, down 0.3 percentage ŌĆŗpoints from its previous forecasts for both years.
Klingbeil said he will ŌüĀdiscuss with finance ministers and international organizations which measures are best suited to ŌĆŗstabilize the economy and markets, as well as to provide targeted support to people ŌĆŗand businesses that have been hit particularly hard.
"This crisis once again shows that we must become more independent, more crisis-proof and more resilient," he said.
He added that he will use the trip ŌĆŗto Washington to present the German government's reform agenda and promote investment in ŌĆŗEurope's biggest economy.
Development Minister Reem Alabali Radovan, who will accompany Klingbeil on his visit to Washington, ŌĆīcalled ŌüĀfor an end to the war.
"The international community must not hesitate now: The world needs peace, stability and an end to the violence," she said. "Every day counts."
Germany and Ukraine agreed on defence cooperation plans during a visit to Berlin ŌĆŗby Ukrainian President Volodymyr ŌĆŗZelenskiy and his ŌüĀdefence minister on Tuesday.
"The fact that the world is now focused on the Middle East must not lead to Ukraine being ŌĆŗforgotten," Klingbeil said. "It continues its courageous fight for freedom and ŌĆŗdemocracy."
He added ŌüĀthat he and his Norwegian counterpart, Jens Stoltenberg, have invited the largest donor countries to a meeting in Washington to discuss how to maintain financial support for Ukraine.
Germany is ŌüĀEurope's ŌĆŗlargest provider of military aid to Kyiv. It ŌĆŗhas delivered about 55 billion euros ($64 billion) since Russia's invasion of Ukraine in 2022 and has set aside ŌĆŗ11.5 billion euros in the current budget.

item.title: ExxonMobil, Energean to sign contract for first test gas drilling off Greece in years
item.description:
April 14 (Reuters) - U.S. oil major ExxonMobil (XOM.N) , opens new tab , Energean (ENOG.L) , opens new tab and Helleniq Energy (HEPr.AT) , opens new tab will sign ŌĆŗa contract on Wednesday with ŌĆīStena Drilling to start exploratory drilling in a western Greek offshore block, Greece's ŌĆŗenergy ministry said on Tuesday.
ŌĆŗExxonMobil, Energean and Greece's biggest oil ŌüĀrefiner Helleniq announced an agreement last ŌĆŗyear to explore for natural gas ŌĆŗin Block 2 in the Ionian Sea.
The consortium will proceed with exploratory drilling in ŌĆŗFebruary 2027, a Greek energy ŌĆŗministry official said on condition of anonymity, ŌĆīthe ŌüĀcountry's first offshore exploration drilling in 40 years.
Stena will use a high-performance vessel able to operate in deep-sea ŌĆŗwaters and ŌĆŗdrill ŌüĀat a depth of up to 35,000 feet to ŌĆŗlook for deposits once considered ŌĆŗhard ŌüĀto access, the official said.
Europe is attempting to diversify its energy ŌüĀsources ŌĆŗas it seeks to ŌĆŗreplace Russian gas following Russia's invasion of Ukraine .

item.title: A shipment of Austrian timber and its tortuous new route to Qatar
item.description:
DOHA, April 15 (Reuters) - Until the Iran war , shipments of Austrian spruce timber to Qatar, where the wood is used to support concrete and make basic frames on construction sites, were a matter of routine.
The standard 2x4, as it is known in the building trade, was typically sourced from Austria ŌĆŗin Europe, shipped to DubaiŌĆÖs Jebel Ali port, transferred to a feeder vessel and delivered to QatarŌĆÖs Hamad Port in about 45 days.
It must now be offloaded, trucked overland and reloaded onto ŌĆīnew ships, adding thousands of dollars in costs and months to delivery times, as the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz has caused unprecedented disruption to oil and other trade.
Anything from medicine to basic foodstuff and the 2x4 beams of spruce, referred to as white wood, are caught in the upheaval.
The two-inch (5 cm) by four-inch beams of white wood that come in various lengths are not a strategic resource, but any shortage would slow activity in the construction sector and drive up costs.
A building materials supplier ŌĆŗin Qatar, who shared details with Reuters on condition of anonymity, said that when the U.S.-Israeli airstrikes were launched on Iran on February 28, unleashing a new phase of Middle Eastern conflict, he had 17 containers ŌĆŗof white wood on their way.
Each container holds around 2,850 beams of Austrian spruce, worth about 15,000 euros ($17,702).
The cargo left the Croatian port of Rijeka as planned, but instead ŌüĀof sailing to Jebel Ali, was diverted to Khor Fakkan on the UAEŌĆÖs east coast, avoiding a transit of the Strait of Hormuz that would have been required to reach Jebel Ali.
After that, the timber was trucked to ŌĆŗAbu Dhabi and reloaded onto feeder vessels bound for Doha. The detour added a surcharge of about $3,600 per container - some shippers quoted the supplier surcharges as high as $5,000 per container - more than triple the normal cost of shipping a 40-foot ŌĆŗcontainer of timber from Europe to Qatar, the supplier said.
Even after being rerouted, the cargo has yet to arrive in Qatar and delivery is expected to take another one to two months.
At the same time, several containers of plywood that the supplier had ordered were loaded onto feeder vessels at Jebel Ali and spent weeks at sea before being returned to port, underscoring how importers lose control over shipments once they are on the water.
Before the conflict, the supplier said he would sell a standard 2x4 for ŌĆŗaround QAR 23ŌĆō25 ($6.30ŌĆō$6.90) per piece. Additional costs caused by rerouting and longer transit have pushed his selling price to QAR 35-37 ($9.60ŌĆō$10.20) per piece.
Future shipments could be more expensive. Routing timber via Saudi ArabiaŌĆÖs Red Sea port of Jeddah, which ŌĆŗis a possibility under consideration, would involve higher shipping charges and trucking 1,500 kilometres across the Arabian Peninsula to Qatar, further raising per-piece costs.
Other supply chains are equally disrupted.
Before the Iran war broke out, logistics firm Geodis had planned to fly medicine from ŌĆīthe UK to ŌüĀDubai in about four days. Now the journey traversing land and sea is expected to take about 40 days.
For a container of onions travelling from the western shores of India to a warehouse in Dubai, a week-long journey is now a three-week ordeal at twice the cost, according to Ravi Punjabi, Managing Director at Avalon General Land Transport, a UAE-based logistics company.
Dubai and the wider United Arab Emirates, which have built their economies on being a regional hub for tourism and trade, have the advantage of ports - Fujairah and Khor Fakkan - on the Arabian Sea outside the Gulf.
For other countries in the region, the issue is much worse, notably Qatar, Bahrain and Kuwait that depend on ŌĆŗthe Gulf and the Strait of Hormuz.
Governments across the Gulf ŌĆŗhave sought to coordinate to ease the bottlenecks.
During ŌüĀa meeting with his counterparts last month, Saudi ArabiaŌĆÖs Minister of Transport Saleh bin Nasser Al-Jasser announced measures, including allowing empty refrigerated trucks from other Gulf countries to enter the kingdom and create shared storage and redistribution zones at King Abdulaziz Port in Dammam.
Dubai has also activated what it described as a green corridor with Oman, allowing goods diverted to ŌĆŗOmani ports to be trucked directly to the UAE with expedited customs clearance and facilitating the export of goods from the UAE to global markets via Omani ports.
The ŌĆŗsolutions are imperfect, however, and ŌüĀtransport executives say flows into Dubai and onward to other Gulf capitals are likely to remain slower and more expensive.
Prices for food items, personal care products and industrial supplies have already risen by 5% to 10% in parts of the region since the end of February, with further increases possible if shipping disruptions persist.
Geodis executive Eric Martin-Neuville said items, including some medicines and food that have to be kept cool, were vulnerable.
ŌĆ£You have only so many plugs for electricity, so you can only accommodate so ŌüĀmany containers in ŌĆŗthe port,ŌĆØ he said.
The uncertainty adds to headaches for businesses that have previously experienced disruption that has endured.
While 45 days to ship white ŌĆŗwood from Europe was standard before February, there was a time before the Red Sea Houthi attacks began in 2023 that shipment took only around a month. Even though the attacks have eased, most shippers detour around the Cape of Good Hope.
In Qatar, the building materials supplier said he ŌĆŗhas enough white wood stock for several months, but must soon place new orders without knowing which routes will be available or at what cost.
($1 = 0.8474 euros)

item.title: Rattled by Trump, US allies eye Japan's biggest arms opening since WW2
item.description:
TOKYO, April 15 (Reuters) - Japan's imminent easing of arms export rules has sparked strong interest from Warsaw to Manila, Reuters reporting found, as President Donald Trump wavers on security commitments to allies and the wars in Iran and Ukraine strain U.S. weapons supplies.
Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi's ruling party approved the changes this ŌĆŗweek as she tries to invigorate the pacifist country's military industrial base. Her government will formally adopt the new rules as soon as this month, three Japanese government officials told Reuters.
Despite largely isolating itself from global arms markets since World War Two, Japan spends enough ŌĆīon its own military - $60 billion this year - to sustain a sizeable defence industry capable of manufacturing advanced systems like submarines and fighter jets.
Among the potential new customers are the Polish military and the Philippine navy, which are undergoing modernization amid regional security challenges, according to Reuters interviews with Japanese officials and foreign diplomats in Tokyo. Defence contractors Toshiba and Mitsubishi Electric (6503.T) , opens new tab are hiring staff and adding capacity to capitalise on demand, their executives said, providing previously unreported details.
One of the first deals Takaichi's government will likely approve are exports of used frigates to the Philippines, which is locked in maritime confrontation with Beijing in the South China Sea, according to two of the Japanese officials. Reuters is the first to report the timeframe of the likely sale, which ŌĆŗmay be followed by missile defence systems, the officials said.
Warsaw and Tokyo can help plug gaps in each other's arsenals, cooperating in areas like anti-drone and electronic warfare systems, said Mariusz Boguszewski, deputy chief of mission at Poland's embassy in Japan.
"There are some bottlenecks that we can overcome having ŌĆŗJapan on board," he added, without providing details of specific deals. Poland's WB Group, one of Europe's largest private defence contractors, last year signed a tentative drone deal with Japanese aircraft maker ShinMaywa (7224.T) , opens new tab .
Three other European diplomats said Japan's easing ŌüĀprovided a chance to lessen their heavy dependence on U.S. weapons production, which is strained by conflicts . Trump's unpredictability, such as his threats to leave the NATO security alliance and invade Greenland, have also heightened the push to diversify, according to the diplomats, who requested anonymity to discuss sensitive matters.
"Offers are coming from everywhere," ŌĆŗsaid Masahiko Arai, senior vice president at Mitsubishi Electric's defence unit, which has been adding staff in London and Singapore to facilitate defence exports.
Takaichi's office declined to answer specific questions for this story, instead referring Reuters to a February 20 speech where she said she was reviewing the controls to bolster Japan's defence production ŌĆŗand strengthen capabilities of allies.
Tokyo's export overhaul has previously been encouraged by successive U.S. administrations, including Trump's, eager for allies to contribute more to collective defence efforts.
White House spokeswoman Anna Kelly did not respond to questions from Reuters about the changes to Japanese policy but said that the two nations were closer than ever under Trump and Takaichi.
China's foreign ministry did not immediately respond to questions about Japanese frigates potentially being sent to the Philippines. Ministry spokeswoman Mao Ning told reporters in April that Beijing was concerned about changes in Tokyo's arms export policy and that it should "act prudently in military and security areas."
The Philippines defence ministry declined to comment.
Japan's first steps to relax the rules began more than a decade ago when Takaichi's ŌĆŗmentor, the late premier Shinzo Abe, eased a near-blanket ban on exports to encourage joint arms development with allies that would help counter China's growing military might.
The push largely stalled, however, as many restrictions - including on lethal equipment - remained. Companies continued to shy away from overseas defence sales.
Buoyed by a bumper election win ŌĆŗand shorn of the longtime coalition partner that had opposed more radical change, Takaichi hopes the latest easing will nudge arms makers to add the production capacity Japan needs for a major military buildup .
Some Japanese defence firms say they are ready to pivot.
Air defence systems builder Toshiba told Reuters it plans to hire about 500 people over the next three ŌĆīyears and is ŌüĀconstructing new testing and manufacturing facilities. It has also established a new department to handle defence exports.
"Reputational risk is not what it used to be," said Kenji Kobayashi, vice president in Toshiba's defence division.
Some big Japanese brands that have sidelines in defence equipment and also make consumer goods have expressed concerns that arms sales will put off their broader range of customers.
"Rather than worrying about that, we focus on fulfilling our role and growing the business," Kobayashi said.
A recruitment listing reviewed by Reuters from Mitsubishi Electric - whose products include fridges and missiles - shows the firm is hiring for an overseas sales role covering fighter aircraft and other military exports.
Demand for finished systems is strongest in Asia, while Europe, Australia and the United States offer markets for components and co-development of new products, said Arai, the Mitsubishi Electric defence executive.
He expects overall sales at his unit, including domestic and international, to increase by 50% to 600 billion yen ($3.8 billion) by 2031.
There remains a gap between the political messaging ŌĆŗand the policies of some companies, however, said Latvia's envoy to Japan, Zigmars ŌĆŗZilgalvis.
He gave the example of carmaker Toyota (7203.T) , opens new tab , whose subsidiary turned down ŌüĀan attempted purchase of engines and related parts by Latvian firm VR Cars for a military utility vehicle in 2023.
The Latvian mission had tried to help broker the failed sale, Zilgalvis said.
Toyota Customising & Development said in response to Reuters questions that it could not accommodate the request for military vehicles "based on our business scope and policy." It declined to comment on the upcoming revisions to Japan's arms export policy.
VR Cars said it respected the decision.
While Tokyo is expected to maintain strict ŌĆŗcontrols on sending arms to conflict zones, even Ukraine has sensed an opportunity.
Kyiv's chamber of commerce in Tokyo will soon launch a new industry group of Ukrainian and Japanese drone firms to spur development of new technologies, timed ŌĆŗto coincide with the rule changes, its head ŌüĀKateryna Yavorska exclusively told Reuters.
The U.S. has long dominated global military supply chains. It accounted for 95% of Japan's defense imports, 85% of Australian and British purchases and 77% of Saudi Arabian buys between 2021-2025, according to a March report by the Stockholm International Peace Research Institute (SIPRI) think-tank.
But Washington's foreign military sales programme, often blamed for late deliveries and rising costs, and its tight control over defence technologies has long been a source of frustration, officials and analysts said.
One objective of Japan's rule changes is to build defence supply chains in Asia that do not rely on the United States, said a ruling party official involved in drafting security policy.
Neighbouring South ŌüĀKorea offers something of ŌĆŗa blueprint: It has become the largest defence supplier to Poland and the Philippines after steady growth over the last five years, SIPRI data shows.
But the potential for Japan - the world's fourth ŌĆŗlargest economy - is greater.
Even with the curbs, Japan's arms industry is on a par with South Korea, Germany, Italy and Israel, and nearly twice the size of India's, according to SIPRI's analysis of leading defence contractor revenues in 2024. The U.S. industry, however, is 25 times bigger.
"Japan has been kind of in the timeout box because of World War Two, frankly. But they were inevitably going ŌĆŗto swing closer towards the centre of global politics," said Andrew Koch, founder of Nexus Pacific, a Tokyo-based defence-industry advisory.
($1 = 159.2100 yen)

item.title: Trump reiterates Pope Leo criticism, says it is 'unacceptable' for Iran to have a nuclear bomb
item.description:
April 14 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump ŌĆŗreiterated his criticisms ŌĆīof Pope Leo on Tuesday and ŌĆŗsaid it ŌĆŗis "unacceptable" for Iran to ŌüĀhave a ŌĆŗnuclear bomb.
"Will someone ŌĆŗplease tell Pope Leo that Iran has ŌĆŗkilled at ŌĆŗleast 42,000 innocent, completely ŌĆīunarmed, ŌüĀprotesters in the last two months, and that ŌĆŗfor ŌĆŗIran ŌüĀto have a Nuclear ŌĆŗBomb is ŌĆŗabsolutely ŌüĀunacceptable," Trump said on Truth ŌüĀSocial.

item.title: China calls US claims of military pressure on Taiwan a 'distortion'
item.description:
BEIJING, April 15 (Reuters) - The United States' claims about ŌĆŗChina exerting military pressure on Taiwan are distorted, and demonstrate its "malicious intentions", a government spokesperson ŌĆīin Beijing said on Wednesday.
China has stepped up military activity around democratically governed Taiwan, which it views as its own territory, holding several rounds of war games , most recently with live-fire drills in late December.
"Certain people on the U.S. side are jumping ŌĆŗup and down, continuously rehashing the so-called 'mainland threat' or 'military pressure,'" Chen Binhua, a spokesperson for ŌĆŗChina's Taiwan Affairs Office, told reporters.
This represented "a complete distortion of the facts and harbours ŌüĀmalicious intentions," he added, saying Taiwan was an internal affair for China, which would brook no outside ŌĆŗinterference.
Chen urged the United States to act with great caution, and handle Taiwan-related matters carefully and prudently.
China has ŌĆŗrepeatedly demanded a halt to weapons sales to Taiwan by the United States, its most important international backer, despite a lack of formal diplomatic ties.
Wednesday's remarks came after the U.S. State Department urged China last week to talk to Taiwan and ŌĆŗhalt its military and other pressure on the island, after Taiwan opposition leader Cheng Li-wun met President Xi Jinping ŌĆŗin Beijing.
China refuses to speak to Taiwan President Lai Ching-te, calling him a "separatist". Lai rejects Beijing's sovereignty claims, saying only ŌĆīTaiwan's ŌüĀpeople can decide their future.
Cheng said she aimed to foster peace with her visit, when China unveiled measures it said would benefit Taiwan, such as easing controls on exports of food, though it did not cease regular military activities around the island during her trip.
Taiwan's government says it should be leading engagement efforts with China rather ŌĆŗthan private party-to-party contacts.
Cheng, whose ŌĆŗvisit was a month ŌüĀbefore one planned by U.S. President Donald Trump, hopes China and the United States can reconcile and cooperate.
"We can definitely go down the path of peace," ŌĆŗCheng, the chairwoman of Taiwan's largest opposition party, the Kuomintang, told a Taiwan ŌĆŗradio station on ŌüĀWednesday. "This is the important message I hope to send to Washington."
China has never renounced the use of force to bring Taiwan under its control, but says it prefers "peaceful reunification ", a message it has ramped up in recent weeks.
Chen ŌüĀhoped Taiwan's ŌĆŗpeople would see the advantages of such a step, from ŌĆŗcheaper living costs to sprucing up aged housing.
"In short, national reunification is not only a great moral cause, but also of great benefit," ŌĆŗhe added.

item.title: North Korea sharply boosting nuclear weapons capacity, IAEA chief says
item.description:
SEOUL, April 15 (Reuters) - North Korea has made ŌĆŗa very serious ŌĆīincrease in its nuclear weapons production ŌĆŗcapabilities, IAEA ŌĆŗchief Rafael Grossi said ŌüĀon Wednesday.
Speaking ŌĆŗin Seoul, the head ŌĆŗof the U.N. nuclear watchdog confirmed a ŌĆŗrapid rise ŌĆŗin activity at THE five-megawatt ŌĆīreactor, ŌüĀthe reprocessing unit, a light water reactor and ŌĆŗother ŌĆŗfacilities ŌüĀat North Korea's Yongbyon nuclear ŌĆŗcomplex, adding ŌĆŗthe ŌüĀcountry's nuclear programme was estimated ŌüĀat ŌĆŗa few ŌĆŗdozen warheads.

item.title: Iran used Chinese spy satellite to target US bases, FT reports
item.description: April 15 (Reuters) - Iran secretly ŌĆŗused a ŌĆīChinese spy satellite ŌĆŗthat ŌĆŗgave the Islamic ŌüĀRepublic ŌĆŗa new ŌĆŗcapability to target U.S. ŌĆŗmilitary ŌĆŗbases across the ŌĆīMiddle ŌüĀEast during the recent ŌĆŗwar, ŌĆŗthe ŌüĀFinancial Times ŌĆŗreported on ŌĆŗWednesday.

item.title: South Korea says Israel accepts explanation, dispute over President Lee's comments resolved
item.description:
SEOUL, April 15 (Reuters) - South Korea's Foreign ŌĆŗMinister Cho Hyun ŌĆīsaid on Wednesday a high-level Israeli ŌĆŗoffical said ŌĆŗit accepted Seoul's ŌüĀexplanation of President ŌĆŗLee Jae ŌĆŗMyung's social media remarks on the Holocaust ŌĆŗand that ŌĆŗthe situation had been ŌĆīresolved.
Lee ŌüĀsparked a diplomatic row and domestic criticism after comparing ŌĆŗIsraeli ŌĆŗmilitary ŌüĀactions against Palestinians to ŌĆŗthe Holocaust ŌĆŗin ŌüĀa post on X, comments ŌüĀthat ŌĆŗIsrael strongly ŌĆŗrejected.

item.title: Japan's decade-long march towards arms exports
item.description:
TOKYO, April 15 (Reuters) - Japan has spent the past decade steadily dismantling some of the world's toughest arms export restrictions as it steps back from the postwar pacifism that shaped its ŌĆŗsecurity policy.
Here are the key events:
2014 - Then-prime minister Shinzo Abe ends a near-blanket ban ŌĆīon arms exports in place since 1976, allowing some transfers for humanitarian assistance and international cooperation seen to enhance Japan's security, as well as joint development programmes.
2016 - The Philippines leases five used TC-90 trainer aircraft for maritime patrols over ŌĆŗthe disputed South China Sea, marking the first significant military equipment transfer since the rule ŌĆŗchange.
2016 - In an early setback for Tokyo's export ambitions, Australia rejects a Japanese government-backed $40 ŌüĀbillion bid by Mitsubishi Heavy Industries (MHI) (7011.T) , opens new tab to supply advanced diesel submarines. Canberra picks a rival French ŌĆŗdesign.
2020 - Mitsubishi Electric (6503.T) , opens new tab becomes the first Japanese company to sell newly manufactured defence equipment overseas with a ŌĆŗdeal to supply air-surveillance radars to the Philippines.
2022 - Japan joins Britain and Italy in the Global Combat Air Programme (GCAP) to build an advanced stealth fighter jet by the mid-2030s, marking its first major joint defence project without the United States.
2023 - Tokyo establishes ŌĆŗOverseas Security Assistance (OSA), a mechanism to provide military aid to like-minded countries in Southeast Asia and ŌĆŗthe Pacific. That programme has delivered patrol boats to Indonesia and Bangladesh, drones to Tonga and Sri Lanka and radar ŌĆīsystems ŌüĀto Djibouti and the Philippines.
2023 - Japan loosens its 2014 export rules for the first time to allow equipment built under licence to be sold back to the country of origin. That change enabled MHI to supply Patriot air defence missiles to the United States, indirectly helping Washington arm Ukraine.
2024 - Another tweak is ŌĆŗmade a few months ŌĆŗlater to allow future ŌüĀoverseas sales of the GCAP fighter as long as exports to countries involved in conflicts remain off limits.
2025 - In what will be Japan's biggest-ever military ŌĆŗexport, Australia selects an upgraded version of MHI's Mogami-class frigate in a $7 billion, 11-ship ŌĆŗorder to ŌüĀreplace its ageing Anzac-class warships.
2025 - Japan in December said it will double OSA funding to nations, including some facing maritime pressure from China.
2026 - Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi's ruling party in April recommends scrapping limits that confine ŌüĀarms sales ŌĆŗto five categories: transport, relief and rescue, early warning systems, ŌĆŗsurveillance and mine clearance. The changes, which her government is expected to approve as soon as this month, maintain a ban ŌĆŗon conflict zone exports except in extraordinary cases.

item.title: Britain's Prince Harry speaks of struggles of fatherhood on Australia tour
item.description:
SYDNEY, April 15 (Reuters) - Britain's Prince ŌĆŗHarry spoke on Wednesday of the struggles of early fatherhood, as he visited a Melbourne football ŌĆīclub on the second day of his tour of Australia with wife Meghan, Duchess of Sussex.
The couple is on a four-day trip to the country, with engagements covering sport, mental health and veterans' affairs.
At the training ground of the Western Bulldogs Australian rules ŌĆŗfootball club, Harry gave a speech in which he spoke about feeling disconnected from his children, and ŌĆŗusing therapy to overcome past issues.
"Certainly, I felt a disconnection because my wife was ŌüĀthe one creating life, and I was there to witness it," he said at the event, co-hosted by ŌĆŗthe Movember mental health charity.
"From a therapy standpoint, you want to be the best version of yourself for your ŌĆŗkids. And I knew that I had stuff from the past that I needed to deal with, and therefore prepare myself to basically cleanse myself of the past."
The Duke and Duchess of Sussex have two children, Archie and Lilibet, who are not accompanying them on ŌĆŗthe trip.
The couple stepped down as working members of the British royal family and moved to the U.S. ŌĆŗin 2020, citing a desire to be financially independent and to escape what they characterised as media intrusion into their private ŌĆīlives.
They last ŌüĀvisited Australia in 2018 while still working royals, announcing Meghan's first pregnancy hours after arriving in Sydney.
Harry and Meghan have received a mixed reception in Australia, where Britain's King Charles is the head of state, though a sizeable minority supports becoming a republic.
The couple were greeted by hundreds of children and parents at a ŌĆŗchildren's hospital in Melbourne on Tuesday, ŌĆŗwho said their presence ŌüĀhad brought hope to young patients there.
But many other Australians have questioned their reasons for visiting the country.
"This is no royal visit but a continuation of tone-deaf hawking ŌĆŗby a couple estranged from reality," an opinion piece in the Sydney Morning Herald ŌĆŗsaid ahead of ŌüĀthe visit.
The couple's travel is being privately funded, though local media reported some policing costs associated with the visit would be paid by Australian taxpayers, sparking a protest petition signed by more than 45,000 people.
In contrast to their previous ŌüĀvisit, the ŌĆŗSussexes will also undertake commercial activities while in Australia, with Meghan ŌĆŗremaining in the country to host a wellness retreat at a luxury beachside hotel in Sydney over the weekend.
Tickets for the event, which includes ŌĆŗyoga, manifestation and sound healing, start at A$2,699 ($1,912) per person.

item.title: Vedanta plant blast in India's Chhattisgarh kills 14, injures 20
item.description:
April 15 (Reuters) - At least 14 people ŌĆīlost their lives and 20 others were injured following a boiler explosion at ŌĆŗa power plant operated by ŌĆŗIndia's Vedanta Ltd (VDAN.NS) , opens new tab in the central Indian state ŌüĀof Chhattisgarh, local police confirmed ŌĆŗon Wednesday.
The cause of the explosion ŌĆŗwas not immediately identified.
The incident took place on Tuesday at Singhitarai, about 230 km away from ŌĆŗthe state capital of Raipur.
"The ŌĆŗdeath toll from the Vedanta power plant blast ŌĆīin ŌüĀChhattisgarh has risen to 14, and the number of injured has increased to 20," District Superintendent of ŌĆŗPolice PK ŌĆŗThakur told ŌüĀReuters.
The blast was likely caused by overheating in ŌĆŗthe boiler tube, said Thakur.
In ŌĆŗa ŌüĀstatement, Vedanta said an "unfortunate incident" had occurred at its Singhitarai plant and ŌüĀthat ŌĆŗa thorough investigation is underway to determine ŌĆŗthe cause.

item.title: Iran war promises green edge for Asia as plastic packaging runs short
item.description:
SEOUL/TOKYO, April 15 (Reuters) - An 'eco-friendly' range of paper tubes and pouches touted by Yonwoo, a South Korean maker ŌĆŗof packaging for cosmetics, has reaped unexpected benefit from the Iran war, which has disrupted supplies of the plastic needed to turn out single-use ŌĆīwrapping.
While the conflict has sent prices of plastic soaring to roughly four-year highs by choking off flows of the required raw materials of oil and petrochemicals, the company says it has fuelled inquiries three-fold for paper-based options.
"Interest initially came from companies focused on sustainability ... but if the plastics issue gets prolonged we expect demand to further increase," said Kim Min-sang, a senior manager at parent Kolmar Korea (161890.KS) , opens new tab .
The supplier to ŌĆŗmajor firms, such as France's L'Oreal (OREP.PA) , opens new tab , has fielded inquiries mainly for paper tubes encasing items such as sunscreen and lotions that use just 20% of the ŌĆŗplastic employed by conventional packaging, Kim told Reuters.
Across Asia, home to some of the world's biggest plastic users and polluters, changes that ŌüĀenvironmental groups have sought for decades are quickly being adopted, even if they may prove a short-term flip.
Asia is not only heavily reliant on feedstock imported from ŌĆŗthe Middle East, but it is hooked on plastic, with China, Japan, South Korea and Southeast Asia together using almost a third of the world's total by 2022, OECD ŌĆŗdata showed, up 900% since 1990.
The region also accounts for more than a third of all plastic waste leaking into the environment, thanks to poor waste collection methods in low-income Southeast Asian nations.
Japan ranks behind only the United States in terms of plastic production and consumption per head, according to a 2025 study by researchers from Beijing's Tsinghua University published in the science journal Nature.
Wholesalers there have been ŌĆŗwarning about possible shortages of plastic trays and bags, said Kensuke Takahashi, product manager for Marutake supermarket in Saitama, adjacent to Tokyo.
"We now have to discuss how to ŌĆŗsell our products if trays are no longer supplied at all," said Takahashi. "I'm very worried. We really don't know what will happen."
Japanese makers of plastic bags and cling wrap, Mitsubishi Chemical and Sanipak, ŌĆīhave said ŌüĀthey will raise prices by about 30% in coming weeks for some products as the conflict drives up costs of raw materials.
Talks for a global treaty to tackle plastic pollution stalled last year after the United States and plastic-producing countries pushed back against a drive to cap plastic production led by the European Union.
It is one of several environmental initiatives to lose steam under U.S. President Donald Trump, who has called climate change a hoax.
Trump's sweeping trade tariffs were also a blow for Taiwan's Lastic, which makes bamboo-based ŌĆŗbiodegradable material, said senior development manager Luke ŌĆŗAnderson.
American airlines eyeing the material to replace ŌüĀdisposable plastic cups and cutlery lost interest after Trump imposed the levies on U.S. imports last year, he said.
Now, as prices of plastic rise, several of his U.S. buyers have sought fresh quotes. "It's not that I like to look at the upside of war, ŌĆŗbut ... if you can't control it, you've got to find the silver lining," he said.
Some companies are adapting to new alternatives.
In ŌĆŗMalaysia, dairy producer Farm ŌüĀFresh (FARM.KL) , opens new tab said it has temporarily switched to paper-based milk cartons because of the plastic supply disruptions.
But there is no quick fix for others, such as South Korea's Gaone International, which makes packaging for face masks.
Testing new materials would take time, so it has slashed daily output to between 10% and 20% from the usual 1 million units as it hunts for new ŌüĀsuppliers.
The 20-year-old factory ŌĆŗis now warning clients of a wait of up to eight weeks for orders to be filled, ŌĆŗand expects revenue to suffer accordingly, said sales team manager Han Kyung-hun.
"I hope things return to normal as soon as possible," said Han, but cautioned recovery could take a couple of months, even if the war ŌĆŗended immediately.

item.title: TSMC confronts new contenders for chipmaking crown
item.description:
HONG KONG, April 15 (Reuters Breakingviews) - Powerful monopolies, like absolute monarchs, tend to attract challengers. Taiwan's TSMC (2330.TW) , opens new tab will this week confirm its undisputed dominance of the business of making high-end contract chips for customers from Nvidia (NVDA.O) , opens new tab to Apple (AAPL.O) , opens new tab . ŌĆŗThat is making governments and rival companies even more determined to loosen its grip on the chipmaking crown.
The $1.7 trillion company formally known ŌĆīas Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing is set to deliver first-quarter earnings of roughly $17 billion, a blistering 49% year-on-year increase, per analyst forecasts compiled by Visible Alpha. That's powered by surging sales of its artificial intelligence chips, which companies from OpenAI to Anthropic just cannot get enough of.
Insatiable demand is a boon for TSMC, whose unmatched manufacturing and technological prowess give ŌĆŗit an effective monopoly on making cutting-edge chips. Though rival Samsung Electronics (005930.KS) , opens new tab was first to the market with the current generation of advanced ŌĆŗprocessors measuring just 3 nanometres, the $880 billion South Korean group has found it hard to secure orders from customers, according ŌüĀto domestic media , opens new tab .
The Taiwanese group looks set to extend its dominance over the next generation of 2-nanometre chips. Analysts at Citi expect Nvidia, Alphabet's Google (GOOGL.O) , opens new tab and ŌĆŗAmazon.com (AMZN.O) , opens new tab to place significant orders for the tiny processors over the next few years. The bank's researchers forecast TSMC's revenue will top $300 billion by 2030, almost ŌĆŗtwice their forecast of $162 billion for this year. Confirming its dominance, analysts expect the company's gross margin to remain above 62%.
But for boss C.C. Wei there is such a thing as being too successful. TSMC's rule comes with rising geopolitical risks: its most advanced research and development and manufacturing operations remain in Taiwan, which Beijing views as its territory.
Japan is ŌĆŗstepping up efforts to nurture a homegrown alternative. Last week, the industry ministry announced an additional $4 billion in state funding for Rapidus, taking the total ŌĆŗso far to over $16 billion. The startup aims to mass-produce 2-nanometre chips in the next fiscal year. Days earlier, Elon Musk unveiled a tie-up with Intel (INTC.O) , opens new tab as part of his ŌĆīambitious Terafab ŌüĀproject, a vertically integrated chipmaking facility that will supply the billionaire's companies SpaceX, Tesla (TSLA.O) , opens new tab and xAI.
These challenges remain nascent. TSMC plans as much as $56 billion in capital expenditures for this year, dwarfing Tokyo's support for Rapidus. Meanwhile, the $330 billion Intel is still recovering from a series of setbacks that saw the U.S. government take a 9% stake in the company. Investors are betting on TSMC's enduring advantage: the stock trades on just over 20 times forecast earnings for ŌĆŗthe next 12 months, above its 5-year ŌĆŗaverage of 18 times, according ŌüĀto LSEG.
Others have tried and failed to break TSMC's grip. Yet as long as the company wears its crown it will have to confront new contenders.
Follow Robyn Mak on X , opens new tab .

item.title: EU measures to ease pain of expensive energy must have end date, EU executive says
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 14 (Reuters) - European governments' measures to alleviate the impact of high energy prices on businesses and consumers must have a clear end-date and be focused ŌĆŗto avoid the mistakes of the energy crisis of 2022, European ŌĆīEconomic Commissioner Valdis Dombrovskis said.
Speaking at the International Monetary Fund, Dombrovskis said any moves to ease the pain of the high prices caused by the closure of the Strait of ŌĆŗHormuz as a result of the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran should avoid ŌĆŗboosting demand for fossil fuels.
"One key lesson from the last energy ŌüĀcrisis is that measures were often insufficiently targeted and remained in place ŌĆŗfor too long, making them much more fiscally costly than necessary. This is something ŌĆŗwe want to avoid now," Dombrovskis said in a conversation with IMF European Department head Alfred Kammer.
He said higher debt and deficits as well as higher interest rates than during ŌĆŗthe 2022 energy crisis caused by Russia's war on Ukraine made it now ŌĆŗmore important for EU governments to avoid costly, lengthy measures to cushion energy price spikes.
"One ŌĆīmessage ŌüĀwe are trying to reinforce is that measures should come with clear sunset clauses to ensure they remain time-limited. They should also avoid increasing demand for oil and gas at a time when demand should be declining," he said.
"The problem ŌĆŗis that broad ŌĆŗmeasures, such as ŌüĀacross-the-board tax cuts, are easier to implement politically and administratively. Targeted measures require defining eligibility thresholds, which takes more effort," ŌĆŗDombrovskis said.
"Given todayŌĆÖs tighter fiscal space and financial conditions, we ŌĆŗneed to ŌüĀbe much more focused and targeted in our response," he said.
Some EU countries seem to be heeding some of the advice. Germany decided on Monday on fuel price ŌüĀrelief for ŌĆŗconsumers and businesses worth 1.6 billion euros for ŌĆŗtwo months, while France said any support measures would be only for sectors most in need ŌĆŗand be renewed on a monthly basis.

item.title: Germany to provide further $23.6 million in aid to Sudan this year
item.description:
BERLIN, April 15 (Reuters) - Germany will provide an additional 20 million euros ($23.58 million) to ŌĆŗSudan this year, with further funding commitments currently ŌĆīunder review, the development ministry in Berlin said in a statement on Wednesday.
At the end of 2025, the ministry had provided 155.4 million ŌĆŗeuros for projects in Sudan and in neighbouring ŌĆŗcountries affected by the war in Sudan , which it would ŌüĀexpand this year by 20 million euros, it said ŌĆŗahead of an international aid conference on Sudan in the ŌĆŗGerman capital later on Wednesday.
Sudan's war between the Sudanese army and the paramilitary Rapid Support Forces, which enters its third year on Wednesday, ŌĆŗhas caused widespread hunger and displaced millions of people amid ŌĆŗone of the world's largest humanitarian crises.
Germany aims to gather funding pledges ŌĆīof ŌüĀat least 1 billion euros at the conference. "That seems to be working," Foreign Minister Johann Wadephul told radio broadcaster Deutschlandfunk.
Ensuring enough funding for such crises, with wars in Iran ŌĆŗand Ukraine also ŌĆŗraging and ŌüĀthe United States pulling back on aid commitments, is a Sisyphean task, he added.
"We must ŌĆŗtry to compensate for what others, including the ŌĆŗUnited ŌüĀStates, unfortunately fail to do," he said.
It is also in Germany's interest to make money available to ensure people do not ŌüĀface ŌĆŗhunger, he said, to prevent a ŌĆŗrepeat of the large influx of migrants coming from the Middle East in ŌĆŗ2015/16.
($1 = 0.8483 euros)

item.title: US shuts down Iran's maritime trade despite optimism for more talks
item.description:
DUBAI/WASHINGTON, April 15 (Reuters) - The United States said on Wednesday its military had completely halted trade going in and out of Iran by sea, even though President ŌĆŗDonald Trump said talks with Tehran on ending the war could resume this week.
Trump said negotiations between U.S. and Iranian officials could resume in Pakistan in the ŌĆīnext two days and Vice President JD Vance, who led weekend talks that ended without a breakthrough, said he felt positive about where things stood.
"I think youŌĆÖre going to be watching an amazing two days ahead," Trump told ABC News reporter Jonathan Karl, adding he did not think it would be necessary to extend a two-week ceasefire that ends on April 21.
"It could end either way, but I think a deal is preferable ŌĆŗbecause then they can rebuild," Trump said, according to a post by Karl on X. "They really do have a different regime now. No matter what, we took out the ŌĆŗradicals."
Officials from Pakistan, Iran and the Gulf also said negotiating teams from the U.S. and Iran could return to Pakistan later this week, although ŌüĀone senior Iranian source said no date had been set.
Despite the optimistic note, more vessels were being turned back under the U.S. blockade on Iranian ports, including a U.S.-sanctioned and Chinese-owned tanker ŌĆŗRich Starry that was making its way back to the Strait of Hormuz on Wednesday after exiting the Persian Gulf.
Admiral Brad Cooper, the head of the U.S. Central Command, said American forces ŌĆŗhad completely halted economic trade going in and out of Iran by sea, which he said fuels 90% of Iran's economy.
"In less than 36 hours since the blockade was implemented, U.S. forces have completely halted economic trade going into and out of Iran by sea," Cooper said in a post on X.
Earlier the U.S. military said it had intercepted eight Iran-linked oil tankers since the start of the blockade on Monday, according to the ŌĆŗWall Street Journal.
Trump, speaking to the New York Post on Tuesday, said his negotiators are likely to be back, thanks largely to the "great job" Pakistan's army chief, Field Marshal ŌĆŗAsim Munir, was doing to moderate the talks.
Later on Tuesday, at an event in Georgia, U.S. Vice President JD Vance said Trump wanted to make a "grand bargain" with Iran but there was a lot of ŌĆīmistrust between ŌüĀthe two countries.
"You are not going to solve that problem overnight," he said.
The signs of diplomatic engagement to end the conflict that began on February 28 helped calm oil markets, pressing benchmark prices down for a second day on Wednesday. Asian stocks rose while the safe-haven dollar stabilised after falling for a seventh straight session overnight.
The war has prompted Iran to effectively shut the Strait of Hormuz, a crucial global waterway for crude and gas transport and cut shipments from the Gulf to global buyers, particularly in Asia and Europe.
About 5,000 people have died in the hostilities, including about ŌĆŗ3,000 in Iran and 2,000 in Lebanon.
Iran's nuclear ambitions were a key sticking ŌüĀpoint at the weekend talks. The U.S. had proposed a 20-year suspension of all nuclear activity by Iran, while Tehran had suggested a halt of three to five years, according to people familiar with the proposals.
Speaking in Seoul, the head of the International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA), Rafael Grossi, ŌĆŗsaid the length of any moratorium on Iranian uranium enrichment was a political decision and it was possible Tehran might accept a ŌĆŗcompromise as a confidence-building act.
The ŌüĀU.S. has also pressed for any enriched nuclear material to be removed from Iran, while Tehran has demanded that international sanctions against it be removed.
One source involved in the negotiations in Pakistan said back-channel talks since the weekend had produced progress in closing that gap, bringing the two sides closer to a deal that could be put forward at a new round of talks.
However, in a major ŌüĀcomplication for peace ŌĆŗprospects, Israel has continued to attack Lebanon as it targets Hezbollah, an Iran-backed militant group. Israel and the U.S. ŌĆŗsay that campaign is not covered by the ceasefire, while Iran insists it is.
On Tuesday, the UK, Canada, Japan and seven other countries condemned the killings of UN peacekeepers in Lebanon and called for "an urgent end to hostilities".
The statement comes ŌĆŗafter the deaths of three Indonesian peacekeepers last month. The countries welcomed the ceasefire agreed between the U.S., Israel and Iran.

item.title: Exclusive: Russia-linked hackers compromised scores of Ukrainian prosecutorsŌĆÖ email accounts, data shows
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 15 (Reuters) - Russia-linked hackers broke into more than 170 email accounts belonging to prosecutors and investigators across Ukraine during the last several ŌĆŗmonths, according to data reviewed by Reuters, a campaign that shows how MoscowŌĆÖs spies are keeping tabs on the Ukrainian officials tasked with rooting out corruption and Russian ŌĆīcollaborators.
The data was inadvertently exposed to the internet by the hackers and discovered by Ctrl-Alt-Intel, a collective of British and American cyber threat researchers. Ctrl-Alt-Intel said data left on the server - including logs of successful hacking operations and thousands of stolen emails - showed that the hackers compromised at least 284 inboxes between September 2024 and March 2026.
Most of the victims were in Ukraine; others are from neighboring NATO countries and the Balkans.
The operation was first described last month in ŌĆŗa Ctrl-Alt-Intel blog post. , opens new tab Reuters reviewed the underlying data and is publishing details of the hacks for the first time, including the identities of more than a dozen compromised European agencies ŌĆŗand officials.
Ctrl-Alt-Intel said the mistake provided a rare opportunity to examine the workings of a Russian espionage campaign.
The hackers ŌĆ£just made a huge operational blunder,ŌĆØ Ctrl-Alt-Intel ŌüĀsaid. ŌĆ£They left their front door wide open.ŌĆØ
The Russian embassy in Washington did not respond to requests for comment. Moscow has repeatedly denied it engages in hacking operations against other countries.
Ctrl-Alt-Intel ŌĆŗattributed the hacking campaign to ŌĆ£Fancy Bear,ŌĆØ one of the nicknames assigned to a well-known Russian military hacking squad. Two researchers who independently reviewed Ctrl-Alt-IntelŌĆÖs work - Matthieu Faou, with the cybersecurity company ESET, and Feike ŌĆŗHacquebord, with the cybersecurity company TrendAI - agreed the hackers were tied to Moscow. However, Faou said he could not verify Fancy Bear was involved, and Hacquebord disputed Fancy Bear's involvement.
The hackers likely targeted Ukrainian law enforcement either to stay ahead of investigators working to expose MoscowŌĆÖs spies or to gather potentially embarrassing information about top officials in Kyiv, said Keir Giles, an associate fellow at LondonŌĆÖs Chatham House think tank, who reviewed a list of the victims.
The data ŌĆŗshowed the hackers broke into accounts managed by the Specialized Prosecutor's Office in the Field of Defense, a wartime body established to fight corruption and unmask spies in the Ukrainian military. They also ŌĆŗtargeted UkraineŌĆÖs Asset Recovery and Management Agency (ARMA), which oversees assets seized from criminals and Russian collaborators, and the Kyiv-based Prosecutor's Training Center.
Among the victims were Yaroslava Maksymenko, who was the chief of ARMA at the time, the ŌĆīdata shows. At ŌüĀthe Prosecutor's Training Center, the data shows the hackers broke into the mailboxes of 44 employees, including one belonging to the centerŌĆÖs deputy director, Oleg Duka.
The Russians allegedly stole data from at least one senior employee of the Specialized Anti-Corruption Prosecutor's Office (SAPO), which has investigated some of UkraineŌĆÖs most high-profile corruption scandals, including one that prompted the resignation of President Volodymyr ZelenskiyŌĆÖs chief peace negotiator Andriy Yermak in November.
Maksymenko, Duka, ARMA, SAPO, and the prosecutors did not respond to requests for comment. Ukraine's Computer Emergency Response Team said it was aware of the hack and had already investigated some of the compromises identified by ŌĆŗReuters.
The hack uncovered ŌĆŗby Ctrl-Alt-Intel represents "a small set of activity ŌüĀin regards to the whole Russia-aligned espionage ecosystem,ŌĆØ said Faou, the ESET researcher.
The data shows the hackers broke into the email inbox of the Central City Hospital in Pokrovsk, a railway hub Russia has been trying to cement its control over , as well as an inbox belonging to the cityŌĆÖs finance committee.
Scores ŌĆŗof officials in surrounding NATO countries were also hacked, the data shows.
In Romania, the hackers compromised at least 67 email accounts maintained by the ŌĆŗRomanian Air Force, including several ŌüĀbelonging to NATO airbases and at least one senior military officer. The Romanian Ministry of Defense did not respond to requests for comment.
The data also shows the spies compromised 27 email inboxes managed by Hellenic National Defense General Staff, GreeceŌĆÖs top military body. Among those hacked were Greek defense attaches in India and Bosnia and the public-facing inbox for GreeceŌĆÖs Joint Armed Forces Mental Health Center. The General Staff did not answer ŌüĀa detailed list ŌĆŗof questions.
In Bulgaria, the hackers broke into at least four inboxes belonging to local officials in Plovdiv province, where Russian interference ŌĆŗwas alleged to have disabled satellite navigation services ahead of a visit by European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen last year. Bulgarian officials did not respond to comment requests.
The data also shows the spies hacked academics and military officials in Serbia, ŌĆŗa traditional Russian ally. SerbiaŌĆÖs Ministry of Defense did not respond to requests for comment.
ŌĆ£A supposedly close relationship with Moscow is no insurance against Russian espionage," Giles said.

item.title: Xi assures Russia of China's friendship as ties grow with other nations
item.description:
BEIJING, April 15 (Reuters) - China and Russia must trust and support each other, deepen ŌĆŗcooperation and defend each other's interests, President Xi Jinping said ŌĆīon Wednesday, assuring Moscow of friendship even as the world's No.2 economy expands ties with other neighbours and nations.
In a meeting with Foreign Minister Sergei Lavrov at ŌĆŗthe Great Hall of the People, Xi said the "stability" and "certainty" of China-Russia ŌĆŗrelations were particularly valuable as he told the Russian envoy to convey ŌüĀhis sincere regards to President Vladimir Putin, according to the official ŌĆŗXinhua news agency.
A handful of Western leaders have paid visits to Beijing ŌĆŗthis year, including the prime ministers of Spain, Britain and Canada, seeking to elevate ties with China. On Wednesday, Vietnam's top leader To Lam met Xi in ŌĆŗBeijing to cement relations at a time when both work to ŌĆŗfend off trade tariff pressure from the United States.
Putin and Xi are expected hold ŌĆīa ŌüĀsummit sometime this year to affirm Sino-Russian partnership and cooperation as the two neighbours observe anniversaries of their ties, including the 2001 Sino-Russia Treaty of Friendship.
"In the face of a once-in-a-century change, China and Russia ŌĆŗmust, through closer ŌĆŗand more robust ŌüĀstrategic cooperation, resolutely defend the legitimate interests of both countries, uphold the unity of the Global South, ŌĆŗand demonstrate the sense of responsibility expected of major ŌĆŗpowers and ŌüĀpermanent members of the United Nations Security Council," Xi told Lavrov.
In recent years, China has pushed for the building of what it says should ŌüĀbe ŌĆŗan equal and orderly multipolar world where ŌĆŗevery country has a say and major powers shoulder more responsibilities, an alternative to what ŌĆŗBeijing describes as U.S. hegemony.

item.title: Pakistan PM Sharif to visit Saudi Arabia, Qatar and Turkey this week
item.description: ISLAMABAD, April 15 (Reuters) - Pakistan Prime ŌĆīMinister ŌĆŗShehbaz ŌĆŗSharif will ŌüĀvisit ŌĆŗSaudi ŌĆŗArabia, Qatar ŌĆŗand ŌĆŗTurkey from April ŌĆī15 ŌüĀto 18, ŌĆŗIslamabad ŌĆŗsaid ŌüĀon ŌĆŗWednesday.

item.title: Hungary's Magyar says to suspend state media broadcast, pass new media law
item.description:
BUDAPEST, April 15 (Reuters) - Hungary's election winner Peter Magyar ŌĆŗsaid on Wednesday ŌĆīthat his government will suspend the broadcast ŌĆŗof state media, ŌĆŗpass a new media ŌüĀlaw, set up ŌĆŗa new media authority ŌĆŗand ensure press freedom after his cabinet takes ŌĆŗpower.
"Every Hungarian deserves ŌĆŗa public service media that ŌĆībroadcasts ŌüĀthe truth," Magyar said in an interview on state Kossuth ŌĆŗradio. ŌĆŗMagyar's ŌüĀTISZA (Respect and Freedom) party won a ŌĆŗlandslide victory ŌĆŗin ŌüĀSunday's election, ending Prime Minister Viktor Orban's ŌüĀ16-year ŌĆŗrule.

item.title: Iranian crew members return home from Sri Lanka
item.description:
COLOMBO, April 15 (Reuters) - Over 200 Iranian crew members from two warships who were rescued by Sri Lanka ŌĆŗhave been sent back to their homeland, ŌĆīa top official said on Wednesday.
Sri Lanka rescued 32 Iranian crew from the warship IRIS Dena on March 4 after ŌĆŗit was hit by a torpedo from a ŌĆŗU.S. submarine.
The ship was returning from a naval ŌüĀexercise organised by India, amid the U.S.-Israeli war ŌĆŗon Iran.
Sri Lanka also rescued 208 crew from a ŌĆŗsecond vessel, IRIS Booshehr on March 5 that had also requested assistance from Colombo after it ran into engine trouble.
"The crew ŌĆŗof both ships were flown out about 11 ŌĆŗp.m. on a special flight on Tuesday night," Deputy Defence Minister ŌĆīAruna ŌüĀJayasekera told Reuters.
After its crew was offloaded in Colombo, IRIS Booshehr was towed to waters off Sri Lanka's eastern coast of Trincomalee eventually reaching on Tuesday ŌĆŗmorning.
"There are ŌĆŗabout 8-10 ŌüĀIranian crew remaining aboard to assist operations," Jayasekera added.
Sri Lanka granted 30-day entry ŌĆŗvisas to the crew members and housed ŌĆŗthem ŌüĀin navy and air force camps before arrangements were made for their return to Iran.
A chartered plane arranged ŌüĀby ŌĆŗIran took back the bodies of ŌĆŗ84 crew members from the Dena who were killed in the ŌĆŗU.S. attack.

item.title: Putin to visit China in first half of 2026, agencies cite Lavrov
item.description:
MOSCOW, April 15 (Reuters) - Russia is ready to increase energy ŌĆŗsupplies to China ahead of an expected visit ŌĆīby President Vladimir Putin, Russian news agencies quoted Foreign Minister Sergei Lavrov as saying on Wednesday at a news ŌĆŗconference in Beijing.
The agencies quoted Lavrov as saying ŌĆŗthat the visit would take place in ŌüĀthe first half of the year, while the ŌĆŗVedomosti newspaper cited sources as saying it would be ŌĆŗduring the week beginning May 18.
President Xi Jinping met Lavrov on Wednesday, assuring Moscow of China's friendship and saying that China ŌĆŗand Russia must trust and support each other, ŌĆŗdeepen cooperation, and defend each other's interests.
Lavrov told the news conference ŌĆīthat ŌüĀRussia was ready to help China and other countries affected by the Middle East crisis with energy supplies.
"Russia can, of course, make up for the ŌĆŗresource shortfall facing ŌĆŗboth China ŌüĀand other countries that are interested in working with us on an equal ŌĆŗand mutually beneficial basis," Lavrov told the ŌĆŗnews ŌüĀconference in China.
Lavrov also said that Russia and China had all the necessary means to avoid reliance ŌüĀon what ŌĆŗhe described as U.S. efforts ŌĆŗto disrupt global energy markets through conflict in the Middle East.

item.title: Trump tells ABC News reporter he was not concerned about Orban's loss in Hungary
item.description:
April 14 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump told ABC News ŌĆŗreporter Jonathan Karl on Tuesday ŌĆīhe was not concerned about Viktor Orban's loss in Hungary, and that he likes incoming ŌĆŗPrime Minister Peter Magyar.
"I think ŌĆŗthe new man's going to do a ŌüĀgood job ŌĆö he's a good man," ŌĆŗTrump told the reporter.
Trump told the ŌĆŗreporter he did not know if it would have made a difference if he had gone ŌĆŗto Hungary instead of Vice President ŌĆŗJD Vance to campaign for Orban.
"He was behind ŌĆīsubstantially," ŌüĀTrump said. "I wasnŌĆÖt that involved in this one. ViktorŌĆÖs a good man, though."
Trump had backed Orban leading up to the ŌĆŗvote, even ŌĆŗspeaking briefly ŌüĀlast week at a campaign rally in Hungary, when Vance telephoned ŌĆŗhis boss upon taking the ŌĆŗstage.
But ŌüĀOrban lost power after 16 years as Hungarians voted in record numbers for ŌüĀa ŌĆŗpro-EU course spearheaded by ŌĆŗcenter-right rival Peter Magyar.

item.title: Kenya raises retail fuel prices as Mideast conflict drives up crude costs
item.description:
NAIROBI, April 15 (Reuters) - Kenya has raised its retail fuel prices by as much as 24.2% amid a spike ŌĆŗin crude prices and squeezed petroleum supplies caused by ŌĆīthe Middle East conflict, the East African country's energy regulator said late on Tuesday.
A statement released by the Energy and Petroleum Regulatory Authority (EPRA), ŌĆŗwhich sets the maximum allowable retail prices for various ŌĆŗproducts monthly, showed a litre of petrol was hiked ŌüĀby 16.1% to 206.97 Kenyan shillings ($1.60).
A litre of diesel ŌĆŗwas also raised by 24.2% to 206.84 Kenyan shillings, while kerosene ŌĆŗwas maintained at 152.78 shillings. The regulator cited the cost of imported products, which they said had risen by as much as 68.7%, ŌĆŗto justify the hiking of retail prices.
In March, the EPRA ŌĆŗleft prices unchanged, saying the impact of the war had yet to ŌĆībe ŌüĀreflected in retail prices.
Kenya imports nearly all of its fuel products from the Middle East via government-to-government deals with Persian Gulf suppliers, including Saudi Aramco Trading Fujairah, Abu Dhabi's ADNOC ŌĆŗGlobal Trading Ltd, ŌĆŗand Emirates National ŌüĀOil Company Singapore Ltd.
Hours before the new prices took effect late on Tuesday, motorists rushed ŌĆŗto pump stations in the capital, Nairobi, to ŌĆŗfill ŌüĀup their tanks, causing long queues.
The EPRA also said it had reduced value-added tax (VAT) on petrol, diesel, and kerosene from 16% ŌüĀto ŌĆŗ13% to "cushion consumers from the high...cost ŌĆŗof petroleum products as a result of the escalated prices in the international ŌĆŗmarket."
($1 = 129.2000 Kenyan shillings)

item.title: European oil refining margins turn negative, bucking global trend
item.description:
LONDON, April 14 (Reuters) - European oil refining margins have turned negative, lagging stronger margins ŌĆīin Asia and the U.S., as competition for crude from Asian buyers due to the Iran war drives up costs even as fuel prices hit record highs, according to IEA data and trade sources.
Northwest European light sweet hydroskimming margins dropped to an average of minus $6.45 a barrel ŌĆŗin the week beginning April 6, the IEA said in its monthly report on Tuesday based on data ŌĆŗfrom Argus Media.
Margins for medium sour cracking were also in negative territory, the data showed. ŌüĀLight sweet cracking margins remain positive, though they have also weakened significantly.
The margin squeeze is a consequence of the ŌĆŗsurge in physical crude prices to record highs as the war in Iran disrupts Middle East flows.
The narrowing European margin effectively ŌĆŗshows that these plants would be running at a loss, and is likely to prompt some to process less crude into fuels, analysts said.
Simpler European refineries, which lack upgrading units to extract more higher-value products such as jet fuel, could be forced to trim runs if ŌĆŗmargins remain under pressure, though there is no sign of widespread cuts yet, trading sources said.
"As things stand, Europe ŌĆŗis going to cut utilisation," Sparta Commodities analyst Neil Crosby said, adding that runs could fall by as much as 500,000 barrels ŌĆīper day.
By contrast, in the U.S. Gulf, heavy sour coking margins strengthened last week compared with the March average, IEA data showed. In Singapore, similarly, medium sour cracking margins were also stronger last week than their March averages.
The squeeze in Europe reflects rising crude costs as Asian refiners compete aggressively for cargoes, several trading sources said, as ŌĆŗwell as higher operating costs ŌĆŗsuch as for electricity ŌüĀand natural gas.
"It's typical of these crises," said a trading source at a European refinery. "Fuel cracks rise first, but as crude and other costs adjust, margins get dented." He added ŌĆŗthat their margin dropped from about $30 a barrel in the first week of the ŌĆŗconflict to just ŌüĀover $4 currently.
The squeeze comes after margins globally soared in March, with those in Europe reaching record highs.
