In Singapore, the IEA said, margins in March were some 14-fold higher than February levels, while in northwest Europe light sweet hydroskimming margins in March were ŌüĀmore ŌĆŗthan nine times higher than in February at $15.20 a barrel.
Some refiners even ŌĆŗdelayed planned shutdowns to take advantage of the higher fuel prices.
Italy's 300,000 barrel per day Sarroch refinery, for instance, pushed a maintenance shutdown from late ŌĆŗMarch to mid-May, industry monitor IIR said. The refinery's operator, Vitol, declined to comment.

item.title: Russia launches more than 300 drones, missiles at Ukraine overnight
item.description:
April 15 (Reuters) - Russia attacked Ukraine overnight ŌĆŗwith hundreds of drones and three ballistic missiles, targeting port ŌĆīinfrastructure in the south and wounding at least seven people, Ukrainian officials said.
Russia launched 324 drones in the period since 6 p.m. (1500 GMT) on Tuesday and ŌĆŗthree ballistic missiles, Ukraine's air force said. Air defence units shot ŌĆŗdown or neutralised 309 drones, but the missiles and ŌüĀ13 drones hit at nine locations, it said.
Three people were ŌĆŗinjured in an overnight Russian drone attack on the southeastern city of ŌĆŗDnipro, the regional governor, Oleksandr Ganzha, said on the Telegram messaging app.
Ganzha said the attack also damaged a nine-story apartment building and an administrative building, posting ŌĆŗphotos of a building with a gaping hole in one of ŌĆŗits sides.
The new attack came after a missile strike on the city killed five ŌĆīand ŌüĀinjured close to 30 people on Tuesday.
Four people sought medical attention after an overnight drone attack in the central city of Cherkasy, Ihor Taburets, the regional governor, said.
A woman was killed in the ŌĆŗshelling of the ŌĆŗsoutheastern city of ŌüĀZaporizhzhia early on Wednesday, the regional governor, Ivan Fedorov, said.
Fedorov, writing on Telegram, said a 74-year-old ŌĆŗsaleswoman was killed in a kiosk, and the attack ŌĆŗalso damaged ŌüĀa car park, business premises, and nearby residential buildings.
Port infrastructure in Ukraine's southern region of Odesa on the Black Sea came under another ŌüĀdrone ŌĆŗattack, the regional governor, Oleh Kiper, said, ŌĆŗreporting damage to administrative and warehouse buildings.
Officials in the Kyiv region also reported a ŌĆŗdrone attack.

item.title: Rohingya survivor recounts ordeal as 250 missing in Andaman sea tragedy
item.description:
DHAKA, April 15 (Reuters) - A Rohingya survivor has recounted a harrowing journey at sea after an overcrowded boat carrying refugees and Bangladeshi nationals capsized in the Andaman Sea, leaving around 250 people missing.
The boat, ŌĆŗwhich departed from Teknaf in southern Bangladesh and was bound for Malaysia, sank in the ŌĆīsecond week of April amid rough seas, strong winds and severe overcrowding, the United Nations' refugee and migration agencies said .
Bangladesh hosts around 1.2 million Rohingya refugees in camps in the south, most of whom fled violence in Myanmar in 2017. Many continue ŌĆŗto attempt dangerous sea journeys in search of safety and livelihoods abroad.
Survivors said nearly 300 people were ŌĆŗpacked onboard, including women, children, crew members and suspected traffickers.
Rafiqul Islam, one of the ŌüĀsurvivors, said passengers endured four days and nights at sea as conditions rapidly deteriorated. In an attempt ŌĆŗto avoid patrols, traffickers forced passengers into cramped storage compartments meant for fish and nets.
ŌĆ£There was hardly any ŌĆŗoxygen," he told Reuters, adding that at least 30 people died from suffocation before the boat capsized. "We could not breathe."
When the boat overturned, hundreds were thrown into the sea. Islam estimated that around 240 people were still onboard at the time, including ŌĆŗabout 20 women and several children. Only a handful survived.
Islam was among a small group who initially made ŌĆŗit out alive. A passing Bangladeshi oil vessel rescued four survivors, who then alerted the crew to others still in ŌĆīthe ŌüĀwater.
ŌĆ£They later found five more people,ŌĆØ he said.
He described a dangerous, multi-stage journey that began on April 4, when passengers first left on a small fishing boat before being transferred to a larger trawler near Myanmar waters. At one point, they were forced to hide in bushes to avoid detection by patrols.
The tragedy highlights ŌĆŗthe desperation of Rohingya refugees, ŌĆŗmany of whom continue ŌüĀto risk perilous sea journeys to countries such as Malaysia, Indonesia and Thailand.
Aid agencies warn the situation is worsening as humanitarian support declines. A recent report by ŌĆŗthe International Rescue Committee (IRC), based on a survey of 500 households in CoxŌĆÖs ŌĆŗBazar, found ŌüĀjust 2% of Rohingya parents feel hopeful about their children's future, compared with 84% among host communities.
With food rations reduced to as little as $7 per person per month, many families are resorting to extreme coping measures. Nearly 69% of ŌüĀrefugee households ŌĆŗreport children dropping out of school, while half say their ŌĆŗchildren have been forced into labour.
The IRC urged donors and authorities to shift from emergency aid to long-term solutions, warning that without sustained ŌĆŗsupport, both refugees and host communities face deepening poverty and vulnerability.

item.title: Georgia overhauls higher education as it shifts away from the West
item.description:
TBILISI, April 15 (Reuters) - When anti-government protests began in Georgia in late 2024, Luka Mishveladze started sleeping on the floor at his university building to be closer to student rallies.
Some 18 months later, the 20-year-old student is now a protest ŌĆŗorganiser, demonstrating outside the same building in the capital Tbilisi against government reforms of higher education that look set to force his university's closure.
"It was hard ŌĆīfor me to realise that this was happening in reality, that I am losing my university, the place I am used to calling home," Mishveladze told Reuters.
Opponents of the government see the overhaul adopted in February as part of a broader anti-Western shift under the governing Georgian Dream party that began in earnest after Russia's invasion of Ukraine in 2022.
Once seen in the West as a burgeoning democracy, on the fast track to joining the European Union and ŌĆŗescaping Russia's orbit, Georgia is increasingly turning its back on the West and deepening ties with Moscow.
Georgian Dream says the higher education reforms, affecting funding and ŌĆŗthe geographical redistribution of faculties, are intended to better reflect the demands of the labour market and promote regional universities.
It says it wants to ŌüĀreduce what it calls an excessive concentration of higher education institutions in Tbilisi and stop the "irrational" use of resources.
Detractors say the reforms are further evidence that the government is turning the country ŌĆŗof 3.7 million away from the West, more than three decades after Georgia gained independence from the Soviet Union.
Under the reforms, guided by the principle of "one faculty, one city", only one university in ŌĆŗa given city will be allowed to offer certain degree programmes.
The government will decide which academic disciplines can be taught at each of the 19 public universities - attended by over half of Georgia's university students - and redistribute admission quotas.
Tbilisi's Ilia State University (ISU), where Mishveladze and about 17,300 others study, will be hit particularly hard. One of the country's top-ranked research institutions, it is widely seen in Georgia as outspoken and liberal, and has strong ŌĆŗties with European partner institutions.
ISU says over 90% of its programmes will be cut and that it will have to wind down over three years. This autumn, ISU will be able to admit ŌĆŗonly 335 new undergraduates, down from the 3,770 admitted last year.
"No other sector in Georgia has been so integrated into the European space than higher education. So they're killing it," said Ketevan Darakhvelidze, the chancellor ŌĆīof ISU. "The more ŌüĀisolated Georgia will be, the better for the government."
Seven other universities received cuts to their admission quotas although only ISU says it faces closure.
Shalva Tabatadze, who runs an education policy research centre, said state funding had often been used in Georgia to support universities "which have political affiliations", describing this as "problematic".
A March report by a mission from the Organization for Security and Co-operation in Europe, a security and rights body, found "marked democratic backsliding" in Georgia and briefly mentioned the higher education reforms. It cited concerns in Georgia that the redistribution of faculties was intended to disperse large numbers of ŌĆŗstudent protesters.
The government did not respond to Reuters' ŌĆŗrequests for comment for this article. Education ŌüĀMinister Givi Mikanadze, a member of a state committee that drew up the reforms, did not respond to Reuters' interview requests.
Georgian Dream, in power since 2012, says its policies are not authoritarian and that it is trying to keep peace in Georgia, which lost a short war to Russia ŌĆŗin 2008. It accuses opposition parties of seeking to foment violent coups.
Resistance to the reforms among students and university staff ŌĆŗhas injected new fervour into ŌüĀthe nightly anti-government rallies that began in late 2024, when the government announced it was suspending talks on joining the EU.
The relatively small rallies do not pose a big threat to the government but have attracted support at universities across Georgia.
"They are going to shut down every free-thinking institution capable of critical reasoning," said ISU sociology professor Nino Rcheulishvili.
Since it was founded in 2006, ISU has forged links ŌüĀwith 145 European ŌĆŗinstitutions to offer double-degree programmes.
"All those programmes are at risk," said Nino Doborjginidze, ISU's rector, adding that the university ŌĆŗcould lose access to most foreign grants under recent legislative changes.
In a small victory for the protesters, the government scrapped a planned merger of two Tbilisi universities in February, but some young people still see little future in Georgia.
Davit Mshvenieradze, 20, ŌĆŗis among students who say they may leave, but he wants Georgian Dream ousted first.
"If they are here (in power), I want to stay here and protest against them," he said.

item.title: Ukraine broaches 'stolen' Russian grain cargo on call with Israel
item.description:
April 15 (Reuters) - Ukrainian Foreign Minister Andrii Sybiha held a call with his Israeli counterpart Gideon Sa'ar to discuss ŌĆŗa Russian vessel carrying what he described ŌĆīas grain stolen from Ukraine that was allowed to dock in an Israeli port.
Kyiv considers all grain produced in the ŌĆŗfour regions Russia claimed as its own ŌĆŗsince invading Ukraine in 2022, and Crimea, annexed ŌüĀby Russia in 2014, to have been stolen ŌĆŗby Moscow.
"I stressed that the illegal export of stolen ŌĆŗUkrainian agricultural products is part of Russia's broader war effort," Sybiha said late on Tuesday in a post on X. "Such ŌĆŗillegal trade with stolen goods must not be ŌĆŗallowed."
Russia refers to the four regions as its "new territories", but they ŌĆīare ŌüĀstill internationally recognised as Ukrainian.
Sybiha said in March that Russia moved more than 2 million tons of stolen Ukrainian grain through the Black Sea last year. ŌĆŗUkrainian official ŌĆŗestimated in ŌüĀAugust that Russia has stolen 15 million tons of Ukrainian grain since the ŌĆŗstart of the full-scale war.
The foreign minister ŌĆŗadded ŌüĀthat the two also talked about security matters and the situation in the Middle East.
"We reaffirmed our mutual ŌüĀinterest ŌĆŗin advancing the bilateral agenda and ŌĆŗmaintaining an active dialogue, including on security-related matters," he said.

item.title: Hungary's Magyar will talk to MOL leadership, focus on fuel supplies
item.description:
BUDAPEST, April 15 (Reuters) - HungaryŌĆÖs election-winner Peter Magyar said on Wednesday he would need to hold talks with the leadership of oil company MOL (MOLB.BU) , opens new tab , adding that ensuring ŌĆŗfuel security would be the top priority in the coming weeks.
Magyar's centre-right TISZA (Respect and ŌĆīFreedom) party won a landslide victory in Sunday's election, ending nationalist Prime Minister Viktor Orban's 16-year rule.
In early March, Orban imposed a cap on fuel prices as soaring oil prices, fuelled by the war in Iran, ŌĆŗdrove global diesel and petrol prices higher.
His government also banned the export of crude ŌĆŗoil, diesel and 95-octane petrol and said it would release 45 days ŌüĀworth of state fuel reserves, as Hungary contended with a halt in supplies through a ŌĆŗkey pipeline carrying Russian oil via Ukraine.
Data from the Hungarian Hydrocarbon Stockpiling Association showed that ŌĆŗHungaryŌĆÖs strategic oil and oil-product reserves fell to 44 days of net imports by the end of March, from 91 days at the end of February. In a statement on Tuesday, the association said replenishment ŌĆŗwas under way and reserves had since risen to 53 days of net imports.
European Union ŌĆŗmember states must maintain 90 days of net imports.
"With the strategic oil reserves, the acting government has ŌĆīa ŌüĀhuge responsibility in what it does in the next 20-30 days," Magyar said. "Everyone hopes that this Druzhba pipeline can restart by the end of April but even if it does it will take some time for strategic reserves to be replenished."
"The most important is that security ŌĆŗof supply must be ŌĆŗensured in the next ŌüĀfew weeks under the outgoing government and then in the first days of the TISZA government."
MOL said in a response to emailed ŌĆŗReuters questions on Tuesday that its crude oil supply was uninterrupted as ŌĆŗoil was ŌüĀcoming via the Adriatic pipeline even though supplies via the Druzhba pipeline were still suspended.
"Following agreements with Libya, Kazakhstan, Norway, and Saudi Arabia, we have also reached agreements on deliveries with companies ŌüĀin ŌĆŗthe United States. The Danube Refinery is operating at ŌĆŗreduced capacity due to a fire that occurred at one of our units in October," MOL said.
"Fuel supply in ŌĆŗHungary remains uninterrupted".

item.title: Former military chief to take over as Philippine National Security Adviser
item.description:
MANILA, April 15 (Reuters) - Philippine National Security Adviser Eduardo Ano has resigned due to health ŌĆŗreasons, a Palace official said on Wednesday, ŌĆīand will be replaced by former military chief Eduardo Oban.
"The President has accepted the resignation of National ŌĆŗSecurity Adviser Eduardo Ano. Secretary Ano ŌĆŗhas decided to retire from public service ŌüĀafter decades of continuous service in government ŌĆŗand the military," communications undersecretary Claire Castro told ŌĆŗa briefing.

item.title: Back to square one? Markets bet Iran war is over
item.description:
LONDON, April 15 (Reuters) - Global investors and the International Monetary Fund seem to agree the Iran war is likely over - bar the shouting. The energy market is less sure, and there could be an awful lot of shouting, but many asset prices have returned to square one, and markets now see the conflict's impact only at the margins.
The IMF's unenviable task of forecasting global economic growth during ŌĆŗa Middle East war and energy shock left as many questions as answers, and a plethora of different scenarios , opens new tab . Take your pick.
The Fund's central conclusion was probably most powerful in what it didn't ŌĆīdo rather than what it did.
The IMF made no change to its global gross domestic product (GDP) growth forecast for 2027 compared with its last update in January - three months before the war. Yes, it clipped this year's expansion and there's a lower starting point as a result. But next year is essentially expected to develop much like the Fund saw it pre-war in January and even back in October - 3.2% world growth.
Even before those forecasts were released on Tuesday, many in the markets had effectively decided the same .
Wall Street stocks returned to ŌĆŗpre-war levels of February 27 on Monday, completing a 565-point, near-10% round trip. The VIX "fear index" of implied volatility (.VIX) , opens new tab has subsided to its lowest since February.
Global stocks captured by the MSCI All Country World Index (.MIWD00000PUS) , opens new tab are not ŌĆŗquite back there yet - but they're just 1% from the record highs set two months ago. The pivotal euro/dollar exchange rate is back to February levels too.
Much has been made in recent weeks of how little full-year corporate earnings forecasts have been affected by the oil shock. Flattered by tech and energy firm upgrades, aggregate 2026 earnings growth estimates have actually climbed 2-3 percentage points since ŌĆŗthe war started.
Calendar 2027 growth forecasts are still cruising at 18% and 11% for U.S. and European blue chips, respectively.
Real or imagined, 12-month forward price/earnings valuations for the S&P 500 (.SPX) , opens new tab and MSCI all-country cheapened more than 10% in the month ŌĆŗthrough early April.
The temptation to screen out the war, bet on eventual de-escalation and focus on the year ahead has been hard to pass up.
That logic drove BlackRock - the world's largest asset manager - to switch back this week to an overweight position in U.S. stocks and emerging markets.
It's not as if nothing has changed, of course.
With global oil and gas still bound up in the ongoing Gulf hiatus, front-month crude futures are still up a third and natural gas and fertilizer prices remain elevated. Airlines fear shortages of jet fuel ahead.
Even ŌĆŗthe IMF admits the longer the Middle East energy crunch persists, the more its "adverse scenarios" come into view.
Billionaire investor Ken Griffin warned on Tuesday of a "treacherous moment" and reckons closure of the Strait of Hormuz for 6-12 months will end in global recession.
But ŌĆŗthe way oil futures are behaving, that's still not the market's best guess. Brent crude futures through this year and next show some normalization. Even though they are still 10-15% higher than February levels, ready-reckoners - quick, rule-of-thumb models - suggest that would only shave 0.2-0.3 percentage points off ŌĆīglobal GDP ŌüĀgrowth.
That's simply not enough for portfolios to go to ground - whatever you think of the wider inflationary or political fallout.
The interest rate and bond markets have not returned to pre-war levels - the lingering risk of resurgent inflation and the outside chance of central bank rate rises keep them there.
While that's still a guessing game that hinges on the length of the conflict too, 10-year Treasury borrowing rates are still some 30 basis points above February levels and Federal Reserve futures markets only see about a 30% chance of rate cuts resuming by year-end. Mortgage rates have barreled higher too and corporate bonds have been rattled by jitters in the private credit world.
Global asset managers polled by Bank ŌĆŗof America this month clearly scaled back their early-year bullishness, ŌĆŗwith sentiment gauges slipping back to last summer's ŌüĀfunk. Inflation expectations have climbed too.
But chiming with oil futures, the weighted average of forecasts for year-end crude prices was $84 per barrel and "long oil" along with long global chip stocks were identified as the most crowded trades on the planet.
Less than 10% of funds surveyed expect recession ahead. While average cash levels stayed at 10-month highs last month, they ŌĆŗremain well below prior extremes during last year's April tariff shock or the Ukraine invasion in 2022.
That's not exactly complacency - but neither is it a panicked herd.
Second-guessing ŌĆŗthe conflict will remain a ŌüĀdaily obsession, even if an increasingly price-insensitive one. But market de-escalation has already begun. Only time will tell if that's premature.
(The opinions expressed here are those of Mike Dolan , a columnist for Reuters.)
Save the date: On April 23 at 1300 GMT/9 a.m. EDT, ROI columnists Mike Dolan and Jamie McGeever will join LSEG for a webinar discussion, "Markets Unpacked with Reuters Open Interest: Rethinking safe havens in uncertain times." Click here to sign up. , opens new tab
Enjoying this column? Check out Reuters Open Interest (ROI), , opens new tab your essential new source for global ŌüĀfinancial commentary.

And listen to the Morning Bid daily podcast on Apple , opens new tab , Spotify , opens new tab , or the Reuters app , opens new tab . Subscribe to hear Reuters journalists discuss the biggest news in markets ŌĆŗand finance seven days a week.

item.title: Europe's summer flights at risk as Iran war exposes refining decline
item.description:
LONDON, April 15 (Reuters) - European holidaymakers may need to rethink their summer travel plans as airlines brace for possible flight groundings amid a mounting fuel supply crunch triggered by the Iran war . This crisis marks another wake-up call for a region grappling with energy security in an era of heightened geopolitical tensions.
EuropeŌĆÖs oil and gas industry has shrunk dramatically ŌĆŗover the past 25 years: North Sea production has dwindled and more than 30 refineries - 16% of refining capacity - have been shuttered across the continent.
The contraction reflects a combination ŌĆīof shrinking domestic demand, rising overseas competition and, increasingly over the past decade, a government-led drive to curb greenhouse gas emissions.
The upshot is that Europe has become heavily reliant on energy imports - a vulnerable underbelly painfully exposed twice in four years.
First came the abrupt loss of Russian natural gas supplies following MoscowŌĆÖs invasion of Ukraine in 2022. Then the closure of the critical Strait of Hormuz during the Iran war - now in its seventh week - has once again laid bare the risks of EuropeŌĆÖs growing exposure to international energy shocks.
Nowhere is that ŌĆŗvulnerability clearer than in aviation fuel.
Europe is more dependent on jet fuel imports than for any other transport fuel. The region consumed about 1.6 million barrels per day (bpd) last year, ŌĆŗof which roughly 500,000 bpd were imported, according to the International Energy Agency. Around 75% of those imports came from the Middle East.
That dependence has left ŌüĀEurope acutely exposed to the Gulf supply shock.
Refineries in Asia, which source about 60% of their crude imports from the Middle East, were forced to scale back operations by some 3 million bpd between February ŌĆŗand April, according to the IEA.
That pullback triggered acute fuel shortages across Asia, prompting governments to respond with fuel-saving measures, subsidies and, in some cases, restrictions on fuel exports - including in China.
With Asian demand competing ŌĆŗfor scarce barrels, benchmark European jet fuel prices surged to an all-time high of $1,800 a ton on March 18, before easing to around $1,450 this week. Jet fuel refining margins have also surged to record highs, exceeding $100 per barrel, more than five times their level a year ago, according to HiLo Analytics.
Europe has struggled to replace the lost Middle Eastern barrels. Jet fuel imports fell to roughly 437,000 bpd in March, down 13% from the 2025 average, and are set to slide further ŌĆŗto just 275,000 bpd in April, according to analytics firm Kpler.
Importantly, around 125,000 bpd of AprilŌĆÖs imports are expected to arrive from the U.S. - a shift to long-haul sourcing that underscores just how stretched ŌĆŗthe supply chain has become.
Regional stockpiles are also being depleted rapidly. Independently held jet fuel and kerosene stocks in the Amsterdam-Rotterdam-Antwerp refining and storage hub fell nearly 8% last week to 646,000 metric tons, the lowest level since March ŌĆī2023, according to ŌüĀDutch consultancy Insights Global.
The last cargoes of jet fuel from the Middle East are now approaching Europe. That means finding replacement barrels will soon prove even more challenging ŌĆō and more expensive.
Industry group Airports Council International Europe (ACI) warned last week that the region could face a systemic jet fuel shortage within just three weeks.
With supplies critically tight, airlines and oil importers face two unpalatable options: bid up prices to compete with Asia for scarce barrels, or cut consumption.
Lufthansa (LHAG.DE) , opens new tab CEO Carsten Spohr warned on Tuesday that grounding aircraft "may be unavoidable" as shortages hit major airports.
Europe's biggest airline has drawn up contingency plans that include cutting capacity by up to 5%, potentially grounding 20-40 older, less fuel-efficient planes earmarked for early ŌĆŗretirement.
Spohr said Lufthansa, which hedges fuel up ŌĆŗto 24 months ahead and was 85% hedged ŌüĀas of December 31, was well placed in the short term.
Not all carriers are so insulated . EasyJet (EZJ.L) , opens new tab has hedged the majority of its fuel needs for the coming months, but those positions begin to unwind by the end of summer, when the British budget carrier has warned ticket prices could rise.
Airlines for Europe (A4E) has requested ŌĆŗthat the European Union introduce crisis response measures, including: EU-level monitoring of jet fuel supplies, a temporary suspension of the bloc's carbon market for ŌĆŗaviation, and scrapping certain aviation taxes. ŌüĀThe industry group's requests were outlined in a document seen by Reuters.
The crisis lays bare the strategic cost of allowing domestic energy infrastructure - from refineries to upstream oil and gas production - to wither without reliable alternatives in place.
Governments' options for short-term relief are limited, and the most obvious ones, such as temporarily easing carbon-related taxes on refiners and airlines, clash directly with climate commitments.
Over the longer term, governments could offer refiners incentives to produce low-carbon ŌüĀfuels, expand sustainable ŌĆŗaviation fuel production or deploy carbon-capture technologies - policies aimed at reconciling the dual challenge of boosting domestic capacity while cutting emissions.
For ŌĆŗnow though, the outlook for European airlines - and summer travellers - is bleak.
(The opinions expressed here are those of Ron Bousso , a columnist for Reuters.)
Enjoying this column? Check out Reuters Open Interest (ROI), , opens new tab your essential new source for global financial commentary. Follow ROI on LinkedIn, , opens new tab and X. , opens new tab
And listen to the Morning ŌĆŗBid daily podcast on Apple , opens new tab , Spotify , opens new tab , or the Reuters app , opens new tab . Subscribe to hear Reuters journalists discuss the biggest news in markets and finance seven days a week.

item.title: Exclusive: China weighs curbs on exports of solar manufacturing equipment to US
item.description:
BEIJING/LOS ANGELES, April 15 (Reuters) - Chinese officials have held initial talks with providers of equipment to make solar panels as they consider limiting exports of the most advanced technology to the United States, said five people with knowledge of the consultations.
Such ŌĆŗa clampdown would risk investments by U.S. firms and set back a race for space-based computing, as China, estimated to make more than 80% of the world's ŌĆīsolar panel components, is also home to the top 10 suppliers of equipment to make solar cells.
No rule has been finalised, and the talks have not advanced to the stage of canvassing formal feedback from an industry grappling with severe overcapacity after years of aggressive expansion, two of the sources said.
China's commerce ministry and its state council, or cabinet, did not immediately respond to faxed requests for comment from Reuters.
If adopted, such a move could threaten plans by U.S. firms, such as Tesla (TSLA.O) , opens new tab , to build new factories or expand existing ones in efforts to boost local production.
It would ŌĆŗalso widen export controls in another area of technology where China has a lead, building on Beijing's move to control rare earth exports a year ŌüĀago in response to U.S. tariffs.
The step comes at a time when the rivalry between China and the United States has spilled into the race to produce space-based computing powered by solar ŌĆŗpanels, a focus for Tesla CEO Elon Musk.
Other U.S. tech companies such as Google (GOOGL.O) , opens new tab and Amazon (AMZN.O) , opens new tab are investing in ground-based solar and energy storage systems, even as they count on similar orbital data centres ŌĆŗto satisfy AI's growing demand for power.
Analysts who track China's solar industry and executives have braced for export controls, in part because concern is growing over efforts by Musk and others to boost solar panel production in the United States, reducing reliance on China.
Musk seeks to exploit China's solar downturn to acquire equipment and talent, Xu Xiaohua, chairman of Anhui Huasun Energy, told the Caijing business magazine ŌĆŗthis year.
He called for greater efforts by Chinese companies to retain their lead in technology.
Huasun did not immediately respond to an emailed request for comment.
The prospect of China's curb comes amid preparations for a ŌĆŗsummit of leaders Xi Jinping and Donald Trump in the Chinese capital next month that both sides view as an opportunity to preserve more stable ties on trade matters.
Reuters reported last month that Tesla was looking ŌĆīto buy $2.9 ŌüĀbillion of equipment for making solar panels from Chinese suppliers such as Suzhou Maxwell Technologies (300751.SZ) , opens new tab , which was seeking export approval from the commerce ministry.
Musk has said solar power could supply all the electricity needs of the United States and Tesla has pushed a goal of making 100 gigawatts of solar manufacturing on American soil before 2028.
"Tesla succeeding in its solar self-sufficiency push could prove a nightmare for China's world-leading solar manufacturers," research firm Trivium China, focused on Chinese government policy, said in a note this month.
Not only would they lose a major potential customer, but they could ŌĆŗface the emergence of a formidable new competitor ŌĆŗat a time when they are already ŌüĀunder enormous financial pressure, it added.
"Beijing won't sit idly by as its industrial champions inadvertently aid the industrial policies of rival countries."
Suzhou Maxwell Technologies was visited by officials after Reuters reported on Tesla's talks with Chinese suppliers, said three of the sources who sought anonymity because the talks with regulators ŌĆŗare confidential.
Talks between regulators and Suzhou Maxwell Technologies focused on possible curbs on shipments to the United States, including high-end equipment needed to produce higher ŌĆŗefficiency panels, a technology ŌüĀknown as HJT, two of the sources said.
Suzhou Maxwell and Tesla did not immediately respond to emailed requests for comment.
Reuters could not determine how sweeping any curbs would be in terms of other export markets, when any licensing requirement could take effect or what products would be covered.
In 2025, China threatened licensing requirements for exports of a related technology, high-end batteries and materials for the energy storage ŌüĀsystems demanded ŌĆŗby large-scale solar projects, but paused implementation until November this year.
Other Chinese solar producers have continued to negotiate and ship ŌĆŗsolar manufacturing equipment to the United States, including other firms bidding for the Tesla order, two people said.
HJT or heterojunction solar technology boosts power output by using a wafer of crystalline silicon sandwiched between very thin layers of silicon in ŌĆŗthe cell, allowing more of the electrons generated by sunlight to be captured as electricity.

item.title: Russia pledges further oil supplies to Cuba after dispatching crude cargo
item.description:
MOSCOW, April 15 (Reuters) - Russia will continue helping fuel-hungry Cuba with crucial supplies of oil, Foreign Minister Sergei Lavrov said on Wednesday, two weeks after Moscow sent a tanker with ŌĆŗaround 700,000 barrels of crude to the Caribbean island.
Washington stopped oil ŌĆŗexports to Cuba from its main ally Venezuela after capturing ŌüĀVenezuelan President Nicolas Maduro on January 3, triggering acute fuel shortages ŌĆŗacross the communist-ruled island of almost 11 million people.
President Donald Trump has ŌĆŗthreatened punishing tariffs on countries sending crude to Cuba as he seeks to put pressure on the government. The U.S. later allowed the Russian oil delivery to Cuba, this ŌĆŗyear's first by Moscow, for humanitarian reasons.
Another major supplier, Mexico, halted ŌĆŗits shipments.
Lavrov, on a visit to China, said Russia will provide humanitarian aid to ŌĆīCuba, ŌüĀits long-standing ally.
"We have dispatched the first tanker with 100,000 tons (700,000 barrels) of oil for Cuba. Of course, this will probably last for a couple of months - I'm not a specialist," he told a briefing at ŌĆŗthe end of the ŌĆŗtwo-day visit.
"But ŌüĀI have no doubt that we will continue providing such assistance, and that (China) will, of course, continue to ŌĆŗtake part in this cooperation as well," added Lavrov, ŌĆŗwithout referring ŌüĀto the issue of U.S. permission or not for future deliveries.
Cuba produces less than a third of the oil it requires.
Though it cleared the recent ŌüĀRussian ŌĆŗdelivery, the Trump administration said it would ŌĆŗreview further oil shipments to Cuba on a "case-by-case" basis.
Lavrov said he hoped the U.S. will not ŌĆŗreturn to times of "colonial wars."

item.title: Malaysia's Petronas says fuel supply secured at stations until June-end
item.description:
April 15 (Reuters) - Malaysia's ŌĆŗstate oil firm Petronas (IPO-PETO.KL) , opens new tab ŌĆīsaid on Wednesday that fuel supply at all its ŌĆŗstations is secured until ŌĆŗthe end of June.
The company ŌüĀsaid it is ŌĆŗactively managing its supply chain ŌĆŗto ensure sufficient stock across its network amid the global ŌĆŗenergy crisis due to ŌĆŗthe Middle East war.
Around 50% ŌĆīof ŌüĀMalaysia's fuel needs are being supplied by Petronas Dagangan (PETR.KL) , opens new tab , the parent firm ŌĆŗsaid.
ŌĆŗMalaysia has pledged ŌüĀto boost the use of biodiesel ŌĆŗto deal with ŌĆŗthe ŌüĀsupply constraints as its economy comes under increasing strain ŌüĀfrom ŌĆŗthe Middle ŌĆŗEast crisis.

item.title: Philippines grants banks┬Āflexibility to extend loan payments amid energy crisis
item.description:
MANILA, April 15 (Reuters) - The Philippines will allow banks to give borrowers more time to settle ŌĆŗtheir loans as part of a package ŌĆīof relief measures designed to support consumers and businesses hit by the energy crisis, its central bank said on ŌĆŗWednesday.
This includes grace periods of up ŌĆŗto six months, and up to one ŌüĀyear for agricultural loans, subject to ŌĆŗbank assessments, according to a resolution approved last ŌĆŗweek by the Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas (BSP).
Banks may also temporarily avoid classifying these loans as past due ŌĆŗor non-performing.
"BSP-supervised financial institutions are expected to ŌĆŗexercise prudent management in availing these measures and to ŌĆīensure ŌüĀrelief is only extended to borrowers whose repayment capacity has been materially affected by the energy emergency," the BSP said in ŌĆŗa statement.
The ŌĆŗBSP also ŌüĀurged banks to temporarily suspend fees for online transactions, to ŌĆŗease costs for consumers and businesses.
On ŌĆŗMonday, ŌüĀPhilippine President Ferdinand Marcos Jr suspended excise taxes on kerosene and liquefied petroleum gas to help ŌüĀthe ŌĆŗpublic cope with soaring ŌĆŗfuel prices driven by the conflict in the Middle ŌĆŗEast.

item.title: Pope Leo, attacked again by Trump, flies to Cameroon on Africa tour
item.description:
ABOARD THE PAPAL FLIGHT, April 15 (Reuters) - Pope Leo set off for Cameroon on Wednesday, ŌĆīwhere he is expected to appeal for peace in the simmering conflict in the country's English-speaking regions on the second leg of an ambitious 10-day tour of four African countries.
Leo, who was attacked again , opens new tab overnight by U.S. President Donald Trump over ŌĆŗthe pope's opposition to the Iran war , will arrive from Algeria, where he blasted violations of international ŌĆŗlaw by "neocolonial" world powers.
Leo left Algiers on Wednesday morning and was expected to ŌüĀland in Yaounde, Cameroon's capital, around 3:20 p.m. (1420 GMT), ahead of a meeting with President ŌĆŗPaul Biya and an address to national leaders.
Cameroon is a former German colony that was divided by ŌĆŗBritain and France after World War One. Over the last decade, thousands have died in violence between government forces and separatist groups in its two anglophone regions.
A separatist alliance said on Monday it would observe a three-day "safe travel ŌĆŗpassage" to allow civilians and visitors to move freely during the pope's visit.
Leo, originally from Chicago, ŌĆŗwas chosen to lead the 1.4 billion-member Catholic Church last May after the death of Pope Francis .
He kept a relatively ŌĆīlow ŌüĀprofile in his first 10 months, but in recent weeks has become outspoken on a range of issues, and a firm critic of the U.S.-Israeli war against Iran.
Trump called the pontiff " terrible " on social media on Sunday, and doubled down on Tuesday night , despite widespread ŌĆŗbacklash from U.S. Christians across ŌĆŗthe political spectrum.
The ŌüĀpope told Reuters on Monday that he planned to keep criticizing the war, regardless of Trump's comments.
Leo, aged 70, relatively young for a pope and in ŌĆŗgood health, is undertaking one of the most complicated tours arranged for ŌĆŗa pontiff ŌüĀin decades.
He is traversing nearly 18,000 km (11,185 miles) over 18 flights to 11 cities and towns and will also visit Angola and Equatorial Guinea.
The pope will travel on Thursday to Bamenda, Cameroon's largest English-speaking ŌüĀcity, holding ŌĆŗMass and a "meeting for peace" in a cathedral.
The biggest event ŌĆŗof Leo's tour will likely come in Cameroon on Friday, when the Vatican said some 600,000 are expected for a ŌĆŗMass in the coastal city of Douala.

item.title: Exclusive: China moves to block entrance to disputed South China Sea shoal, images show
item.description:
HONG KONG/MANILA, April 15 (Reuters) - China is employing ships and a barrier to tighten control of the entrance to the Scarborough Shoal in the South China Sea amid roiling ŌĆŗtension with the Philippines over the disputed feature, satellite imagery obtained by Reuters shows.
Scarborough is one of Asia's most hotly disputed maritime sites, where ŌĆīsome diplomats and analysts fear long-running frictions and confrontations could degenerate into armed conflict.
The presence of four fishing boats, a Chinese naval or coast guard ship and a new floating barrier comes as the Philippines sends its own coast guard and fisheries vessels to support its fishermen frequently driven away by larger Chinese patrols.
Photographs taken on April 10 and 11 show the fishing boats anchored along ŌĆŗthe entrance to the shoal, in addition to a floating barrier stretching across it in the April 11 image.
Satellite image provider Vantor, formerly Maxar Technologies, ŌĆŗsaid a probable Chinese naval or coast guard patrol vessel can be glimpsed just outside the entrance on April 10.
China's defence ministry ŌüĀdid not immediately respond to Reuters' requests for comment on the deployment to the entrance to the shoal or its timing.
The traditionally rich fishing ŌĆŗground of the Scarborough Shoal lies entirely within the Philippines' exclusive economic zone, but China also claims it as part of its territory.
Last year, China approved establishment of a ŌĆŗnational nature reserve there, alarming Philippine security officials, who called the move a "clear pretext for occupation".
Jay Tarriela, a spokesperson for the Philippine coast guard, told Reuters on Wednesday the Chinese government had installed a 352-m (1,150-ft)floating barrier at the entrance on April 10 and April 11.
"Six Chinese maritime militia vessels were observed within the shoal, while three others were spotted outside, seemingly obstructing the entrance to BDM," ŌĆŗhe said.
He was referring to the shoal by its Philippine name of Bajo de Masinloc, while China calls it Huangyan Island.
While the Philippines coast guard has cut barriers ŌĆŗin the past, Tarriela said the Chinese side appear to have removed the latest one since the weekend, but the Philippine Navy says its patrols continue.
"According to our assessment in the ŌĆīpast, they ŌüĀconsistently exhibit suspicion whenever they monitor a group of Filipino fishing boats," Tarriela added.
Ten Chinese coast guard vessels were sighted at the shoal from April 5 to April 12, Philippine Navy spokesperson Roy Trinidad said on Tuesday.
Despite the competing claims, sovereignty has never been established and the shoal is effectively under Beijing's control even if Philippine boats still try to operate there.
In January, the militaries of the Philippines and the United States sailed together at the shoal in the 11th such drill by the ŌĆŗtreaty allies.
Military engagements between them have soared ŌĆŗunder Philippine President Ferdinand Marcos Jr., ŌüĀwho has pivoted closer to Washington in response to China's growing presence in the busy waterway of the South China Sea.
Thousands of troops from both countries are set to begin large-scale exercises across the Philippine archipelago this month, including in Zambales, whose coast is ŌĆŗabout 120 nautical miles from the Scarborough Shoal.
Diplomats say the drills and broader tensions are being closely watched amid fears ŌĆŗthat China could take advantage ŌüĀof perceptions that the U.S. is distracted by the Iran conflict and its effort to re-open the vital Straits of Hormuz waterway.
China has kept a deployment of coast guard and fishing trawlers at the shoal since seizing it in 2012 after a standoff with the Philippines.
Manila has said Chinese maritime militia operate some trawlers at the shoal and other disputed ŌüĀareas of ŌĆŗthe South China Sea, but Beijing has never acknowledged this.
A landmark 2016 ruling on various South China ŌĆŗSea issues by the Permanent Court of Arbitration backed Manila, but establishing sovereignty over Scarborough Shoal was outside its scope.
The court said Beijing's blockade there violated international law as it was a traditional fishing ground ŌĆŗfor several countries, including China, the Philippines and Vietnam.

item.title: Hungary election winner Magyar expects to form government by mid-May
item.description:
BUDAPEST, April 15 (Reuters) - Election winner Peter Magyar said ŌĆŗon Wednesday that ŌĆīPresident Tamas Sulyok would ask him at ŌĆŗthe inaugural session ŌĆŗof parliament to form ŌüĀHungary's next government, ŌĆŗwhich could be sworn ŌĆŗin by the middle of next month or ŌĆŗsomewhat sooner.
"The President ŌĆŗhas informed me that he ŌĆīwill ŌüĀask me at the inaugural session of the new parliament ŌĆŗto ŌĆŗbe ŌüĀPrime Minister and form a government, ŌĆŗas the ŌĆŗleader ŌüĀof the party that got the most ŌüĀvotes," ŌĆŗMagyar said ŌĆŗafter meeting Sulyok.

item.title: Hungary's election winner Magyar to meet president, calls on him to resign
item.description:
BUDAPEST, April 15 (Reuters) - Hungary's election ŌĆŗwinner Peter Magyar, ŌĆīleader of the TISZA ŌĆŗparty which ŌĆŗwon a landslide ŌüĀvictory in ŌĆŗSunday's election, ŌĆŗarrived for a meeting with President ŌĆŗTamas Sulyok ŌĆŗon Wednesday.
As Magyar ŌĆīwas ŌüĀgoing into the meeting, speaking to reporters ŌĆŗhe ŌĆŗreiterated ŌüĀthat Sulyok must ŌĆŗleave his ŌĆŗrole ŌüĀafter a TISZA ŌüĀgovernment ŌĆŗtakes office.

item.title: China's Xi calls for strategic clarity, political security with Vietnam
item.description:
BEIJING, April 15 (Reuters) - Chinese President Xi Jinping called for ŌĆŗChina and Vietnam to ŌĆīmaintain a high degree of strategic clarity and ŌĆŗprioritise safeguarding political security ŌĆŗduring a meeting with ŌüĀhis Vietnamese counterpart on ŌĆŗWednesday.
Vietnam's top leader, To ŌĆŗLam, is in China until Friday in his first overseas ŌĆŗtrip since being ŌĆŗelected state president last week, a ŌĆīsign ŌüĀof warming ties between the two Communist neighbours.
The countries have grown closer ŌĆŗin ŌĆŗrecent ŌüĀyears, cooperating in railways, technology and ŌĆŗother sectors, even ŌĆŗas ŌüĀthey remain at odds over maritime boundaries in ŌüĀthe ŌĆŗSouth China Sea.

item.title: Portugal, unlike Spain, rejects separate European army
item.description:
LISBON, April 15 (Reuters) - Portugal is against the creation of a separate European army and instead backs strengthening and modernising its armed forces within the ŌĆŗU.S.-led NATO alliance, the country's defence minister said, contrasting with neighbouring ŌĆīSpain's position.
Nuno Melo said late on Tuesday that Portugal was a founding NATO member which valued the United States, describing Washington as a fundamental transatlantic partner.
"We're not in favour ŌĆŗof a single European army," he told a parliamentary committee, adding that ŌĆŗPortugal needed to invest in its armed forces to ensure that ŌüĀthey were able to fulfil their assigned missions within NATO.
Amid doubts over ŌĆŗU.S. President Donald Trump's commitment to Europe's security, Spain has urged the European Union ŌĆŗto move towards creating a separate joint army as a deterrent.
NATO chief Mark Rutte has dismissed calls for a European army, arguing it would cost member states far more than the ŌĆŗ5% of gross domestic product that NATO countries - except Spain - have agreed to ŌĆŗspend on defence and related investments by 2035. Madrid said it could meet its commitments ŌĆīby ŌüĀspending 2.1% of GDP .
Melo said that Portugal increased its defence spending, under NATO criteria, to 6.12 billion euros ($7.22 billion) - or 2% of GDP - in 2025, four years ahead of the original schedule. In 2024, spending was around 4.5 billion euros, ŌĆŗor 1.58% of GDP.
Spain ŌĆŗalso reached its ŌüĀtarget of 2% of GDP last year, spending 33.5 billion euros - a 44.5% increase from 2024.
Portugal has applied for ŌĆŗ5.8 billion euros in lowŌĆæcost EU loans to strengthen its ŌĆŗarmed forces, ŌüĀMelo said. The new equipment including frigates, armoured vehicles, satellites and drones was set to be delivered by 2030, "if all goes well", he added.
The programme, known as ŌüĀSecurity ŌĆŗAction for Europe (SAFE), is a 150 billion euro ŌĆŗEU initiative aimed at reinforcing defence capabilities, closing critical gaps and jointly procuring equipment to counter potential ŌĆŗthreats to the bloc.
($1 = 0.8477 euros)

item.title: Japan offers $10 billion support to help Asian neighbours secure oil
item.description:
TOKYO, April 15 (Reuters) - Japan said on Wednesday it would establish a financial framework worth about $10 billion to help Asian countries procure energy resources and bolster their stockpiles as Middle East tensions drive prices higher and disrupt supply chains.
The support, aimed at preventing knock-on effects on Japan's ŌĆŗown supply chains, would be channelled mainly through state-backed financial institutions such as Japan Bank for International ŌĆŗCooperation (JBIC) and Nippon Export and Investment Insurance (NEXI).
Announcing the plan, Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi said ŌüĀthe support would be equivalent to as much as 1.2 billion barrels of oil, or about one year's ŌĆŗworth of crude oil imports by the Association of Southeast Asian Nations (ASEAN). She was speaking after a meeting of ŌĆŗthe "AZEC Plus" under the Japan-led initiative, Asia Zero-Emission Community (AZEC).
Leaders of the Philippines, Malaysia, Singapore, Thailand and Vietnam attended the meeting, among others.
"We are closely interconnected with Asian countries through supply chains and other channels, and we are mutually dependent,ŌĆØ Takaichi told reporters after the ŌĆŗtalks, citing examples such as dialysis equipment and surgical drains.
"Supporting Asian countries' supply chains would in turn bolster ŌĆŗJapanŌĆÖs own economy."
The plan includes credit provision for local companies to procure alternative resources such as U.S. crude oil, as well ŌĆīas financing ŌüĀand loans for companies and governments that are part of Japan's supply chain.
Support to help diversify energy sources and boost partner countries' stockpiles through storage tank construction is also included.
Compared with Japan, Southeast Asian countries hold smaller oil stockpiles, leaving supplies of crude and petroleum products such as naphtha ŌĆō a key feedstock for plastics ŌĆō increasingly tight. A ŌĆŗdisruption in Southeast Asian production ŌĆŗhas fuelled anxiety among ŌüĀJapanese healthcare providers that rely on Asia for critical supplies such as containers, tubes and gloves.
About 90% of crude oil passing through the Strait of Hormuz is destined ŌĆŗfor Asia, according to Japan's Agency for Natural Resources and Energy.
Tokyo has said it ŌĆŗhas secured some ŌüĀfour months' worth of naphtha for domestic use, but a growing number of manufacturers have flagged a disruption to deliveries in recent days. The government also said on Wednesday it plans to release another 36 million barrels from its national ŌüĀoil reserves ŌĆŗfrom early May.
Asked whether countries had requested access to Japan's oil ŌĆŗreserves following the AZEC Plus meeting, Takaichi declined to comment but stressed the agreement did not involve a release of its stockpile and would ŌĆŗnot affect domestic supply.

item.title: Sanctioned China tanker turns back to Strait of Hormuz, day after Gulf exit
item.description:
SINGAPORE, April 15 (Reuters) - The U.S.-sanctioned tanker Rich Starry made ŌĆŗits way back to the Strait of Hormuz on Wednesday after exiting the Gulf the day before, shipping data ŌĆīshowed, failing to break through a U.S. blockade on vessels calling at Iranian ports.
U.S. President Donald Trump announced the blockade on Sunday after weekend peace talks in Islamabad between the U.S. and Iran failed to reach a deal .
"During the first 24 hours, no ships made it past the U.S. blockade," the U.S. Central Command ŌĆŗsaid , opens new tab on X, adding that six vessels complied with directions from U.S. forces to turn around to re-enter an Iranian port.
The ŌĆŗChinese-owned tanker was among at least eight ships crossing the waterway on Tuesday, the first day of the ŌüĀU.S. blockade.
A U.S. destroyer stopped two oil tankers attempting to leave the Iranian port of Chabahar on the Gulf of Oman on ŌĆŗTuesday, a U.S. official said.
The Rich Starry and its owner, Shanghai Xuanrun Shipping Co, were placed under U.S. sanctions for dealing ŌĆŗwith Iran. The company could not be reached for a comment.
The Rich Starry is a medium-range tanker carrying about 250,000 barrels of methanol that was loaded at the port of Hamriyah in the United Arab Emirates, Kpler data showed. It is currently anchored off Iran.
The blockade has created even further uncertainty for shippers, ŌĆŗoil companies and war risk insurers. Traffic remains at only a fraction of the 130-plus daily crossings before the U.S. and Israel's ŌĆŗwar on Iran began on February 28, industry sources said on Tuesday.
There have been no Iranian tankers carrying crude for export and passing through the ŌĆīstrait since ŌüĀthe U.S. blockade began, according to Kpler and LSEG data.
The OPEC producer has unused onshore crude storage capacity of about 90 million barrels which can sustain its current output of 3.5 million barrels per day (bpd) for about two months if exports are halted, consultancy FGENexant said in a note.
A production cut of around 500,000 bpd to 3 million bpd would extend this window to about three months, it added.
Another U.S.-sanctioned vessel, the ŌĆŗVery Large Crude Carrier Alicia ŌüĀthat has a record of carrying Iranian oil since 2023, is entering the Gulf via the strait on Wednesday, Kpler and LSEG data showed. The empty tanker, capable of carrying 2 million barrels of ŌĆŗoil, is heading to Iraq to load a cargo on Thursday, Kpler data showed.
Separately, the Malta-flagged ŌĆŗVLCC Agios Fanourios I ŌüĀentered the Gulf via the strait on Wednesday in a second attempt to transit, according to the data. The tanker was among several vessels that tried to enter the Gulf on Sunday during the U.S.-Iran ceasefire deal.
It is heading to Iraq to load Basra crude for Vietnam's Nghi Son ŌüĀrefinery, according ŌĆŗto the data and trade sources.
Eastern Mediterranean Maritime, which manages the Agios Fanourios I, ŌĆŗand Nghi Son Refinery and Petrochemical did not immediately respond to requests for comment.

item.title: Philippines' Marcos urges ASEAN to activate fuel-sharing pact
item.description:
MANILA, April 15 (Reuters) - Philippine President Ferdinand Marcos Jr on Wednesday called for the immediate activation and ŌĆŗtesting of the ASEAN Petroleum Security ŌĆīAgreement (APSA), saying it should be implemented while there is an oil crisis due to the Middle East conflict.
Speaking ŌĆŗat the Asia Zero Emission Community (AZEC) ŌĆŗPlus online summit hosted by Japan, Marcos ŌüĀsaid the closure of shipping routes had ŌĆŗexposed the vulnerability of Asian economies that are ŌĆŗheavily dependent on imported petroleum.

item.title: Japan naphtha-dependent firms flag supply issues despite government assurances
item.description:
TOKYO, April 15 (Reuters) - Japanese companies whose output is dependent on naphtha or naphtha-based products are halting orders or slashing production, highlighting ŌĆŗa disconnect between official assurances of sufficient supply and realities on the ground.
Over the past week, more than a dozen firms ŌĆīincluding Toto (5332.T) , opens new tab and Asahi Kasei (3407.T) , opens new tab have flagged disruption in delivery or hiked prices, citing concern over the procurement of materials such as adhesives made from naphtha - an oil derivative chiefly used as a solvent or diluent.
Manufacturers of thinner-related products such as Kansai Paint (4613.T) , opens new tab , which by law can only hold limited stock of the hazardous material, have also adjusted delivery arrangements and ŌĆŗraised prices, clouding the outlook downstream for goods and services as varied as plastic toys and housing construction.
Just 2.7% of companies are able ŌĆŗto obtain thinner as usual, showed a survey conducted last week by the Japan Painting Contractors Association.
The picture amounts ŌüĀto a headache for the government, which has sought to prevent disruption by repeating its message that Japan has enough naphtha for the next four months.
The ŌĆŗgovernment has said it is working to secure supplies outside the Middle East, from which Japan sourced 40% of its naphtha before the U.S. and Israel attacked ŌĆŗIran on February 28. It has also set up consultation services to help businesses with procurement before they opt for changes in operations.
Even so, Toto this week suspended the acceptance of orders for modular bathroom units requiring adhesives that rely on solvents containing naphtha. Rivals Lixil (5938.T) , opens new tab , Panasonic (6752.T) , opens new tab and Cleanup (7955.T) , opens new tab have similarly flagged delivery impact.
Countries including South ŌĆŗKorea , Thailand and Bangladesh have responded to disruption in the supply of Middle East oil products by calling on the public to take conservation measures, such as ŌĆŗby driving less or even taking shorter showers.
Asked why Japan has not adopted a similar approach, Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi said she did not want to slow economic activity. She ŌĆīhas tasked ŌüĀindustry minister Ryosei Akazawa with ensuring a steady supply of oil-related products.
On Tuesday, Akazawa said the "blockage" was in the middle of the supply chain, with thinner wholesalers, for example, halving supply to customers in April upon being told that shipments would be uncertain in May.
"For Prime Minister Takaichi, the economy comes first. She doesn't say anything that would be difficult for citizens," a government official involved in economic policy told Reuters, declining to be identified because of ŌĆŗthe sensitivity of the matter. "This is the ŌĆŗadministration's policy."
Another government official said ŌüĀthe administration and industry ministry are afraid of consumer panic reminiscent of the hoarding of toilet paper and other goods during the 1970s oil crisis.
"We should really be calling on people to save energy now but the prime minister's ŌĆŗoffice has put a stop to that," said the second official on condition of anonymity. "We've already said we ŌĆŗhave enough supply so ŌüĀif we were to ask for conservation measures, we might invite criticism, and that's the fear."
Shares of naphtha-dependent companies including Toto, Kansai Paint and Mitsubishi Chemical (4188.T) , opens new tab are underperforming Tokyo's benchmark Nikkei share price average (.N225) , opens new tab , which has now recouped most of the loss suffered since the start of Middle East conflict triggered general risk-off sentiment.
"Even ŌüĀif producers ŌĆŗcould ramp up output regardless of cost ... they risk being stuck with high-priced inventory if ŌĆŗprices eventually drop," said Tomoichiro Kubota, senior market analyst at Matsui Securities.
"The Takaichi administration prioritises economic sentiment. While it has announced support for specific items, such an approach is like a game ŌĆŗof whack-a-mole."

item.title: South Korea says secures 273 mln barrels of crude via routes outside Strait of Hormuz
item.description:
SEOUL, April 15 (Reuters) - South Korea has secured 273 ŌĆīmillion barrels of crude oil from the Middle East and Kazakhstan through the end of the year, with supplies routed outside the Strait of Hormuz, presidential chief of staff Kang Hoon-sik said on Wednesday.
Asia's fourth-largest economy has also secured 2.1 million metric tons of naphtha over the same ŌĆŗperiod, Kang said at a press briefing following his visit as a special presidential envoy to Kazakhstan, Oman, Saudi ŌĆŗArabia and Qatar over the past week.
"In particular, the crude oil and naphtha secured this time ŌüĀwill be sourced through alternative supply routes unrelated to closure of the Strait of Hormuz, and will therefore make a ŌĆŗdirect and tangible contribution to stabilising domestic supply," Kang said.
Saudi Arabia had agreed to ship about 50 million barrels of crude oil ŌĆŗalready allocated to South Korean companies, using alternative ports near the Red Sea in April and May, Kang said.
Riyadh had also pledged to prioritise South Korean companies in allocating and shipping 200 million barrels of crude oil between June and the end of the year, and promised to ŌĆŗsupply as much naphtha as possible through year-end, including 500,000 tons requested by South Korea's government, he said.
Kang said Kazakhstan would ŌĆŗsupply 18 million barrels of crude oil, while Oman has promised 5 million barrels of crude oil and 1.6 million tons of naphtha.
He ŌĆīsaid the ŌüĀsecured crude oil would be sufficient to power the economy for more than three months under normal conditions based on last yearŌĆÖs usage, while the naphtha volumes were equivalent to about one month of imports.
Kang said the oil and naphtha would be sourced from alternative supply routes not affected by a potential closure of the Strait of Hormuz.
He described his trip as driven by ŌĆŗthe urgent need to secure ŌĆŗkey energy supplies amid what ŌüĀhe called an economic emergency triggered by the conflict in the Middle East.
South Korea relied on the Strait of Hormuz for 61% of its crude oil imports and 54% of its ŌĆŗnaphtha imports last year, Kang said, adding the government could not afford to wait passively ŌĆŗfor the regional ŌüĀsituation to improve.
President Lee Jae Myung conveyed deep concern over the prolonged Middle East conflict in letters sent to the leaders of the countries visited, expressing solidarity and calling for joint efforts to address the energy security crisis, Kang said.
South Korea also held discussions with ŌüĀoil producers ŌĆŗincluding Saudi Arabia and Oman on cooperation in areas such as constructing ŌĆŗbypass pipelines and building oil storage facilities outside the Strait of Hormuz to mitigate risks from a potential blockade.
With additional funding allocated to expand domestic storage ŌĆŗfacilities, Kang said joint stockpiling with major oil producers could be expanded, helping secure stable supplies.

item.title: Nigeria orders probe and defends deadly airstrike in insurgent-held Jilli
item.description:
ABUJA, April 15 (Reuters) - Nigeria has ordered ŌĆīa full and independent investigation into a military airstrike on a market that left at least 200 people feared dead , while defending the operation that it said targeted Islamist ŌĆŗmilitants based in an enclave controlled by insurgents.
Saturday's airstrike is the ŌĆŗlatest to have caused heavy civilian casualties in northeast Nigeria, where ŌüĀbattlelines are blurred.
As Islamist militants control territories, markets and supply routes, while ŌĆŗcivilians move through restricted areas for survival, rights groups have warned strikes ŌĆŗon contested spaces carry a high risk of killing bystanders.
Information Minister Mohammed Idris expressed regret for civilian casualties but said the Air Force hit "confirmed terrorist targets" in Jilli village, ŌĆŗa high-risk no-go zone in Gubio district that has for years served ŌĆŗas a logistics hub for Boko Haram and Islamic State offshoot, ISWAP, in the ŌĆīregion.
"The ŌüĀFederal Government reiterates that this was a deliberate, intelligence-led operation, not an indiscriminate attack, conducted in one of the most active insurgent corridors in the northeast," Idris said in a statement late on Tuesday.
He said the investigation ŌĆŗwould look into the ŌĆŗoperation's planning and ŌüĀexecution.
Authorities said Jilli, known locally as the "terrorists' market", and the nearby Gazabure market were shut about five years ŌĆŗago after militants took control, with intelligence showing the ŌĆŗarea was ŌüĀused to collect levies, buy supplies, and plan attacks, including assaults on April 9 in Ngamdu and Benisheikh that killed a general.
Militants have waged a 17-year insurgency ŌüĀseeking ŌĆŗto carve out an Islamic state in northeast ŌĆŗNigeria, killing thousands and displacing at least 2 million people, aid groups say, despite major ŌĆŗmilitary campaigns.

item.title: Tanzania investigates death of American social media influencer
item.description:
DAR ES SALAAM, April 15 (Reuters) - Police in Tanzania said they are questioning the fianc├® of U.S. social ŌĆŗmedia influencer Ashly Robinson, who died after ŌĆīan alleged suicide attempt while the couple were on vacation in Zanzibar, a tourist hub off the east ŌĆŗAfrican country's mainland.
Police have confiscated the passport of ŌĆŗher partner, Joseph Isaac McCann, 45, also ŌüĀa U.S. citizen, they said in a statement ŌĆŗissued late on Tuesday.
Robinson, 31, who is also ŌĆŗknown by her social media name Ashlee Jenae, and McCann arrived in Zanzibar on April 4 and booked into a ŌĆŗhotel.
They later moved to a second hotel ŌĆŗwhere they "appeared to be having domestic conflicts," police said, citing ŌĆīhotel ŌüĀstaff. Management subsequently separated the two into different rooms.
"According to reports, she attempted to hang herself using a clothing belt tied inside a wardrobe in ŌĆŗRoom 25 ŌĆŗof the ŌüĀhotel," the police statement said. She died in hospital on April 9 around ŌĆŗ9 p.m. local time while receiving ŌĆŗtreatment.
"Investigations into ŌüĀthe matter are ongoing, pending professional medical reports and forensic examination from doctors," police said. McCann ŌüĀhas ŌĆŗnot been charged. Reuters was not ŌĆŗimmediately able to contact his lawyer for comment.

item.title: Britain calls for international effort to stop arms flow to Sudan at Berlin conference
item.description:
April 15 (Reuters) - The international community has failed in Sudan, British ŌĆŗForeign Minister Yvette Cooper ŌĆīsaid on the sidelines of a Berlin conference on the ŌĆŗwar in the ŌĆŗcountry, as she called for ŌüĀan international concerted effort ŌĆŗto stop the arms flow.
"Countries ŌĆŗfrom across the world are coming together here in Berlin to ŌĆŗdiscuss the way, frankly, ŌĆŗthe international community has failed the ŌĆīpeople ŌüĀof Sudan," Cooper said on Wednesday.
"We need to ensure that every possible pressure ŌĆŗis put ŌĆŗon ŌüĀthose warring parties to reach the urgent ŌĆŗceasefire that we ŌĆŗdesperately ŌüĀneed to see," she said, as she reiterated the ŌüĀneed ŌĆŗfor humanitarian support.

item.title: Serbia's president tells military to create units armed with attack drones
item.description:
BELGRADE, April 15 (Reuters) - The Serbian army should create units with attack drones, President Aleksandar Vucic said on Wednesday, as the Balkan country seeks to modernise its ŌĆŗarmed forces from its obsolete Soviet-era technology.
Belgrade has already bought modern ŌĆīweapons and equipment from Europe, Israel and China and it wants to adapt its military strategy to conform with modern warfare.
Serbia is military neutral and is balancing a partnership with ŌĆŗNATO and aspirations to join the European Union with its ties ŌĆŗwith Russia and China.
After meeting top military commanders in Belgrade, Vucic, ŌüĀthe supreme commander of the army, said he had proposed the formation of ŌĆŗunits equipped with long-range attack drones and drones that hover over a strike ŌĆŗarea before flying into targets.
"In addition to the increased production of drones, which I expect to explode this year ... we will significantly devote ourselves to the ... digitisation of our army," ŌĆŗhe said.
"I think that in this regard we will be one of the ŌĆŗarmies with the most pronounced interoperability and the greatest capabilities, not only in our region, ŌĆībut ŌüĀalso wider than that."
Serbia maintains a 22,500-strong army and in 2026 it allocated 3.3% of its GDP for defence expenditure.
Serbia's rearmament has triggered criticism from Croatia, Bosnia and Kosovo, its neighbours and wartime foes from the 1990s, who see it ŌĆŗas a threat ŌĆŗto regional stability. ŌüĀBelgrade denies that.
Vucic's comments come after the announcement of the creation of a joint Serbian-Israeli drone manufacturing plant a day earlier.
In ŌĆŗearly 2025, Serbia also bought Israel's PULS artillery systems ŌĆŗand Hermes ŌüĀdrones manufactured by Elbit Systems (ESLT.TA) , opens new tab for $335 million and in August 2025 it also purchased long-range missiles, drones and electronic warfare equipment for around $1.6 billion.
It has bought cargo aircraft ŌüĀand ŌĆŗhelicopters from Airbus (AIR.PA) , opens new tab and Chinese missiles and drones.
Belgrade ŌĆŗalso purchased Rafale jet fighters manufactured by France's Dassault (AM.PA) , opens new tab for 2.7 billion euros ($3.18 billion), to replace ŌĆŗits ageing Soviet-made MiG-29 planes.
($1 = 0.8484 euros)

item.title: Trump adviser Sebastian Gorka seeks top counterterrorism job, WaPo reports
item.description:
April 15 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump's security aide ŌĆŗSebastian Gorka is ŌĆīseeking to become the next head of the ŌĆŗNational Counterterrorism Center, ŌĆŗthe Washington Post reported ŌüĀon Wednesday, nearly ŌĆŗa month after the ŌĆŗcenter's previous head quit due to differences over the war ŌĆŗin Iran.
Joe Kent, ŌĆŗwho previously headed the center, ŌĆīhad said ŌüĀwhile resigning last month that Iran posed no imminent threat ŌĆŗto ŌĆŗthe ŌüĀUnited States.
The U.S. National Counterterrorism Center ŌĆŗand the White ŌĆŗHouse ŌüĀdid not immediately respond to Reuters' requests ŌüĀfor ŌĆŗcomment on ŌĆŗthe Washington Post report.

item.title: Swedish power plant targeted by pro-Russian group in 2025, government says
item.description:
STOCKHOLM, April 15 (Reuters) - A pro-Russian cyber group tried to disrupt operations at a Swedish thermal power plant last year, ŌĆŗthe Swedish government said on Wednesday, adding that ŌĆīRussian hybrid attacks had become more frequent and serious.
Carl-Oskar Bohlin, minister for civil defence, said the group had targeted a power ŌĆŗplant in western Sweden during spring of 2025, ŌĆŗbut that the attack had been unsuccessful. Bohlin ŌüĀdid not name the plant.
"The Security Police handled ŌĆŗthe case and were able to identify the actor behind ŌĆŗit, who had connections to Russian intelligence and security services," Bohlin told a news conference.
"Fortunately, no serious consequences occurred due to a ŌĆŗbuilt-in protection mechanism," the minister said.
The Russian embassy ŌĆŗin Stockholm did not immediately reply to a request for comment but ŌĆīMoscow ŌüĀregularly denies responsibility for malicious cyber activity in Europe.
A Security Police spokesperson said the agency no longer had an open investigation into the matter.
Bohlin compared the incident ŌĆŗwith similar attacks in ŌĆŗPoland on ŌüĀenergy infrastructure, which Polish officials have blamed on Russian spy agencies .
He said Russian hybrid attacks ŌĆŗhad become more frequent since Russia's invasion ŌĆŗof ŌüĀUkraine and recently also more dangerous.
"We have seen for some time now that pro-Russian individuals and groups that have ŌüĀpreviously carried ŌĆŗout denial-of-service attacks are attempting ŌĆŗto carry out destructive cyberattacks against businesses in Europe, also against Swedish ŌĆŗtargets," he said.

item.title: Back to business
item.description:
April 15 (Reuters) - What matters in U.S. and global markets today
By Mike Dolan , opens new tab , Editor-at-Large, Finance and Markets
Global stock markets are largely back near pre-war levels and Wall Street is once again flirting with record highs, as the mood music from the ŌĆŗMiddle East and Washington turns positive on hopes of more peace talks .
Real or imagined, that narrative is capping crude prices, allowing investors to ŌĆīrefocus on earnings and economic fundamentals.
IŌĆÖll get into that and more below.
But first, check out my latest column , where I dig into markets' apparent belief that the war is likely over, bar the shouting.
And listen to the latest episode of the Morning Bid daily podcast , where I discuss corporate earnings and the IMF's latest global growth forecasts.
Finally, don't forget to mark April 23 in ŌĆŗyour calendar, when I'll be joining my ROI colleague Jamie McGeever for a timely webinar discussion on rethinking safe-haven assets in uncertain times. Sign up ŌĆŗhere , opens new tab .
Brent and WTI crude were still comfortably below $100 a barrel heading into Wednesday, trading at around $96/bbl and $92/bbl, respectively. That ŌüĀfollowed comments from President Trump on Tuesday that talks with Iran could resume in days , even as the U.S. military said it had halted IranŌĆÖs maritime trade as ŌĆŗpart of its naval blockade of the Strait of Hormuz.
This helped fuel another Wall Street rally on Tuesday. The Nasdaq jumped 2%, while the S&P 500 rose 1% ŌĆŗto close just shy of its record high. Asian equities followed suit on Wednesday, with JapanŌĆÖs Nikkei up 0.9% and South KoreaŌĆÖs KOSPI surging 3%. European stocks traded flat , as did U.S. futures before the bell.
In a sign that the recovery hasn't only been on Wall Street, the MSCI all-country index, excluding U.S. stocks, hit its best levels since March 2 on Tuesday. ŌĆŗElsewhere, the VIX volatility index returned to its February levels and the dollar continued to give back its safe-haven gains, hovering near its lowest point since the ŌĆŗwar.
The optimism at large looks to be justified by corporate earnings, which kicked off in earnest this week with mostly bumper results from the U.S. banks. JPMorgan beat first-quarter profit expectations, helped ŌĆīby robust ŌüĀtrading revenue and dealmaking, while Citi reported its strongest quarterly revenue in a decade, sending its shares to their highest point since 2008. Bank of America and Morgan Stanley will report later today.
ASML, the world's largest chipmaking tool supplier, added to the upbeat tone on Wednesday as it beat earnings expectations and lifted its 2026 revenue outlook on AI-driven demand.
Although the IMF trimmed its global growth forecasts on Tuesday - and even warned the world could be drifting toward an ŌĆ£adverse scenarioŌĆØ amid the war - its reference ŌĆŗforecast assumes a short-lived conflict, with ŌĆŗits global growth forecast unchanged for ŌüĀ2027.
Meanwhile, U.S. producer prices jumped in March due to the energy shock, but the increase was almost half what economists had expected - a reassuring sign, particularly as the data covered a period after the start of the Iran conflict.
Chart of the day
As ŌĆŗfinancial markets return to pre-war levels, many investors are looking at just why the March oil shock has not ŌĆŗshifted the dial much ŌüĀon global economic forecasts. One of many reasons is the world economy's dwindling dependency on fossil fuels, with the share of power generated by wind and solar more than trebling over the past decade.
Today's events to watch
* U.S. March import prices (8:30 a.m. EDT)
* FedŌĆÖs Michael Barr and Michelle Bowman speak
* Fed issues the Beige Book
* U.S. ŌüĀcorporate earnings: Bank ŌĆŗof America, Morgan Stanley
Want to receive the Morning Bid in your inbox every weekday morning? ŌĆŗSign up for the newsletter here . You can find ROI on the Reuters website , opens new tab , and you can follow us on LinkedIn , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .
Opinions expressed are those of the author. They do not reflect the views of Reuters News, ŌĆŗwhich, under the Trust Principles , opens new tab , is committed to integrity, independence, and freedom from bias.

item.title: Hormuz pipeline workaround looks worth the cost
item.description:
LONDON, April 15 (Reuters Breakingviews) - Middle Eastern oil exporters need a plan B. IranŌĆÖs effective blockade of the Strait of Hormuz means Saudi Arabia, Qatar, the United Arab Emirates and others might end up having to pay tolls to get their exports out to buyers around the world. Unless Tehran sets these charges at a lowball level, there's a major financial incentive for Gulf states to start building alternative pipeline routes in ŌĆŗearnest.
There is little recent historical precedent for a nation to tax passage through a natural waterway. Under the 1936 Montreux Convention , opens new tab , Turkey doesnŌĆÖt charge for entry to the Black Sea, while Danish tolls for shipping to ŌĆīpass through the Oresund strait were abolished in 1857 amid international pressure. Egyptian fees for Suez Canal transit, like those levied by the Panama Canal, are different: both are artificial passages that need cash for their upkeep.
Thus far, Iran has merely said that safe passage through Hormuz hinges on coordination with its authorities, stopping short of setting a public and specific toll. Still, there is plenty of scope to charge one. Gulf oil producers stand to lose hundreds of billions of dollars per year if the strait stays shut, which suggests that Tehran has the long-term negotiating power to influence the terms ŌĆŗof any re-opening.
Putting a number on the toll takings is tough. In the past month, at least one of the handful of ships that has transited the strait has paid $2 million , opens new tab ŌĆö the same figure the New York Times said , opens new tab Tehran would ŌĆŗlook to levy, citing two senior Iranian officials. Trade publication LloydŌĆÖs List says , opens new tab that before the war, up to 50 tankers carrying crude, other oil products and liquefied natural gas typically passed ŌüĀthrough each day, or 18,250 a year. On that basis, Tehran could theoretically pocket $37 billion annually if traffic normalises.
Other approaches yield much smaller figures, though. Another toll idea , opens new tab floating around is a $1 per barrel charge. Around 20 million daily barrels flowed through Hormuz before ŌĆŗthe war, of which perhaps half could conceivably find another route based on existing infrastructure. Assume the $1 fee applied to 10 million barrels per day, then, and the annual total would be $3.7 billion per year for Tehran. That looks like the low end of ŌĆŗreasonable estimates.
Even this relatively small yearly toll would stack up. Apply a 5% discount rate, which is roughly Saudi Arabia's 10-year borrowing cost, to 25 years of payments at $3.7 billion annually. The total sum is $54 billion in today's money. The question is whether that conservative number is high enough to incentivise Iran's Gulf adversaries to construct a workaround to dodge the tolls, in the form of oil pipelines that skirt the strait.
One headache is working out where they should go. Merely replicating SaudiŌĆÖs 1,200-kilometre, 7-million barrel-a-day pipeline to the port of Yanbu on the Red Sea would create new ŌĆŗproblems. Gulf states would still have to get their product past the Bab el-Mandeb Strait near Yemen. The nearby territory is controlled by Iran-aligned Houthi rebels, raising the risk of further drone attacks on new and old pipelines.
Gabriel Collins, of Rice UniversityŌĆÖs Baker ŌĆŗInstitute for Public Policy, has had a stab at a holistic workaround , opens new tab . He envisages twin pipelines with 56-inch diameters, each carrying 5 million barrels of daily supply, stretching 1,800 kilometres from southern Iraq through Kuwait and then along the coast to pick up oil from Saudi and the ŌĆīUAE. Terminating in ŌüĀtwo Omani ports, Duqm and Salalah, it would avoid both the Red Sea and Gulf chokepoints, meaning crude could flow freely east into the Indian Ocean to Gulf statesŌĆÖ main Asian customers. But it would take up to seven years to build ŌĆö and cost a lot.
Gulf states might have to fork out $18 billion alone on the construction cost of the pipelines and pumping stations to move the oil along them, Collins reckons. Add a further $12 billion in project development expenses and $7 billion headroom in the plausible event of the project taking longer, or hitting snags. He also assumes nearly $8 billion for new oil loading terminals at Duqm and Salalah, and $10 billion to fortify critical sections with C-RAM and Patriot anti-missile systems.
The overall price tag comes to $55 billion, which is roughly the same ŌĆŗas the $54 billion low-end estimate for the present value of ŌĆŗ25 years of possible Hormuz toll payments. In other words, ŌüĀthe pipeline projects could potentially pay for themselves over a generation by allowing the Gulf states to avoid Tehran's strait fees. That's surely worth doing, since the real tolls could be higher, and ending dependence on an adversary has incalculable value.
Even for hugely wealthy states like Saudi and the UAE, these numbers represent an expensive undertaking ŌĆö and an extremely unwelcome one, given all they would ŌĆŗdo is provide new infrastructure for the same fossil fuels they intend to increasingly pivot away from. Even if they take the plunge, they will still have to pay tolls ŌĆŗbefore the pipelines become operational.
Still, it seems ŌüĀlogical for Saudi and its neighbours to get building. Over 80% of the burden of paying the tolls would likely fall on Gulf states, according to Bruegel research , opens new tab . They could split the cost of new pipelines in step with their intended usage.
Of course, there's another way to conceive of these numbers. Arguably they give Iran a maximum target for how much it could hope to raise. It should rationally set the tolls at a level that would be as high as possible without incentivising Gulf rivals to build the new pipelines.
And plenty could ŌüĀchange in the ŌĆŗnext seven years. U.S. President Donald Trump is now implementing a blockade of Iran-approved ships that are making it through Hormuz, and an intensified war that ŌĆŗultimately unblocks Hormuz is still possible.
