Yet Iran has proved that it has the ability to wage asymmetric warfare around Hormuz, and the regime seems hard to budge. Even if Saudi and its neighbours pay $55 billion for pipelines that remain as a backup, they might still view that as a sound investment to ŌĆŗcreate some geopolitical breathing room.
Follow George Hay on Bluesky , opens new tab and LinkedIn , opens new tab .

item.title: Carmakers navigating the costly and tricky transition to battery storage systems
item.description:
April 15 (Reuters) - Carmakers and battery companies facing a weak U.S. market for electric vehicles are scrambling to repurpose battery factories to make energy-storage systems to fuel AI's thirst for power instead. But converting plants to new types of batteries wonŌĆÖt be easy, ŌĆŗnor will the demand for energy storage materialize fast enough to absorb a glut of unused factory space for EV batteries.
The expected surge in demand for electricity and energy storage comes at ŌĆīan opportune time for automakers like General Motors (GM.N) , opens new tab and Ford Motor (F.N) , opens new tab and their battery suppliers -- Asian manufacturers that include Japan's Panasonic Holdings (6752.T) , opens new tab , and South Korea's Samsung SDI and LG Energy Solution. These companies over the past decade spent or earmarked more than $100 billion toward battery factories to feed a U.S. electric-vehicle market that has been decimated by the Trump administrationŌĆÖs pro-fossil-fuel policies.
Stationary storage systems use lithium-ion battery cells ŌĆō similar to those in EVs ŌĆō that store power often generated by renewable sources like solar and wind, and release it during times of high demand ŌĆŗor grid stress. U.S. demand is expected to grow partly to support data centers and cloud computing, which draw gobs of electricity.
But the predicted energy storage boom wouldnŌĆÖt come close to offsetting the bust in demand ŌĆŗfor EV batteries, a Reuters review of projected U.S. battery-factory capacity this decade found. U.S. demand for EVs already had been falling short of automakers' original projections even before ŌüĀa $7,500 consumer tax credit expired September 30, sending sales down more than 25% over the past six months.
Converting factories to the type of battery chemistry that many energy storage systems use will take time and yet more money, and ŌĆŗrequire the companies to delve into a technology that today is dominated by China.
Bob Lee, head of North America for LG Energy Solution (LGES), a battery arm of Seoul-based LG Group, said his company is converting three of its factories in North ŌĆŗAmerica to produce batteries for storage systems. He expects the battery business in the United States to continue to struggle with excess capacity, what he calls "fallout" from the EV bust.
ŌĆ£Like any other industry that goes through a difficult period like this, I don't think it's going to be all rosy,ŌĆØ he said.
Ford said it is spending $2 billion on a new battery-storage division over the next two years to "create a new, diversified and profitable revenue stream." GM's joint venture with LGES, called Ultium Cells, said last month it will convert an EV-battery ŌĆŗplant in Tennessee to make battery cells for storage.
Demand for stationary batteries in North America will hit 76 gigawatt-hours this year, according to consultancy Benchmark Mineral Intelligence. But the auto industryŌĆÖs investment binge on ŌĆŗEV battery capacity has left it with far more factory space than that: roughly 275 GWh. While storage demand is expected to nearly double over the next five years, to 125 GWh, that still wonŌĆÖt be enough to mop up the excess capacity installed ŌĆīfor the auto ŌüĀindustry.
For example, LGES, which produces battery cells in North America at its own facilities and in joint ventures with General Motors, Honda and Hyundai, is converting factory space to be able to produce up to 50 GWh of batteries for storage a year by the end of this year. Still, thatŌĆÖs only about a third of the company's overall capacity in the region.
Most energy storage systems use a breed of batteries called lithium iron-phosphate, or LFP, which is cheaper than the nickel-heavy chemistry that is predominantly used for EV batteries in North America. Switching factories to LFP can take as long as 18 months and cost several hundred million dollars, battery executives told Reuters.
For U.S. battery makers, producing LFP is also complicated ŌĆŗby ChinaŌĆÖs dominance of that technology and its supply chain. ŌĆŗU.S.-based battery makers are working to cut their ŌüĀreliance on Chinese materials to qualify for generous federal tax credits for domestic battery production that took effect during the Biden administration and were left in place under Trump, said Iola Hughes, BenchmarkŌĆÖs head of research. Producers must gradually phase out Chinese content in coming years to get the full tax breaks.
Trade barriers are another problem. U.S. tariffs on Chinese-made cathode and ŌĆŗanode materials ŌĆō the two electrodes that ping-pong the electric charge back and forth inside a battery ŌĆō stand at 35%, according to Benchmark Mineral Intelligence.
The pivot to energy storage ŌüĀby U.S. carmakers and battery companies has accelerated in recent months.
In March, the GM-LGES joint venture said it was spending $70 million and retraining workers to make battery cells for storage at its plant south of Nashville. Ford Motor in December disclosed it would repurpose some underused factory space in Kentucky to make storage batteries.
Traditional automakers are playing catch-up to Tesla. Elon Musk's company has spent about a decade developing its energy storage business, which has become one of the EV maker's fastest-growing divisions.
It was also more ŌüĀprofitable than its ŌĆŗEV business in 2025, with gross margins of about 30% compared to about 15% for its automotive business, excluding earnings from regulatory credits.
Deployments ŌĆŗof TeslaŌĆÖs so-called Megapack storage units have surged, including $430 million in revenue last year from sales to MuskŌĆÖs xAI, an example of how AI-driven data-center demand is translating directly into battery orders.
Kurt Kelty, GM's battery chief and a former Tesla executive, told Reuters in January that the company is ŌĆŗcommitted to building a battery manufacturing industry and supply chain in the U.S. Whether itŌĆÖs for EVs or storage systems, he said, ŌĆ£it really doesnŌĆÖt matter.ŌĆØ

item.title: Ukraine retakes 50 sq km of territory in March, army chief says
item.description:
April 15 (Reuters) - Ukraine regained control of nearly 50 sq km (19 sq miles) of ŌĆŗits territory from Russia in March, its army chief ŌĆīOleksandr Syrskyi said on Wednesday, building on its gains since the start of the year.
He added that with the change of weather conditions, Moscow's ŌĆŗforces have stepped up their offensive operations and were ŌĆŗconducting them along almost the entire 1,200-km front line.
Syrskyi ŌüĀsaid the area around the embattled eastern city of Pokrovsk, ŌĆŗwhich Russia has been trying to capture fully since mid-2024, was ŌĆŗamong the most intense places of fighting in March.
He also singled out Oleksandrivka, Kostiantynivka, and Lyman directions in the southeast and east as "the hottest" spots ŌĆŗduring the month.
Ukrainian counterattacks in the southeast were helping to ŌĆŗdisrupt Russian efforts around Pokrovsk and the Russian spring offensive overall, military ŌĆīanalysts ŌüĀhave said.
Syrskyi said last week that Ukraine has regained control of 480 sq km of territory since late January and President Volodymyr Zelenskiy earlier this month described the frontline situation as the best ŌĆŗfor Ukraine since ŌĆŗthe middle ŌüĀof last year.
To reduce Russia's offensive capabilities, Ukrainian forces have kept up a high pace of ŌĆŗstrikes on military, defence-industrial and other facilities in ŌĆŗRussia, Syrskyi ŌüĀsaid.
In March, Ukraine struck 76 such targets including 15 facilities in the oil refining industry, he said.
Ukraine has intensified its attacks on ŌüĀRussian ŌĆŗports, refineries and fertiliser plants in ŌĆŗan attempt to curb Moscow's revenue from commodities exports as the Iran war drove up global ŌĆŗprices.

item.title: Hezbollah says Lebanon's talks with Israel widen national rift
item.description:
BEIRUT, April 15 (Reuters) - Hezbollah said on Wednesday the Lebanese government's decision to hold talks with Israel was "a national sin" that would widen divisions in Lebanon, underlining deep polarisation ŌĆŗin the country as the Iran-backed group wages war with Israel.
Hezbollah lawmaker Hassan Fadlallah said ŌĆīTuesday's U.S.-mediated meeting between Lebanon's Washington ambassador and her Israeli counterpart did not reflect Lebanon's national identity or "the choices of its people".
The meeting hosted by U.S. Secretary of State Marco Rubio was the first ŌĆŗsuch direct contact in decades between the two countries that have remained in ŌĆŗa state of war since Israel was established in 1948.
Both sides said ŌüĀthe talks were positive, though ahead of the meeting, Israel had ruled out any ŌĆŗdiscussion of Lebanon's demand for a ceasefire in the war, which erupted on March 2 ŌĆŗwhen Hezbollah opened fire in support of Tehran.
"Does the government not realize the danger of what it has undertaken? And does it understand that it has entered a wrong path that leads only to ŌĆŗincreasing the rift among the Lebanese?" Fadlallah said.
"It has obtained nothing from the enemy ŌĆŗexcept praise without achieving any demand," he said in a televised statement.
Tuesday's meeting took place at a ŌĆīcritical juncture ŌüĀin the crisis in the Middle East, a week into a fragile ceasefire between the United States, Israel and Iran.
The wider conflict in the region began with U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran on February 28. Israel's offensive in Lebanon has killed more than 2,000 people and ŌĆŗforced 1.2 million from ŌĆŗtheir homes, according ŌüĀto Lebanese authorities.
Fadlallah said his group wants a comprehensive ceasefire, rather than a return to near-daily Israeli strikes and assassinations as seen after ŌĆŗit agreed to a previous ceasefire with Israel in November 2024.
The Lebanese ŌĆŗstate has ŌüĀbeen seeking to disarm Hezbollah peacefully since the 2024 war. Any move by Lebanon to disarm it by force risks igniting conflict in a country shattered by civil war from 1975 ŌüĀto ŌĆŗ1990. Moves against Hezbollah by a Western-backed government in ŌĆŗ2008 prompted a short civil war.
The current government banned Hezbollah's military wing after it opened fire on Israel last ŌĆŗmonth.

item.title: Trump says he asked China's Xi not to give Iran weapons
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 15 (Reuters) - Donald Trump asked Chinese President Xi ŌĆŗJinping in a letter not to give ŌĆīIran weapons, and Xi responded that China was not supplying Tehran, the U.S. president ŌĆŗtold Fox Business Network in ŌĆŗan interview that aired on Wednesday.
Trump, ŌüĀin the interview taped on ŌĆŗTuesday, did not say when the ŌĆŗletters were exchanged. Last week, he threatened countries with an immediate 50% tariff if they ŌĆŗsupplied Iran with weapons.
"I wrote ŌĆŗhim a letter asking him not to do ŌĆīthat, ŌüĀand he wrote me a letter saying that, essentially, he's not doing that," Trump told FBN's "Mornings with ŌĆŗMaria" program.
He ŌĆŗalso said ŌüĀhe did not expect shifts in the global ŌĆŗoil market over the war ŌĆŗon ŌüĀIran and changes in Venezuela to impact the dynamics of his planned ŌüĀmeeting ŌĆŗwith Xi next month . "He's somebody ŌĆŗthat needs oil. We don't," Trump said.

item.title: Lula, Flavio Bolsonaro tied in potential election runoff, poll shows
item.description:
SAO PAULO, April 15 (Reuters) - Brazilian President Luiz ŌĆŗInacio Lula da Silva and Senator ŌĆīFlavio Bolsonaro are statistically tied in a potential runoff in October's general election, ŌĆŗwith the challenger leading Lula ŌĆŗfor the first time within the ŌüĀsurvey's margin of error, a ŌĆŗQuaest poll commissioned by brokerage Genial showed ŌĆŗon Wednesday.
(This story has been refiled to fix a garble in the headline)

item.title: At least one killed in Turkey school shooting, NTV reports
item.description:
ANKARA, April 15 (Reuters) - At least one ŌĆŗperson was ŌĆīkilled and six people were ŌĆŗwounded in ŌĆŗa shooting at a ŌüĀmiddle school ŌĆŗin the ŌĆŗsouthern Turkish province of Kahramanmaras on ŌĆŗWednesday, broadcaster ŌĆŗNTV reported citing the ŌĆīlocal ŌüĀgovernor, marking the second such attack in ŌĆŗas ŌĆŗmany ŌüĀdays.
No other details were ŌĆŗimmediately ŌĆŗavailable. ŌüĀSchool shootings are very rare ŌüĀin ŌĆŗTurkey.

item.title: Erdogan says Turkey working to extend Iran ceasefire, continue talks
item.description:
ANKARA, April 15 (Reuters) - Turkish President Tayyip Erdogan said on Wednesday that Ankara was working to extend a ceasefire between ŌĆŗthe U.S. and Iran, ease tensions, and ensure the continuation of ŌĆītalks, and added Turkey was hopeful of negotiations despite stumbling blocks.
Turkey, a neighbour of Iran, has been in close touch with the U.S., Iran, and mediator Pakistan. It ŌĆŗhas repeatedly called for an end to the war that it ŌĆŗsays is unjustified.
Speaking to lawmakers in parliament, Erdogan said Israel's ŌüĀattacks in Lebanon were harming hopes for peace, and added the window of ŌĆŗopportunity created by the ceasefire must be seized.
"Statements by the sides show ŌĆŗthat, while the negotiation table has not been toppled, they have reached a road bump on the nuclear issue," he said, adding tensions were also flaring again over the Strait ŌĆŗof Hormuz.
"We are conveying the necessary suggestions and carrying out initiatives ŌĆŗto ease tensions, extend the ceasefire, and maintain talks. There can be no negotiating with ŌĆīclenched ŌüĀfists," Erdogan added.
U.S. President Donald Trump said talks with Iran could soon resume and end in a deal, after weekend negotiations in Pakistan ended with no breakthrough, while telling the world to watch out for an "amazing two ŌĆŗdays".
A Turkish diplomatic ŌĆŗsource has ŌüĀsaid foreign ministers from Turkey, Pakistan, Saudi Arabia, and Egypt will meet on the margins of a diplomacy forum ŌĆŗin the southern Turkish province of Antalya at the ŌĆŗweekend. Pakistan's ŌüĀPrime Minister Shehbaz Sharif is also due to attend .
"There may be difficulties and complicated issues that will take a long time to solve, but when the ŌüĀfocus ŌĆŗis on the benefits of peace, and action ŌĆŗis taken with a long-term perspective, the majority of these can be resolved," Erdogan said.

item.title: French government's proposed cap on retail margins at gas stations faces stiff industry opposition
item.description:
PARIS, April 15 (Reuters) - France's proposal to cap profit margins on transport fuels sold throughout the country's service stations prompted an immediate backlash from the distribution industry, according to a lobbying letter seen by Reuters on Wednesday.
The French government said on Tuesday it had ŌĆŗsubmitted for consultation a draft decree that would fix prices to ensure fuel distributors did ŌĆŗnot earn margins higher than those seen in January and February, based on a ŌüĀsliding five-day average of wholesale prices at Rotterdam.
It is one of a host of measures European countries ŌĆŗare considering from Dublin to Bucharest to ease consumer pain from record high oil and fuel prices due to disruptions ŌĆŗcaused by the war in Iran.
In a letter to the prime minister dated April 14, France's Federation of Commerce and Distribution (FCD) argued the decree would force distributors operating on margins of 1 to 2 eurocents per litre to sell at a ŌĆŗloss due to the lack of real-time price updates ŌĆö and leave untouched the vertically integrated oil companies who ŌĆŗthey say are earning much larger profits from the crisis.
The letter, which has not been made public, was signed by ŌĆīCarrefour (CARR.PA) , opens new tab ŌüĀCEO Alexandre Bompard, Cooperative U CEO Dominique Schelcher, Mouvement E. Leclerc Executive Committee Chair Philippe Michaud, Groupement Les Mousquetaires President Thierry Cotillard, and Barthelemy Guislain, president of the Mulliez Family Association, which owns the Auchan supermarket chain.
Together the five companies represent more than a third of France's service stations, according to Reuters calculations.
"This decree ŌĆŗwould make large distribution chains ŌĆŗthe convenient variable of ŌüĀadjustment of a crisis it has not created... while imposing absolutely nothing on the refiner-distributors who have captured considerable gross margins since the beginning of the ŌĆŗconflict in Iran," the letter reads.
Though not named in the letter, French oil ŌĆŗmajor TotalEnergies (TTEF.PA) , opens new tab owns ŌüĀthree of France's six oil refineries and a quarter of the country's service stations.
TotalEnergies is expected to announce significant trading profits on Thursday in its first-quarter earnings snapshot from rising oil prices and higher refining margins.
TotalEnergies declined ŌüĀto comment ŌĆŗon the letter, but said it was the only distributor ŌĆŗin France to voluntarily cap prices at the pump.
Canadian energy group North Atlantic also recently acquired the French Esso refinery in Gravenchon, which supplies about ŌĆŗ7% of French fuel stations.

item.title: EU warns of prolonged energy shock, forced cuts if Iran war continues
item.description:
BRUSSELS, April 15 (Reuters) - The ŌĆŗEuropean Union warned member countries on Wednesday that if the Iran conflict ŌĆīcontinues, energy markets will face a prolonged supply shock that would force cuts to fuel consumption, EU diplomats told Reuters.
Global energy supplies are reeling from the war's effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz, usually a transit ŌĆŗroute for 20% of the world's oil and liquefied natural gas (LNG).
Europe has not ŌĆŗyet faced supply shortages, but is grappling with soaring oil and gas ŌüĀprices and airports have warned the first jet fuel shortages could hit within weeks.
In a ŌĆŗclosed-door meeting with EU countries' ambassadors on Wednesday, the European Commission said it was considering two ŌĆŗmain scenarios, diplomats with knowledge of the talks said.
A Commission spokesperson declined to comment.
In a scenario where the ceasefire agreed between the U.S. and Iran holds, and the U.S. blockade of the strait is ŌĆŗlifted, oil and gas flows would recover in a few months and prices should decline, ŌĆŗthe Commission said.
Diesel and jet fuel prices would ease later, by the end of summer, while the ŌĆīglobal ŌüĀmarket for LNG would remain tightened until 2030, due to damage to infrastructure in Qatar, it added.
But if tensions continue, energy markets would face a prolonged supply shock and extreme price spikes, with ripple effects across industries' supply chains. Continued oil supply disruption would increasingly force "demand destruction" - ŌĆŗmeaning reduced fuel ŌĆŗuse, the Commission said.
In ŌüĀthis scenario, Europe could struggle to fill its gas storage ahead of winter. Localised shortages of jet fuel are also possible, the ŌĆŗCommission said, according to the diplomats.
Europe's reliance on oil and gas imports ŌĆŗhas left ŌüĀit exposed to spiralling global prices - even though its top suppliers are the U.S., Norway and other producers outside the Middle East.
The Commission is drafting proposals to attempt to offset the ŌüĀenergy fallout. ŌĆŗA draft, previously reported by Reuters, shows plans to cut ŌĆŗelectricity taxes and seek to scale up clean technologies faster, to slash Europe's dependence on fossil fuels and protect ŌĆŗthe bloc from future oil and gas shocks.

item.title: Central Asia, Afghanistan crank up Russian fuel imports as MidEast supplies dry up
item.description:
MOSCOW, April 15 (Reuters) - Central Asia and Afghanistan increased fuel imports from Russia and Belarus by ŌĆŗrail by more than 50% in the ŌĆīfirst quarter as Moscow diverts energy flows from Europe and the Iran war curbs deliveries from the ŌĆŗMiddle East, traders said on Wednesday.
The ŌĆŗtraders said supplies to the region in ŌüĀJanuary-March increased to 3.347 million metric tons.
Since ŌĆŗthe European Union introduced an embargo on ŌĆŗRussia's oil products in February 2023, the region has become the main export market for Russian fuel.
While Russia ŌĆŗhas banned gasoline exports until the end ŌĆŗof July, many Central Asian countries, with which Russia ŌĆīhas ŌüĀinter-governmental agreements on fuel supplies, are exempt from the restrictions.
Mongolia is the biggest Russian fuel importer in the region. Supplies to the ŌĆŗcountry in ŌĆŗthe first ŌüĀquarter rose by 29% year on year to 840,000 tons.
Afghanistan is ŌĆŗnot exempt from Russia's gasoline export ŌĆŗban, ŌüĀbut Belarus can continue supplies.
The Taliban-ruled country's imports from Russia and Belarus jumped fourfold in ŌüĀthe ŌĆŗfirst quarter from the same ŌĆŗperiod in 2025 to 530,000 tons, including 231,000 tons ŌĆŗof gasoline.

item.title: Goldman Sachs flags twoŌĆæway risks to their 2026 oil price outlook
item.description: April 15 (Reuters) - Goldman Sachs on Tuesday flagged both upside and downside risks to its average 2026 crude forecasts for Brent/WTI at $83/78 per barrel, citing growing ŌĆŗuncertainty around Middle East developments and oil flows through the Strait ŌĆīof Hormuz.

item.title: Botswana has not requested stake in Lobito refinery, Angola's Sonangol says
item.description:
CAPE TOWN, April 15 (Reuters) - State-owned oil firm Sonangol has not ŌĆŗreceived any formal communication from Botswana signalling its desire to acquire a stake in ŌĆīAngola's new $6.6 billion Lobito refinery, a senior company executive said late on Tuesday.
Angola has been seeking partners to help finance the delayed refinery ŌĆö the country's largest and aimed at cutting its reliance on imported fuel ŌĆö with financing shortfalls ŌĆŗand potential partnerships drawing interest from neighbours.
Widespread media reports quoting Botswana's energy minister, Bogolo Joy ŌĆŗKenewendo, in parliament on March 27, suggested that the government was offered and ŌüĀis weighing options to secure a 30% shareholding in the 200,000 barrel per day (bpd) plant once ŌĆŗit comes online.
Botswana's ministry of energy did not immediately respond to multiple requests for comment from Reuters.
The ŌĆŗinformation from the media was surprising, Joaquim Kiteculo, CEO of Sonangol's refining division, told Reuters on the sidelines of an energy conference in Cape Town, adding that it was Zambia that had aimed to join the Lobito project ŌĆŗsince the beginning.
"It was our first time to hear that Botswana is interested," Kiteculo said.
Angola and ŌĆŗZambia have a memorandum of understanding covering participation in the refinery. Botswana, a land-locked country, is keen to boost storage ŌĆīcapacity and ŌüĀdiversify its fuel supplies as it also considers partnering with Namibia on their proposed first refinery.
Angola is open to partnerships with new investors but will retain a 51% majority in any restructuring, Kiteculo said.
A source in Angola's energy ministry said the refinery issue would likely be discussed once President Dumo ŌĆŗBoko returns to Angola.
Lobito refinery, part of Angolan plans to reduce its dependency on imported petroleum products, has been delayed for years due to the lack of financing.
Angola's ŌĆŗmines and petroleum minister is currently in China with a senior Sonangol delegation, ŌĆŗseeking to drum ŌüĀup support to overcome a $4.8 billion funding shortfall for Lobito.
"I would say, in the first stage, it is going to be $2.2 billion and later, an additional $2.6, but the situation currently remains the same as ŌüĀbefore," Kiteculo ŌĆŗsaid of ongoing talks.
Sonangol, which has already spent $1.4 billion of its ŌĆŗown capital for road and water infrastructure in the first phase of construction, will continue to invest with or without partners ŌĆŗuntil the project is completed, he said.

item.title: Italy's Enel to hire 1,000 workers at power distribution unit, unions say
item.description: MILAN, April 15 (Reuters) - Italy's biggest utility Enel (ENEI.MI) , opens new tab will hire 1,000 ŌĆŗnew workers at its ŌĆīpower distribution unit EŌĆæDistribuzione under an agreement with trade unions ŌĆŗaimed at ending a ŌĆŗtwoŌĆæyear labour dispute, unions said ŌüĀon Wednesday.

item.title: Australia to raise defence spending to 3% of GDP by 2033
item.description:
SYDNEY, April 15 (Reuters) - Australia on Wednesday said it would ŌĆīraise defence spending to 3% of gross domestic product by 2033 from about 2% currently, in response to the most "complex and threatening" circumstances since World War Two.
The expansion would be achieved through ŌĆŗan additional A$14 billion ($10 billion) in expenditure over four years and A$53 ŌĆŗbillion over the next decade, the government said.
Australia has been looking to ŌüĀdevelop its defence capabilities amid concerns over rising global geopolitical tensions and China's ŌĆŗtussle with the U.S. for influence in the Indo-Pacific.
The Trump administration in the U.S. has ŌĆŗcalled on Canberra to lift defence spending to 3.5% of GDP.
Australia's centre-left Labor government will outline the spending in the Integrated Investment Program due to be released on Thursday, with the biennial ŌĆŗNational Defence Strategy.
"Australia faces its most complex and threatening strategic circumstances since the end ŌĆŗof World War Two," Defence Minister Richard Marles is expected to say in a speech in ŌĆīCanberra ŌüĀon Thursday to launch the latest strategy.
"International norms that once constrained the use of force and military coercion continue to erode."
"The result is that we are now seeing the biggest peacetime increase in defence spending in our nation's history."
Australia's defence spending currently sits ŌĆŗat around 2% ŌĆŗof GDP.
The government has ŌüĀalready committed an additional A$50 billion in spending over the next decade to reach 2.33% in 2033-34.
Taken together, it means Prime ŌĆŗMinister Anthony Albanese's government has committed an additional A$117 billion ŌĆŗin spending ŌüĀover the next decade.
The government said the money would be spent on "capabilities that increase the ADF's (Australian Defence Force) ability to deter and respond to current and emerging threats, while also ŌüĀinvesting ŌĆŗin the longer term to build a more self-reliant ŌĆŗADF."
Australia will pay for the expanded expenditure through bigger defence appropriations and accessing private capital, Marles is ŌĆŗexpected to say.
($1 = 1.4000 Australian dollars)

item.title: South African politician Malema back in court as prosecutors seek 15-year prison sentence
item.description:
KUGOMPO CITY, South Africa, April 15 (Reuters) - South African opposition politician Julius Malema was back in court on Wednesday, as prosecutors pushed for ŌĆŗhim to be given the maximum 15-year prison sentence for ŌĆīfiring a rifle in the air at a rally.
Malema, one of South Africa's most prominent politicians, was convicted last year of charges, including unlawful possession of a firearm ŌĆŗand discharging a weapon in a public place over the ŌĆŗ2018 incident.
The 45-year-old leader of the fourth-biggest party in parliament, ŌüĀthe far-left Economic Freedom Fighters, had pleaded not guilty, arguing the ŌĆŗgun was a toy.
A prison term of more than 12 months, if ŌĆŗconfirmed after all appeals, would bar Malema from serving as a lawmaker.
That would be a major setback to EFF, which has strong support among young South Africans frustrated ŌĆŗby the racial inequality that has persisted since the end of ŌĆŗwhite minority rule in 1994.
The state ŌĆīprosecutor ŌüĀtold a magistrate's court in KuGompo City, formerly East London, that it would set a bad precedent if Malema were to get off without jail time.
"The accused is a political leader with a huge following ... young ŌĆŗpeople emulate this ŌĆŗkind of behaviour," ŌüĀprosecutor Joel Cesar said. "He's a member of parliament, a lawmaker, but he breaks the law."
Malema's lawyers argued he ŌĆŗdid not intend to cause any harm by firing ŌĆŗthe ŌüĀgun in a celebratory gesture, and called for a more lenient sentence such as a fine.
The court adjourned to Thursday.
Addressing hundreds of his supporters from ŌüĀa ŌĆŗpodium set up outside the court building, Malema ŌĆŗsaid he would appeal whatever sentence he was given.

item.title: Dubai's landmark Burj Al Arab hotel will shut for 18-month refurb amid tourism decline
item.description:
April 15 (Reuters) - Luxury hotel Burj Al Arab in Dubai will shut during a major 18-month renovation, a ŌĆŗstaff member confirmed on Wednesday, its first since ŌĆīopening in 1999 and at a time when tourism in the region has slowed due to the U.S.-Israeli war with Iran.
The ŌĆŗhotel's owner Jumeirah said in a statement on ŌĆŗTuesday the work would be carried out in ŌüĀphases over some 18 months and would be ŌĆŗled by Paris-based interior architect Tristan Auer. It did ŌĆŗnot specify that the property would be closed during the renovation.
The staff member said the hotel is offering alternative accommodation ŌĆŗin nearby hotels to guests with bookings during ŌĆŗthe work. The period of closure is subject to change, the ŌĆīperson ŌüĀsaid.
The sail-shaped hotel, one of Dubai's best-known landmarks and the flagship property of the Jumeirah group, suffered some damage when debris from an interception of an Iranian ŌĆŗdrone attack hit ŌĆŗits facade ŌüĀin early March.
The "long-awaited" work is not linked with the incident in March, the ŌĆŗemployee said. The United Arab Emirates-based Jumeirah did ŌĆŗnot ŌüĀlink the project to the war in its statement.
The timing, however, is notable because the conflict has hurt ŌüĀtravel to ŌĆŗDubai, with flight disruptions affecting the ŌĆŗUAE and luxury groups warning of pressure on profits as visitor demand weakens.

item.title: Peru seeks second candidate for presidential runoff, leftist Sanchez gains ground
item.description:
LIMA, April 15 (Reuters) - Peru entered on Wednesday its fourth day of vote counting ŌĆŗwith no clear second candidate to face conservative ŌĆīleader Keiko Fujimori in a presidential runoff, official data showed with about 90% of ballots counted.
Left-wing congressman, Roberto Sanchez, and ŌĆŗright-wing former Lima mayor, Rafael Lopez Aliaga, are ŌĆŗvying for second place with about 12% of ŌüĀthe votes, followed closely by center-left candidate Jorge Nieto ŌĆŗwith roughly 11%, Peru's electoral body, ONPE, said.
Sanchez moved ŌĆŗinto second place in the tight election , opens new tab overnight, while Fujimori, a former congresswoman and the daughter of late former President Alberto Fujimori, remained ŌĆŗin first place with 16.9% of the vote ŌĆŗin Sunday's election, according to the official tally.
She is set to ŌĆīadvance ŌüĀto a runoff expected to occur on June 7, in her fourth bid for the presidency of the Andean country. Her opponent remains unclear, with three candidates neck ŌĆŗand neck ŌĆŗin the vote ŌüĀcount, while none of them was able to achieve the 50% of the ŌĆŗvotes needed to win outright.
As counting continues, allegations ŌĆŗof ŌüĀfraud have started to spread more widely. Lopez Aliaga and Sanchez have shown concerns about the integrity of the ŌüĀprocess ŌĆŗwith Lopez Aliaga sharing early ŌĆŗWednesday a post that called for Keiko to request the proceedings to ŌĆŗbe annulled.

item.title: Four killed, 20 wounded in school shooting in southern Turkey, local governor says
item.description:
ANKARA, April 15 (Reuters) - Four people were killed ŌĆŗand 20 others ŌĆīwere wounded when a middle school ŌĆŗstudent opened ŌĆŗfire at a school ŌüĀin the ŌĆŗTurkey's southeastern province ŌĆŗof Kahramanmaras on Wednesday, Governor Mukerrem Unluer told ŌĆŗreporters.
Unluer said ŌĆŗa teacher was among ŌĆīthose ŌüĀkilled, and added the shooter was a student ŌĆŗwho used ŌĆŗhis ŌüĀfather's weapons, concealed in ŌĆŗa backpack, ŌĆŗto ŌüĀcarry out the attack. The shooter ŌüĀalso ŌĆŗdied, ŌĆŗhe added.

item.title: Bosnia selects US investor for gas link with Croatia to cut reliance on Russia
item.description:
SARAJEVO, April 15 (Reuters) - A regional parliament in Bosnia and ŌĆīHerzegovina on Wednesday named U.S. company AAFS Infrastructure and Energy LLC as the main investor and leader of a long-delayed project to build a natural gas pipeline with Croatia that is seen as an ŌĆŗalternative to Russian gas.
Both houses of the Bosniak-Croat Federation's parliament agreed to amend ŌĆŗa law regulating the Southern Interconnection gas pipeline project, which will bring ŌüĀU.S. natural gas to Bosnia from a liquefied natural gas terminal on the Croatian ŌĆŗisland of Krk.
The Federation is one of two autonomous regions making up postwar Bosnia. The ŌĆŗother is the Serb Republic.
Sarajevo-based state company BH Gas, which had previously been appointed to lead the project, was removed from the legislation which Croatian deputies in parliament's upper house blocked for years, insisting ŌĆŗon the establishment of a new transmission system operator in the Croat-dominated part of ŌĆŗwestern and southern Bosnia, through which the pipeline will pass.
The Federation government previously said it was necessary ŌĆīto ŌüĀamend the legislation in order to diversify energy sources and avoid a possible energy crisis when the European Union halts Russian gas deliveries from 2028.
AAFS' director in Sarajevo, Amir Bekan, said the total value of the company's investment in Bosnia came to around $1.5 billion, ŌĆŗwith most of the ŌĆŗamount accounted for ŌüĀby the pipeline, but with around $300 million for the modernisation of the airports in Sarajevo and Mostar.
Natural gas accounts for up to ŌĆŗ8% of Bosnia's energy requirement and is supplied solely by Russia ŌĆŗvia Serbia ŌüĀthrough the TurkStream pipeline as the country does not have any reserves of its own.
The government will now sign a direct agreement with AAFS. It is also expected to sign an ŌüĀagreement on ŌĆŗthe pipeline with Croatia at the end of ŌĆŗApril.
Observers have cautioned that Bosnia's complex and fragmented legal system may slow down procedures, especially those related to the ŌĆŗexpropriation of state-owned land on the pipeline's route.

item.title: Slovak refiner says no deal yet on Adria pipeline tests
item.description:
April 15 (Reuters) - Slovak oil refiner Slovnaft said on Wednesday it had still not reached an agreement with Croatia over tests ŌĆŗto see if the Adria oil pipeline can carry enough supplies for Hungary ŌĆīand Slovakia, although existing shipments had enabled it to boost production.
The pipeline from Croatia is an alternative oil route to Slovakia and Hungary, and its importance has grown with the Druzhba pipeline - which carries Russian crude and ŌĆŗruns through Ukraine - out of operation since the end of January.
Supplies through Adria have ŌĆŗhelped Slovnaft - a unit of Hungarian oil and gas group MOL (MOLB.BU) , opens new tab - to ŌüĀramp up processing after a drop caused by the Druzhba outage, Slovnaft's Chief Executive Gabriel ŌĆŗSzabo told a televised press conference.
He said that tests of the Adria pipeline, including in different ŌĆŗweather conditions, were needed to discover whether it had sufficient capacity to serve the two countries.
MOL and Croatian pipeline operator JANAF had previously agreed on the testing to start in March, according to the Hungarian company, but there ŌĆŗwere still disagreements over details, Szabo said.
Slovnaft had to ŌĆŗcut output and borrow from national crude oil reserves after the Druzhba outage, which Ukraine says was caused ŌĆīby a ŌüĀRussian attack on the pipeline in late January.
Ukraine's President Volodymyr Zelenskiy said on Tuesday that Druzhba would be restarted by the end of April.
Slovnaft has since returned the borrowed crude thanks to shipments of seaborne non-Russian oil through the Adria and the 6-million-metric-ton-per-year refinery, which exports diesel to ŌĆŗneighbouring countries, is now ŌĆŗrunning near 100% capacity, ŌüĀSzabo said.
"We are going at 90 if not 100%, but the technological capacity is limited by the fact that we have to process other ŌĆŗcrudes than Russian," Szabo said, without giving details on exact volumes.
He ŌĆŗsaid supplies to ŌüĀSlovnaft were also enabled by an outage at another MOL refinery in Hungary following a fire, which limited the amounts required through Adria.
Slovnaft has been investing in technological changes to use exclusively non-Russian ŌüĀoil ŌĆŗblends from 2027.
The European Union plans to wean itself completely ŌĆŗoff Russian oil, though Hungary and Slovakia have objected to the plans and the European Commission has delayed submitting a legal ŌĆŗproposal on the matter.

item.title: UN experts condemn Israeli strikes on Lebanon, UN rights body says
item.description:
April 15 (Reuters) - United Nations experts on Wednesday condemned Israel's bombing in ŌĆŗLebanon earlier this month as "illegal ŌĆīaggression and indiscriminate bombing campaign", the United Nations Human Rights Council ŌĆŗsaid.
"This is not self-defence. ŌĆŗIt is a blatant violation ŌüĀof the UN Charter, a ŌĆŗdeliberate destruction of prospects for ŌĆŗpeace, and an affront to multilateralism and the UN-based international order," the ŌĆŗexperts were quoted as saying ŌĆŗin a statement.
Israel carried out its heaviest ŌĆīstrikes ŌüĀon Lebanon since the conflict with Hezbollah broke out last month, killing more than 250 ŌĆŗpeople on ŌĆŗApril ŌüĀ8, as the Iran-aligned group resumed rocket attacks ŌĆŗon northern Israel after ŌĆŗa ŌüĀbrief pause under a two-week U.S.-Iran ceasefire.
Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin ŌüĀNetanyahu ŌĆŗhas said Lebanon ŌĆŗwas not part of the ceasefire.

item.title: Pope Leo says world needs message of peace and coexistence
item.description:
ABOARD THE PAPAL FLIGHT, April 15 (Reuters) - Pope ŌĆŗLeo said on Wednesday ŌĆīthat the world needs to hear ŌĆŗa message of ŌĆŗpeace and coexistence, in ŌüĀremarks hours after ŌĆŗU.S. President Donald ŌĆŗTrump's latest social media attack against the pontiff.
Speaking on ŌĆŗhis flight from ŌĆŗCameroon to Algeria for the ŌĆīsecond ŌüĀleg of the tour, the first U.S. pontiff urged ŌĆŗrespect ŌĆŗfor ŌüĀall the peoples of the ŌĆŗworld and ŌĆŗsaid ŌüĀhis tour has shown the importance of ŌüĀpursuing ŌĆŗdialogue between ŌĆŗdifferent communities.

item.title: Kenyan court fines and jails Chinese man in ant-smuggling case
item.description:
NAIROBI, April 15 (Reuters) - A Kenyan court on Wednesday ordered a ŌĆīChinese man to pay a fine of 1 million shillings ($7,746) and gave him a 12-month jail term for trying to smuggle live ants out of the country.
The ŌĆŗmagistrate in the case said a stiff sentence was needed ŌĆŗas a deterrent given a spate of cases in Kenya ŌüĀof ant-trafficking.
It serves markets, such as China, where enthusiasts have paid ŌĆŗlarge sums to maintain ant colonies in large transparent vessels known as ŌĆŗformicariums that allow them to study the species' complex social structures and behaviours.
Chinese national Zhang Kequn was arrested last month at Nairobi's main international airport with more than ŌĆŗ2,200 live garden ants in his luggage.
Zhang's lawyer said he would ŌĆŗappeal against his sentence.
He initially pleaded not guilty to charges including dealing in live ŌĆīwildlife ŌüĀspecies but later changed his plea to guilty.
"Noting the increasing and rising cases of dealing in large quantities of garden ants and the negative ecological side effects of massive harvesting, there is a need for ŌĆŗa stiff deterrent," ŌĆŗmagistrate Irene Gichobi ŌüĀsaid.
A Kenyan man, Charles Mwangi, was also charged in the case, accused of supplying the ants to Zhang.
Mwangi has ŌĆŗpleaded not guilty and is out on bail. His ŌĆŗcase ŌüĀwas not before the court on Wednesday.
Last year four men were fined 1 million shillings each for trying to traffic thousands of ants. Wildlife experts said ŌüĀat ŌĆŗthe time that the case signalled a ŌĆŗshift in biopiracy from trophies like elephant ivory to lesser-known species.
($1 = 129.1000 Kenyan shillings)

item.title: Energean hopes for major discovery off western Greece, says CEO
item.description:
ATHENS, April 15 (Reuters) - Oil and gas producer Energean (ENOG.L) , opens new tab will aim for a big discovery of hydrocarbons off western Greece ŌĆŗwhere the first test drilling could take place ŌĆīas early as next year, CEO Mathios Rigas said on Wednesday.
ExxonMobil (XOM.N) , opens new tab last year joined Energean and Greece's biggest oil refiner Helleniq Energy (HEPr.AT) , opens new tab to ŌĆŗexplore for natural gas in the Ionian Sea ŌĆŗas the United States seeks a growing role ŌüĀin helping Europe replace much of the Russian gas ŌĆŗfollowing Moscow's invasion of Ukraine.
"We are aiming for a big ŌĆŗdiscovery but it will mean nothing if the first drilling is not successful," said Rigas in a ceremony in Athens where Energean ŌĆŗmandated Sweden's Stena Drilling to drill the first well.
The ŌĆŗdrilling in Block 2, an area extending across 1,000 square kilometres, ŌĆīis ŌüĀthe first deep-sea test drilling in Greece in almost 40 years and could take place in February 2027 once permits are secured, Rigas said.
The "Asopos" structure could carry recoverable deposits ŌĆŗof gas ŌĆŗand oil ŌüĀof 6-7 trillion cubic feet and the likelihood of success was about 16%, he added.
"The ŌĆŗchances are very good," he said, adding ŌĆŗthat ŌüĀthe development would involve a floating offshore facility to link subsea wells to Greece and Italy.
Greece now produces only very ŌüĀsmall ŌĆŗquantities of oil and any discovery ŌĆŗof gas will help the country cover both domestic demand and start ŌĆŗexporting to Europe.

item.title: IMF cautions countries against broad fuel subsidies to deal with war-driven energy shock
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 15 (Reuters) - The war in the Middle East has intensified strains on an already fragile global fiscal situation, with higher interest rates and rising energy prices already fueling calls for support from emerging markets and developing economies, the International Monetary Fund said on Wednesday in its ŌĆŗFiscal Monitor report.
Rodrigo Valdes, the IMF's new fiscal affairs chief, said countries should eschew fuel subsidies to help their citizens ŌĆīdeal with a shortage of oil and the corresponding surge in energy prices. Targeted, temporary cash transfers that do not mask higher prices would be a far better option, he said.
"We don't have oil. We don't have energy. Energy needs to be more expensive for everybody, so that the adjustment happens and we consume less," Valdes told ŌĆŗReuters in an interview.
The IMF on Tuesday cut its growth outlook due to war-driven energy price spikes and supply disruptions, warning that the ŌĆŗglobal economy could be driven to the brink of recession if the conflict worsens and oil stays above $100 ŌüĀper barrel through 2027.
"You can pass through (higher energy prices) and then you can do other things to help," Valdes said. "It's a global shock and ŌĆŗif countries suppress the price signal, the global price will be higher ... It's very important to give price signals so demand can adjust."
Valdes said export ŌĆŗcontrols, the extent of damage to energy infrastructure and the capacity of other countries to boost oil output would shape the assessment of the war's impact, and its policy implications.
Once conditions stabilized, he said it was critical that countries stay focused on longer-term challenges as public debt continued to increase, driven by expanded permanent spending on entitlement ŌĆŗprograms or reduced revenues, particularly in some of the largest economies.
Global government debt reached 93.9% of gross domestic product (GDP) in 2025, up nearly ŌĆŗtwo percentage points from 92% a year earlier, and was expected to reach 100% of GDP by 2029, a year earlier than expected just a year ago, according ŌĆīto the ŌüĀIMF's latest Fiscal Monitor.
That would mark the highest government debt burden since the aftermath of World War Two, the report said. Government debt was expected to keep rising and could reach 102.3% of GDP by 2031, it added.
Interest payments had also risen sharply, hitting nearly 3% of GDP in 2025, up from 2% four years ago, the IMF said.
Valdes warned of emerging risks, including a reshaping of debt markets that gives a larger role ŌĆŗto investors such as hedge funds, ŌĆŗwho he said were "less firm hands ŌüĀto hold debt for the long run." The duration of debt had also been declining, which meant that short-term interest rates transmitted more quickly to debt dynamics.
Other challenges included higher security costs, energy and climate transition spending ŌĆŗand rising interest bills at a time when revenues had not kept pace, the IMF said in ŌĆŗa blog accompanying the ŌüĀreport. Trade and financial fragmentation could further sap growth and push up borrowing costs, while political instability may undermine reforms and revenue collection. Abrupt shifts in markets, including in AI stocks, could tighten financial conditions quickly.
Valdes said countries needed to start working on fiscal consolidation once the immediate crisis was resolved.
"There are ŌüĀsome countries ŌĆŗthat are taking this seriously but many others we don't see yet a plan ŌĆŗthat is spelled out," he said, adding that even those with plans still had more work to do.
"We're not at a crisis point ... but the more you delay the measures, the ŌĆŗsteeper will be the effort that you need, and the higher the risk of having a disorderly consolidation later."

item.title: Oilfield services firm NOV says Middle East disruptions to weigh on first quarter
item.description:
April 15 (Reuters) - NOV (NOV.N) , opens new tab said on Wednesday its first-quarter revenue and earnings will ŌĆŗcome in below its prior outlook due to delays in ŌĆīequipment deliveries and higher logistics costs linked to the war in the Middle East.
Shares of the oilfield equipment and services provider fell nearly 3% in premarket trading.
The ŌĆŗcompany estimated the conflict reduced revenue by around $54 million and ŌĆŗadjusted earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortisation (EBITDA) by about $32 ŌüĀmillion.
"These challenges disproportionately affected quarter-end deliveries of capital equipment and products, ŌĆŗincluding spare parts, in the region, while our more service-oriented offerings were ŌĆŗless affected," said CEO Jose Bayardo.
"Higher shipping and freight costs and reduced absorption in our manufacturing facilities further amplified decremental margins."
Rising security risks and damage to energy infrastructure ŌĆŗhave driven drilling and oilfield activity sharply lower across the Middle ŌĆŗEast, cutting demand for oilfield services and equipment in one of the world's top ŌĆīenergy-producing ŌüĀregions.
Industry bellwether SLB (SLB.N) , opens new tab last month said the conflict would reduce its first-quarter earnings by about 6 cents to 9 cents per share and push revenue below expectations.
Major energy companies are also taking precautionary steps. ExxonMobil (XOM.N) , opens new tab has evacuated non-essential ŌĆŗstaff from some ŌĆŗMiddle East operations ŌüĀafter the war broke out between Iran and U.S.-Israel.
NOV had previously forecast first-quarter adjusted EBITDA in the range ŌĆŗof $200 million to $225 million and its revenue to ŌĆŗdecline 1% ŌüĀto 3% from a year earlier.
It now expects to report revenue of about $2.05 billion for the quarter, with operating profit of $47 million and adjusted ŌüĀEBITDA of $177 ŌĆŗmillion.
The company said none of its facilities ŌĆŗin the region were damaged and it is working to mitigate delays and rising costs.

item.title: Russia's Novatek says its gas output up 3% y/y in Q1
item.description:
MOSCOW, April 15 (Reuters) - Russia's largest liquefied ŌĆŗnatural gas ŌĆīproducer Novatek (NVTK.MM) , opens new tab said on Wednesday ŌĆŗits first-quarter ŌĆŗnatural gas production edged ŌüĀup by ŌĆŗ3.1% from ŌĆŗthe same period in 2025 to ŌĆŗ22.03 billion ŌĆŗcubic metres.
It also said ŌĆīits ŌüĀoutput of oil and gas condensate increased ŌĆŗby ŌĆŗ4.2% ŌüĀto 3.62 million tons ŌĆŗin ŌĆŗthe ŌüĀJanuary - March quarter.

item.title: Explainer: Why is Sudan at war, and what is the impact?
item.description:
April 15 (Reuters) - The war in Sudan entered its fourth year on April 15 overshadowed by other regional and global turmoil, despite having created what the United Nations calls the world's worst humanitarian crisis.
The war pits the Sudanese army, led by General Abdel Fattah al-Burhan, against the Rapid Support Forces, a ŌĆŗparamilitary group led by General Mohamed Hamdan Dagalo.
The two men had joined forces in 2019 to overthrow veteran autocrat Omar al-Bashir, ŌĆīafter which Burhan became head of state. But having staged a coup that derailed a faltering transition towards civilian rule, they fell out over plans to launch a new transition and integrate their forces.
After war erupted on April 15, 2023, it quickly drew in myriad local militias fighting on either side, as well as foreign powers.
Most prominently, U.N. researchers, U.S. lawmakers, ŌĆŗand the Sudanese army say the United Arab Emirates has provided critical support to the RSF via several of Sudan's neighbors , a charge the Gulf ŌĆŗpower denies.
The Sudanese army counts other regional powers including Egypt , Turkey, Saudi Arabia , and Qatar as supporters to varying extents.
The RSF last year cemented control of the vast Darfur region, its traditional power base and the westernmost area of the country, and ŌĆŗquickly began setting up a parallel government. The army maintains control over the eastern half of the country.
The fight is currently centered around the Kordofan region that lies ŌĆŗin between, as well as a new front opened up by the RSF along the Sudanese border with Ethiopia in the southeast.
Drone warfare has displaced ground campaigns as the primary mode of fighting, and has allowed the RSF to overcome the army's previous air dominance. Like the shelling and bombardments that marked the war's earlier stages, it has also added to the human ŌĆŗtoll of the conflict, claiming at least 700 civilian lives this year, according to the U.N.
The war has had a devastating impact ŌĆŗon the Sudanese people, with the U.N. estimating that almost three-quarters are in need of humanitarian aid.
Famine or risk of famine has been declared in conflict hotspots across the country, ŌĆīoften exacerbated ŌüĀby blockades and bureaucratic impediments placed by the warring parties.
Diseases including dengue fever have run rampant as the country's healthcare system collapsed in many areas.
Throughout the war, the RSF has carried out waves of ethnically targeted killings later cited as bearing the hallmarks of genocide by U.N. researchers , most recently in North Darfur's al-Fashir .
Violence and reduced funding have prevented the calculation of a comprehensive death toll. Sudan's health ministry tells Reuters it has documented 11,209 deaths across most of Sudan's states, but experts ŌĆŗsay excess deaths since the war began ŌĆŗare in the hundreds of ŌüĀthousands.
Despite overwhelming need, the U.N.'s 2026 appeal for aid to Sudan is only 17% funded, coming at a time when the U.S. has retreated from foreign aid, European donors make cuts, and Gulf powers focus on bilateral donations.
Aid agencies report ŌĆŗscaling back their services, while Sudanese mutual aid groups, including the Nobel Peace Prize-nominated Emergency Response Rooms, have tried ŌĆŗto fill the gap.
A ŌüĀstudy by aid agency Islamic Relief this week, however, showed that more than 40% of the local community kitchens run by these groups have had to shut down due to lack of support.
While the war has caught the attention of world leaders, including U.S. President Donald Trump, few attempts to ŌüĀend it ŌĆŗhave shown promise and competing regional interests have hindered efforts.
The U.S. has led a so-called ŌĆŗQuad that includes Egypt, Saudi Arabia, and the United Arab Emirates, which presented a preliminary proposal for a ceasefire to both sides last year.
As realities on the ground have changed, both the army ŌĆŗand RSF have by turns welcomed and rejected mediation, with no let-up in fighting on the horizon.

item.title: NATO on track to fund Ukraine defence aid despite burden-sharing gaps, Rutte says
item.description:
BRUSSELS, April 15 (Reuters) - NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte expressed ŌĆŗoptimism Wednesday that the ŌĆīalliance would successfully fund defence aid to Ukraine through ŌĆŗthe Prioritised Ukraine Requirements ŌĆŗList (PURL) mechanism before the end ŌüĀof the year.
"I'm ŌĆŗoptimistic," Rutte told reporters following ŌĆŗa Ukraine Defence Contact Group meeting in Berlin, while acknowledging ŌĆŗthat burden-sharing within the ŌĆŗalliance remained uneven. "A limited number of ŌĆīcountries ŌüĀare doing the heavy lifting ŌĆö but we are seeing change for the ŌĆŗbetter."
"We ŌĆŗhave to ŌüĀmake sure Ukraine has what it ŌĆŗneeds to stay ŌĆŗin ŌüĀthe fight. So there is broad agreement on that ŌüĀon ŌĆŗboth sides ŌĆŗof the Atlantic."

item.title: Man goes on trial accused of attempting terrorist attack at London's Israeli embassy
item.description:
LONDON, April 15 (Reuters) - A man armed with two knives and carrying a "martyrdom note" tried to carry out a terrorist attack at Israel's British embassy because he wanted to send a message about "the killing ŌĆŗof children", prosecutors told a London court on Wednesday.
Abdullah Albadri, 34, who was born ŌĆīin Kuwait and twice entered Britain from France in a small boat, was arrested by two armed officers trying to scale the fence outside the embassy in west London last April, prosecutor Catherine Pattison said.
They found he was ŌĆŗcarrying two knives and a piece of paper which appeared to be a note about ŌĆŗmartyrdom, and later told police he wanted to send a message about stopping ŌüĀthe war, a reference to the conflict between the Israelis and Palestinians, Pattison told London's Old ŌĆŗBailey court.
"The prosecution say that Mr Albadri's intention was to use or threaten serious violence against the ŌĆŗIsraeli government, to exact revenge for its alleged murder of children," she said.
"The existence and contents of his suspected martyrdom note, along with his two knives and material downloaded from his mobile phone, demonstrate his intention to use violence ŌĆŗagainst people inside the Israeli embassy and sacrifice his own life in the process ŌĆō to die, ŌĆŗin his words, 'for the glory of God'."
Albadri denies charges of preparing an act of terrorism and possession of two ŌĆībladed ŌüĀarticles, and he said in a statement to police that he had the knives because he was homeless.
Pattison said Albadri had first come to Britain on a small boat in August 2021 but about two years later, having withdrawn an application seeking permission to stay, he appeared to have left the ŌĆŗcountry.
On April 12 last ŌĆŗyear he again arrived ŌüĀin Dover via a small boat, and began an application for asylum, saying he had left Kuwait for his and his family's safety because he ŌĆŗwas a human rights activist.
However, exchanges on his phone showed his asylum ŌĆŗclaim was ŌüĀrejected on April 24, after which his intention began to shift to "far more sinister matters" and he started searching for the location of the Israeli embassy, Pattison said.
On the afternoon of April 28, Albadri sent ŌüĀa picture ŌĆŗof a handwritten note alongside a knife to his mother.
Hours ŌĆŗlater, Albadri was detained by the officers having almost succeeded in scaling the fence outside the embassy, Pattison said.
"'Why didn't you ŌĆŗlet me in?'," Pattison said he told them.
The trial continues.

item.title: Kuwait detained US-Kuwaiti journalist over war posts, CPJ says
item.description:
LONDON, April 15 (Reuters) - Kuwaiti authorities have detained U.S.-Kuwaiti journalist Ahmed Shihab-Eldin who has not been seen for six weeks, a ŌĆŗmedia watchdog said, during a wider crackdown by Gulf nations on people for filming or posting footage during the Iran ŌĆīwar .
Shihab-Eldin, a 41-year-old journalist who was visiting family in Kuwait, has not posted online or been seen in public since March 2, the New York-based Committee to Protect Journalists said on Monday.
CPJ said he had been charged with spreading false information, harming national security and misusing his mobile phone.
Kuwait enacted ŌĆŗa law on March 15 including a provision carrying prison terms of up to 10 years for spreading false rumours ŌĆŗrelated to military entities with the intent of undermining confidence in them.
Government spokespeople did not immediately respond ŌüĀto Reuters' attempts to obtain comment on Shihab-Eldin's case via email and telephone.
Elsewhere in the Gulf, authorities have reported hundreds ŌĆŗof arrests of people filming sites affected by the Middle East conflict or sharing related material online.
Iran responded to U.S.-Israeli strikes from February ŌĆŗ28 by targeting U.S. military and energy installations in neighbouring Gulf states.
Shihab-Eldin had posted before his detention on publicly available videos and images related to the war. The last posts on his Substack account include footage showing a U.S. fighter jet crash near a U.S. air base in Kuwait.
Shihab-Eldin describes himself ŌĆŗas an independent journalist who has worked for various international media outlets including the New York Times, HBO, Vice, PBS, BBC ŌĆŗand Al Jazeera. It was unclear if he was working, as well as seeing family, during his visit to Kuwait.
"We call on ŌĆīKuwait ŌüĀto release Ahmed Shihab-Eldin and drop all charges against him,ŌĆØ said CPJ Regional Director Sara Qudah. ŌĆ£Journalism is not a crime."
U.N. official Francesca Albanese, who is the special rapporteur for the Palestinian territories, also called for his release, praising him as a "journalist of rare strength, integrity and compassion."
Reuters could not independently verify the circumstances of his detention or the reported charges.
In Kuwait, the Interior Ministry said on February ŌĆŗ28 that the public should refrain ŌĆŗfrom filming missile interceptions, sharing ŌüĀsuch footage on social media, or filming security personnel at work.
It urged residents to rely on official sources.
On March 2, state news agency KUNA reported that the ministry had arrested a number of ŌĆŗpeople over what it described as violations affecting public security, including filming events and circulating video ŌĆŗclips.
Reuters could ŌüĀnot independently confirm the arrests.
Qatar's Interior Ministry said on March 9 that 313 people of various nationalities had been arrested for filming and circulating video clips, spreading misleading information and rumours, and publishing material aimed at stirring public concern.
In the United Arab Emirates, Abu Dhabi police in ŌüĀa statement ŌĆŗon April 8 said 375 people of various nationalities had been arrested over ŌĆŗfilming locations and sharing misleading information on social media in connection with "current events", and that the cases had been referred to prosecutors.
Reuters could not determine how the ŌĆŗcases have proceeded.
There was no immediate response from Qatar or the UAE to Reuters requests for comments.

item.title: UN human rights office raises concern around trial of Chinese dissident artist
item.description:
GENEVA, April 15 (Reuters) - The United Nations human rights office on Wednesday said it was concerned by the ŌĆŗtrial of Chinese dissident artist Gao Zhen, famous ŌĆīfor making provocative satirical sculptures of former leader Mao Zedong.
Gao was tried at the end of March over accusations of "slandering national heroes and ŌĆŗmartyrs", the U.N. human rights office said ŌĆö a law ŌĆŗwhich it said came into force more than ŌüĀa decade after the satirical sculptures were created.
"The case against ŌĆŗhim raises concerns with regard to retroactive application of criminal ŌĆŗlaw and use of criminal sanctions to punish artistic expression, undermining the principle of legality," the office said in a statement.
The Chinese mission ŌĆŗin Geneva did not immediately respond to a Reuters ŌĆŗrequest for comment.
Gao, who was detained in 2024 during a visit from ŌĆīthe ŌüĀU.S., faces a maximum three-year prison sentence, said his wife Zhao Yaliang and Shane Yi, a researcher at the Chinese Human Rights Defenders group which operates outside the country.
The ŌĆŗU.N. rights office ŌĆŗcalled for ŌüĀGao's immediate release from detention, where he is being held pending judgment after his closed-door ŌĆŗtrial concluded on March 30, citing concerns ŌĆŗthat his ŌüĀhealth is deteriorating.
The one-day trial took place at Sanhe City People's Court in Hebei province, neighbouring the capital Beijing, and ŌüĀended ŌĆŗwithout a verdict, Zhao and Yi ŌĆŗtold Reuters, citing information from his lawyers.
Verdicts are often announced months later in ŌĆŗsuch trials.

item.title: Middle East war damage to energy assets may cost up to $58 billion, research firm Rystad says
item.description:
April 15 (Reuters) - The Middle East conflict could saddle the region with as much as $58 billion in repair costs for energy-linked infrastructure, with oil ŌĆŗand gas facilities alone accounting for up to $50 billion, according to ŌĆīa report by Rystad Energy.
The estimate marks a sharp increase from the research firm's initial $25 billion projection three weeks ago, reflecting a broader scope of damage ŌĆŗbefore an April 8 ceasefire between the U.S. and Iran.
"Repair work does ŌĆŗnot create new capacity. It redirects existing capacity, and that ŌüĀredirection will be felt in project delays and into inflation far ŌĆŗbeyond the Middle East," Rystad senior analyst Karan Satwani said.
"The $58 billion bill ŌĆŗis the headline, but the knock-on effects on energy investment timelines globally may prove just as significant."
Rystad said total repair spending is likely to average ŌĆŗaround $46 billion, with downstream refining and petrochemical assets accounting for ŌĆŗthe largest share due to their complexity and extent of damage.
Industrial, power, and ŌĆīdesalination ŌüĀassets may add a further $3 billion to $8 billion in costs, the report added.
Recovery timelines are starting to diverge between assets and countries, showcasing differences in domestic execution capabilities and access to supply chains, ŌĆŗRystad added.
Iran faces the ŌĆŗmost widespread ŌüĀdamage, with repair costs potentially reaching $19 billion, affecting gas processing, refining and export infrastructure.
In contrast, Qatar's impact ŌĆŗis more concentrated but technically complex, particularly at ŌĆŗits ŌüĀRas Laffan industrial hub, where repair work may overlap with ongoing LNG expansion projects.
Rystad said engineering and construction will account for the largest ŌüĀshare ŌĆŗof spending, but delays in procuring critical ŌĆŗequipment will likely dictate recovery timelines.
According to Rystad, procuring equipment and workers remains the ŌĆŗbiggest challenge.

item.title: Spain's Senate blackout probe blames grid operator, government, watchdog
item.description:
MADRID, April 15 (Reuters) - An inquiry by the Spanish Senate blames the government, as well the country's grid operator and energy ŌĆŗwatchdog, for last year's unprecedented blackout, according to ŌĆīpreliminary conclusions made public on Wednesday.
The probe by the upper house - which is controlled by the opposition People's Party (PP) - is the first ŌĆŗto assign blame for the outage that plunged large ŌĆŗparts of Spain and Portugal into darkness for ŌüĀup to 16 hours on April 28, 2025.
The nineŌĆæmonth ŌĆŗinvestigation said the blackout was not an unforeseeable accident, but ŌĆŗrather caused by longŌĆæstanding structural weaknesses that were already known.
"The blackout was the result of a known vulnerability, of a system that had ŌĆŗbeen sending warnings for some time,
and of a failure ŌĆŗto act with the required diligence," PP senator Alicia Garcia told reporters.
The ŌĆīpreliminary ŌüĀreport cited repeated voltage swings in the weeks and months leading up to the blackout as evidence of mounting problems in the electricity system.
The Senate commission held grid operator ŌĆŗRed Electrica, a ŌĆŗunit of ŌüĀRedeia (REDE.MC) , opens new tab , and the Energy Ministry primarily responsible for the outage, while also criticising energy and ŌĆŗantitrust regulator CNMC for what it described as ŌĆŗregulatory ŌüĀand supervisory inaction.
The inquiry heard testimonies from dozens of witnesses, including Energy Minister Sara Aagesen, Redeia chair Beatriz Corredor and ŌüĀCNMC ŌĆŗhead Cani Fernandez.
The final report is ŌĆŗdue later this week, though no changes to the preliminary conclusions are expected.

item.title: Counter-terrorism police investigate attempted arson attack on London synagogue
item.description:
LONDON, April 15 (Reuters) - British police, including specialist counter-terrorism officers, have launched an investigation after an attempted arson attack on a synagogue in north ŌĆŗLondon, in the latest incident involving the capital's Jewish community.
Two ŌĆīsuspects wearing dark clothing and balaclavas threw two bottles, suspected of containing petrol, and a brick at the synagogue in Finchley shortly after midnight on Wednesday, ŌĆŗpolice said. Neither bottle ignited and there was no damage ŌĆŗor any injuries.
However, the incident, which is being treated ŌüĀas an antisemitic hate crime, comes just weeks after four ambulances ŌĆŗbelonging to the Jewish volunteer emergency service Hatzola were torched while they were ŌĆŗparked near a synagogue in the Golders Green area of north London.
"We are aware of the significant concern that this incident will cause in the community, ŌĆŗparticularly in the wake of the arson attack in Golders Green ŌĆŗlast month," said Detective Chief Superintendent Luke Williams.
Local member of parliament Sarah Sackman said ŌĆīshe ŌüĀrefused to accept such incidents becoming "the new normal".
"This shocking attempt to harm a local synagogue follows a series of alarming attacks on the Jewish community in Finchley and Golders Green," she said in a ŌĆŗstatement.
"British Jews must ŌĆŗbe free to ŌüĀgo about their lives without fear - whether taking their children to nursery or attending synagogue. We do ŌĆŗnot want to live behind ever higher walls."
Attacks on Jewish ŌĆŗpeople and ŌüĀJewish targets have risen in Britain since the October 2023 Hamas assault on Israel that triggered the war in Gaza, according to police ŌüĀand community ŌĆŗgroups.
In February, the government announced about ŌĆŗ28 million pounds ($38 million) would be provided to help provide security at synagogues, Jewish schools ŌĆŗand community centres.
($1 = 0.7369 pounds)

item.title: Russian LNG under US sanctions heads for the first time to India
item.description:
LONDON, April 15 (Reuters) - A cargo ŌĆŗof liquefied natural gas from Russia's Portovaya plant on the Baltic Sea that ŌĆīis under U.S. sanctions is on its way to India, LSEG shipping data showed on Wednesday.
If it reaches its destination, it would be the first such delivery to India since U.S. President Donald Trump last year said ŌĆŗPrime Minister Narendra Modi had assured him India would stop buying Russian energy.
India has ŌĆŗnever publicly confirmed any such pledge and has said its buying decisions are ŌüĀguided by price, supply security and consumer interests. As one of the world's biggest energy ŌĆŗimporters, it is now greatly exposed to the disruption and price rises caused by the Middle Eastern war ŌĆŗand the resulting closure of the Strait of Hormuz.
The small-scale Portovaya plant, which has a production capacity of 1.5 million tons of LNG per year, started operations in September 2022.
Exports from the plant have been interrupted by ŌĆŗadditional sanctions over the Ukraine war that were imposed in January 2025 to disrupt Russia's ability ŌĆŗto produce and export LNG and reduce its revenue from the trade.
So far Russia ŌĆīhas ŌüĀsupplied LNG under sanctions to China's port of Beihai from Portovaya and also from its Arctic LNG 2 plant.
Earlier on Wednesday, Russian energy giant Gazprom (GAZP.MM) , opens new tab delivered a second post-sanctions LNG cargo from Portovaya plant to China, LSEG data showed.
If India is buying Russian LNG, that would provide another market as ŌĆŗRussian President Vladimir Putin seeks ŌĆŗto divert LNG ŌüĀfrom Europe before the European Union enforces its import ban on Russia's LNG from 2027.
Kunpeng, a 138,200 cubic metre tanker, is heading to Dahej ŌĆŗLNG import terminal in western India, according to LSEG data.
"The cargo, if ŌĆŗdelivered, would ŌüĀopen up a second market for U.S.-sanctioned Russian LNG, with all previous deliveries from sanctioned Russian terminals delivering to ChinaŌĆÖs Beihai," said Martin Senior, head of LNG pricing at industry publication Argus.
Before the sanctions, ŌüĀPortovaya shipped ŌĆŗtwo cargoes a month on average during winter. Since ŌĆŗMarch 2025, apart from the two shipments to China, it has been sending one cargo per month to the Russian ŌĆŗwestern exclave of Kaliningrad.

item.title: Eni's Vaar Energi plans to boost oil output from Arctic Goliat field
item.description:
OSLO, April 15 (Reuters) - Norway's Vaar Energi (VAR.OL) , opens new tab , a subsidiary of Italy's Eni (ENI.MI) , opens new tab , said on Wednesday it plans to ŌĆŗboost crude production from its Arctic Goliat ŌĆīoilfield along with minority stakeholder Equinor (EQNR.OL) , opens new tab .
The companies will invest around $360 million in the project, which will increase Goliat's ŌĆŗoil output from 2029 onwards and extend the ŌĆŗfield's production lifetime by approximately 10 years ŌüĀto around 2050, Vaar said in a statement.
The ŌĆŗproject will establish a pipeline connection to the ŌĆŗnearby Snoehvit gas facility, allowing the removal of large amounts of natural gas from Goliat that currently restricts the flow ŌĆŗof oil.
"The incremental oil will be sold ŌĆŗfrom the start, while the gas will be exported to ŌĆīthe ŌüĀHammerfest LNG plant under a gas bank arrangement, agreed with Snoehvit, and sold when processing capacity is available," Eni said.
The Goliat field produced more gas ŌĆŗthan oil ŌĆŗlast year ŌüĀbut has no ability to export the gas, forcing the operator to reinject ŌĆŗmost of it into the reservoir, ŌĆŗthe company ŌüĀhas said.
Goliat's average daily oil production stood at just over 22,000 barrels per day in 2025, data ŌüĀfrom ŌĆŗthe Norwegian Offshore Directorate shows.
Vaar owns ŌĆŗ65% of the Goliat licence while Equinor owns 35%.

item.title: Japan refiners run at two-thirds capacity, awaiting crude from outside the Gulf
item.description:
TOKYO, April 15 (Reuters) - Japanese refineries ran at two-thirds of their capacity last week, little changed from the ŌĆīprevious week, highlighting the short-term challenges of replacing their Middle Eastern crude supply, cut by the Iran war.
Refinery runs stood at 67.8% of the designed capacity for the week to April 11, in line with 67.7% in the previous week and well below the ŌĆŗlevels of more than 80% seen before the start of the war in late February, Petroleum Association ŌĆŗof Japan data showed on Wednesday.
The outlook for next month may be better, as the ŌüĀcountry has released oil from its national stockpile and managed to secure substitutes for over half the volume it ŌĆŗimported last May via the Strait of Hormuz, officials have said. Overall, Middle Eastern crudes normally make up 95% ŌĆŗof Japan's imports.
However, Japanese refineries have limitations on the volume of other crudes they can run, as their plants are designed to process mainly medium-sour Middle Eastern crude.
"Japan could likely push non-Middle East crude to around 30-50% of its slate in the short ŌĆŗterm. But fully replacing Middle Eastern supply is difficult," said Nithin Prakash, an analyst with Rystad Energy.
The new ŌĆŗsupply is largely coming from the U.S., Japan's closest ally. Tokyo has also contacted producers in countries including Malaysia, Azerbaijan, Brazil, ŌĆīNigeria and ŌüĀAngola.
The refiners can mix the Middle Eastern crudes with light-sweet crude from the U.S. and West Africa along with some medium grades from the Caspian region and parts of Latin America, Prakash said, adding that the shift would increase yields of gasoline and naphtha and reduce diesel and jet output.
PAJ has suspended publication of Japan's petroleum product stocks, ŌĆŗciting the change in ŌĆŗsupply structure.
Idemitsu Kosan (5019.T) , opens new tab said it ŌüĀis exploring crude purchases from various countries, including in North and South America, adding there are challenges related to refinery equipment when processing non-Middle Eastern oils.
Taiyo Oil said it ŌĆŗcan process more than 50% of non-Middle Eastern grades, including those from North ŌĆŗAmerica, Southeast Asia ŌüĀand grades from Oceania.
Japan's top refiner Eneos (5020.T) , opens new tab and Cosmo Energy (5021.T) , opens new tab declined to comment on their refinery specifications.
Japanese refiners have not made major upgrades at their plants, built mainly during the industrial boom of the 1960s to 1970s, or invested ŌüĀin new ŌĆŗones, as fuel demand is declining.
Gasoline demand in Japan is forecast ŌĆŗto decline 10% over the five years to 2030, according to an April 7 document from the industry ministry, and all major refining groups ŌĆŗhave been expanding into renewables in the recent years.

item.title: Exclusive: Iran offers proposal allowing ships to exit Oman side of Hormuz free of attack, source says
item.description:
DUBAI, April 15 (Reuters) - Iran could consider allowing ships to sail freely through the Oman side ŌĆŗof the Strait of Hormuz without risk of attack ŌĆīas part of proposals it has offered in negotiations with the United States if a deal is clinched to prevent renewed conflict, a ŌĆŗsource briefed by Tehran said.
The war has resulted in ŌĆŗthe largest-ever disruption of global oil and gas supplies ŌüĀdue to Iran's interruption of traffic through the strait, ŌĆŗwhich handles about 20% of the world's oil and liquefied natural gas ŌĆŗflows.
Hundreds of tankers and other ships and 20,000 seafarers have been stuck inside the Gulf since the Iran war began on February 28.
The source, ŌĆŗwho declined to be identified due to the sensitivity of ŌĆŗthe matter, said Iran could be willing to let ships use the ŌĆīother ŌüĀside of the narrow strait in Omani waters without any hindrance from Tehran.
The source did not say whether Iran would also agree to clear any mines it may have placed ŌĆŗin that stretch ŌĆŗof water or ŌüĀif all ships - even those linked to Israel - would be allowed to pass freely.
But the ŌĆŗsource added that the proposal hinged on whether ŌĆŗWashington ŌüĀwas prepared to meet Tehran's demands, a condition that was central to any potential breakthrough with the Strait of Hormuz.
The White ŌüĀHouse ŌĆŗdid not immediately respond to a ŌĆŗrequest for comment.

item.title: Kremlin says US has rejected its proposal that Russia take Iranian uranium stocks
item.description:
April 15 (Reuters) - The Kremlin's spokesman was quoted as saying on Wednesday that the United States had rejected its ŌĆŗproposal that Russia take all of Iran's ŌĆīenriched uranium out of the country as a way to help resolve the Middle East conflict.
Russia first proposed last June that ŌĆŗit take control of Iran's uranium stock, ŌĆŗbut no action was taken. According to news ŌüĀreports, Russia issued the proposal again this week.
"Russia ŌĆŗwas prepared to accept Iran's enriched uranium on its ŌĆŗterritory," state news agency RIA said Kremlin spokesman Dmitry Peskov told the Indian television channel India Today.
"This would be a good ŌĆŗdecision. But unfortunately the American side rejected this ŌĆŗproposal."
U.S. news reports have quoted sources as saying that the ŌĆīadministration ŌüĀof U.S. President Donald Trump had ruled out the proposal. For its part, Iran had said any decision would depend on whether it is able to ŌĆŗreach an agreement ŌĆŗwith the ŌüĀU.S., including on its nuclear programme.
The U.S. has cited Iran's stockpile of enriched ŌĆŗuranium -- and the possibility it might be ŌĆŗable ŌüĀto secure a nuclear weapon -- as grounds for its attacks on Iran.
A Russian deputy foreign minister last year ŌüĀsuggested ŌĆŗRussia was willing to remove ŌĆŗthe stockpile from Iran and convert it to civilian reactor fuel to ŌĆŗhelp facilitate negotiations.

item.title: El Salvador to apply life sentences to minors for serious crimes
item.description:
SAN SALVADOR, April 15 (Reuters) - Salvadoran President Nayib Bukele has enacted legal reforms allowing ŌĆŗlife imprisonment for minors as young ŌĆīas 12 convicted of homicide, terrorism, or rape, according to the country's official gazette.
The measures, published ŌĆŗon Tuesday and scheduled to take ŌĆŗeffect April 26, follow a constitutional amendment ŌüĀapproved in March by the government-controlled Congress.
The ŌĆŗchanges eliminate special legal procedures previously available ŌĆŗto offenders aged 12 to 18, though they include provisions for periodic sentence reviews and potential supervised ŌĆŗrelease.
The constitutional amendment came days after an ŌĆŗinternational legal panel reported " reasonable grounds " to suggest El Salvador ŌĆīhas ŌüĀcommitted crimes against humanity during a years-long state of emergency.
The United Nations human rights office criticized the reforms for violating children's ŌĆŗrights. Bukele ŌĆŗdefended the ŌüĀmeasures, saying the previous legal framework granted impunity to young criminals.
The ŌĆŗ44-year-old president's ongoing state of emergency, ŌĆŗwhich ŌüĀsuspends various constitutional guarantees, has led to the detention of more than 90,000 people. ŌüĀHumanitarian ŌĆŗorganizations estimate at least 500 ŌĆŗof those detainees have died in state custody.

item.title: At least 16 reported dead from heavy rains in Hispaniola
item.description:
April 15 (Reuters) - Days of torrential rainfall over northwest Haiti and neighboring Dominican Republic have killed ŌĆŗsome 16 people, according to civil ŌĆīprotection and local media reports on Wednesday.
At least 12 people were killed in northern Haiti as floods ŌĆŗhit communities in Port-de-Paix, Saint-Louis du ŌĆŗNord and Anse-a-Foleur, local newspaper Le Nouvelliste ŌüĀreported.
In the Dominican Republic, which shares ŌĆŗthe Caribbean island of Hispaniola with Haiti, local ŌĆŗoutlet Listin Diario reported that four people had died, some swept away by swollen rivers, while an ŌĆŗinfant girl was killed in her home ŌĆŗwhen a wall collapsed on her.
Dominican authorities said on ŌĆīMonday ŌüĀthat more than 30,000 people were forced to evacuate their homes, and that the rains were expected to strengthen again this weekend.
In ŌĆŗthe nearby U.S. ŌĆŗterritory ŌüĀof Puerto Rico, authorities urged residents to avoid flooded highways as ŌĆŗrains are expected to continue into ŌĆŗthe ŌüĀafternoon.
Just days earlier, a stampede at northern Haiti's Laferriere Citadel, a UNESCO World Heritage site, killed ŌüĀ25 ŌĆŗpeople after people began forcing ŌĆŗtheir way onto the site during an annual event ŌĆŗas it started to rain.

item.title: Netherlands to spend nearly $300 million euros on drones for Ukraine
item.description:
AMSTERDAM, April 15 (Reuters) - The Netherlands will spend 248 million euros ($293 million) ŌĆŗon producing drones for Ukraine , Dutch ŌĆīDefence Minister Dilan Yesilgoz-Zegerius said on Wednesday.
"Drones play a crucial role ŌĆŗon the modern battlefield. ŌĆŗUkrainians deploy them with incredible ŌüĀskill to repel the incessant ŌĆŗRussian attacks," she said after ŌĆŗmeeting her counterparts from NATO countries and the alliance's Secretary General Mark Rutte ŌĆŗin Berlin on Wednesday.
