Opinions expressed are those of the author. They do ŌĆŗnot reflect the views of Reuters News, which, under the Trust Principles , opens new tab , is committed to integrity, independence, and freedom from bias.

item.title: Trafigura signs $1 billion oil prepayment deal with Gabon
item.description:
April 15 (Reuters) - Commodities trader Trafigura said on Wednesday it had signed a $1 billion prepayment agreement ŌĆŗwith Gabon, under which it will receive crude oil deliveries ŌĆīover a sevenŌĆæyear period.
The Central African country seeks to strengthen cash flow and boost foreignŌĆæexchange reserves amid high global oil prices, Gabon's economy and ŌĆŗfinance ministry said.
Under the agreement, Trafigura will provide upfront ŌĆŗfinancing to the African state in exchange for future ŌüĀcrude oil deliveries and will act as the exclusive offtaker ŌĆŗof Gabon's profit oil for the duration of the deal, the ŌĆŗcompany said in a statement. Profit oil refers to the governmentŌĆÖs share of oil production after companies have recovered their costs.
The government will receive the $1 ŌĆŗbillion in preŌĆæfinancing with a maturity of seven years, the ŌĆŗministry said in a separate statement, adding that no guarantees or collateral were ŌĆīpledged ŌüĀagainst oil cargoes in the deal advised by Algest Consulting.
These resources are exclusively earmarked for funding investment programs and addressing the social needs of the population, it said.
The oil underpinning the ŌĆŗprepayment will be ŌĆŗsourced from production ŌüĀacross multiple petroleum-sharing contracts and from a range of producing assets and operators, Trafigura said.
"We are pleased to ŌĆŗhave signed this agreement with the Republic of ŌĆŗGabon, continuing ŌüĀour long-standing trading relationship and contributing to the country's development agenda," Dave Gallagher, Trafigura's global head of structured finance, said in the ŌüĀstatement.
Trafigura ŌĆŗhas also begun syndicating part of ŌĆŗthe exposure to international financial institutions.

item.title: Iran can go up to two months without oil exports before cutting output, analysts say
item.description:
LONDON, April 15 (Reuters) - Iran can withstand a complete halt ŌĆŗin oil exports of up to two months before being forced to curb production, ŌĆīanalysts said, after the U.S. began blocking shipping in and out of the country's ports on April 13.
The blockade could prevent roughly 2 million barrels per day (bpd) of Iranian crude from reaching its main buyer China.
Any Iranian production shutdowns would add ŌĆŗto more than 12 million bpd of supply already disrupted by the regional war, tightening markets ŌĆŗfurther and lifting oil prices.
With its exports blocked, Iran faces having to divert crude ŌüĀinto onshore storage tanks. Once those tanks are filled, the OPEC member would be required to curb ŌĆŗupstream output.
Consultancy FGE NextantECA estimates Iran has about 90 million barrels of available onshore crude storage capacity, ŌĆŗout of total capacity of roughly 122 million barrels.
"Iran can sustain current production of around 3.5 million bpd for roughly two months without exports, extendable to around three months with a modest 500,000 bpd production cut," FGE NextantECA said in ŌĆŗa note.
Iranian domestic refineries process about 2 million bpd of oil, they added.
The relevant Iranian authorities were ŌĆŗnot immediately available for comment.
Energy Aspects assumes significantly lower available onshore storage of about 30 million barrels, based on ŌĆīdata from ŌüĀKayrros.
Under that scenario, Iran could maintain current export levels for about 16 days before storage capacity runs out, based on export levels of 1.8 million bpd.
"The blockade may not have a significant impact on Iranian production in April, but if it continues into May then output would need to be reduced substantially," ŌĆŗsaid Richard Bronze, co-founder ŌĆŗof Energy Aspects.
He said ŌüĀthe consultancy assumes Iran cannot utilise its full nameplate storage capacity, adding that historic data show stocks peaked at 92 million barrels in May 2020, which ŌĆŗlikely marks a realistic ceiling.
Bronze also said Iran will likely deploy available oil ŌĆŗtankers in ŌüĀports as floating storage, delaying production cuts.
The U.S. military said more vessels were being turned back under the blockade, including the Chinese-owned tanker Rich Starry, which is under U.S. sanctions and which was seen heading back through the ŌüĀStrait ŌĆŗof Hormuz on Wednesday.
Eight Iran-linked oil tankers have been intercepted since ŌĆŗthe blockade began on Monday, the Wall Street Journal reported. A U.S. destroyer stopped two tankers attempting to leave Iran's Chabahar ŌĆŗport on the Gulf of Oman on Tuesday, a U.S. official said.

item.title: IMF chief says 12 or more countries seeking loans to cope with Middle East war energy shock
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 15 (Reuters) - The International Monetary Fund expects at least a dozen countries to seek new loan programs to cope with surging energy prices and ŌĆŗsupply chain disruptions caused by the Middle East war , with several sub-Saharan African nations seeking help, the head of the global crisis lender said on Wednesday.
IMF Managing Director Kristalina ŌĆīGeorgieva also warned of deepening supply disruptions from the closure of the Strait of Hormuz even if the conflict ends quickly and urged countries to take measures to reduce their fuel usage.
Speaking at a press conference during the IMF and World Bank spring meetings in Washington, Georgieva repeated her estimate that disruptions from the war could trigger new demand for $20 billion to $50 billion in financial support that could include new loans and augmentation of some of the global lender's ŌĆŗ39 existing country financing programs.
She did not name specific countries that have requested aid, although she said the IMF was not currently discussing an augmentation of Egypt's $8 billion loan program despite ŌĆŗthe war's impact on its economy.
IMF strategy chief Christian Mummsen said the needs estimate was developed before the start of the meetings and could ŌüĀbe expanded after bilateral meetings with finance officials from the IMF's member countries.
"This is preliminary. We're still taking stock," Mummsen said, adding that the list of countries seeking help would likely expand beyond ŌĆŗa dozen.
Georgieva said she was concerned about the physical breakdown of supply chains, especially for Asian countries dependent on oil, natural gas, naphtha, helium, fertilizer and other inputs from Gulf countries.
She said ŌĆŗsuch disruptions are "not going to evaporate overnight, even if the war ends tomorrow. Why? Because a tanker is a slow-moving vessel, it would take 40 days to get all the way to Fiji. So we need to be prepared that the impact of the supply disruptions in the weeks ahead is going to be deeper."
The IMF already has said global economic conditions are worsening beyond those that informed the relatively mild cut in growth it projected on Tuesday in ŌĆŗits updated World Economic Outlook. The global lender's 3.1% forecast for 2026 was based on a swift end to the conflict and drop in oil prices.
Instead, the IMF's chief economist, Pierre-Olivier Gourinchas, said ŌĆŗthe global economy was now "drifting" past that forecast towards a more adverse scenario in the IMF's World Economic Outlook, with 2026 growth falling to 2.5% and oil prices averaging about $100 a barrel for the year.
In its worst-case, "severe scenario" of ŌĆīa deeper ŌüĀand longer conflict, global growth falls to 2% to the brink of global recession.
With more shortages looming, Georgieva said countries should take measures to conserve energy use and create incentives to reduce the oil intensity of their economies, such as temporarily making public transport free.
She repeated the IMF's warnings against countries taking untargeted actions such as broad energy subsidies to offset the impact of higher prices, saying they would only "prolong the pain of high prices."
The IMF's Fiscal Monitor , released on Tuesday, also urged countries to refrain from subsidies and, instead, aid their citizens with targeted, temporary cash transfers for the most vulnerable that do not obscure ŌĆŗhigher fuel prices and do not stoke demand.
IMF ŌĆŗFiscal Affairs Director Rodrigo Valdes said broad fuel ŌüĀsubsidies would do that and shift supplies away from poorer countries, telling a press conference: "If you try to undo a supply shock by trying to prop up demand, you will end up with more inflation."
To prevent the war-driven energy shock from morphing into a 1970s-style runaway inflation problem, ŌĆŗthe IMF has been urging central banks this week to stay vigilant for signs of wage-price spirals but not move right away to tighten ŌĆŗmonetary policy and cool demand.
"What ŌüĀwe tell central banks is, if you have high credibility, signal that your objective is to protect price stability, but don't rush," Georgieva said. "Wait to see how conditions would evolve."
Central banks with less credibility on controlling inflation may need to take stronger measures, she added, without naming specific countries.
Mummsen said financial markets had remained orderly, but some tightening had occurred for emerging markets and developing countries, where borrowing costs are already high.
"In a ŌüĀworld where uncertainty ŌĆŗis high, and shocks keep coming, our message is basically A, economic fundamentals matter and B, policy agility is key," ŌĆŗhe said.
Mummsen said delays or cancellation of fertilizer shipments were hitting developing countries particularly hard, with some estimates predicting that 45 million more people will now face food insecurity. He said higher food costs hit low-income countries harder since they spent ŌĆŗabout 36% of their consumption on food, while the rate was about 20% for emerging markets and just 9% for advanced economies.

item.title: Former Brazil spy chief freed in the US after ICE detention, Bolsonaro ally says
item.description:
SAO PAULO, April 15 (Reuters) - Former Brazilian intelligence chief ŌĆŗAlexandre Ramagem has ŌĆībeen freed in the United States, said Paulo ŌĆŗFigueiredo, an ally ŌĆŗof former President Jair ŌüĀBolsonaro who lives ŌĆŗin the U.S., on ŌĆŗWednesday.
Ramagem was also no longer listed on the website ŌĆŗof U.S. Immigration ŌĆŗand Customs Enforcement among people ŌĆīdetained ŌüĀby the entity. Reuters was not immediately able to determine his ŌĆŗimmigration ŌĆŗstatus ŌüĀin the country.
Ramagem, who fled Brazil ŌĆŗin September following his ŌĆŗconviction ŌüĀfor plotting a coup with Bolsonaro, had been ŌüĀdetained ŌĆŗby ICE ŌĆŗon Monday.

item.title: Australia's Viva Energy says refinery units operating at minimum rates after fire
item.description:
SYDNEY, April 16 (Reuters) - Australia's Viva Energy (VEA.AX) , opens new tab said on ŌĆŗThursday that most ŌĆīunits at its Geelong refinery were still ŌĆŗoperating and processing ŌĆŗfuel though at minimum ŌüĀrates to maintain ŌĆŗsafety across the site ŌĆŗafter a fire overnight.
"We'll only start increasing production ŌĆŗagain once we're ŌĆŗconfident that we can do ŌĆīthat ŌüĀsafely," CEO Scott Wyatt told reporters.
Firefighters were battling a ŌĆŗfire at ŌĆŗViva's ŌüĀmotor gasoline or MOGAS production ŌĆŗunit at the ŌĆŗGeelong ŌüĀrefinery in Victoria state, which processes ŌüĀcrude ŌĆŗinto petrol.

item.title: Colombia announces renewable energy auction
item.description:
BOGOTA, April 15 (Reuters) - Colombian Energy ŌĆŗMinister Edwin Palma on Wednesday announced an upcoming renewable ŌĆīenergy auction, saying that he would travel to the United States next week to promote it.
ŌĆ£We will be ŌĆŗin New York on Monday and Tuesday ŌĆŗdiscussing energy and promoting the renewable energy ŌüĀauction, which we hope will be on the ŌĆŗmarket soon,ŌĆØ Palma said during a conference in ŌĆŗthe city of Cartagena, without providing further details.
Palma also proposed an open-season auction for regasification projects, which the ministry ŌĆŗsaid would be a transparent and competitive ŌĆŗmechanism to secure firm gas-sector capacity, support new infrastructure and ŌĆīstrengthen ŌüĀthe reliability of the energy system.
Open-season auctions are used in the energy and infrastructure sectors to measure demand before projects are developed, helping ensure ŌĆŗfinancial viability ŌĆŗand efficient ŌüĀcapacity allocation, especially in natural gas transportation and regasification.
ŌĆ£This will allow us ŌĆŗto ensure sufficient backup for the system ŌĆŗand ŌüĀfacilitate the integration of clean energy,ŌĆØ Palma said.
Colombia is facing a gas production deficit, as production ŌüĀis ŌĆŗinsufficient to meet domestic demand, ŌĆŗforcing the South American country to increase fuel imports.

item.title: Permian-focused EagleRock plans rare US oil and gas IPO, sources say
item.description:
NEW YORK, April 15 (Reuters) - EagleRock, a company that collects royalties and fees from oil and gas ŌĆīproduction on the land it controls in the Permian Basin, is exploring a U.S. initial public offering that could value it at up to $2 billion, people familiar with the matter said.
The Houston-based firm has hired Goldman Sachs (GS.N) , opens new tab on the potential rare U.S. oil and gas listing, four of ŌĆŗthe sources said, with two adding it could come as soon as the second quarter.
The listing plan comes as ŌĆŗcrude prices have soared due to the conflict in the Middle East . The turmoil has triggered one ŌüĀof the greatest-ever global energy shocks and boosted the appeal of U.S. assets, which have continued to move oil and gas unencumbered ŌĆŗby the war.
EagleRock owns and controls land in the Delaware and Midland portions of the Permian, the heart of the U.S. ŌĆŗshale industry in Texas and New Mexico, as well as infrastructure to handle water used in the hydraulic fracturing process, according to its website.
By holding surface rights, it collects royalties from energy companies operating on its land, allowing EagleRock to earn revenue without producing.
Volatile energy and stock markets mean ŌĆŗthe IPO's timing and the value EagleRock achieves could fluctuate significantly, with the sources forecasting a valuation of between $1 billion ŌĆŗand $2 billion. They cautioned that plans remain subject to change, and spoke on condition of anonymity to discuss confidential deliberations.
EagleRock did not respond to ŌĆīa ŌüĀcomment request. Goldman declined to comment.
Should EagleRock's listing proceed, it would mark a rare U.S. oil and gas IPO. For many years, investors' prioritization of environmental concerns limited appetite for new listings, while private owners found buyers among listed competitors eager for additional resources. While the sale option remains, demand for natural gas to support rising power generation, especially for artificial intelligence ŌĆŗinfrastructure, is drawing many investors back ŌĆŗto oil and gas.
Logical ŌüĀpublic comparisons for EagleRock would be Permian-focused LandBridge (LB.N) , opens new tab , and its water infrastructure affiliate WaterBridge (WBI.N) , opens new tab . LandBridge shares have gained almost 300% since its June 2024 IPO, while WaterBridge is up 26% since listing in ŌĆŗSeptember.
EagleRock's website does not disclose its ownership. Sources said the company's portfolio combines oil and ŌĆŗgas assets from ŌüĀa handful of entrepreneurs. They include Greg Mabee, part of the famed Texas oil family that owns one of the largest ranches within the Permian, according to one of the sources. Mabee did not respond to a comment request.
Among the management listed on EagleRock's website ŌüĀare CEO ŌĆŗGreg Pipkin Jr., who previously led corporate strategy at Infinity Natural Resources (INR.N) , opens new tab , ŌĆŗwhich itself listed in January last year, and Chief Financial Officer Neal Shah, who held the same role at Pioneer Natural Resources when it was bought ŌĆŗfor $60 billion by Exxon Mobil in 2024.

item.title: Nuclear reactor developer XŌĆæEnergy targets $7.5 billion valuation in US IPO
item.description:
April 15 (Reuters) - Amazon-backed (AMZN.O) , opens new tab X-Energy is targeting a valuation of up to $7.51 billion in its initial public offering ŌĆŗin the United States, the nuclear reactor developer said on Wednesday.
The ŌĆīMaryland-based company is seeking up to $814.3 million by offering roughly 42.9 million shares priced between $16 and $19 apiece.
The IPO roadshow launch coincides with the S&P 500 reaching record closing highs, driven by ŌĆŗstrong corporate earnings and optimism in U.S.-Iran negotiations.
"We've seen the IPO ŌĆŗwindow slam shut before, so when companies see an opportunity, they ŌüĀtake it," said Matt Kennedy, senior ŌĆŹstrategist at Renaissance Capital, a provider of IPO-focused ŌĆŗresearch and ETFs.
A choppy market in February and March, weighed down by AI ŌĆŗdisruption worries and geopolitical risks in the Middle East, slowed new listings.
"This is not yet a broad reopening, which is obvious from the glaring lack of tech issues on the ŌĆŗcalendar. Still, it's a step in that direction," Kennedy added.
Nuclear energy is experiencing ŌĆŗa resurgence, driven by surging electricity demand from hyperscalers that operate massive cloud-computing infrastructure to ŌĆīmanage ŌüĀrising AI processing.
Morgan Stanley reports global electricity demand is projected to rise more than 1 trillion kWh annually through 2030, with data centers anticipated to contribute nearly 20% of this growth.
Founded in 2009, X-Energy designs nuclear reactor technology known as ŌĆŗsmall modular reactors (SMR) ŌĆŗand manufactures nuclear ŌüĀfuels.
SMRs are more compact and cost-effective compared to full-sized reactors that often take decades to build.
Cathie Wood's ARK Investment Management ŌĆŗand its affiliated entities have indicated an interest in ŌĆŗpurchasing up ŌüĀto $105 million of shares from X-Energy's offering.
XŌĆæEnergy had previously planned to go public via an Ares Management (ARES.N) , opens new tab -backed blank-check firm in 2023, but later decided not to proceed, citing ŌüĀtough ŌĆŗmarket conditions.
J.P. Morgan, Morgan Stanley, Jefferies, and Moelis ŌĆŗare acting as the lead joint book-running managers. XŌĆæEnergy will list on the Nasdaq under the ŌĆŗsymbol "XE".

item.title: Two children dead,┬ĀRussia's Tuapse port area hit in Ukrainian drone attacks, governor says
item.description:
MOSCOW, April 16 - Two children aged ŌĆŗ5 and 14 ŌĆīhave been killed and a ŌĆŗnumber of ŌĆŗenterprises in the area ŌüĀof Russia's ŌĆŗBlack Sea port ŌĆŗof Tuapse were hit in Ukrainian drone ŌĆŗattacks, local ŌĆŗgovernor Veniamin Kondratiev said ŌĆīon ŌüĀThursday.
In his post on the Telegram messaging ŌĆŗapp, ŌĆŗhe ŌüĀalso said several residential ŌĆŗhouses were ŌĆŗdamaged ŌüĀin the massive drone attacks ŌüĀon ŌĆŗthe region.

item.title: Hopes for Middle East peace grow as Israel discusses Lebanon ceasefire
item.description:
WASHINGTON/JERUSALEM, April 16 (Reuters) - Optimism grew on Thursday that the war in the Middle East may be near an end, with a key Pakistani mediator in Tehran and the administration of President Donald Trump talking up hopes for a deal that would open the ŌĆŗcrucial Strait of Hormuz.
Israel's cabinet met on Wednesday to discuss a possible ceasefire in neighbouring Lebanon, a senior Israeli official said, more than six weeks into its war with Iran-backed Hezbollah. ŌĆīAccording to Lebanese officials, a ceasefire could be announced soon, the Financial Times reported.
Ending the fighting in Lebanon was a key sticking point in earlier peace talks, along with how to deal with Tehran's nuclear ambitions.
"We feel good about the prospects of a deal," White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt said at a news conference on Wednesday, calling conversations mediated by Pakistan "productive and ongoing." She denied reports ŌĆŗthat the U.S. had formally requested an extension of a two-week ceasefire agreed by the two sides on April 8.
More in-person talks had not yet been ŌüĀconfirmed but would likely take place in Pakistan again, Leavitt said.
Pakistan's military confirmed Munir had arrived in Tehran. A senior Iranian source told Reuters that Munir, who had mediated the last round of talks, would seek "to ŌĆŗnarrow gaps" between the two sides. Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araqchi posted on X welcoming Munir and said Tehran was committed to "promoting peace and stability in the region."
The talks last weekend broke down without an agreement to ŌĆŗend the war, which Trump began alongside Israel on February 28, triggering Iranian attacks on Iran's Gulf neighbours as well as reigniting the Israel-Hezbollah conflict.
Stock markets have rallied strongly in recent days on expectations of a swift resolution to the fighting, with indexes on Wall Street hitting record highs on Wednesday as crude oil prices steadied.
Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent predicted that China's purchase of Iranian oil would "pause" given the U.S. blockade on vessels calling at Iranian ports. He said the U.S. could ŌĆŗimpose secondary sanctions on countries that purchase Iranian crude.
The U.S. Treasury had warned two Chinese banks not to process Iranian money or face sanctions, he said, without naming the banks. China previously bought more than 80% ŌĆŗof Iran's shipped oil.
Trump said on Wednesday he had told Chinese President Xi Jinping not to give Iran weapons , which Xi had said he wasn't. Trump also said that China was very happy he was "permanently opening" the Strait of Hormuz.
"I am ŌĆīdoing it for ŌüĀthem, also - And the World," he wrote on social media, adding: "President Xi will give me a big, fat, hug when I get there in a few weeks."
The war has led Iran to effectively shut the Strait - a vital artery for global crude and gas shipments - to ships other than its own, sharply reducing exports from the Gulf and leaving energy importers scrambling for alternative supplies.
During the first 48 hours of the U.S. blockade on ships entering and exiting Iranian ports, no vessels have made it past U.S. forces, the U.S. military said. Additionally, nine vessels complied with direction from U.S. forces to turn around and return toward an Iranian port ŌĆŗor coastal area.
However, Iran's Fars News agency said an ŌĆŗIranian supertanker subject to U.S. sanctions crossed the ŌüĀstrait towards Iran's Imam Khomeini port despite the blockade. Fars did not identify the tanker or give further details of its voyage.
Iran's joint military command warned it would halt trade flows in the Gulf, the Sea of Oman and the Red Sea - which connects to the Suez Canal - if the U.S. blockade continued.
Iran could consider allowing ŌĆŗships to sail freely through the Omani side of the strait without risk of attack as part of proposals it has offered in negotiations with the ŌĆŗU.S., providing a deal is ŌüĀclinched to prevent renewed conflict, a source briefed by Tehran said.
Trump has also threatened to escalate if the war resumes.
"We could take out every one of their bridges in one hour. We could take out every one of their power plants, electric power plants, in one hour. We don't want to do that...so we'll see what happens," he told Fox Business Network.
Iran's nuclear ambitions were a key sticking point at last weekend's talks. The ŌüĀU.S. proposed a ŌĆŗ20-year suspension of all nuclear activity by Iran - an apparent concession from longstanding demands for a permanent ban - while Tehran suggested a ŌĆŗhalt of three to five years, according to people familiar with the proposals.
Washington has also pressed for any enriched nuclear material to be removed from Iran, while Tehran has demanded that international sanctions against it be lifted.
One source involved in the talks said back-channel discussions ŌĆŗhad made progress in narrowing gaps, bringing the two sides closer to a deal that could be presented at a new round of talks.

item.title: Russian missiles hit Kyiv and other Ukrainian cities, three dead
item.description:
April 16 (Reuters) - Russian forces attacked the Ukrainian capital Kyiv and other cities early on Thursday, killing three people, including ŌĆŗa 12-year-old child, injuring more than 20 and badly damaging ŌĆībuildings, officials said.
In Kyiv, Mayor Vitali Klitschko said two people, including the child, had died. In the southeastern city of Dnipro, where Russian attacks set ŌĆŗresidential buildings ablaze, the regional governor said one person was killed.
"At ŌĆŗresult of the enemy attack on the capital, two ŌüĀpeople have been killed, a 12-year-old boy and a 35-year-old woman," Klitschko ŌĆŗwrote on Telegram. "Ten residents have been injured. Six are being treated in ŌĆŗhospital."
Tymur Tkachenko, head of the capital's military administration put the injury toll in the city at 18, including a child.
An air raid alert remained in ŌĆŗeffect in both Kyiv and Dnipro more than two hours after ŌĆŗit had been imposed in the capital.
Photos posted on line showed fires burning ŌĆīout ŌüĀof control and smoke billowing skyward.
Klitschko said rescue teams had rescued a mother and child from a building in a central district where the ground floor was badly damaged.
He also said a missile had hit the ŌĆŗsixth floor of ŌĆŗan apartment building ŌüĀin the central Podil district.
Klitschko said a large fire had broken out in a building in a ŌĆŗdistrict in the north of the capital and four ŌĆŗemergency medical ŌüĀworkers were injured, while debris had fallen in several locations.
In Dnipro, Regional Governor Oleksandr Ganzha wrote on Telegram that one resident had died. He listed 10 people as ŌüĀinjured and ŌĆŗposted pictures showing residential buildings ablaze.
In Kharkiv, ŌĆŗUkraine's second largest city in the northeast, officials said two people had been injured in ŌĆŗdrone strikes.
(This story has been refiled to add the dropped word 'and' in the headline)

item.title: US Senate Republicans back Trump military sales to Israel
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 15 (Reuters) - The U.S. Senate on Wednesday blocked two resolutions that would have stopped the sale ŌĆīof some $450 million in bombs and bulldozers to Israel, as President Donald Trump's fellow Republicans rallied behind his support for the Jewish state.
But support for the resolutions from a large majority of the 47-member Senate Democratic caucus underscored growing frustration within that party about the effect on civilians ŌĆŗfrom Israeli strikes on Gaza, Lebanon and Iran .
A decades-long tradition of strong bipartisan support for Israel in the ŌĆŗU.S. Congress means resolutions to stop weapons sales are unlikely to pass, but backers ŌüĀhope raising the issue will encourage Israel's government and U.S. administrations to do more to protect civilians.
Supporters of ŌĆŗthe sales say Israel is an important ally to whom the United States should sell military equipment.
Senator Bernie Sanders ŌĆŗof Vermont, an independent who caucuses with Democrats, forced votes on the resolutions, saying the sales violate criteria for foreign assistance in the Foreign Assistance Act and the Arms Export Control Act.
The first resolution would have prohibited the $295 million sale of D9R ŌĆŗand D9T Caterpillar bulldozers, parts and other support. The vote was 59 to 40 against advancing the measure.
Seven ŌĆŗDemocrats voted with every Republican against advancing the resolution of disapproval of the bulldozer sale. Republican Senator Cynthia Lummis of Wyoming ŌĆīdid not ŌüĀvote.
The second would have prohibited the $151.8 million sale of 12,000 BLU-110A/B general purpose 1,000-pound "dumb" bombs and related logistics and technical support services.
Eleven Democrats joined every Republican to block the measure by 63 to 36. Republican Senator Thom Tillis of North Carolina did not vote.
Israel uses the bombs in attacks on Gaza and Lebanon and uses the bulldozers to ŌĆŗdemolish homes in Gaza, Lebanon ŌĆŗand the West Bank, ŌüĀSanders said.
"The United States must use the leverage we have - tens of billions in arms and military aid - to demand that Israel ends these atrocities," he said, urging ŌĆŗsupport for the resolutions.
Israel says it does not intentionally target civilians, and that its ŌĆŗstrikes are ŌüĀintended to neutralize militants and military infrastructure.
Wednesday's vote showed an uptick in support for efforts to limit military sales to Israel. In July, two resolutions that would have blocked arms sales in response to civilian casualties in Gaza were blocked ŌüĀin the ŌĆŗSenate.
Also introduced by Sanders, they failed by 73 to 24 and 70 ŌĆŗto 27 in the 100-member chamber.
The Trump administration bypassed the normal congressional review of military sales early in the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran, saying there ŌĆŗwas an emergency that made it necessary to immediately transfer the weapons.

item.title: US-sanctioned supertankers enter Gulf despite blockade
item.description:
SINGAPORE, April 16 (Reuters) - A second U.S.-sanctioned supertanker ŌĆŗhas entered the Gulf via the Strait of Hormuz, shipping data showed, despite ŌĆīa U.S. blockade on vessels visiting Iranian ports.
U.S. President Donald Trump announced the blockade on Sunday after weekend peace talks in Islamabad between the U.S. and Iran failed to reach a deal .
The U.S. Central Command said on X that 10 ŌĆŗvessels have been turned around and no ships have broken through since the start of ŌĆŗthe blockade on Monday.
Still, Iran's Fars News Agency said on Wednesday that an Iranian ŌüĀsupertanker subject to U.S. sanctions crossed the strait towards Iran's Imam Khomeini port despite the blockade. ŌĆŗFars did not identify the tanker or give further details of its voyage.
The empty Very Large Crude ŌĆŗCarrier (VLCC) RHN entered the Gulf on Wednesday, data from LSEG and Kpler showed. It was not immediately clear where the VLCC, which is capable of carrying 2 million barrels of oil, is heading.
The tanker's entry into the Gulf ŌĆŗcomes a day after U.S.-sanctioned VLCC Alicia passed through the Strait of Hormuz. The Alicia is ŌĆŗheading to Iraq, Kpler data showed.
Both tankers have records of carrying Iranian oil in the past few years, according ŌĆīto ŌüĀKpler data.
Vessels that have been forced to turn back include the U.S.-sanctioned tanker Rich Starry , which returned to the Gulf on Wednesday, a day after exiting.
The U.S. has warned it could add secondary sanctions on buyers of Iranian oil in an apparent effort to gain leverage ahead of more negotiations, just weeks ŌĆŗafter Washington loosened the ŌĆŗenforcement of some Iran ŌüĀenergy sanctions.
Iran could consider allowing ships to sail freely through the Omani side of the Strait of Hormuz without risk of attack as part of ŌĆŗproposals it has offered in negotiations with the U.S., providing a deal is ŌĆŗclinched to ŌüĀprevent renewed conflict, a source briefed by Tehran said.
The U.S. blockade is expected to reduce Iran's crude exports, although the OPEC producer could sustain its current production at 3.5 million barrels per day (bpd) for weeks ŌüĀby ŌĆŗstoring oil in onshore tanks, analysts say.
Iran exported 1.84 million ŌĆŗbpd of crude in March and has shipped 1.71 million bpd thus far in April, compared with an average of 1.68 ŌĆŗmillion bpd in 2025, according to Kpler data.

item.title: Former US Marine pilot loses appeal against┬Āextradition from Australia
item.description:
SYDNEY, April 16 (Reuters) - A former ŌĆŗU.S. Marine Corps pilot on Thursday lost an appeal against his extradition from Australia ŌĆīto the United States on charges of violating U.S. arms control laws while training Chinese pilots.
Daniel Duggan, a naturalised Australian citizen, was arrested in the state of New South Wales in October 2022 by Australian Federal Police on a ŌĆŗU.S. request, shortly after returning from China, where he had lived since 2014.
In December ŌĆŗ2024, Australia's then attorney-general Mark Dreyfus approved a U.S. extradition request for him.
Duggan challenged the extradition ŌüĀdecision, arguing it was invalid because the attorney-general made legal errors by failing to properly apply the ŌĆŗcountry's Extradition Act in light of the extradition treaty between Australia and the United States.
But Federal Court of ŌĆŗAustralia Judge James Stellios dismissed his appeal on Thursday, finding no errors in the decision.
"I am not persuaded that the impugned decisions were infected by jurisdictional error. Therefore, the application must be dismissed," he said.
A spokesperson for Attorney-General Michelle Rowland said ŌĆŗthe government noted the Federal Court's ruling.
"Mr Duggan will remain in extradition custody in Australia until his surrender ŌĆŗto the United States of America," they said in a statement.
Duggan, who has six children in Australia, has been ŌĆīin custody ŌüĀsince his arrest.
His wife, Saffrine, told public broadcaster ABC that the family was disappointed by the ruling but would consider its options. "Make no mistake: we will not give up," she said. An appeal can be lodged within 28 days.
The U.S. sought Duggan's extradition for four offences linked to the ŌĆŗtraining of Chinese pilots ŌĆŗin South Africa between ŌüĀ2009 and 2012.
The charges include one count of conspiring to violate U.S. arms export laws and defraud the U.S., which carries a five-year jail term, two arms ŌĆŗexport control violations each carrying potential 20ŌĆæyear prison terms, and one count ŌĆŗof conspiracy to ŌüĀcommit money laundering, also punishable by up to 20 years in jail.
Duggan's legal team had argued that his conduct was not an offence in Australia at the time or when the U.S. requested extradition and so ŌüĀdid not ŌĆŗmeet the requirement for "dual criminality" in Australia's extradition treaty with ŌĆŗthe United States.
The court said the treaty did not modify the Extradition Act to require "dual criminality", and therefore did not require ŌĆŗAustralia to refuse the extradition request from the U.S.

item.title: Chile's Kast unveils slew of reforms including corporate tax rate cut
item.description:
SANTIAGO, April 15 (Reuters) - Chile's new president, Jose Antonio Kast, ŌĆīunveiled on Wednesday details of a long-awaited reform package that includes cuts to the corporate tax rate, as his government seeks to revive growth and promote job stability.
Some 40 measures were outlined in the package, with Kast ŌĆŗsaying that it had five main aims: to make Chile more tax competitive, strengthen formal ŌĆŗemployment, streamline regulations, provide more legal and regulatory certainty and exercise restraint in ŌüĀpublic spending.
"This bill is not an ideological agenda. It is a concrete response to ... real emergencies," ŌĆŗKast said in his first address to the nation since taking office last month, urging members ŌĆŗof Congress to pass the measures quickly.
The right-wing leader has painted Chile, the world's largest copper producer, as a country riddled with organized crime and weak finances.
His government has said it wants to lift Chile's economic annual growth ŌĆŗto around 4% from last year's 2.5%, though analysts have expressed skepticism about whether the goal ŌĆŗis realistic.
Kast also lacks a congressional majority, which could hamper his ability to deliver his agenda. Allied right-wing ŌĆīblocs hold ŌüĀjust 76 of 155 lower-house seats and 25 of 50 Senate seats.
The centerpiece of the bill is proposed gradual cuts in the corporate tax rate to 23% from 27%, which his government has previously said would be over four years. Some opposition members have, however, argued that the real ŌĆŗimpact of a corporate ŌĆŗtax cut remains unclear.
Other ŌüĀtax measures include the creation of a tax credit for wage payments, designed to encourage many smaller companies to pay employees on the books instead ŌĆŗof under the table.
"This injects $1.4 billion annually into the productive sector, benefits ŌĆŗ235,000 SMEs (representing ŌüĀ86% of the credit's recipients), and protects more than 4 million workers. Formal employment will no longer be a penalty but an advantage," he said.
Other reforms include steps to speed up the issuing of ŌüĀenvironmental permits ŌĆŗfor projects such as mines, a temporary VAT exemption on ŌĆŗsales of new homes, 400 billion pesos ($450 million) in funds for fire-hit regions and an exemption for homeowners over 65 ŌĆŗfrom property taxes on their primary residence.
($1 = 884.0000 Chilean pesos)

item.title: US military says its strike in Eastern Pacific killed 3
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 15 (Reuters) - The U.S. military ŌĆŗsaid on Wednesday ŌĆīit conducted a strike on ŌĆŗa vessel ŌĆŗin the Eastern ŌüĀPacific that ŌĆŗkilled 3 people.
The ŌĆŗU.S. Southern Command alleged that the ŌĆŗvessel was ŌĆŗoperated by "Designated Terrorist Organizations" ŌĆīthat ŌüĀit did not identify.
It added that no ŌĆŗU.S. ŌĆŗmilitary ŌüĀforces were harmed and ŌĆŗdescribed those ŌĆŗkilled ŌüĀas "male narco-terrorists," without offering details.

item.title: South Korea, Vietnam leaders to meet in Hanoi next week, sources say
item.description:
HANOI, April 16 (Reuters) - Vietnam's president To Lam is set to meet his South Korean counterpart Lee Jae Myung in Hanoi next week, five people familiar with the matter said.
Lee's visit would ŌĆŗbe the first by a foreign leader to Vietnam since Lam was elected ŌĆīpresident last week, a move that allows him to combine the post with the leadership of the Communist Party, the most powerful job in the one-party state.
It would not be however Lam's first top-level meeting in his ŌĆŗnew position, as he met China's President Xi Jinping in Beijing on Wednesday.
The meeting ŌĆŗis currently scheduled for April 23, the sources said.
Government cooperation agreements are ŌüĀlikely to be signed. Seoul is also interested in some of Vietnam's largest infrastructure projects, ŌĆŗsuch as railways and nuclear power plants, but no major decisions are expected in those areas, ŌĆŗthey said.
South Korea's foreign ministry declined to comment. Vietnam's foreign ministry did not respond to a request for comment.
South Korea is the largest investor in the Southeast Asian nation in terms of accumulated capital, with output from Samsung ŌĆŗfactories alone accounting for more than one-tenth of the country's exports. Yet Korean investment pledges fell ŌĆŗabout 25% last year from 2024, according to Vietnam's government data, amid trade uncertainties and concerns over Vietnam's regulations .
The ŌĆīmeeting would ŌüĀbe the leaders' second in about eight months, after Lam visited Seoul in August , the first foreign leader hosted by Lee after he was elected president in Seoul.
Lee will be accompanied by a large business delegation including officials from Samsung, with multiple corporate agreements expected to be signed, ŌĆŗpossibly on April 23, ŌĆŗofficials have said without ŌüĀelaborating on the possible deals.
Samsung's talks with Vietnamese authorities to build a back-end semiconductor factory have made progress after years of negotiations, but it ŌĆŗremains unclear when any announcement could be made, three people familiar ŌĆŗwith the matter ŌüĀsaid.
Reports of a planned $4 billion investment in a semiconductor testing, and possibly packaging, facility were removed from Vietnamese media in recent days.
The three people said Samsung's potential outlay had not been finalised. The total ŌüĀinitial ŌĆŗinvestment would be smaller and could expand over time, potentially ŌĆŗincluding a cluster of suppliers, one of them said.
Samsung declined to comment.

item.title: Nepal panel to probe property, assets of politicians and officials
item.description:
KATHMANDU, April 16 (Reuters) - Nepal's new government, led by rapper-turned politician Balendra Shah, has set up a panel ŌĆŗto investigate the property and assets of past and ŌĆīpresent politicians and officials, a move aimed at controlling corruption in the Himalayan nation.
Shah, 35, became prime minister after his Rastriya Swatantra Party (RSP) ŌĆŗscored a sweeping victory in the March 5 ŌĆŗparliamentary election ŌĆō the country's first vote after the ŌüĀanti-graft ŌĆśGen ZŌĆØ protests last September.
During his three-year stint ŌĆŗas mayor of Kathmandu, NepalŌĆÖs capital city, Shah gained popularity ŌĆŗfor his fight against corruption and reformist credentials.
Sasmit Pokhrel, a cabinet spokesperson, said the five-member panel would be headed by Rajendra Kumar Bhandari, ŌĆŗa retired Supreme Court judge.
ŌĆ£An impartial investigation will be ŌĆŗcarried out on the basis of evidence based on legal standards ŌĆ” Its ŌĆīreport ŌüĀand recommendations will be implemented by concerned agencies of the government,ŌĆØ Pokhrel told reporters after a cabinet meeting late on Wednesday without mentioning the time frame given ŌĆŗto the panel ŌĆŗto complete ŌüĀwork.
The probe is expected to cover hundreds of politicians and officials who held public ŌĆŗoffices after the popular movement that led ŌĆŗto ŌüĀthe abolition of the 239-year-old monarchy in 2008, analysts said.
The three-year-old RSP had made corruption control one of its ŌüĀmajor promises ŌĆŗduring the election and scored ŌĆŗa comfortable victory over the parties that had dominated politics in the ŌĆŗcountry for decadessuffered.

item.title: Ukraine says it downs 31 missiles, 636 drones in Russian attacks over past 24 hours
item.description:
April 16 (Reuters) - Ukraine's air force downed or ŌĆŗneutralised 31 Russian ŌĆīmissiles and 636 drones in ŌĆŗattacks at the ŌĆŗcountry over the ŌüĀpast 24 hours, ŌĆŗit said on ŌĆŗThursday.
"During this period, the enemy launched two ŌĆŗwaves of ŌĆŗcombined attacks on Ukrainian territory, ŌĆīusing ŌüĀground-based and air-launched missiles, as well as attack ŌĆŗdrones," ŌĆŗthe ŌüĀair force said in ŌĆŗa statement ŌĆŗon ŌüĀTelegram.
Overall, it detected 703 Russian aerial ŌüĀtargets, ŌĆŗit ŌĆŗadded.

item.title: Can Iran legally impose tolls on the Strait of Hormuz?
item.description:
April 16 (Reuters) - Tehran has sought to tighten its grip over the Strait of Hormuz by charging tolls on vessels to ensure safe passage, in conjunction with Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps.
The following explains law governing toll collections and ŌĆŗactions that countries opposed to tolls might take.
The Strait of Hormuz is ŌĆīa waterway connecting the Gulf with the Gulf of Oman, and located within Iran's and Oman's territorial waters. It is perhaps the world's most important energy shipping lane. About 20% of the world's oil passes through it.
The waterway is about 104 miles (167 km) long. Its width varies, ŌĆŗand at its narrowest point provides 2-mile channels for inbound and outbound shipping, separated by a 2-mile buffer ŌĆŗzone.
Iran effectively closed the strait following U.S.-Israeli strikes on the country, and has demanded a right ŌüĀto collect tolls as a precondition to ending the war. The status of any toll collections so far could not ŌĆŗimmediately be confirmed.
The UN Convention on the Law of the Sea , opens new tab , sometimes known as UNCLOS, was ŌĆŗadopted in 1982 and has been in force since 1994.
Article 38 provides vessels a right of unimpeded "transit passage" through more than 100 straits worldwide, including the Strait of Hormuz.
The treaty allows a country bordering a strait to regulate passage within its "territorial sea," up to 12 nautical ŌĆŗmiles from its border, but shall permit "innocent passage."
Passage is innocent if it is not prejudicial to a country's peace, good ŌĆŗorder and security. Military action, serious pollution, spying and fishing are not permitted. The concept of innocent passage was key to a 1949 International Court ŌĆīof ŌüĀJustice case concerning the Corfu Channel, along the coasts of Albania and Greece.
Approximately 170 countries and the European Union have ratified UNCLOS. Iran and the United States have not. This raises the question of whether the treaty's rules affording freedom of maritime navigation have become part of customary international law, or bind only ratifying countries.
Experts say UNCLOS has become or is generally ŌĆŗviewed as customary international law. ŌĆŗSome non-ratifying countries may ŌüĀargue that they need not follow the treaty because they persistently and consistently object. Iran has argued that it has made such objections. The United States disputes Iran's authority to charge ŌĆŗtolls.
There is no formal mechanism to enforce UNCLOS. The International Tribunal for ŌĆŗthe Law ŌüĀof the Sea in Hamburg, Germany, which the treaty established, and the International Court of Justice in The Hague, Netherlands could issue rulings but cannot enforce them.
Countries and businesses have other potential means to counteract tolls.
A willing state or coalition of states could ŌüĀtry to ŌĆŗenforce the treaty. The UN Security Council could pass a resolution opposing ŌĆŗtolls.
Companies could redirect shipments away from the Strait of Hormuz, and have begun doing so. Countries could expand sanctions targeting financial transactions believed to benefit ŌĆŗIran's government, by sanctioning companies willing to pay tolls.

item.title: Malaysia's Petronas to supply excess fuel to Australia as leaders vow closer energy ties
item.description:
SYDNEY/KUALA LUMPUR, April 16 (Reuters) - Malaysia Prime Minister Anwar Ibrahim said on Thursday that state energy firm ŌĆŗPetronas (IPO-PETO.KL) , opens new tab would give priority to providing Australia with its excess fuel supplies, ŌĆīas the two countries sought to bolster energy security following the disruptions caused by the Middle East conflict .
Anwar said he had obtained an assurance from Petronas on the matter following discussions with his ŌĆŗAustralian counterpart Anthony Albanese on boosting agricultural and fuel trade.
"The priority ŌĆŗis for domestic requirements... but here is where negotiations among friends ŌüĀare critical," Anwar said at a press conference after the meeting ŌĆŗat his office in Malaysia's administrative capital Putrajaya.
Malaysia also requires mineral phosphates from ŌĆŗAustralia, which it could offer in exchange for urea supplied to Canberra, Anwar said.
Malaysia is Albanese's third stop in Southeast Asia in the past week, following trips to Singapore and Brunei, as Canberra looks ŌĆŗto shore up food and energy supplies.
Albanese said his government had secured ŌĆŗan additional 100 million litres of diesel from two shipments, one from Brunei and ŌĆīone ŌüĀfrom South Korea.
"This is the first of many expected shipments secured under the government's new strategic reserve powers with the support of Export Finance Australia," he said.
Australia also pledged to honour existing contracts with Malaysia, despite disruptions, Albanese ŌĆŗsaid.
Australia is a ŌĆŗmajor supplier of ŌüĀnatural gas to Malaysia, which imports about 20% of its domestic supplies, as well as a provider of wheat, ŌĆŗlamb and beef products.
Anwar and Albanese also witnessed the ŌĆŗsigning of ŌüĀa memorandum of understanding between the two countries on halal meat trade, a deal that the Australian leader said would boost the country's red meat exports ŌüĀand ŌĆŗsupport Malaysia's food security.
Majority-Muslim Malaysia is widely ŌĆŗconsidered as a global leader in halal processing due to its established certification standards.

item.title: Aluminium in crisis: war, tariffs and a market running on empty
item.description:
LONDON, April 16 (Reuters) - The Iran war is triggering an unprecedented crisis in the global aluminium market with potentially devastating knock-on effects across sectors as diverse as construction, packaging, transport and green energy.
Even if the war were to end tomorrow, it could take up to a year for Emirates Global Aluminium to recover from ŌĆŗthe damage inflicted by a missile strike on its Al Taweelah smelter last month.
Aluminium Bahrain (ALBH.BH) , opens new tab , the largest single-site production plant outside of China, has also been hit, although the extent ŌĆīof the damage is currently unknown. Alba had already reduced output prior to the attack, as had Qatar Aluminium, due to a shortage of power.
With shipping through the Strait of Hormuz severely constrained, the loss of production could rise further as smelters run through their stocks of raw materials.
The global market is looking at a supply deficit of up to 4 million metric tons this year, according to consultancy Wood Mackenzie.
Western buyers will bear the brunt of that massive ŌĆŗsupply hit and policymakers will face some hard choices in the weeks ahead if they want to cushion the impact.
In times gone by the market could have ŌĆŗturned to the London Metal Exchange (LME) for extra metal. Registered inventory exceeded 5 million tons in the first part of the last decade.
LME stocks have ŌüĀsince shrunk to under 400,000 tons with another 100,000 tons sitting in the off-warrant category.
CME warehouses have also been raided. Total deliverable stocks have slumped by 70% since the start of the year ŌĆŗand now total just 1,864 tons.
Even those figures flatter to deceive. Russian metal, which many Western users can't use due to sanctions imposed after the invasion of Ukraine , accounted for 270,000 tons of registered LME ŌĆŗinventory at the end of March.
Traders have been tussling over the non-Russian component. Someone cancelled 98,000 tons of LME-registered Indian aluminium in the first week of March, only to re-warrant most of it last week as time-spreads exploded.
The benchmark cash-to-three-months spread flexed out to a backwardation of $95.50 per ton, the tightest the market's been since 2007.
There is idled smelter capacity, particularly in the U.S. and Europe, that could in theory be reactivated to help ŌĆŗalleviate the squeeze on physical metal supplies.
However, most of this capacity was taken offline during previous energy crises. Smelters produce metal by electrolysis, and a typical one can use as much power as ŌĆŗa city the size of Boston.
Given the impact of the Iran war on energy prices, it seems highly unlikely much, if any, of the mothballed capacity will return.
Indeed, the global shortage of affordable power was already ŌĆīforcing more closures ŌüĀeven before the start of hostilities in the Gulf.
The Mozal aluminium smelter in Mozambique, majority owned and operated by Australia's South32 (S32.AX) , opens new tab , was placed on care and maintenance in March after the company failed to get an economically viable power supply contract.
Even allowing for greater recycled production and softer demand due to the energy hit on manufacturing activity, "there is no escaping a large deficit in the global aluminium market over the next 18 months," according to Wood Mackenzie.
That deficit is going to be felt most acutely in the West, which is going to force governments to make some unpalatable choices.
Two countries ŌĆŗcould help alleviate the shortfall.
The first is China, ŌĆŗthe world's largest aluminium producer. The problem ŌüĀis that China tends to process most of its metal into semi-manufactured products such as rod, plate and wire.
The rest of the world has spent the last decade erecting trade barriers against the Chinese export flood, accusing Beijing of undermining competitors.
Western aluminium users need primary metal and alloy, not ŌĆŗmore cheap Chinese product exports.
That leaves Russia, which produces both primary metal and the array of value-added alloys produced in the Gulf.
Japanese manufacturers are already ŌĆŗshowing signs of returning to Russian ŌüĀsupply, having self-sanctioned after the 2022 invasion.
U.S. and European buyers would need government sanction waivers to follow suit.
The situation in the United States is compounded by President Donald Trump's decision to hike import aluminium tariffs to 50%.
That has sent the cost of imported ingot to over $2,500 per ton above the LME price , which is itself hovering at four-year highs of $3,580 per ton.
For now, this remains a price and cost equation. The longer the ŌüĀGulf disruption runs, the ŌĆŗfaster stocks deplete.
At some stage it may stop being a question of price altogether and become a question of simply having enough ŌĆŗmetal to meet manufacturing orders.
(The opinions expressed here are those of Andy Home , a columnist for Reuters.)

item.title: China added more crude to its massive stockpile in March, but outlook shifts
item.description:
LAUNCESTON, Australia, April 16 (Reuters) - China continued to build the world's largest stockpile of crude oil in March even as the rest of the world started to draw on inventories to compensate for the loss of millions of barrels from the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz.
China, ŌĆŗthe world's biggest crude importer, had surplus oil of 1.74 million barrels per day (bpd) in March, according to calculations based on official data.
For the first quarter ŌĆīChina's excess crude was 1.41 million bpd, down from the record high of 2.67 million bpd in December but up from the average of 1.13 million bpd for the whole of 2025.
China does not disclose the volumes of crude flowing into or out of its strategic and commercial stockpiles, but an estimate can be made by deducting the amount of oil processed from the total crude available from imports and domestic output.
It ŌĆŗis worth noting that not all the surplus crude was likely to have been added to storage, with some being processed in plants not captured by the official ŌĆŗdata.
But even allowing for those gaps, it is clear that from March 2025 onwards, China has been importing crude at a far higher rate ŌüĀthan necessary to meet domestic fuel demand.
China's imports of crude oil were 11.77 million bpd in March, while domestic output was 4.49 million bpd.
Refinery processing was 14.52 million bpd, leaving a surplus ŌĆŗof 1.74 million bpd available for storage.
The continuing building of China's crude stockpiles in March came as imports were largely unaffected by the conflict in the Middle East, which started on February ŌĆŗ28 when the United States and Israel launched an aerial campaign against Iran.
The oil delivered to China in March would have exited the Strait of Hormuz prior to the conflict's start, but imports from April onwards are likely to be affected.
This raises the question as to how China responds to lower crude imports from April onwards, which may be further constrained if the U.S. military is successful in executing President Donald Trump's ŌĆŗorder to blockade vessels from Iranian ports.
China's seaborne crude imports in April are estimated by commodity analysts Kpler at 8.7 million bpd, and while this figure is likely to increase as ŌĆŗmore cargoes are assessed as arriving prior to month end, it's still likely that arrivals will be the lowest since the 7.97 million bpd in August 2022.
China can tap some of its vast stockpile ŌĆīof crude oil ŌüĀif it wants to keep domestic refinery processing around current rates.
The exact amount in both commercial and strategic inventories is not disclosed by Beijing, but analyst estimates are that it is at least 1.2 billion barrels.
If China did release up to 2 million bpd from reserves in order to maintain refinery throughput, it would take almost two years for the stockpile to deplete.
This is obviously an extreme situation and the reality is that crude oil flows from the Middle East will at some point return to levels close to what they were prior to the ŌĆŗstart of the conflict.
This means China could easily ŌĆŗmaintain refinery processing, and even increase it ŌüĀto allow for exports of fuels, something Beijing has placed informal restrictions on since the start of the conflict.
China exported 1.19 million bpd of refined fuels in March, according to calculations based on official customs data, but this is likely to drop sharply in April, with Kpler ŌĆŗtracking shipments of about 524,000 bpd for the month.
The loss of refined fuel exports from China has tightened an already stressed market ŌĆŗfor products in Asia, with ŌüĀjet fuel and diesel both hitting record high prices during March.
It's likely that Beijing is expecting the Iran conflict to end in the coming weeks and that the flow of crude and products from the Middle East will resume.
However, if the Strait of Hormuz remains closed for an extended period the risks mount for China.
While it can maintain domestic fuel supplies for an extended period because of ŌüĀits huge crude ŌĆŗreserves, if Asian neighbours experience shortages and their economies start to shut down it won't take long for ŌĆŗthis to blow back on China's export-focused industries.
Enjoying this column? Check out Reuters Open Interest (ROI) , your essential new source for global financial commentary. ROI delivers thought-provoking, data-driven analysis of everything from swap rates to soybeans. Markets are moving faster than ŌĆŗever. ROI can help you keep up. The views expressed here are those of the author, a columnist for Reuters.

item.title: Spain's Repsol wins back control of Venezuelan oil operations, FT says
item.description:
April 16 (Reuters) - Spanish energy group Repsol (REP.MC) , opens new tab is ŌĆŗpoised to take back operational control of its ŌĆīVenezuelan oil assets and boost production following a deal signed with the South American government, the Financial Times reported on ŌĆŗThursday.
Repsol is expected to announce the agreement as ŌĆŗearly as Thursday, FT added, citing a ŌüĀperson familiar with the matter.
The agreement will include ŌĆŗplans to triple production from its Venezuelan oil operations within ŌĆŗthree years and establish a "guaranteed" payment system that will avoid previous pitfalls under which the capital city of Caracas failed ŌĆŗto pay up, according to the report.
Reuters could ŌĆŗnot immediately verify the report. Repsol did not immediately respond to ŌĆīReuters' ŌüĀrequest for a comment.
Venezuela holds one of the largest oil reserves in the world but has dilapidated energy infrastructure.
In 2023, Repsol reached an agreement with ŌĆŗVenezuela to continue ŌĆŗoperating its ŌüĀfacilities there. The deal later lapsed after U.S. President Donald Trump revoked licences granted ŌĆŗto Repsol and other Western companies to ŌĆŗoperate ŌüĀin the country.
After the U.S. captured President Nicolas Maduro in January, Washington eased sanctions on Venezuela's energy sector, ŌüĀissuing general ŌĆŗlicences that allow global energy ŌĆŗcompanies to operate oil and gas projects in the OPEC member.

item.title: Thailand's PTT offers more fuel oil for May loading, sources say
item.description:
SINGAPORE, April 16 (Reuters) - Thai state-owned energy company PTT has offered a ŌĆŗlarger volume of high-sulphur ŌĆīfuel oil (HSFO) for loading in May than in previous months, according ŌĆŗto market sources.
PTT offered four ŌĆŗcargoes of HSFO in a ŌüĀtender that closes on ŌĆŗThursday, sources said. The cargoes will ŌĆŗload from the port of Sriracha.
One of the cargoes consist of 18,000 ŌĆŗtons of fuel oil ŌĆŗscheduled to load between May 5 and ŌĆī7, ŌüĀthe sources said, while the other three cargoes each consist of 25,000 tons, scheduled to ŌĆŗload ŌĆŗbetween May ŌüĀ15-17, May 23-25, and May 29-31, respectively.
The ŌĆŗrefiner typically offers one ŌĆŗto ŌüĀtwo HSFO cargoes for loading per month via Sriracha, based ŌüĀon ŌĆŗpast records.
Companies typically do ŌĆŗnot comment on commercial tenders.

item.title: Duke Energy seeks price increase in North Carolina to recoup winter power costs
item.description:
April 15 (Reuters) - Duke Energy (DUK.N) , opens new tab has sought approval from North Carolina regulators to raise its prices and ŌĆŗrecover more than $800 million from customers for ŌĆīhigher fuel and power purchase costs incurred during an extreme winter cold snap.
It was seeking to recover winter ŌĆŗfuel and power costs totaling about $500 million ŌĆŗat Duke Energy Carolinas and $309 million at ŌüĀDuke Energy Progress, it said on Wednesday, ŌĆŗwhich would on approval raise monthly bills by ŌĆŗabout $6.90 and $7.88, respectively, starting June 1.
Duke Energy Carolinas serves about 2.3 million customers in central and western North Carolina, ŌĆŗwhile Duke Energy Progress supplies about 1.6 ŌĆŗmillion customers in the central and eastern parts of the ŌĆīstate.
The ŌüĀextreme cold in late January and early February drove demand beyond the existing generation and storage capacity, forcing it to buy additional power from ŌĆŗneighboring utilities ŌĆŗat high ŌüĀmarket prices, the company said.
Duke Energy has proposed spreading the recovery over ŌĆŗ19 months, instead of the typical ŌĆŗ12-month ŌüĀperiod, to limit the immediate impact on customers.
It said electricity demand hit a new winter high ŌüĀof ŌĆŗ37,308 megawatt-hours on January 27, ŌĆŗthe highest level ever recorded across Duke Energy's Carolinas system.

item.title: Trump says Lebanese-Israeli leaders to speak for first time in decades
item.description:
BEIRUT/JERUSALEM, April 16 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump said leaders of Lebanon and Israel will ŌĆŗspeak, saying he was "trying to get a little breathing room" between the countries, ŌĆīafter more than six weeks of war between Israel and the Lebanese armed group Hezbollah.
"It has been a long time since the two leaders have spoken, like 34 years. It will happen tomorrow. Nice!" ŌĆŗTrump wrote in a social media post published before midnight on Wednesday, Washington ŌĆŗtime.
It did not say which Lebanese and Israeli leaders would speak, or ŌüĀgive any further details.
The Israeli Prime MinisterŌĆÖs office did not immediately respond to a request ŌĆŗfor comment. There was no immediate response to a request for comment from the offices ŌĆŗof Lebanese President Joseph Aoun and Prime Minister Nawaf Salam.
The conflict spiralled out of the U.S.-Israeli war with Iran, with the Iran-backed Hezbollah opening fire in support of Tehran on March 2, prompting an Israeli ŌĆŗoffensive in Lebanon just 15 months after the last conflict.
Washington on Wednesday expressed optimism about reaching ŌĆŗa deal to end the war with Iran.
Israel's security cabinet convened late on Wednesday to discuss a possible ŌĆīLebanon ŌüĀceasefire , a senior Israeli official said. Another senior Israeli official and a senior Lebanese official said Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu's government was under heavy pressure from Washington to reach a ceasefire in Lebanon.
Netanyahu, in a video statement released late on Wednesday, said the Israeli military ŌĆŗcontinued to strike at ŌĆŗHezbollah and was ŌüĀabout to "overcome" the southern Lebanese town of Bint Jbeil.
The senior Lebanese official said that LebanonŌĆÖs assessment was that Israel wanted to secure a ŌĆŗvictory in Bint Jbeil before diplomatic progress could be made.
The Israeli ŌĆŗmilitary said ŌüĀits troops were continuing "targeted ground operations in southern Lebanon".
In Israel, sirens rang out warning of incoming rocket fire, sending residents of several Israeli northern towns running to bomb shelters. There were no ŌüĀimmediate ŌĆŗreports of injuries.
Hezbollah kept up its attacks, firing rockets ŌĆŗat two towns in Israel, the group's al-Manar television reported.

item.title: Prince Harry praises Australia's leadership on social media ban as Meghan tells of online bullying
item.description:
SYDNEY, April 16 (Reuters) - Britain's Prince Harry on Thursday praised Australia's "epic" leadership on curbing harmful social ŌĆīmedia use for teens, as his wife Meghan, Duchess of Sussex, spoke of a decade of online abuse.
The Duke and Duchess of Sussex are on a four-day trip to Australia, with engagements covering sport, mental health and veterans' affairs.
ŌĆŗAustralia in December became the first country in the world to ban social media for ŌĆŗchildren under 16, blocking them from platforms including TikTok, Alphabet's (GOOGL.O) , opens new tab YouTube and Meta's (META.O) , opens new tab ŌüĀInstagram and Facebook, in legislation being copied around the world.
"Now we can sit here and debate ŌĆŗthe pros and cons of a ban ŌĆō I'm not here to judge that. All I will say ŌĆŗis from a responsibility and leadership standpoint ŌĆō epic," Harry said in a discussion with young people organised by Australian mental health organisation Batyr in Melbourne on Thursday.
Meghan told the same discussion she had been the target of relentless online ŌĆŗbullying.
"For now, 10 years, every day for 10 years, I have been bullied and attacked. And I ŌĆŗwas the most trolled person in the entire world," she said.
The couple stepped down as working members of the ŌĆīBritish ŌüĀroyal family and moved to the U.S. in 2020, citing a desire to be financially independent and to escape what they characterised as media intrusion into their private lives.
They last visited Australia in 2018 while still working royals, announcing Meghan's first pregnancy hours after arriving in Sydney.
Harry and Meghan have ŌĆŗreceived a mixed reception in ŌĆŗAustralia, where Britain's ŌüĀKing Charles is the head of state, though a sizeable minority supports becoming a republic.
The couple's travel is being privately funded, though local media reported some ŌĆŗpolicing costs associated with the visit would be paid by Australian taxpayers, ŌĆŗsparking a ŌüĀprotest petition signed by more than 45,000 people.
In contrast to their previous visit, the Sussexes are also undertaking what their office called "private meetings and special projects" while in Australia.
While Harry met military veterans on Wednesday, Meghan ŌüĀfilmed ŌĆŗan episode of cookery show MasterChef Australia, where she was a guest ŌĆŗjudge.
She is also co-hosting a luxury wellness retreat in Sydney over the weekend. Tickets for the event, which includes yoga, manifestation ŌĆŗand sound healing, start from A$2,699 ($1,912) per person.

item.title: The Iran war has shattered oil's price compass
item.description:
LONDON, April 16 (Reuters) - The Iran war , opens new tab has torn open a dangerous rift in global oil markets: physical crude is trading at record highs while futures benchmarks signal calm. That disconnect is forcing consumers, companies and policymakers to navigate without a reliable compass - and it could leave a lasting scar on the global economy.
Investors have struggled to price the unprecedented disruption to global oil ŌĆŗsupplies since Iran effectively closed the Strait of Hormuz following the U.S.-Israeli strikes on February 28.
The blockade has cut off nearly a fifth of global oil flows, triggering acute supply shortages in Asia and, increasingly, ŌĆīin Europe and forcing Gulf producers to shut in around 9 million barrels per day (bpd) of production.
Global benchmark Brent crude futures surged 64% in March, setting a record monthly gain, to a peak of $118 a barrel. Prices then fell back to around $95 after the U.S. and Iran agreed to a ceasefire on April 7. But after the failure of talks to end the war, U.S. President Donald Trump on Monday imposed a blockade on ships entering and exiting Iranian ports and coastal areas, pushing prices back up to around $100.
Those moves may appear extreme, but given the sheer scale of the actual ŌĆŗsupply losses ŌĆō now topping some 600 million barrels ŌĆō they look surprisingly restrained.
Indeed, prices paid by refineries for oil needed to make products such as gasoline, diesel and jet fuel have reacted far more forcefully. Dated Brent , ŌĆŗwhich refers to the price of physical crude delivered to northwest Europe, is trading at $120 a barrel - about 65% above pre-war levels and the highest since 2022.
Other physical ŌüĀbenchmarks have surged to record highs. North Sea Forties crude briefly hit a peak of nearly $150 a barrel on Monday. All this points to an acute supply shortage.
The current disconnect between paper and physical Brent is as wide as it ŌĆŗhas ever been ŌĆō even compared with the depths of the COVID-19 pandemic, when global oil demand collapsed by around 20 million bpd as economies shut down.
This disparity is baffling many traders and analysts.
Technical factors explain part of the divergence. Brent ŌĆŗfutures reflect barrels delivered roughly two months forward, while Dated Brent is used for oil loading within 10 to 30 days. In short, the gap simply shows that futures markets are betting on a rapid easing of the crisis.
One explanation for this optimism is the widespread market belief ŌĆō or hope ŌĆō that Trump will avoid prolonging the conflict for fear of driving U.S. gasoline prices sharply higher . Relatively ample global inventories heading into the war may also have cushioned initial concerns.
But that optimism flies in the face of the reality on the ground ŌĆō Middle East ŌĆŗoil production and exports would take months, if not years , to recover to pre-war levels even once Hormuz is fully reopened.
If oil supplies currently being priced into the futures market fail to materialise by early summer - the peak demand ŌĆŗperiod in the northern hemisphere - the misalignment within the Brent complex will only expand ŌĆō with broad consequences for the global economy.
A breakdown in Brent futures pricing - the bedrock of global oil markets - risks distorting the economic decisions of governments, companies and consumers ŌĆīworldwide.
Policymakers appear to be ŌüĀhedging their bets.
On Tuesday, the International Monetary Fund raised its baseŌĆæcase forecast for 2026 oil prices by 30% to $82 a barrel compared with its previous forecast in January. It simultaneously cut its global growth forecast for this year by 0.2 percentage point to 3.1%.
That looks far more aligned with what the oil futures market is signalling than with the facts on the ground.
The shape of the futures curve suggests oil prices in the coming months will remain well within the recent historic norm.
But, once again, the physical world tells a very different story.
While oil feedstock prices skyrocketed last month, logistical realities have thus far limited demand destruction among refiners. Because of shipping delays, many refineries were still processing preŌĆæwar feedstock cargoes last month, ŌĆŗmeaning they were paying lower prices while selling refined products ŌĆŗat inflated levels, generating windfall profits.
While some Asian ŌüĀrefiners throttled back early in the conflict to conserve inventories, the positive economics meant that much activity continued unabated.
That cushion has now largely disappeared, however. The last feedstock cargoes that left the Middle East before the blockade have been absorbed in Asia, which relies on the region for about 60% of its crude imports, and in Europe.
Refining margins ŌĆŗare thus compressing rapidly, raising the likelihood that plants will soon cut run rates significantly or shut units altogether, causing a pronounced drop in economic activity that could take ŌĆŗmany by surprise.
Energy prices also impact ŌüĀgovernmentsŌĆÖ inflation calculations ŌĆō even if central banks are expected to look through short-term energy price shocks ŌĆō and distorted market signals may cause policymakers to avoid taking necessary actions or to take the wrong ones.
The steep Brent forward curve also complicates hedging strategies for airlines and other fuelŌĆæintensive businesses, potentially adding further costs or discouraging risk-taking altogether, further weighing on economic output.
Ultimately, what makes this crisis especially dangerous is not just the loss of oil, but the loss of clarity.
When physical barrels trade at $150 ŌüĀwhile futures imply ŌĆŗrelief around the corner, consumers, companies and policymakers are forced to navigate without a reliable compass.
That leaves them especially vulnerable if the Hormuz ŌĆŗdisruption drags on and the futures market is forced to abandon its optimism and reprice violently, upending global economies along the way.
(The opinions expressed here are those of Ron Bousso , a columnist for Reuters.)

item.title: US coal gets a reprieve, but it wonŌĆÖt last
item.description:
NEW YORK, April 16 (Reuters) - The combination of U.S. policy support and the Iran war has handed coal an unlikely reprieve. But don't call it a comeback.
IranŌĆÖs closure of the Strait of Hormuz - the worldŌĆÖs critical energy artery - drove global seaborne thermal coal prices up over 25% in the ŌĆŗfirst few weeks of TehranŌĆÖs war with the U.S. and Israel . Prices rose to their highest level since late 2024, adding fresh momentum to an already unexpected rebound.
However, this price increase reflected short-term market ŌĆīconditions rather than a structural recovery in coal economics, which has become apparent as prices have already slipped to just 15% above pre-war levels.
These recent moves come after coal recorded several positive milestones over the past year. U.S. consumption rose 10% in 2025, snapping a 15-year decline, driven by an increase in natural gas prices , U.S. President Donald Trump's policy support , and record electricity demand from data centers and other artificial intelligence infrastructure. Global coal production and consumption also marginally increased last year, mainly on the back of growing energy demand in emerging economies.
Nevertheless, thermal coal prices in ŌĆŗthe first half of 2025 still hit a four-year low after demand failed to keep pace with the production surge that followed Russia's invasion of Ukraine. China, which accounts for about 56% of global coal demand , opens new tab and is the worldŌĆÖs ŌĆŗlargest coal consumer, fueled much of the extra consumption with domestic output , which left the seaborne market oversupplied.
Looking past recent years, the U.S. coal industry has had a brutal few ŌüĀdecades punctuated by bankruptcies, restructurings, and consolidations. From 2012 to 2022, more than 60 , opens new tab U.S. coal companies filed for bankruptcy, including some of the largest in the industry, with at least eight more since then , opens new tab .
The future does not look much rosier. In December, ŌĆŗthe International Energy Agency projected that global coal demand would likely plateau and drop slightly by 2030, due to slowing demand from China , as renewables, nuclear power and abundant gas erode its dominance in power generation. While that was before the Iran energy ŌĆŗshock, there is no indication that this projection has changed significantly.
Therefore, despite ŌĆ£black goldŌĆÖsŌĆØ short-term gains last month, the long-term trajectory is clear: coal, the single largest source of global carbon emissions, is in structural decline in the U.S. and likely to stagnate globally.
Coal was once the primary source of U.S. electricity, but its diminished importance has long been visible in the worldŌĆÖs largest economy.
There has been little meaningful new investment over the past decade. While there is currently one new coal plant under construction in Alaska , opens new tab , itŌĆÖs the countryŌĆÖs first since 2013.
Most ŌĆŗU.S. operators are instead retiring coal-fired plants because the facilities, many of which are over 50 years old, are increasingly uneconomic to maintain and operate.
The Trump administration is seeking to counter this. It has made expanding energy production a top ŌĆŗpriority to help deliver the vast amount of electricity needed to fuel the AI revolution.
But even though Energy Secretary Chris Wright said in September that Washington had been in talks with U.S. utilities and expected the majority of the several dozen U.S. coal plants nearing ŌĆīretirement to delay ŌüĀclosure, this may just be postponing the inevitable.
ThatŌĆÖs because keeping aging plants online can impose real costs on ratepayers .
A case study , opens new tab of a Michigan coal plant illustrates the risk. The Environmental Defense Fund found that forcing the uneconomic facility to remain available shifted costs onto customers and crowded out investment in cheaper energy sources.
Government funding to prop up the industry may make the problem worse for consumers, as federal mandates to extend coal plant operations could cost ratepayers as much as $6 billion per year, according to Grid Strategies , opens new tab .
Moreover, extending coal plant operations requires continued policy intervention, cost recovery measures, and potentially emergency orders. Such support is unlikely to continue if the White House switches parties following the next election in 2028 - and that outlook is ŌĆŗtoo uncertain to anchor long-term investment.
Even if U.S. federal policy ŌĆŗdoes boost coal supply in the coming years, it ŌüĀcannot guarantee that demand follows suit ŌĆō especially given stiff competition from natural gas and renewables. If a supply-demand mismatch materializes, the result will be a glut and, consequently, lower prices. ThatŌĆÖs exactly what happened after the post-Ukraine-invasion supply surge.
ItŌĆÖs true that institutional capital has recently been returning to coal, after shunning it for more than a decade. ThatŌĆÖs largely ŌĆŗbecause coal companies have consolidated, cut costs, and returned cash to shareholders in recent years. Consequently, their share prices have recovered from depressed levels to outperform broader equity indices ŌĆŗover the past five years.
But ŌüĀsuch outperformance is unlikely to prove persistent. Environmental liability and litigation risk are increasingly priced into coal companiesŌĆÖ valuations, a drag on long-term performance.
The Middle East energy shock and U.S. executive actions in favor of the coal industry could continue to draw some investor interest, but a temporary demand boost will not resolve the industry's structural challenges.
Unless coal companies find creative ways to raise profitability, their recent outperformance is likely to prove fleeting - as coal equity prices, now below pre-war levels, already suggest.
(The views expressed here ŌüĀare those of ŌĆŗPreetha Jenarthan, a Senior Research Associate, and Dr. Gautam Jain, a Senior Research Scholar, at the Center on Global Energy Policy (CGEP) of Columbia UniversityŌĆÖs ŌĆŗSchool of International and Public Affairs (SIPA).)
